name: Aardonyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses arm features that are intermediate between basal sauropodomorphs and more derived sauropods, including a y-shaped ulna and a radius shifted cranially.
It was a plant-eating dinosaur that normally moved on two legs but could drop to four, with an estimated length of around 4 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a key evolutionary link showing how early ancestors of giant four-legged giants began to acquire the specific skeletal features necessary for quadrupedal life.

name: Abdarainurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Alagteeg Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It is known only from a small collection of tail vertebrae and chevrons, lacking a complete skeleton.
This giant herbivorous sauropod reached massive sizes and lived during the Late Cretaceous period in Mongolia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It belongs to the titanosaur group, a lineage of sauropods characterized by their immense size and barrel-shaped bodies.

name: Abditosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Conques Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses an ossified sternal rib and an ossified calcaneum, bones that had previously been considered to have completely disappeared in titanosaur specimens.
It is estimated to have reached a length of 17.5 meters and weighed approximately 14 tonnes, making it the largest titanosaur found on the Ibero-Armorican Island.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known as the most complete titanosaur skeleton ever discovered in Europe, featuring a rare semi-articulated neck with twelve preserved cervical vertebrae.

name: Abelisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

A relatively deep skull featuring very large window-like openings (fenestrae) to reduce weight, with rough ridges on the snout and above the eyes that likely supported a keratinous crest rather than bony horns
An estimated body length of approximately 7.4 to 11 meters and a weight of around 1.65 to 3 tonnes, functioning as a bipedal carnivore and apex predator
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs known exclusively from a single partial skull, lacking lower jaws and most of the palate

name: Abrictosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A distinctive set of large, canine-like tusks (caniniforms) in the upper and lower jaws, separated from the cheek teeth by a gap, along with a hard beak at the front of the mouth.
A small, bipedal herbivore or omnivore measuring approximately 1.2 meters (3.9 ft) in length and weighing less than 45 kilograms (99 lb).
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being one of the earliest known members of the Heterodontosauridae family, characterized by its unusual mix of different tooth types including prominent tusks.

name: Abrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A boxy head topped with a tall, bony arch containing the nostrils, featuring large openings separated by thin, bony struts
A relatively small quadrupedal herbivore measuring no more than 9.1 meters (30 ft) long
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A Middle Jurassic sauropod from China known for its delicate, hollow skull structure

name: Abydosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive skull with narrow, sharp teeth and relatively small external nostrils, which differentiates it from other sauropods like Giraffatitan.
An estimated length of approximately 18.3 meters (60 feet), classified as a brachiosaurid sauropod that fed on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very few sauropods known from a nearly complete skull, specifically the first complete Cretaceous sauropod skull described from the Americas.

name: Acantholipan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pen Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a spike-like osteoderm, a bony armor plate, which is a defining characteristic of its armored dinosaur family.
This herbivore was a relatively small nodosaurid, likely measuring around two to three meters in length, and lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the first species of its heavily armored dinosaur group to be formally named from fossils discovered in Mexico.

name: Achelousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A pair of long, outward-curving spikes on the neck frill and large, rough bony bosses above the eyes and on the snout instead of horns
Approximately 6 meters long and weighing 3 tons, this four-legged herbivore used a hooked beak to eat plants
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a horned dinosaur with a large bony frill and distinctive head ornaments that look like a mix between a triceratops and a styracosaurus

name: Acheroraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A complete right maxilla with several maxillary teeth and a referred dentary (lower jaw)
A small dromaeosaurid with relatively weak jaws adapted for rapid, slashing bites on smaller prey
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the very last non-avian dinosaurs to exist, living in the Hell Creek Formation immediately before the mass extinction event

name: Achillesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It is a theropod dinosaur belonging to the alvarezsaurid family, a group known for having highly specialized, reduced forelimbs with a single massive claw on each hand.
It was a relatively large basal alvarezsaurid, estimated to be around 1.5 meters long, and likely fed on insects such as ants or termites.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its genus name translates to 'Achilles lizard,' referencing the famous Greek hero's vulnerable heel, because the diagnostic fossil features were found in the ankle and foot bones.

name: Achillobator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A deep, robust maxilla and a vertically oriented pubis that differs from the typical horizontal orientation seen in most other dromaeosaurids.
A large, heavily-built carnivore measuring approximately 4.5 to 5 meters in length and weighing between 250 and 350 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the largest known members of the dromaeosaurid family, often compared in size to Utahraptor and known for its stocky, powerful build.

name: Acristavus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine / Wahweap Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur that notably lacks the elaborate crests, horns, or other cranial ornamentation found on most of its relatives.
It was a herbivore that grew to be approximately 6 to 7 meters long and lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It belongs to the group of 'duck-billed' dinosaurs known for their flat, broad snouts that resemble a duck's bill.

name: Acrocanthosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Antlers / Cloverly Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses exceptionally tall neural spines on its vertebrae that likely supported a prominent ridge of muscle along its neck, back, and hips.
It was a massive bipedal predator measuring approximately 11 to 12.2 meters in length and weighing up to 9 metric tons, feeding on large prey like sauropods and ankylosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as one of the largest theropods from North America during the Early Cretaceous period, often depicted as a formidable apex predator in popular media.

name: Acrotholus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Milk River Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A thick, oval-shaped cranial dome formed by fused frontal and parietal bones, reaching a maximum thickness of 55 millimeters.
A small-bodied, bipedal herbivore weighing approximately 40 kilograms or less.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, heavily thickened, dome-shaped skull used for intra-species combat.

name: Adamantisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Adamantina Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from a set of tail vertebrae and chevrons, indicating a long, whip-like tail typical of its group.
It was a medium-sized titanosaur, estimated to be between 13 and 18 meters long and weighing up to 14.4 tonnes, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked sauropod dinosaur that lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Adasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A notably reduced and blunt sickle claw on the second toe, unlike the large, sharp claws of most relatives, and a recurved lacrimal bone in the skull.
A medium-sized predator measuring between 2.4 and 3.5 meters long and weighing up to 87 kilograms, likely hunting small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a feathered, bird-like dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia that is famous for having a significantly smaller killing claw than other dromaeosaurs.

name: Adelolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wahweap Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a lambeosaurine hadrosaur, a type of dinosaur known for possessing large, hollow head crests, though this specific specimen is named for its 'unknown' or missing crest.
It was a plant-eating herbivore that lived during the Upper Cretaceous period in what is now Utah, sharing its estuarine habitat with fish, turtles, and tyrannosaurids.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is closely related to Parasaurolophus, the famous dinosaur recognized by its long, tube-like cranial crest, and represents the oldest known lambeosaur remains found in North America.

name: Adeopapposaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañon del Colorado Formation, approximately 174.7–201.4 million years ago.
It possessed a notably elongated neck and keratinous beaks at the tips of its upper and lower jaws.
This Early Jurassic herbivore from Argentina was roughly comparable in size and build to Massospondylus.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a foundational example of early sauropodomorph dinosaurs found in South America, often studied alongside its close relative Massospondylus.

name: Adratiklit
hints: Fossils recovered from the El Mers III Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
A dorsal vertebra featuring a small triangular rough protrusion on top of the prezygapophysis and centroparapophysal laminae ending in rough areas protruding to the front
An estimated body length of up to 7 meters (23 feet), classified as a plant-eating stegosaurian
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the oldest known stegosaurs and the first described eurypodan taxon from North Africa

name: Adynomosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Conques Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

A uniquely underdeveloped shoulder blade (scapula) that is proportionally shorter and narrower than those of other relatives, featuring a heavily reduced deltoid ridge and a thin proximal constriction.

A large, plant-eating herbivore that could walk on both two and four legs, likely reaching lengths of several meters, equipped with a long skull, a beak, and complex dental batteries for grinding vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the duck-billed dinosaur family, characterized by a hollow cranial crest formed from nasal bones and internal air passages, despite its distinctively weak shoulder structure.

name: Aegyptosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baharîje / Echkar Formation, approximately 93.9–143.1 million years ago.
A massive, long-necked quadrupedal herbivore belonging to the titanosaur group of sauropods.
Estimated to be approximately 15 meters (49 feet) long and weighing around 7 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs whose name literally translates to 'Egypt's lizard'.

name: Aeolosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen / Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.

It possesses highly distinctive tail vertebrae with down-curved prezygapophyses and chevrons featuring concave posterodorsal surfaces with double articular facets, along with a tail that curves strongly downward.
It was a large, heavily built quadrupedal herbivore, with estimates ranging from 14 to 18 meters in length and weighing between 6 and 15 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of South America that bore unusual bony armor plates (osteoderms) along its flanks.

name: Aepyornithomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive foot structure with a complete calcaneum and lower tarsal III that closely resembles the foot of the extinct elephant bird, Aepyornis.
An ornithomimid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, likely a small, bipedal, bird-like dinosaur that inhabited arid desert environments.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'elephant bird mimic,' highlighting its unique resemblance to the giant, flightless ratite bird.

name: Aerosteon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Distinctive pneumatic hollowing within its bones, including the furcula and ilium, indicating a bird-like respiratory system with air sacs.
A large theropod estimated to be between 6 and 10 meters long and weighing up to one metric ton, which was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its scientific name translates to 'air bone', referring to the unique air-filled cavities found in its skeleton that functioned like a bellows for breathing.

name: Afromimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
The tail vertebrae feature broad centra with 'peanut-shaped' depressions on the top and bottom surfaces, along with prezygapophyses that have rough, textured sides.
This medium-sized theropod was approximately 4.14 meters (13.6 feet) long and weighed significantly less than a Gallimimus, likely feeding on small prey or scavenging.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a theropod dinosaur discovered in the Ténéré Desert of Niger, known for its partial skeleton that was initially misclassified as an ornithomimosaur before being reclassified as an abelisauroid.

name: Afrovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiourarén / Irhazer II Formation, approximately 143.1–170.9 million years ago.
A distinctive rounded horn on top of the lacrimal bone in the skull
Approximately 7 to 8 meters long, weighing around 800 kilograms, and a carnivore
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the few theropod dinosaurs discovered on the African continent

name: Agilisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
It possessed a lightweight skeleton with long legs and a tibia that was slightly longer than its femur, indicating it was an extremely fast bipedal runner.
This small herbivore measured approximately 1.2 to 1.7 meters in length and weighed around 40 kilograms, feeding on abrasive plants using leaf-shaped teeth with wavy enamel.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is known as the 'agile lizard' due to its speed and is notable for having one of the most complete small ornithischian skeletons ever discovered, found in the Sichuan province of China.

name: Agnosphitys
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
It possesses a semi-perforate acetabulum and an ilium with a well-defined brevis fossa, features that place it among the earliest dinosaur relatives.
This Late Triassic animal was a small, bipedal herbivore or omnivore, likely measuring only about one meter in length.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is often cited in scientific literature as one of the oldest known members of the dinosaur lineage, living alongside the ancestors of true dinosaurs.

name: Agujaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aguja Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a large, elongate frill that is circled by small hornlets and features a strong notch at the back, giving the frill a heart-like shape with a scalloped edge.
This four-legged herbivore reached approximately 4.3 meters in length and weighed around 1.5 metric tons, feeding on plants in a Late Cretaceous swamp environment.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the horned dinosaur family known for its distinctive facial horns, specifically characterized by a short nose horn and long brow horns that curve backwards.

name: Agustinia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lohan Cura Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
It was originally described as having distinctive armor plates or spikes down its back, similar to Stegosaurus, though later research suggested these were actually fragments of ribs and hip bones.
It was a large sauropod dinosaur, approximately 15 meters long, that lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a South American sauropod that was once thought to be armored like a Stegosaurus, but is now classified as a rebbachisaurid.

name: Ahshislepelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive scapular acromion process that overhangs dorsolaterally by 25% of the bone's width, and its skin armor features uniformly pitted rugosity with sparse neurovascular grooves.
This small subadult or adult ankylosaurid measured approximately 31 cm in humerus length, placing it larger than Pinacosaurus but smaller than Talarurus, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, quadrupedal dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of New Mexico, known for its shield-like bony plates and osteoderms arranged in rosette patterns.

name: Ahshislesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a notably deeper front part of the jaw compared to other members of its family, and lacks the long, hollow, tube-like crests seen in relatives like Parasaurolophus.
This plant-eating dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now New Mexico and was roughly comparable in size to other large hadrosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur that was once confused with Kritosaurus but is distinguished by its solid, low-profile snout bump rather than a dramatic head crest.

name: Ahvaytum
hints: Fossils recovered from the Popo Agie Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
It is known only from fragmentary hindlimb bones, specifically an isolated left astragalus and a partial left femur.
It was a very small dinosaur, estimated to be only 3 feet (0.91 meters) long and 1 foot tall, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
It holds the distinction of being the oldest known named dinosaur from the ancient landmass of Laurasia.

name: Ajkaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Csehbánya Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive hooked beak formed by a hypertrophied premaxilla, which was initially mistaken for a rostral bone characteristic of ceratopsians.
An estimated body length of approximately 1 meter, classifying it as a small herbivore that consumed tougher vegetation than its contemporary armored dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very few ceratopsian dinosaurs known to have lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Ajnabia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Couche III Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A duck-billed herbivore with a specialized skull structure characteristic of the lambeosaurine subfamily, featuring a complex arrangement of nasal passages and crests.
A relatively small dinosaur estimated to reach approximately 3.5 meters (11 feet) in length, feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the first definitive duck-billed dinosaur discovered on the African continent, representing a rare case of these animals crossing deep ocean barriers to reach isolated landmasses.

name: Akainacephalus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A massive, back-swept ridge of bone over the eye sockets and triangular cheek horns pointing vertically downward, along with a skull covered in symmetrical rows of pyramidal and conical bony bumps called caputegulae.
A medium-sized armored dinosaur, approximately 4 to 5 meters long, that was a herbivore feeding on low-lying vegetation using a narrow, U-shaped beak.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive 'thorn head' appearance, characterized by the unique, spiky, and heavily ornamented skull that resembles a crown of thorns or spikes.

name: Alamosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ojo Alamo / Javelina Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A quadrupedal sauropod with a body partly covered in bony armor (osteoderms) and rod-shaped teeth, despite no skull ever being found.
A gigantic herbivore measuring approximately 26 to 30 meters long and weighing up to 35 tonnes, with some estimates suggesting it could rival the largest dinosaurs in history.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the largest dinosaur known from North America and one of the few titanosaurs to inhabit the continent during the very end of the Cretaceous period.

name: Alaskacephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Prince Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive skull feature consisting of two divergent rows of polygonal bony nodes along the squamosal bone, rather than smooth or rounded nodules.
An estimated length of 1.5 meters and weight of 10 kilograms, identifying it as a small, bipedal, herbivorous dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as a pachycephalosaur, known for having a thick, dome-shaped skull used for head-butting or display.

name: Albalophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kuwajima Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
Possesses cranial features such as a distinctive skull structure and dental morphology that have led to ongoing scientific debate regarding its classification within the marginocephalian lineage.
A relatively small herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period, with the known remains representing a juvenile individual.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'white crest lizard,' referencing the snow-covered peak of Mount Hakusan in Japan where its fossils were discovered.

name: Albertaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman / Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive skull featuring unusually long brow horns for its group, a bony ridge over the nose, and two large outwardly-projecting hooks on the frill
An estimated length of 5.8 meters (19 ft) and weight of 3,500 kilograms (7,700 lb), classified as a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a horned dinosaur with a large bony frill and prominent brow horns, representing a transitional form between earlier and later horned dinosaurs

name: Albertadromeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
The distal fibula is fused to the anterior surface of the tibia for the distal one-third of its length, and the lateral condyle of the proximal tibia is strongly bilobed.
It was a small-bodied, bipedal herbivore, approximately 1 meter long, that specialized in eating ferns and low vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'runner from Alberta', reflecting its highly elongated tibia which indicates it was built for speed.

name: Albertavenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A notably short and broad frontal bone with a length-to-width ratio of less than 1.3 and a unique frontoparietal suture configuration.
A small-bodied theropod dinosaur that lived approximately 71 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
A member of the Troodontidae family, a group of small, bird-like dinosaurs known for their large brains and keen senses.

name: Albertonykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed unusually short, powerfully built forelimbs ending in a single, massive thumb claw specialized for digging.
This small theropod measured approximately 1.1 meters in length and weighed around 5 kilograms, feeding on wood-nesting termites.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few alvarezsaurids discovered in North America, characterized by its tiny body size and specialized digging claws.

name: Albertosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon / Judith River Formation, approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive enlarged pneumatic opening in the back rim of the side of the palatine bone, which serves as a unique identifying trait for the species.
This large bipedal predator measured approximately 8 to 9 meters in length and weighed between 1.7 and 3.0 metric tons, feeding on other animals with a powerful, bone-crushing bite.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a close relative of the Tyrannosaurus rex, sharing the iconic features of a massive head, tiny two-fingered arms, and a reputation as an apex predator of the Late Cretaceous.

name: Albinykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Javkhlant Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a unique skeletal trait where the ankle bones are completely fused to the shin and foot bones, a feature not found in other relatives.
It was exceptionally tiny, weighing less than one kilogram and measuring only a few feet long, making it one of the smallest non-avian dinosaurs ever discovered.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its fossil remains were found preserved in a sitting or crouching position with its feet tucked underneath its body, resembling the posture of a modern bird.

name: Alcovasaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Alcova Quarry within the Morrison Formation of Natrona County, Wyoming, dating to the Late Jurassic period.
This dinosaur is distinguished by exceptionally long, slender dermal tail spikes that can reach nearly the length of its thigh bone.
It was a large herbivore, measuring approximately 6.5 meters in length and weighing around 3.5 to 5 metric tons.
It lived in North America during the Late Jurassic epoch, sharing its environment with other famous dinosaurs like Apatosaurus and Stegosaurus.
It is a stegosaurian known for having four prominent spikes on its tail, similar to the more famous Stegosaurus.

name: Alectrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty / Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.

A long, spike-like process extending from the caudodorsal surface of the medial condyle of the femur, along with a tibial joint surface on the fibula that features an abrupt expansion in length on its anterior margin.

A medium-sized bipedal carnivore, estimated to be 5 to 6 meters long and weighing between 454 and 907 kg, with hindlimbs that were gracile and subequal in length, indicating it was a fast-running pursuit predator.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the earliest known members of the tyrannosauroid lineage, representing a slender, long-legged ancestor that contrasts sharply with the later, robust and short-armed giants like T. rex.

name: Aletopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Point Loma Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a pelvic shield composed of co-ossified hexagonal osteoderms that form a semi-continuous sheet, along with hollow, cap-like armor plates across its back.
This basal ankylosaurid measured approximately 5 meters (16 feet) in length and weighed around 2 tonnes, feeding on low-lying vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period, easily recognizable by its extensive bony armor and osteoderms covering its body.

name: Alioramus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Beds of Nogon Tsav / Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A row of five irregular bony crests protruding upwards from the midline of the snout and a skull that is notably long and low.
An estimated length of 5 to 6 meters and a weight of roughly 500 kilograms, with a carnivorous diet and a unique count of 76 to 78 teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive row of five bony crests along the top of the snout, which makes it visually distinct from other large tyrant dinosaurs.

name: Allosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Alcobaça Formation, approximately 137.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possessed a light, robust skull equipped with dozens of sharp, serrated teeth and featured lightweight vertebrae that originally inspired its name meaning 'different lizard'.

This large bipedal predator averaged about 8.5 meters (28 ft) in length and weighed several tons, serving as the top apex predator of the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation by hunting large herbivores like sauropods and stegosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe during the Late Jurassic.

It is famously depicted in a classic museum mount poised aggressively over a partial Apatosaurus skeleton, a pose originally interpreted as scavenging but now often seen as a symbol of its role as a fierce hunter.

name: Alnashetri
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.

A low ridge on the distal end of the tibia that separates the articulation surface for the astragalus from the outer face of the lateral malleolus, along with small notches extending ventrally from the collateral ligament pits at the base of specific toe bones.
A small coelurosaurian theropod with long, slender hind limbs, estimated to be roughly the size of a modern turkey or small dog, belonging to the alvarezsauroid lineage.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'slender thighs' in the Tehuelche language, directly referencing the distinctive long and thin structure of its leg bones found in the fossil record.

name: Alpkarakush
hints: Fossils recovered from the Balabansai Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
A pronounced, rugose orbital brow (decorative structure over the eye) and a high, arched skull
An estimated body length of 7–8 meters (23–26 feet) as a large predator
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A large, meat-eating dinosaur from the Jurassic period with slender hindlimbs and a powerful bite

name: Altirhinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khuren Dukh Formation, approximately 100.5–110.1 million years ago.
A distinctive tall arch on top of its snout formed by the nasal bones
Approximately 6.5 to 8 meters long, weighing around 1.1 tonnes, and herbivorous
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its unique high-arched nose or snout

name: Altispinax
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Wadhurst Clay Formation of East Sussex, England, dating to the Valanginian stage of the Early Cretaceous, approximately 140 to 133 million years ago.
The most distinctive anatomical feature is a series of three posterior dorsal vertebrae with extremely tall, high neural spines that form a prominent back crest.
It was a large predatory theropod, estimated to be around 8 meters long and weighing approximately 1 tonne.
This dinosaur lived during the Early Cretaceous period on the landmass that is now Europe, specifically in what is now England.
It is visually defined by its massive, sail-like back crest formed by the elongated neural spines of its vertebrae.

name: Alvarezsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.

A distinctive skeletal feature where the third metatarsal is the longest bone in the foot, followed by an unusually long fourth metatarsal, along with a visibly curved and proportionally small scapula.
A small, lightweight bipedal theropod measuring approximately 1 to 1.4 meters in length and weighing around 3 kilograms, likely feeding on insects.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the basal ancestor of a family of dinosaurs famous for having tiny, vestigial forelimbs that resemble chicken wings or spades, despite this specific species retaining more functional arms.

name: Alwalkeria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maleri Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
A partial skull, several vertebrae, and fragmentary hindlimb bones, including an astragalus (ankle bone) and femur fragments
Approximately 235 to 228 million years old, originating from the Late Triassic Lower Maleri Formation in India
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Triassic.
A historically problematic and chimaeric fossil specimen whose classification has shifted between theropod dinosaur, basal saurischian, and avemetatarsalian

name: Alxasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bayangobi Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed fairly long, recurved hand claws and a short tail, with broad feet that converged with those of basal sauropodomorphs.
It was a bipedal herbivore with a large gut, measuring approximately 3.8 to 4 meters in length and weighing around 380 to 400 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is an early member of the therizinosaurs, a group of theropods known for their unique combination of carnivorous ancestry and herbivorous adaptations.

name: Amanzia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Reuchenette Formation, approximately 152.2–154.8 million years ago.
It is a sauropod dinosaur, characterized by a long neck and tail, with fossilized cartilage found in its limb joints.
This Late Jurassic herbivore reached significant sizes, with some individuals estimated to be 15% to 20% larger or smaller than the average adult in its group.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It holds the distinction of being the first sauropod dinosaur to be formally named from fossil remains discovered in Switzerland.

name: Amargasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Amarga Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses two parallel rows of extremely tall, bifurcated neural spines along its neck and back, which are taller than those of any other known sauropod.
It was a small sauropod, measuring 9 to 13 meters in length and weighing approximately 2.6 to 4 metric tons, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its most iconic feature is the double row of long, backward-bowing spines running down its neck and back, which likely supported a sail or keratinous sheath.

name: Amargatitanis
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Amarga Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed tall neural spines extending from the vertebrae of its neck and trunk, a feature characteristic of its dicraeosaurid family.
This small sauropod measured approximately 12 meters in length and weighed around 3.5 metric tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a large, long-necked, quadrupedal dinosaur from the Cretaceous period of Argentina.

name: Amazonsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Itapecuru Formation, approximately 106.3–119.6 million years ago.
Tall neural spines on its tail vertebrae
Approximately 12 meters long and weighing around 5000 kg, it was a quadrupedal herbivore
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A large, long-necked dinosaur with a whip-like tail from the Early Cretaceous

name: Ambopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a unique 'styliform element,' an elongated bone extending from the wrist, which supported a bat-like membranous wing stretching from its hyper-elongated third finger to its abdomen.
This tiny dinosaur measured only about 32 centimeters in length and weighed roughly 300 grams, and evidence of gastroliths and bone fragments suggests it was an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the very few known dinosaurs to have possessed bat-like membranous wings in addition to feathers, a feature shared only with its close relative Yi.

name: Ampelognathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lewisville Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A nearly-complete left dentary (lower jawbone) recovered from the Late Cretaceous Lewisville Formation in Texas
A basal ornithopod dinosaur, likely a small to medium-sized herbivore related to iguanodontians
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the few dinosaur genera discovered and named from the state of Texas

name: Ampelosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marnes Rouges Inférieures / Grès à Reptiles Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed armor in the form of osteoderms, which is a rare feature among sauropods.
It was a titanosaurian sauropod measuring approximately 14 meters in length that lived during the Late Cretaceous.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known as the 'vine lizard' because its name was derived from the vineyards of the region where its fossils were discovered in France.

name: Amphicoelias
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Kadzi Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
Its dorsal vertebrae are amphicoelous, meaning both the front and back ends of the bone are concave rather than one being ball-shaped.
It was a moderately sized herbivorous sauropod, reaching approximately 18 meters in length and weighing around 15 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is a close relative of Diplodocus, sharing similar long-necked, long-tailed body plans but distinguished by its unique biconcave spinal bones.

name: Amtocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A nearly complete frontoparietal dome formed by the fusion of frontal and parietal bones, featuring an exceptionally large parietal portion that makes up 41% of the dome's length.
A small subadult individual with a dome measuring 53.2 millimeters in length and 19 millimeters in thickness, belonging to a herbivorous group of ornithischian dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, thick, rounded skull roof or 'bony dome,' which is the defining characteristic of the pachycephalosaur family, often depicted as a helmet-like structure on the head.

name: Amurosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Udurchukan / Yuliangzi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive hollow crest on the top of its skull, supported by modified bones on the roof of the skull, and a sigmoidal (S-shaped) ulna bone in the lower arm.
An herbivore estimated to be 8 meters (26 feet) long and weighing approximately 3 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being recognized as the most completely known dinosaur species discovered in Russia.

name: Amygdalodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Carnerero Formation, approximately 182.9–192.9 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive almond-shaped teeth, which is the origin of its scientific name.
This quadrupedal herbivore was a basal sauropod estimated to be 12 meters long, 4 meters tall, and weighed over 5 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the very few dinosaurs from the Jurassic period discovered in South America, representing a rare early giant from that continent.

name: Anabisetia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Lisandro Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed the largest acromial process on the upper-lower rim of the shoulder blade ever found in its group, and its fifth metatarsal was flattened with straight edges rather than rounded.
It was a small bipedal herbivore, estimated to be around two meters long and weighing approximately twenty kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a lesser-known South American relative of the more famous Iguanodon, often recognized by paleontologists for its unique pelvic structure and association with other Patagonian dinosaurs like Gasparinisaura.

name: Analong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chuanjie Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive transverse processes on its first ten tail vertebrae and a unique 45-degree angle between two processes on its ulna bone.
This giant sauropod reached massive proportions, featuring an extremely long neck composed of 11 articulated cervical vertebrae, and was a herbivore that fed on Middle Jurassic conifers.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a member of the Mamenchisauridae family, a group of dinosaurs famous for having necks that were disproportionately long compared to the rest of their bodies.

name: Anasazisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive tab or flange of bone from the nasals that rises between and above the eyes and folds back under itself, creating a short nasal crest.
Approximately 7.5 meters long and weighing up to 2500 kg, this large herbivore used sophisticated dental batteries to grind plant material.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a 'duckbill' dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous, known for its unique short crest that distinguishes it from similar hadrosaurs like Gryposaurus.

name: Anchiceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon / St. Mary River Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A rectangular skull frill edged with exceptionally wide, coarse triangular bony projections and a pair of sideways-pointing bony knobs near the rear edge.
A medium-sized, quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be around 4.3 meters long and weighing approximately 1.2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Possessing three facial horns: two long brow horns and a shorter nose horn, resembling a close relative of Triceratops.

name: Anchiornis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 152.2–168.2 million years ago.
A prominent feathered crest on its head and four wings formed by long vaned feathers on both the arms and legs.
A small, crow-sized bipedal dinosaur weighing approximately 110 grams, covered in shaggy plumage and downy feathers.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its exceptionally preserved fossils revealed detailed color patterns and feather structure, making it one of the first dinosaurs with a known life appearance.

name: Anchisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portland Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses an unusually large thumb on its forelimb, a feature that originally led to its first proposed name meaning 'large finger'.
It was a small, lightweight dinosaur measuring just over 2 meters in length and weighing approximately 27 kilograms, with a diet that included both plants and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.

It is historically significant as the first dinosaur genus to be named in North America, with its initial discovery in 1818 being so puzzling that the bones were originally mistaken for human remains.

name: Andesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses tall, rectangular neural spines on its back vertebrae and amphiplatyan tail vertebrae where both ends are flat rather than ball-and-socket joints.
It was a medium-sized sauropod measuring approximately 15 to 18 meters long and weighing around 7 metric tons, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most basal known member of the Titanosauria clade, representing an early stage in the evolution of these massive South American dinosaurs.

name: Angolatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Itombe Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
A partial right forelimb including a 110-centimeter upper arm bone, ulna, radius, and three slender metacarpals, with an absent olecranon and less robust structure compared to derived titanosaurs.
A massive titanosauriform sauropod that likely reached great lengths and weights, feeding on vegetation in a desert-like environment.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
The first non-avian dinosaur ever discovered in Angola, representing a rare 'Angolan giant' from the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Angulomastacator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aguja Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A partial upper jaw bone that curves downward at a sharp 45-degree angle at the front, unlike any other known duck-billed dinosaur.
A plant-eating dinosaur that could walk on two or four legs, possessing complex dental batteries for grinding vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of duck-billed dinosaur, specifically one with a hollow crest, known for its uniquely bent snout.

name: Anhuilong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongqin Formation, approximately 161.5–174.7 million years ago.
A forelimb featuring a humerus with a very low width-to-length ratio and an elliptical cross-section at the mid-shaft of the ulna.
A large sauropod dinosaur that likely reached significant lengths and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period.

name: Aniksosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses an extremely expanded brevis shelf on its ilium, which serves as a large attachment site for tail muscles, along with a deep notch on the femur for the M. iliotrochantericus muscle.
It was a small, bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 2 to 3 meters in length and weighing between 35 and 45 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known for being a small, agile coelurosaurian theropod from Patagonia that likely lived in social groups, evidenced by monospecific bonebeds containing multiple individuals.

name: Animantarx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A highly domed skull back with small horns on the postorbital and quadratojugal bones, and a mandible that is only armored on half of its length.
A slow-moving quadrupedal herbivore covered in heavy armor scutes, reaching a maximum length of approximately 3 meters (10 feet).
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored dinosaur from the Cretaceous period that lacks the distinctive tail club found in its close relatives.

name: Ankylosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ferris / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a massive bony club at the end of its tail and is covered in heavy armor plates and osteoderms.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 6 to 8 meters in length and weighing up to 8 tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the archetypal armored dinosaur, instantly recognizable by its low-slung body, wide skull with backward-pointing horns, and distinctive tail club used for defense.

name: Anodontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon / Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A wide, pointed tail club at the end of its armored tail and small polygonal bone plates (postocular caputegulae) located immediately behind the eyes.
A medium-sized, quadrupedal herbivore measuring up to 5 meters in length and weighing approximately 2 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its heavily armored, tank-like body covered in bony plates and spikes, resembling a prehistoric walking fortress.

name: Anomalipes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a uniquely shaped foot with a distinctively long and slender third metatarsal, which is the feature that gave the genus its name meaning 'unusual foot'.
This small theropod dinosaur measured approximately 1.5 meters in length and was an omnivore or herbivore belonging to the oviraptorosaur group.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is characterized by its bird-like appearance and lack of teeth, resembling a large, feathered ostrich with a beak.

name: Anoplosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Upper Greensand Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a high acromion process on its scapula, a key anatomical trait confirming its classification as an armored dinosaur despite the initial lack of discovered armor plates.
This quadrupedal herbivore was quite small, with the type specimen measuring only about five feet in length, and it likely fed on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famously known as the 'unarmored lizard' because its original fossil remains lacked the protective bony plates typical of its close relatives, leading to decades of taxonomic confusion.

name: Anserimimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses unusually powerful forelimbs with large muscle attachment crests on the shoulder and upper arm bones, along with distinctive long, straight claws that have a nearly flat lower surface.
It was a medium-sized dinosaur, estimated to be around 3 meters long and weighing between 50 and 170 kilograms, and is believed to have been an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the 'ostrich mimic' group of dinosaurs, known for being lanky, fast-running theropods that lived in the Late Cretaceous period of Mongolia.

name: Antarctopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Snow Hill Island Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a unique tail weapon called a 'macuahuitl,' consisting of a row of spiked osteoderms resembling an Aztec club, rather than a solid bony club.
This medium-sized herbivore measured approximately 4 to 6 meters in length and weighed around 350 kilograms, featuring a small head with proportionally large, leaf-shaped teeth.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first dinosaur ever discovered on the continent of Antarctica, found on James Ross Island.

name: Antarctosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta / Plottier Formation, approximately 66.0–89.8 million years ago.
A sauropod with a distinctive squared-off lower jaw where teeth are restricted to the very front, suggesting specialized grazing habits.
A massive quadrupedal herbivore estimated to reach lengths of around 17 meters and weigh approximately 34 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia, South America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the largest dinosaurs known, with the species Antarctosaurus giganteus represented by fragmentary remains that indicate enormous size.

name: Anteavis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
A proportionally elongate lower hindlimb, specifically the tibia and fibula, which is referenced in its species name meaning 'long leg'.
A small, bipedal carnivore from the Late Triassic period, likely measuring less than two meters in length and weighing only a few kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is an early ancestor-like theropod whose name literally translates to 'before birds', highlighting its position as a primitive relative to modern avian dinosaurs.

name: Antetonitrus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses a flexible, twisted first digit (thumb) on its hand that was still capable of grasping, a primitive trait lost in later, more derived members of its group.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 10 meters in length and weighing around 5.6 metric tons, representing an early stage in the evolution of massive body size.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the oldest known sauropods, dating to the Early Jurassic, and its name translates to 'before thunder' to signify its existence prior to the famous 'thunder lizards' like Brontosaurus.

name: Aoniraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a partial skeleton consisting of sacral and caudal vertebrae along with haemal arches, characterized by complex pneumatic structures within the bones.
This theropod reached an approximate length of 6 meters and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known relative of the famous giant predators like Giganotosaurus, belonging to the megaraptoran group from the Late Cretaceous of South America.

name: Aorun
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
A large, curved thumb claw that contrasts with two smaller, straighter hand claws, along with teeth that are either unserrated or bear only very fine serrations on their rear edges.
A small, bipedal carnivore estimated to be about 1 meter (3.3 feet) long and weighing roughly 1.5 kilograms (3.3 lbs), representing a juvenile individual.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the oldest known members of the coelurosaur lineage, a group that eventually includes birds, making it a rare and ancient relative of modern feathered dinosaurs.

name: Apatoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Distinctive quill knobs found on the ulna bone, indicating the presence of wings and feathers.
A medium-sized theropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Alberta, Canada, that was initially misidentified as an ornithomimid.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'deceptive thief,' referencing how its true identity as a caenagnathid was hidden for years because it lacked a skull during initial preparation.

name: Apatosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
A robust, stocky build with thick, pillar-like leg bones and a neck that is deeper than it is wide, distinguishing it from the more slender Diplodocus.
A massive herbivore measuring 21 to 30 meters long and weighing up to 33 tons, feeding on high vegetation with chisel-like, spatulate teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Its long, whip-like tail and the historical confusion where its skeleton was famously mounted with the wrong, smaller skull for decades.

name: Appalachiosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Blufftown / Demopolis Chalk Formation, approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
A row of six low crests lines the top of the snout, and the foot claws feature an unusual protrusion on the end closest to the body.
This juvenile specimen measured approximately 6.5 meters (21 ft) in length and weighed around 623 kilograms (1,373 lb), and it possessed a bone-crushing bite force of roughly 32,500 newtons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the tyrannosauroid lineage, closely related to the famous giant predators, and was discovered in the eastern United States during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Aquilarhinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aguja Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive shovel-shaped beak formed by a unique predentary bone, which differs from the typical beak structure of other hadrosaurs.
A medium-sized herbivorous ornithopod that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'eagle snout,' referring to the unusual, spade-like shape of its front jaw.

name: Aquilops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly Formation, approximately 100.5–110.1 million years ago.
A downward-curving rostral bone (snout beak) with an arched keel on top and a bump on the front.
A small, early herbivorous ceratopsian approximately 60 cm long and weighing 1.5 kg.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the earliest known horned dinosaurs, representing a basal member of the lineage that would later produce massive giants like Triceratops.

name: Arackar
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hornitos Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Distinctive vertebral anatomy featuring high and wide hollowing between the front joint process and the vertebral body, with posterior joint processes narrower than the neural spine
A juvenile specimen estimated at approximately 6.3 meters (21 feet) in length, belonging to a giant, long-necked herbivorous group
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
The most complete sauropod skeleton ever discovered in Chile

name: Aragosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villar del Arzobispo Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possessed a short, compact skull with large, wide teeth adapted for slicing through tough vegetation, and its forelimbs were only slightly shorter than its hind limbs.
This quadrupedal herbivore reached approximately 18 meters in length and weighed around 25 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is broadly similar in appearance to Camarasaurus, featuring the classic sauropod silhouette of a small head, long neck, bulky body, and powerful tail.

name: Aralosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bostobe Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.

A distinctive hollow bony structure located on the nasal bone just in front of the orbits, which communicated with the respiratory tract, marking it as a primitive member of the crested duck-billed dinosaurs.
An estimated length of approximately 6.5 meters, classifying it as a medium-sized herbivore that likely fed on low-lying vegetation during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.

Being one of the earliest known duck-billed dinosaurs to possess a hollow cranial crest, a feature that distinguishes it from its non-crested relatives and places it at the base of the lambeosaurine evolutionary tree.

name: Aratasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Romualdo Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A partial articulated right leg including the distal femur, proximal tibia, metatarsals, phalanges, and three pedal claws.
An estimated body length of 3.12 meters and a weight of around 34 kilograms, likely a juvenile or young adult carnivore with feather covering.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its specific name honors the National Museum of Brazil, referencing the fact that the holotype specimen survived the devastating 2018 museum fire.

name: Archaeoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou / Xiagou Formation, approximately 110.1–121.4 million years ago.
A comparatively large head with a small bony frill projecting from the back, but completely lacking horns.
A small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 0.9 meters in length and weighing around 10 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a primitive member of the horned-face dinosaur group, distinguished by its parrot-like beak and absence of the iconic horns seen in later relatives.

name: Archaeocursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ziliujing Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
An isolated nearly complete left femur measuring 93 millimeters in length, which is the sole fossil evidence for this genus.
A small dinosaur approximately 1 meter (3.3 feet) in length, identified as a basal ornithischian from the Early Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It holds the distinction of being the oldest and most basal ornithischian dinosaur described from Asia.

name: Archaeodontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Isalo IIIb Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive, blade-like teeth that are serrated and adapted for slicing through tough vegetation, a feature that gives the genus its name meaning 'ancient-toothed lizard'.
This creature was a massive quadrupedal herbivore, likely reaching lengths of around 10 to 12 meters, belonging to the group of long-necked dinosaurs known as sauropods.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.

It is primarily recognized by its discovery in Madagascar, where it stands as one of the few known sauropod species from that island, distinguished by its unique dental structure compared to other long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Archaeornithoides
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses small, conical, and smooth teeth that completely lack the serrations or wrinkles typically found in other theropods.
It was an exceptionally tiny dinosaur, with an estimated body length of only fifty to sixty centimeters, making it one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is historically famous for being initially classified as the closest known relative to birds due to its bird-like skeletal features, though it is now widely considered to be a juvenile troodontid.

name: Archaeornithomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu / Dabrazhin Formation, approximately 72.2–89.8 million years ago.
It possessed a keratinous beak and robust hindlimbs, with a foot structure that was not pinched at the upper end of the third metatarsal.
This medium-sized theropod measured approximately 3.4 meters in length and weighed over 71 kilograms, consuming an omnivorous diet that included plants, eggs, and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'ancient bird mimic,' reflecting its bird-like appearance and likely feathered body, which distinguished it as one of the earlier members of its group.

name: Arcovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Argiles rutilantes / Vegas de Matute Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive midline foramen on the skull roof situated on a slight dome formed by the frontal bones, along with a thick, rough-surfaced bony brow ridge dorsal to the eye socket.
An estimated length of approximately 4.8 meters (16 feet), classified as a carnivore within the abelisaurid family known for short, tall skulls and drastically reduced forelimbs.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few abelisaurid theropods discovered in Europe, specifically from the Late Cretaceous period in France, rather than the more common South American or Indian locations for this group.

name: Arcusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses unique bony shelves on the inner side of the premaxilla that form part of the palate, along with a nasal bone featuring four distinct processes including a tongue-shaped lower extension.
It was a basal sauropodomorph from the Early Jurassic of South Africa, likely representing juvenile individuals with small size and open bone sutures, indicating it was a relatively small herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a basal sauropodomorph dinosaur known from fragmentary skull and postcranial remains found in the Elliot Formation of South Africa.

name: Ardetosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 149.2–154.8 million years ago.
A unique arrangement of laminae on its vertebrae that distinguishes it from other diplodocines.
A large, long-necked, quadrupedal herbivore with pencil-like teeth and a whip-like tail, estimated to be around 18 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Its name literally means 'burning lizard,' referencing the fact that its holotype skeleton was damaged by a museum fire in Germany.

name: Arenysaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Posa Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A hollow, decorative cranial crest on its head
A medium-sized herbivore measuring 5–6 meters long and weighing about 1 metric ton
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the last non-avian dinosaurs to survive before the mass extinction event

name: Argentinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A massive sauropod with a long neck and tail, characterized by enormous, pillar-like legs and a body supported by a robust spine with specialized vertebrae.
One of the largest land animals ever known, estimated to reach lengths of 30 to 35 meters and weigh between 65 and 100 tonnes, it was a herbivore that fed on high vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its reputation as a potential record-holder for the heaviest and longest dinosaur, often depicted as a colossal, gentle giant in popular culture.

name: Argyrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
The midshaft of its humerus is extremely compressed, and the medial side features a wide ridge projecting forward.
It was a medium-sized sauropod estimated to be between 17 and 28 meters long and weighing up to 50 tonnes, feeding on plants.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina, known as a titanosaur.

name: Aristosuchus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
The femur features a wing-like anterior trochanter and a markedly reduced fourth trochanter.
It was a small, bipedal carnivore approximately 2 meters long and weighing between 7 and 30 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a small coelurosaurian theropod from the Early Cretaceous of the Isle of Wight, originally misidentified as a crocodile relative.

name: Arkansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
A nearly complete right foot featuring strong claw curvature and a laterally compressed third metatarsal that is ovoid in proximal view
Approximately 4.6 meters long and weighing 380.5 kilograms, this bipedal ornithomimosaurian was likely an omnivore or carnivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is the only dinosaur fossil currently described from the state of Arkansas, where it was officially designated as the state dinosaur in 2017

name: Arrhinoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a broad, square neck frill featuring two distinct oval-shaped openings and is characterized by a nose horn that is notably shorter and blunter than its brow horns.
This herbivore measured approximately 4.5 to 6 meters in length and weighed around 1.3 tonnes, feeding on ferns, cycads, and conifers.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a close relative of Triceratops and Anchiceratops, belonging to the same group of horned dinosaurs that lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Arrudatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Adamantina Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A relatively gracile build for a titanosaur, characterized by a femur length of approximately 1.55 meters.
A large herbivorous sauropod that grew up to 15 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a massive, long-necked plant-eater from the Late Cretaceous period of Brazil.

name: Asfaltovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 168.6–180.4 million years ago.
A distinctive horizontal ridge on the exoccipital bone at the rear of the skull, along with triangular neural spines on the third and fourth neck vertebrae that are swept backwards.
A large carnivore estimated to be 7–8 meters long, comparable in size to Allosaurus, with a skull measuring 75–80 cm in length.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a basal member of the allosauroid group, representing an early branch in the evolutionary lineage that includes the famous Allosaurus.

name: Asiaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khodzhakul Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It is a member of the Ceratopsia group, characterized by a distinctive parrot-like beak used for cropping vegetation.
This herbivore was a relatively small dinosaur, likely measuring around one to two meters in length, and fed on low-growing plants like ferns and cycads.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It belongs to the 'horned face' family of dinosaurs, sharing the general body plan of having a beak and frill, though it is known from fragmentary remains rather than a complete skeleton.

name: Asiatyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nanxiong Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A deep-snouted skull with a robust, boxy shape, contrasting with the long, narrow snouts of other contemporary Asian tyrannosaurs.
An estimated body length of 3.5 to 4 meters, making it one of the smallest known tyrannosaurines, and a carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a 'tyrant lizard' from Asia, representing a smaller, deep-headed relative of the famous Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Astigmasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive anatomical feature is the specific orientation of the hindlimbs in the holotype specimen, which were found angled backward, giving the specific name meaning 'knee-flexed' or 'kneeling'.
This is a medium-sized herbivorous sauropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina, belonging to the family Rebbachisauridae.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur known from a partially articulated skeleton discovered in the Huincul Formation.

name: Astrophocaudia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Paluxy Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
Its tail vertebrae have a unique three-pointed star shape when viewed from behind, and the bones feature a specific 'non-twisting' joint structure called hyposphene-hypantrum articulations.
It was a large, long-necked plant-eater estimated to be between 36 and 60 feet long, weighing anywhere from 6,300 to 44,000 pounds.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, four-legged herbivore with a very long neck and tail, representing the classic 'brontosaurus' body plan from the Early Cretaceous period.

name: Asylosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Magnesian Conglomerate Formation, approximately 201.4–205.7 million years ago.
Distinctive structure of the humerus (upper arm bone) that differentiates it from contemporaneous basal sauropodomorphs like Thecodontosaurus
Approximately 2 meters (6 feet 7 inches) in length and weighing around 250 kilograms (550 pounds), likely omnivorous or herbivorous
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
A basal sauropodomorph from the Late Triassic period of England, known for its partial skeleton that survived World War II bombardment

name: Atacamatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tolar Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
Its right femur is notably more gracile than its other limb bones, suggesting it possessed disproportionately strong forelimbs.
It was a massive titanosaurian sauropod weighing between 8 and 16 tonnes, with an estimated length typical of large long-necked herbivores.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, four-legged plant-eater from the Late Cretaceous of Chile, known for its enormous size and long neck.

name: Athenar
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 145.1–149.2 million years ago.
A braincase and partial skull roof featuring frontoparietal and postparietal fenestrae (holes in the skull) and a distinct prong on the squamosal bone.

A sauropod dinosaur from the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation that lived alongside Allosaurus and Stegosaurus, belonging to the dicraeosaurid group known for shorter necks compared to other long-necked dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
A recently discovered dinosaur from 2025 that was originally misidentified as Diplodocus before being reclassified into its own genus based on unique skull features.

name: Atlasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Guettioua Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
It possesses a proportionately larger skull and a shorter, more uniform neck compared to its relative Brachiosaurus, along with spoon-shaped teeth featuring denticles.
This Middle Jurassic sauropod reached an estimated length of 15 meters (49 feet) and weighed approximately 22.5 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'Atlas lizard,' referencing the Atlas Mountains of Morocco where it was discovered and the mythological Titan Atlas who held up the heavens.

name: Atlascopcosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Eumeralla / "Wonthaggi" Formation, approximately 110.1–125.8 million years ago.
A partial maxilla with teeth and dentaries, indicating it was a basal iguanodont with herbivorous dentition.
Approximately two to three meters long and weighing around 125 kilograms, identified as a small bipedal herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is the only dinosaur genus named after the industrial company Atlas Copco, which provided equipment for its discovery in Australia.

name: Atrociraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A deep, robust snout with teeth that are strongly inclined backwards and are nearly uniform in size, distinguishing it from other dromaeosaurids.
A small predator measuring approximately 1.8 to 2 meters in length and weighing around 15 kilograms, likely hunting larger prey with its powerful jaws.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being featured as a 'brutish' raptor in the 2022 film Jurassic World Dominion, recognizable by its sickle-shaped claw and feathered body.

name: Atsinganosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by a massive, quadrupedal body plan with a long neck and tail, and robust pillar-like legs.
It was a herbivore that grew to an estimated length of 5 to 14 meters and weighed between 2.5 and 5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a large, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of France, known for its distinctive vertebrae and association with other titanosaur remains.

name: Aucasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a pair of low bony ridges above each eye instead of the prominent horns found in its close relative Carnotaurus.
It was a medium-sized carnivore measuring approximately 5.5 to 6.2 meters in length and weighing around 700 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known for having extremely reduced arms with almost no fingers, a trait characteristic of abelisaurid theropods.

name: Augustynolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moreno Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A solid, un-hollowed crest extending from the back of the skull, distinguishing it from the hollow-crested hadrosaurs like Parasaurolophus.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and weighing around 3 metric tons, specialized for chewing tough plant material.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the official state dinosaur of California.

name: Auroraceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A short, wide, and flat skull featuring paired rugose areas on the face that likely supported keratinous structures, along with striated, fang-like teeth in the premaxilla.
