A moderate-sized dinosaur measuring approximately 1.25 meters in length and weighing around 15.5 kilograms, which walked bipedally and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the earliest known members of the horned dinosaur lineage, representing a basal stage in the evolution of ceratopsians.

name: Aurornis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 152.2–168.2 million years ago.
It possessed clawed wings and a long, bony tail, with traces of downy feathers found along its neck, chest, and tail.
It was roughly the size of a modern pheasant, measuring about 50 cm in length, and lived approximately 160 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is often cited as one of the earliest known members of the bird lineage, predating Archaeopteryx by about 10 million years.

name: Australodocus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
A distinctive anatomical feature is the presence of double (bifurcate) neural spines on its vertebrae, a trait initially used to classify it among diplodocids.
This large sauropod was approximately 17 meters long and weighed around 4,000 kilograms, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the many giant, long-necked dinosaurs discovered in the Late Jurassic fossil beds of Tanzania, often associated with other massive titanosauriforms or diplodocoids.

name: Australovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Winton / "Wonthaggi" Formation, approximately 89.8–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed unusually flexible arms with a sliding radius and ulna, allowing its fingers to extend far beyond typical theropods to grasp prey against its chest.
It was a lightweight bipedal carnivore, estimated at 6 meters long and weighing around 310 to 500 kilograms, capable of running at speeds approaching 30 mph.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most complete predatory dinosaur ever discovered in Australia, nicknamed 'Banjo' after the famous Australian poet.

name: Austrocheirus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Fortaleza Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is known from a partial hand, tibia, and foot bone, and is notable for having relatively normal-sized forelimbs rather than the tiny, vestigial arms seen in many of its close relatives.
This medium-sized theropod measured approximately 5.5 to 6.5 meters in length and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'southern hand', referencing the specific fossilized hand bones that were the first and primary remains discovered for this species.

name: Austroposeidon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Presidente Prudente Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Its vertebrae feature a unique internal texture known as camellate bone, which is punctuated by many small air chambers, and lack the hyposphene-hypantrum articulations found in other sauropods.
It was a massive titanosaurian sauropod measuring approximately 25 meters (82 feet) in length, making it one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered in Brazil.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the largest dinosaur species ever found within the country of Brazil.

name: Austroraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed unusually short forelimbs relative to its hind legs, a trait that has drawn comparisons to Tyrannosaurus, along with small, conical, non-serrated teeth similar to those of spinosaurids.
This large, bipedal carnivore measured approximately 5 to 6 meters in length and weighed between 300 and 520 kilograms, making it one of the largest known members of its family.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is often recognized as the 'Southern Giant' or 'Southern Thief' of the raptor family, distinguished by its massive size compared to other feathered predators and its discovery in Argentina.

name: Austrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allaru Mudstone Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
A massive, long-necked herbivore with a columnar limb structure adapted for supporting enormous weight on land.
Approximately 20 meters long and weighing around 16 metric tons, it was a large quadrupedal plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaur genera named after Australia, representing the continent's prehistoric fauna.

name: Avaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive frill at the back of the skull with a large squamosal bone featuring a continuously curving edge and a raised area at its base that divides it into two equal halves.
A small herbivore, with the type specimen estimated at 2.3 meters long and larger individuals reaching up to 4.2 meters and weighing around one tonne, feeding on ferns, cycads, and conifers.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a horned, plant-eating dinosaur with a parrot-like beak and a bony frill, representing a basal member of the centrosaurine group.

name: Aviatyrannis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Alcobaça Formation, approximately 149.2–154.8 million years ago.

An elongated and low ilium featuring a distinctive vertical ridge on the outer blade surface above the hip joint, along with teeth that have a D-shaped cross-section in the premaxilla and perpendicular denticles on both edges.
A small theropod estimated to be approximately 1 meter (3.3 feet) in length and weighing around 4 kilograms (8.8 pounds), likely a juvenile or young individual.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is considered one of the oldest known members of the tyrannosauroid lineage, representing an early, primitive stage of the group that would later include giant predators like Tyrannosaurus.

name: Avimimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a toothless, parrot-like beak and had fused hand bones similar to those of modern birds.
It was a small, bipedal runner measuring about 1.5 meters (5 feet) long, likely feeding on plants or insects.
This dinosaur lived in Asia, North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized for its extremely bird-like anatomy, including a long neck and long legs, which led early researchers to mistakenly believe it was a direct ancestor of modern birds.

name: Baalsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
A distinctive squared-off right dentary with teeth crowded into the front of the jaw
A large titanosaurian sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia, Argentina
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur with a tail and pillar-like legs

name: Bactrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu / Baynshire Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
Large, club-shaped neural spines projecting from its vertebrae
Approximately 6 to 6.5 meters long and weighing 1.2 metric tons, this herbivore possessed an unusually powerful build with three stacked teeth for each visible tooth
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the earliest known relatives of the duck-billed dinosaurs, yet notably lacks the hollow head crests that characterize its more famous later cousins

name: Bagaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot / Djadokhta Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a small, triangular skull frill and lacked the blunt, cylindrical premaxillary teeth found near the tip of the upper jaw in its close relatives.
It was a small herbivore, measuring only 1 to 1.5 meters in length and weighing between 22 and 45 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a diminutive, hornless relative of the famous Protoceratops, often recognized for its tiny size compared to other ceratopsians.

name: Bagaraatan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
[0] It possesses a robust mandible and pelvic structure characteristic of early tyrannosauroids, though its classification has been debated due to chimaeric fossil material.
[1] It was a small carnivorous theropod, measuring approximately 3 to 4 meters in length, representing one of the smallest known juvenile tyrannosaurids.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
[2] It is a juvenile relative of the famous T-Rex, discovered in the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia, known for being a small, bird-like predator from the Late Cretaceous.

name: Bagualia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 180.4–182.9 million years ago.
A robust, beak-like process at the anteroventral end of the premaxilla and uniquely thick enamel on its spoon-shaped teeth.
A large, quadrupedal herbivore that likely reached significant size, feeding on conifers with a long neck.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as the oldest known definitive eusauropod dinosaur.

name: Bagualosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Santa Maria Formation, approximately 205.7–237.0 million years ago.
It possessed notably strong hindlimbs, a feature highlighted by its generic name which translates to 'strongly built fellow' in the local dialect.
This modest-sized basal sauropodomorph measured approximately 2.25 meters in length and was primarily herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is a relatively obscure Triassic dinosaur from Brazil, lacking the iconic visual features of more famous contemporaries like Plateosaurus or later giants.

name: Bahariasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baharîje / Echkar Formation, approximately 66.0–113.2 million years ago.
Its holotype fossils, including a massive femur and vertebrae, were destroyed in a World War II bombing raid on Munich, leaving only illustrations as evidence.
It was a giant carnivorous theropod measuring approximately 11 to 12 meters long and weighing around 4 tonnes, comparable in size to Tyrannosaurus rex.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is an enigmatic 'lizard from Baharia' discovered in the Bahariya Formation of Egypt, known primarily for its immense size and the tragic loss of its original specimens.

name: Baiyinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wangjiashan Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
A paired row of large plates and spines running along its back from neck to tail tip
A quadrupedal herbivore with a broad, short skull roof and small teeth featuring denticles on the edges
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its distinctive dorsal armor consisting of alternating plates and spikes along the spine

name: Bajadasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajada Colorada Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
It possessed extremely elongated, two-pronged neural spines on its neck vertebrae that curved forward.
It was a relatively small sauropod, roughly the size of a modern Asian elephant, that was a four-legged herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its most famous feature is the pair of long, forked spikes projecting from its neck, which resemble a crown or a comb.

name: Bambiraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses long arms with mutually opposable first and third fingers, allowing the hand to reach its mouth to hold food like a small mammal.
It was a tiny, bird-like predator, with the juvenile holotype measuring only about one meter in length and weighing approximately two kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Bambi thief,' referencing the young age of the specimen and the popular Disney movie character.

name: Banji
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A tall, crested skull formed by nasal bones and premaxillae, uniquely adorned with vertical striations and grooves on the top of the lower jaw.
A small, lightly built, bipedal carnivore estimated to be only 65 cm long as a juvenile.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'striped crest dragon,' referring to the distinctive striped pattern on its head crest.

name: Bannykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bayangobi Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A forelimb that shows significant reduction and modification compared to earlier relatives, but is not as highly specialized or shortened as in later members of its group.
An estimated length of approximately 2.77 meters (9.1 ft) and a body mass of 24–30 kilograms, with evidence suggesting a carnivorous diet rather than insectivory.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It represents a transitional form in the evolution of alvarezsauroids, filling a major gap between earlier theropods with normal arms and later species with tiny, specialized limbs.

name: Baotianmansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiaguan Formation, approximately 72.2–93.9 million years ago.

Distinctive vertebral features including short neural spines on the middle back vertebrae, a developed system of pneumatic openings on the vertebrae, and amphicoelous tail vertebrae that are shorter than they are wide
A medium-sized sauropod estimated to be between 20 and 22.5 meters long, weighing between 15 and 21 tons, and feeding on vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A massive, long-necked herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period of China, known for its distinctive spongy back bones and short tail vertebrae

name: Barapasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kota Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
It possesses spoon-shaped teeth with wrinkled enamel and distinctive coarse serrations along the edges, alongside elongated cervical vertebrae that form a long neck.
This early sauropod measured approximately 12 to 14 meters in length and weighed around 7 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as an obligate quadruped.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the earliest known members of the giant, long-necked dinosaur lineage, representing a foundational stage in the evolution of massive herbivores.

name: Barilium
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wadhurst Clay / Weald Clay Formation, approximately 121.4–137.1 million years ago.
A robust build featuring large vertebrae with short neural spines and a heavy ilium
An herbivore approximately 8 meters (26 feet) long
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
A sturdy, heavy-bodied plant-eater from the Early Cretaceous period of England

name: Barosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Kadzi Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.

It possessed an exceptionally long neck with up to 16 cervical vertebrae, which were significantly longer than those of its close relative Diplodocus, while having a shorter tail and fewer dorsal vertebrae.

It was a massive quadrupedal herbivore, with adults estimated to reach lengths of 25 to 40 meters and weigh between 12 and 60 metric tons, feeding on high vegetation using peg-like teeth confined to the front of its jaws.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the longest dinosaurs ever discovered, famous for having a neck that could be nearly 15 meters long, allowing it to browse treetops without moving its heavy body.

name: Barrosasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto / Portezuelo Formation, approximately 72.2–93.9 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by massive, pillar-like limbs and a long neck, with fossil evidence including distinctive dorsal vertebrae.
This dinosaur reached approximately 18 meters in length and weighed around 13.5 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eater from the Cretaceous period of South America, known for its enormous size and quadrupedal stance.

name: Barsboldia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Very tall neural spines, particularly over the hips, with the tips of the first few tail vertebrae being club-shaped
A large herbivore estimated to reach 12–14 meters in length and weighing around 5 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a duck-billed dinosaur from Mongolia that was originally misidentified as a different species before being recognized as its own genus

name: Baryonyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pinilla de los Moros / Weald Clay Formation, approximately 110.1–139.1 million years ago.
A massive, sickle-shaped claw measuring approximately 31 centimeters long located on the first finger of its robust forelimb.
An elongated, narrow snout resembling that of a modern gharial, with a length of 7.5 to 10 meters and a weight of 1.2 to 2 metric tons, primarily feeding on fish.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
Being the first theropod dinosaur scientifically proven to have eaten fish, evidenced by fish scales found preserved in its stomach region.

name: Bashanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
A small, triangular acromial process on the scapula and a fourth trochanter on the femur positioned below the midpoint of the shaft
A quadrupedal herbivore approximately 4 to 5 meters long with a row of paired plates and spines along its back
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its distinctive row of bony plates and spikes running down its spine and tail

name: Bashunosaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Middle Jurassic Shaximiao Formation in Kaijiang, Sichuan Province, China.
The known skeleton includes six cervical vertebrae, eight dorsal vertebrae, and partial limb bones such as a humerus and femur.
It was a large, quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be between 10 and 12 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Middle Jurassic period.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur commonly known as a sauropod.

name: Batyrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bostobe Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
The rear parietal bones form bony flaps that overlap the supraoccipital bone, and the frontal bones are notably elongated, being about 70% longer than they are wide.
This medium-sized herbivore measured approximately five to six meters in length and possessed a thumb claw roughly four centimeters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the duck-billed dinosaur lineage, representing an early evolutionary stage before the development of the large crests seen in later relatives.

name: Baurutitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Serra da Galga Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is distinguished from related genera by its distinctive caudal vertebrae.
It was a sauropod estimated to be 12–14 metres long and 3.5 metres high.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the sauropods described from Brazil.

name: Bayannurosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bayingebi Formation, approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
It is an ornithopod dinosaur belonging to the group Ankylopollexia, representing an intermediate evolutionary form between earlier iguanodontians and the later duck-billed dinosaurs.
This large herbivore measured up to 9 meters (30 feet) in length and lived during the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known Asian relative of the more famous iguanodonts, distinguished by its position as a transitional species in the evolution of duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Beibeilong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Gaogou Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A toothless skull featuring a prominent horny beak and a unique deep depression on the lacrimal bone that allowed the frontal bone to overlap.
An estimated adult length of 7.5 meters and a weight of 1.5 to 2 metric tons, classifying it as one of the largest oviraptorosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being identified from the famous 'Baby Louie' specimen, a well-preserved embryonic skeleton discovered inside a clutch of giant, ring-shaped eggs.

name: Beipiaosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive three-toed foot structure, differing from its more advanced relatives which typically had four functional toes.
This small dinosaur measured approximately 2.2 meters in length and weighed around 27 kilograms, and despite being a theropod, it was herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is famous for being one of the heaviest dinosaurs known from direct fossil evidence to have been covered in feathers, with preserved skin impressions revealing brownish plumage.

name: Beishanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed a robust build with long arms and legs, but notably lacked the extremely elongated hands, feet, and claws seen in later, more derived relatives.
This subadult individual measured approximately 5.9 to 7 meters in length and weighed between 375 and 626 kilograms, making it one of the largest ornithomimosaurs ever discovered.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, ostrich-like theropod from the Early Cretaceous of China, closely related to the massive Deinocheirus and often compared in size to Gallimimus.

name: Bellusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It is a sauropod dinosaur with broad teeth, classified within the Macronaria group, and recent studies suggest it belongs to the Mamenchisauridae family.
It was a relatively small and lightly built sauropod, measuring approximately 4.8 meters (16 feet) in length, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for being discovered as a mass burial site of seventeen juvenile individuals in a single quarry in China, suggesting a herd killed by a flash flood.

name: Berberosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 174.7–192.9 million years ago.
Its cervical vertebrae are highly pneumatic with short centra and holes in the neural arch, while its sacral series is markedly arched and narrow transversely.
It was a bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 4.58 to 5 meters in length and weighing around 300 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the oldest formally identified theropods from North Africa, dating back to the Early Jurassic period.

name: Berthasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Goio-Erê Formation, approximately 72.2–121.4 million years ago.
A toothless, short beak and convergent adaptations with oviraptorids, including the loss of teeth in adulthood.
Approximately 1 metre (3.3 ft) in length, likely a herbivore or omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
One of the most complete dinosaur fossils known from the Cretaceous of Brazil, preserving the most complete axial series of any noasaurid.

name: Bicentenaria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.

The quadratojugal bone features an anterior branch that is twice as long as its ascending branch, and the retroarticular protrusion at the back of the lower jaw is flattened, widened transversely, and spoon-shaped.
This small bipedal predator measured approximately 2.5 to 3 meters (8 to 10 feet) in length and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is named after the 200th anniversary of the May Revolution in Argentina, a fact reflected in its genus name which translates to 'two hundred year anniversary'.

name: Bissektipelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A distinctive 'Y'-shaped groove pattern on the skull roof that separated three polygonal areas of flat, remodelled bone tissue, along with a braincase featuring three exits for the hypoglossal nerve.

A Late Cretaceous herbivore with an omnivorous diet capable of filter-feeding, possessing a primitive brain structure with extremely large olfactory bulbs and a complex vascular network for heat exchange.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Bissekty shield', referring to the heavy bony armor and osteoderms characteristic of ankylosaurine dinosaurs.

name: Bistahieversor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fruitland / Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed 64 teeth, an extra opening above the eye to accommodate an air sac, and a keel along the lower jaw.
It was a large predator measuring approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing between 2.5 and 3.3 metric tons, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the tyrannosaur lineage, often recognized as a 'primitive' or 'generalized' ancestor to the famous giant apex predators like Tyrannosaurus.

name: Bisticeratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A chasmosaurine ceratopsian skull featuring preserved postorbital horncores and a distinctive facial structure, with evidence of healed tyrannosaurid bite marks on the bone.
A herbivorous dinosaur from the late Cretaceous period of North America, known primarily from a nearly complete skull specimen.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
A horned, plant-eating dinosaur with a frilled face, closely related to other famous ceratopsians like Triceratops.

name: Blasisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Arén Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
The cheekbone features a rear projection with a hook-shaped upper edge, and the lower temporal fenestra is narrow and D-shaped.
It was a medium-sized ornithopod that lived approximately 66 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a crested duck-billed dinosaur from the Lambeosaurinae subfamily, known from fossils found in Spain.

name: Bolong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A cavity at the interface of the lacrimal, maxilla, and prefrontal bones above the eye socket, and a vertical ledge dividing the inside of the maxillary teeth.
Approximately 4 meters long and weighing 200 kilograms, this herbivore possessed powerful mandibles and relatively large teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the earliest known members of the group that would later evolve into the famous duck-billed dinosaurs with their distinctive flattened snouts.

name: Bonapartenykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

It possesses a distinctive scapular blade that is medially deflected, unlike the straight scapulae found in most other members of its family, along with a well-developed notch on the posterior margin of the bone.
It is a large member of its clade, measuring between 2.5 and 3.3 meters in length and weighing approximately 43 to 72 kilograms, and it was a bipedal carnivore or insectivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the most geologically young dinosaurs of its type found in South America, living during the very end of the Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina.

name: Bonapartesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Extremely long neural spines on the anterior tail vertebrae, which are three to four times the height of the centra
A large herbivorous hadrosaurid reaching significant size, likely comparable to other giant duck-billed dinosaurs of its time
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a South American relative of the more famous North American duck-billed dinosaurs, often grouped with them in popular imagination despite lacking a crest

name: Bonatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a braincase and caudal vertebrae, characteristic of titanosaurian sauropods with robust, pillar-like limb bones adapted for massive weight support.
This Late Cretaceous herbivore was a giant titanosaur, likely reaching lengths of around 15 meters and weighing several tons, feeding on high vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from Argentina, representing the diverse group of sauropods that dominated South American ecosystems during the final era of the dinosaurs.

name: Bonitasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive sharp ridge on the lower jaw that supported a sharp, beak-like keratin sheath, functioning like a guillotine to crop vegetation.
An approximate length of 10 meters, featuring reversed limb proportions where the front limbs were shorter than the hind limbs.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unusual combination of a long, low skull similar to diplodocids and a short neck, which contrasts with the typical long-necked appearance of most titanosaurian dinosaurs.

name: Borealopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Clearwater Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A broad, heavily armored body covered in closely spaced rows of osteoderms and a pair of long, bull-like spines protruding from the shoulders, but lacking a tail club.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 5.5 meters in length and weighing around 1.3 metric tons, with stomach contents revealing a selective diet of ferns.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the most perfectly preserved dinosaur fossils ever found, retaining its original reddish-brown camouflage skin, keratin sheaths, and soft tissue in three dimensions.

name: Borealosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sunjiawan Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
Possesses a distinctive mid-distal caudal vertebra that lacks procoely, a feature that distinguishes it from lithostrotian titanosaurians.
This massive titanosaurian sauropod was a long-necked herbivore that likely reached lengths of around 10 to 12 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China, known only from fragmentary fossil remains.

name: Boreonykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wapiti Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive acute angle of 55 degrees formed by the ridges bordering the depressions around the supratemporal fenestrae on the skull.
A small, meat-eating predator from the Late Cretaceous period, known primarily from fragmentary remains including a skull bone, teeth, and sickle-shaped claws.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a feathered, bird-like dinosaur with a characteristic sickle claw on its foot, belonging to the same family as Velociraptor.

name: Borogovia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed an unusually elongated tibiotarsus (shin bone) and lacked the characteristic hyperextendable 'sickle claw' on its second toe that is typical of many other troodontids.
This small theropod measured approximately two meters in length and weighed around twenty kilograms, and it was a carnivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its genus name is derived from the bizarre, bird-like creatures called 'borogoves' described in Lewis Carroll's poem 'Jabberwocky'.

name: Brachiosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possessed disproportionately long forelimbs compared to its hindlimbs, creating a steeply inclined trunk and a shorter tail than other sauropods.
This massive herbivore reached lengths of 18 to 22 meters and weighed up to 47 metric tons, allowing it to browse vegetation as high as 9 meters off the ground.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Africa, Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is famously depicted with its neck held vertically upright, a posture popularized by its appearance in the 1993 film Jurassic Park.

name: Brachylophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A short, blunt, paddle-shaped crest extending from the top of its skull
A large herbivore measuring approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its status as one of the most complete and well-preserved dinosaur 'mummies' ever discovered

name: Brachytrachelopan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Calcáreo Formation, approximately 145.1–154.8 million years ago.
Its cervical vertebrae are shorter than they are long, creating a neck that is approximately 40% shorter than that of other dicraeosaurids and the shortest of any known sauropod.
It was a small sauropod, measuring about 10 to 11 meters in length and weighing around 5 metric tons, and it specialized in low-browsing on plants growing between 1 and 2 meters high.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Jurassic.
It is the only known sauropod with a neck so drastically shortened that it appears almost stubby compared to the long necks typical of its group.

name: Brasilotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Adamantina Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive L-shaped lower jaw that creates a squared-off snout, along with slightly twisted symphyseal dentary bones.
A relatively small titanosaurian sauropod, measuring less than 12 meters in length, that was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from Brazil, fitting the general silhouette of a massive sauropod.

name: Bravasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ciénaga del Río Huaco Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Possessed a robust skeletal structure including a right quadrate, quadratojugal, multiple cervical and dorsal vertebrae, and complete limb bones such as the femur and humerus.
Measured approximately 7 meters in length and weighed nearly 2.9 metric tons, feeding as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Was a massive, long-necked titanosaurian sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, closely associated with one of the world's largest concentrations of dinosaur eggs.

name: Bravoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Javelina Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive hollow, inverted-tear-shaped depression on the median bar of its frill, which was originally interpreted as the base for a single central horn resembling a cyclops's eye.
It was a large chasmosaurine ceratopsid that lived approximately 70 million years ago in what is now Texas, United States.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur known for its elaborate frill and brow horns, belonging to the group of herbivorous dinosaurs that lived alongside Triceratops.

name: Breviceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A moderately pronounced neck frill formed by the parietal and squamosal bones, along with a narrow, straight-shaped antorbital fenestra located in front of the eye socket.
A small herbivore with a parrot-like beak, known only from juvenile specimens, indicating it was significantly smaller than its adult relatives.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'short horned face,' referencing its lack of prominent horns despite belonging to a group of horned dinosaurs.

name: Brighstoneus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive S-shaped bulge on the nasal bone above the nostril, known as a nasal bulla, and a heart-shaped predentary bone with denticles on the lower beak.
An estimated length of 6 meters (20 feet) and a weight of approximately 1 ton, classified as a large herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a basal member of the Hadrosauriformes, representing an early stage in the evolutionary lineage that would eventually lead to the famous duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Brontomerus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a uniquely shaped, deep hipbone (ilium) with a front section much larger than the rear, designed to anchor the largest thigh muscles of any known sauropod.
This early Cretaceous herbivore was a relatively small sauropod, with adults reaching approximately 14 meters in length and weighing around six tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.

It is nicknamed the 'thunder thigh' dinosaur due to its exceptionally powerful leg muscles, which scientists hypothesize may have been used for kicking or traversing rough terrain like a four-wheel drive.

name: Brontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses extremely robust and heavily built cervical vertebrae, giving it a thick, sturdy neck that contrasts with the more slender necks of its relatives.
It was a massive quadrupedal herbivore, with the largest species reaching up to 23 meters in length and weighing between 15 and 20 tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is widely recognized as the archetypal 'long-necked' dinosaur in popular culture and media.

name: Brontotholus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive, thickened, bony dome formed by the fused frontoparietal bones on the top of its skull
An herbivore approximately 3.8 meters (12 feet) long, making it one of the largest of its kind in North America
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its skull features a prominent, rounded bony dome, a hallmark trait of the 'bone-headed' dinosaur group

name: Buitreraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a slender, extremely elongated snout filled with many small, grooved teeth that lack the meat-tearing serrations found in its northern relatives.
It was a small predator, approximately 1.5 meters long and weighing around 3 kilograms, likely hunting small animals like lizards and mammals.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a dromaeosaurid, a group of dinosaurs famously known for the sickle-shaped claw on their second toe, similar to Velociraptor.

name: Burianosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Peruc-Korycany Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from a single fossilized femur bone, making it one of the most fragmentarily known dinosaurs.
This ornithopod lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now the Czech Republic and is estimated to have been a medium-sized, plant-eating dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first validly named dinosaur species discovered in the Czech Republic.

name: Buriolestes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Santa Maria Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
A backward-projecting medial condyle on the end of its tibia, which is a unique skeletal feature not found in other dinosaurs.
A small, lightweight predator weighing between 6.4 and 15.4 kg, equipped with finely serrated, curved teeth adapted for eating small vertebrates and invertebrates.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is a rare early dinosaur that looks like a meat-eating theropod but is actually an ancestor of the giant plant-eating sauropods.

name: Bustingorrytitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A massive titanosaur sauropod with a partial skeleton including a tooth, vertebra, scapula, humerus, femur, tibiae, and fibula.
An estimated weight of approximately 67.3 tonnes and a length typical of giant titanosaurs, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the largest known dinosaurs, discovered in the Huincul Formation of Argentina alongside other gigantic titanosaurs like Argentinosaurus.

name: Byronosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Its teeth lack the serrations typically found in other troodontids, a feature likely linked to a dietary shift.
It was a small theropod measuring approximately 1.5 to 2 meters in length and weighing between 4 and 20 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Fossilized juvenile skulls were discovered inside a nest of eggs belonging to a different dinosaur species, an oviraptorid.

name: Caenagnathasia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty / Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed a short, beaked skull with fused lower jaw bones, characteristic of its oviraptorosaur lineage.
It was exceptionally tiny, measuring only about 2 feet in length and weighing roughly 3 pounds, and was an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the smallest known oviraptorosaur and one of the tiniest non-avian dinosaurs to have ever existed.

name: Caenagnathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A toothless, shallow lower beak and less gracile body proportions compared to its relative Elmisaurus.
Approximately 2.5 meters long and weighing between 85 and 100 kilograms, likely feeding on plants or small animals.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a feathered dinosaur with a distinctive bird-like beak and long legs, resembling a large, flightless turkey.

name: Caieiria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Serra da Galga Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Vertebrae with robust side processes vertically expanded to almost half the height of their centra and a deep excavation between specific laminae in the front tail vertebrae
A titanosaurian sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Brazil, likely a large herbivore with a massive body and long neck
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its specific name 'allocaudata' translates to 'strange tail', highlighting its uniquely unusual tail anatomy

name: Caihong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
A distinctive horn-like process on the lacrimal bone of the skull that projects strongly to the side and curves upward at the tip.
A tiny dinosaur measuring only 40 cm in length and weighing under 500 grams, with a wingspan of about 44 cm.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
The fossil is famous for preserving extensive, colorful plumage that inspired its name, which means 'rainbow' in Chinese.

name: Calamosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
Two articulated cervical vertebrae that are opisthocoelous (convex at the front, concave at the back) with strongly reduced epipophyses, indicating a small and lightly built head.
An estimated body length of approximately 3.5 meters (11 feet), classified as a small theropod likely belonging to the tyrannosauroid lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'reed lizard,' referencing the slender and lightweight structure of its fossilized neck vertebrae.

name: Caletodraco
hints: Fossils recovered from the Saint Jouin Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive shape of the transverse process on the first caudal vertebra that serves as a unique identifying feature
Approximately 6 meters long and a carnivore belonging to the abelisaurid family
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
A recently discovered European relative of South American abelisaurids, representing the first definitive furileusaurian named from outside of South America

name: Callovosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
A nearly complete left thigh bone measuring 28 centimeters long, which serves as the primary fossil evidence for this genus.
An estimated body length of approximately 2.5 meters, classifying it as a small, plant-eating basal iguanodontian.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Being recognized as one of the earliest known members of the iguanodontian dinosaur lineage from the Middle Jurassic period.

name: Calvarius
hints: Fossils recovered from the Talarn Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

A highly modified, slender, and elongated fourth metatarsal bone that is convergently similar to those of basal bipedal ornithischians like Hypsilophodon, despite the animal being a member of the typically quadrupedal Styracosterna.
An ornithopod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Spain, likely adapted for running (cursorial ecology) with a body size and build inferred from its single known leg bone.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.

A dinosaur whose name translates to 'suffering' and was named in reference to its proximity to the K-Pg extinction event, with its specific name 'rapidus' meaning 'fast' due to its inferred running capabilities.

name: Camarasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Kadzi Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a large, tall, and boxy skull with spoon-shaped teeth that have continuous cutting edges, contrasting with the low, narrow skulls of diplodocids.
It was a medium-large quadrupedal sauropod, reaching lengths of up to 23 meters and weighing around 42 tonnes, functioning as a selective medium browser.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Africa, Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the most common and well-understood dinosaurs from the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation, often found alongside Stegosaurus and Allosaurus.

name: Camarillasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Camarillas Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It is a spinosaurid theropod, a group of dinosaurs characterized by elongated, crocodile-like snouts and conical teeth adapted for catching fish.
This Early Cretaceous predator lived in what is now northeastern Spain and was approximately 6 to 7 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its most defining visual trait is a long, narrow snout filled with peg-like teeth, similar to a modern crocodile, which indicates a semi-aquatic lifestyle focused on fishing.

name: Camelotia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Westbury Formation, approximately 201.4–205.7 million years ago.
A robust, large-bodied sauropodomorph with a femur approximately one meter long, indicating a heavy, pillar-like limb structure.
An estimated body length of 8 to 10 meters and a mass of 3.8 tonnes, feeding on vegetation with serrated, leaf-shaped teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
One of the largest dinosaurs known from the Late Triassic period, representing a massive herbivore from the era before the giant sauropods of the Jurassic.

name: Campananeyen
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–113.2 million years ago.

It possesses an unusually large quadrate fossa that is 30–70% larger than that of any other known rebbachisaurid, and its ilium is exceptionally thin and fragile due to extensive internal pneumatic chambers.
It is a sauropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina, likely reaching a substantial body size typical of its group, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a long-necked, plant-eating dinosaur known for having a highly specialized, hollow, and fragile skeletal structure with unique pneumatic features in its pelvis and skull.

name: Camposaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chinle Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive sharp longitudinal ridge on the back of the tibia where it connects to the fibula, along with an ankle bone (astragalus) that lacks a large medial condyle.
It was a small, carnivorous theropod dinosaur, though its exact size and weight remain unknown due to the fragmentary nature of the fossil remains.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
It is widely recognized by paleontologists as one of the oldest known members of the neotheropod lineage, dating back to the Late Triassic period.

name: Camptosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Süntel Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possessed a distinct triangular skull with a pointed snout and a keratinous beak, along with robust hindlimbs that retained four toes on its broad feet.
This plant-eating ornithischian typically measured between 5 and 6 meters in length and weighed approximately 500 to 1,000 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is widely recognized as a transitional 'missing link' between the earlier, smaller Hypsilophodonts and the later, larger Iguanodonts.

name: Canardia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marno-calcaires jaunes de Gensac / Marnes d'Auzas Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive subrectangular flange on the maxilla that rises vertically above the rostroventral process, along with dentary teeth featuring an accessory ridge in front of the sub-central carina.
A juvenile individual with small bone fragments (such as a 16.9 cm maxilla) indicating a small body size, belonging to a plant-eating duck-billed dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very last non-avian dinosaurs to exist in Europe just before the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event.

name: Carcharodontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Continental Intercalaire / Ifezouane Formation, approximately 72.2–143.1 million years ago.
Its jaws were lined with sharp, recurved, serrated teeth that bear a striking resemblance to those of the great white shark.
It was a massive theropod reaching approximately 12 meters in length and weighing between 5 to 7 metric tons, and it was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the largest known meat-eating dinosaurs, often recognized as a giant rival to Tyrannosaurus rex from the Cretaceous period of North Africa.

name: Cardiodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Forest Marble Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
A heart-shaped, spatulate tooth with a convex inner side, no serrated edges, and a wrinkled enamel surface.
A large, quadrupedal herbivorous sauropod dinosaur, though its exact size remains uncertain due to the scarcity of fossil remains.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It holds the distinction of being the very first sauropod genus ever formally named in scientific history.

name: Cariocecus (dinosaur)
hints: Fossils were found in the Papo Seco Formation, dating to the Barremian age of the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 129 to 125 million years ago.
It possesses a unique skull feature where the maxilla and jugal bones of the upper jaw are completely fused into a single unit, unlike any other known ornithopod.
It was a medium-sized herbivore with a skull base estimated at about 42 centimeters long, likely capable of chewing tougher plant material due to its reinforced jaw structure.
This dinosaur lived on the landmass that is now Portugal, in the region of Europe, during the Early Cretaceous period.
It has a distinctive 'eagle-eyed' appearance caused by a large, low-set bony brow ridge over the eye socket that may have protected it from sun glare.

name: Carnotaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Colonia Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses two thick, distinctive horns located directly above its eyes, a feature unique among carnivorous dinosaurs.
This lightly built predator measured approximately 7.5 to 8 meters in length and weighed between 1.3 and 2.1 metric tons, likely hunting both large and small prey.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is instantly recognizable by its bull-like horns and its extremely tiny, vestigial forelimbs that are too small to reach its mouth.

name: Caseosaurus
hints: Its fossils were found in the Tecovas Formation of Texas, dating to the Norian stage of the Late Triassic, approximately 221.5 to 212 million years ago.
It is distinguished by a unique sharp ridge connecting the supracetabular crest to the preacetabular process on its hip bone.
It was a small, lightly-built, bipedal carnivore that grew up to 2 meters (6.6 feet) long and weighed around 50 kilograms.
It lived in North America during the Late Triassic period.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaur relatives, often associated with the discovery of the first dinosaur fossils in Texas.

name: Cathartesaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive pneumatic chambers within its neck vertebrae and bony laminae associations characteristic of the Rebbachisauridae family.
This medium-sized herbivore had a long, lightly built, and well-muscled neck with a limited range of up-and-down movement.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, often overshadowed by more famous titanosaurs like Argentinosaurus.

name: Caudipteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed a short, stiff tail ending in a fan of long, symmetrical pennaceous feathers, alongside similar wing-like feathers on its hands.
This small theropod measured approximately 72 to 89 centimeters in length and weighed around 5 kilograms, likely feeding on small animals or plants.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as a key piece of evidence in the debate over bird evolution, often depicted as a bird-like dinosaur with a beak and full feather coverage.

name: Cedarosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain / Paluxy Formation, approximately 110.1–137.1 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive nasal crest and had more gracile forelimb bones compared to its relative Venenosaurus.
This Early Cretaceous sauropod reached approximately 15 meters in length and weighed around 10 metric tons, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is known for the discovery of over 100 gastroliths found in its gut region, which it likely used to grind up plant material.

name: Cedarpelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
Its skull is one of the few known ankylosaurs where the individual bones are not completely fused together, and it lacks the extensive cranial ornamentation seen in later forms.
It was a large, heavy herbivore estimated to be around 7 meters long and weighing approximately 5 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur known for its bony skin plates and club-like tail, resembling a living tank.

name: Cedrorestes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
A tall ilium featuring a large lateral bony process above and behind the acetabulum, a trait typically associated with hadrosaurids.
A large herbivore capable of moving on both two legs and four legs, with hip structure indicating hadrosaurid-like leg muscles.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Named after Michael Crichton, the author of Jurassic Park, and discovered in the Cedar Mountain Formation.

name: Centrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A single large nasal horn and two prominent forward-curving hornlets on the brow ridge, accompanied by a row of small spikes along the edge of a relatively short frill.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 5 to 5.5 meters in length and weighing between 2 and 2.5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive facial ornamentation featuring a prominent nose horn and multiple frill spikes, often depicted in popular media as a classic 'horned dinosaur'.

name: Cerasinops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive parrot-like beak and lacks the large brow horns or extensive neck frills seen in more derived relatives, featuring a relatively small and simple skull structure.
This herbivore measured approximately 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) in length and weighed around 175 kilograms (386 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a small, early member of the horned dinosaur group that lived in North America during the late Cretaceous period, often recognized for its lack of prominent horns compared to giants like Triceratops.

name: Ceratonykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a disproportionately large, single claw on its first digit that is shaped like a horn.
It was a tiny dinosaur, measuring only about 75 centimeters in length and weighing less than a kilogram, likely feeding on insects.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'horned claw,' referring to the distinctive oversized digit on its hand.

name: Ceratosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive ridge-like horn on the midline of its snout, in addition to a pair of smaller horns located above its eyes.
This medium-sized carnivore measured approximately 5 to 7 meters in length and featured deep jaws filled with long, blade-like teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Africa, South America during the Late Jurassic.
It is widely recognized for the prominent nasal horn that gives its name, meaning 'horn lizard', to this Late Jurassic predator.

name: Ceratosuchops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive bony lump, or boss, extending from each postorbital bone on the skull
Approximately the size of Baryonyx, likely weighing several tons, and feeding on fish, small turtles, and young crocodiles
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a crocodile-faced dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of the Isle of Wight with a name meaning 'horned crocodile face'

name: Cetiosauriscus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
It possessed a moderately long tail and forelimbs that were as long as its hindlimbs, a proportion unusual for its group.
This herbivore measured approximately 15 meters in length and weighed between 4 and 10 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a large, long-necked dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic period of England, often confused with or compared to other early sauropods like Cetiosaurus.

name: Cetiosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the El Mers / Chipping Norton Limestone Formation, approximately 119.6–174.7 million years ago.
A quadrupedal build with a relatively short neck and tail compared to other sauropods, along with notably long forelimbs.
An estimated length of 16 meters and a weight of roughly 11 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'whale lizard' because its discoverer initially mistook the massive bones for those of a giant sea creature.

name: Chadititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed particularly gracile and relatively straight limbs, including a humerus and femur that were slender compared to other related titanosaurians.
This sauropod was relatively small for its group, measuring approximately 7 meters (23 feet) in length, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, distinguished by its unusually slender build among its giant relatives.

name: Chakisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive protonic posture where the base of its tail curved downward, a feature otherwise typically observed in titanosaurs.
This medium-sized herbivore measured approximately 2 to 3 meters in length and moved exclusively on two legs without adaptations for quadrupedalism.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal ornithopod from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, representing the first ornithischian dinosaur named from the Huincul Formation.

name: Changchunsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quantou Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive projection sticking out from the side of the cheekbone (jugal) featuring a unique 'nubble structure'.
A small basal ornithopod herbivore measuring approximately 1 meter (3.3 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first named dinosaur genus discovered in the Jilin province of China.

name: Changmiania
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.

Elongated frontal bones that are over four times longer than they are wide, a straight and elongated front branch of the squamosal bone, and a distinctive boss on the upper rear corner of the squamosal.
Approximately 1.17 meters in length, this small herbivore likely weighed only a few kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its fossils were found in a life-like sleeping position, suggesting it may have been buried alive while resting in its own burrow.

name: Changyuraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed long pennaceous feathers on all four limbs, creating a distinctive 'four-winged' appearance.
It was approximately 1.2 meters long and weighed around 4 kilograms, making it roughly the size of a turkey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the largest known four-winged dinosaur and one of the heaviest paravians capable of gliding or flight.

name: Chaoyangsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tuchengzi Formation, approximately 132.6–152.2 million years ago.
A distinctive boss on the jugal bone with a smooth surface and a convex quadratojugal bone that overlaps the rear of the quadrate shaft.
A small herbivore approximately 1 metre long and weighing around 6 kilograms, equipped with up to forty-four teeth including imbricated cheek teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the earliest known members of the horned face dinosaur group, representing a primitive stage in ceropsian evolution.

name: Charonosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yuliangzi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A long, backward-projecting hollow crest on the skull, similar in shape to that of Parasaurolophus
A massive herbivore measuring approximately 10 meters in length and weighing around 5 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Charon's lizard', referencing the Greek ferryman of the dead

name: Chasmosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a large, triangular neck frill featuring two very large, distinct openings (fenestrae) near the center.
It was a medium-sized herbivore, measuring approximately 4.3 to 4.8 meters in length and weighing between 1.5 and 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a classic example of a horned dinosaur with a prominent frill, often compared to the more famous Triceratops but distinguished by its specific frill structure.

name: Chebsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aïssa Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
It is a sauropod, a type of dinosaur characterized by a long neck and tail, and four sturdy legs.
This juvenile specimen was approximately 8 to 9 meters long and was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Jurassic.
It is known as the most complete sauropod fossil discovered in Algeria and was nicknamed 'the Giant of Ksour'.

name: Chenanisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Couche III Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A deep, slightly bowed lower jawbone with a U-shaped cross-section and a vertical front margin, distinct from the rounded jaws of related species.
An estimated length of 7 to 8 meters, making it one of the largest known members of its family, and a carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very last non-avian dinosaurs to exist, living in North Africa just one million years before the asteroid impact that ended the Cretaceous period.

name: Chilantaisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tangshang / Ulansuhai Formation, approximately 83.6–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed large claws on its forelimbs that were initially thought to be unique to spinosaurids, though it is now classified as a neovenatorid or primitive coelurosaur.
It was a massive carnivore measuring approximately 11 meters (36 feet) in length and weighing between 2.5 and 6 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a large, fearsome meat-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China, often compared in size and predatory nature to other giant theropods like Allosaurus.

name: Chilesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Toqui Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
It possesses uniquely spatula-shaped, elongated teeth that protrude forward from the jaw, a feature typically associated with plant-eaters rather than meat-eaters.
It was a small dinosaur, with adults reaching approximately 3.2 meters (10.5 feet) in length, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for its bizarre mix of features, appearing to be a meat-eating dinosaur lineage that evolved to eat plants, confusing scientists about its true family tree.

name: Chindesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chinle Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses a partial skeleton featuring a complete right femur, limb bones, and hip fragments, with classification debates centering on whether it was a herrerasaurid or an early theropod.
It was a bipedal carnivore approximately 2 to 4 meters long, weighing roughly as much as a modern wolf.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
It is known from a specimen nicknamed 'Gertie' that was airlifted by helicopter from Petrified Forest National Park in Arizona in 1985.

name: Chinshakiangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.

It possessed a U-shaped, broad snout formed by a curved dentary, which contrasts with the V-shaped, tapered snouts of its prosauropod ancestors, and featured a prominent diagonal ridge on the jawbone interpreted as an attachment point for fleshy cheeks.
This basal sauropod was a large, quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be 12 to 13 meters in length, utilizing its broad snout to act as a bulk feeder that consumed large amounts of foliage.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.

It is one of the rare early sauropods known from preserved skull bones, providing key evidence that some members of this group may have retained fleshy cheeks for oral processing, a feature lost in later, more derived sauropods.

name: Chirostenotes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed long arms ending in slender, relatively straight claws, with the second finger being particularly elongated and adapted for probing into crevices.
It was a small dinosaur measuring approximately 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) in length and weighing around 100 kilograms (220 lbs), likely feeding on soft-bodied prey, insects, and plants.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famously known from a confusing history of discovery where its hands, feet, and jaws were initially named as separate species before being recognized as belonging to the same animal.

name: Choconsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive cervical vertebrae with very large secondary protuberances on the hyposphene and an additional crest located between the posterior and secondary crests.
It was a large, herbivorous sauropod dinosaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eater with a tail, representing a basal member of the titanosaur lineage.

name: Choyrodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khuren Dukh Formation, approximately 100.5–110.1 million years ago.
A distinctive open antorbital fenestra in the skull, which differentiates it from similar contemporaries like Altirhinus.
An Early Cretaceous iguanodontian herbivore from Mongolia, known from partial skulls and cervical ribs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the duck-billed dinosaur lineage, closely related to Eolambia, representing an early stage in hadrosauroid evolution.

name: Chromogisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
A partial skeleton lacking the skull, featuring distinct elements of the front and hind limbs, pelvis, and caudal vertebrae.
A small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 2 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
One of the oldest known dinosaurs, dating back to the Late Triassic period in Argentina.

name: Chuandongocoelurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A partial skeleton consisting of a thighbone, vertebrae, and pelvic bones, with the holotype thighbone measuring 201 millimeters in length
A small carnivorous theropod from the Middle Jurassic period of China, likely weighing only a few kilograms
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the earliest described theropods from China, originally classified within the Coeluridae family

name: Chuanjiesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chuanjie Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
It belongs to the Mamenchisauridae family, a group of sauropods known for having some of the longest necks relative to their body size among dinosaurs.
This massive herbivore reached an estimated length of 25 meters (82 feet), making it a true behemoth of the Middle Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, four-legged plant-eater from the Jurassic period, representing the classic giant dinosaur silhouette with a small head on a towering neck.

name: Chuanqilong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A triangular skull and a neck protected by bands of osteoderms known as cervical half rings, with the rest of the body covered in osteoderms of various shapes and sizes.
A medium-sized quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be around 4.5 meters long, distinguished by the absence of a bony club at the end of its tail.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its heavily armored, turtle-like appearance with a broad, low-slung body covered in bony plates, characteristic of ankylosaurid dinosaurs.

name: Chubutisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Barcino Formation, approximately 110.1–119.6 million years ago.
A robust radius bone, distinguishing it from closely related sauropods like Venenosaurus
A massive quadrupedal herbivore estimated to reach lengths between 18 and 23 meters and weigh approximately 12 tonnes
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A giant, long-necked plant-eater from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina

name: Chucarosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A slender-limbed titanosaur with a thickened deltopectoral crest on the humerus and a shorter, more robust iliac peduncle on the ischium compared to other titanosaurs
A large herbivore with a femur measuring approximately 1.9 meters long, indicating a massive body size typical of colossosaurian titanosaurs
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina, known for its immense size and elephant-like appearance

name: Chungkingosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.

It possessed a unique tail weapon described as a 'pin-cushion array,' featuring an additional pair of long, thin spikes oriented almost horizontally at the very end of the tail, alongside stout vertical spikes.
This was a small stegosaurian herbivore, with the holotype measuring less than four meters in length, making it one of the smallest members of its group.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.

It is a stegosaur from the Late Jurassic of China that strongly resembles Tuojiangosaurus in anatomy but is distinguished by its smaller size, deeper snout, and non-overlapping teeth with less pronounced denticles.

name: Cienciargentina
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It is a rebbachisaurid sauropod, a group characterized by relatively lightweight builds and often possessing distinctive osteoderms or bony armor plates along their backs.
This Late Cretaceous herbivore reached a substantial size, with known specimens including partial skeletons that indicate it was a large, long-necked browser.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is one of several distinct sauropod species discovered in the Huincul Formation of Argentina, sharing its ancient ecosystem with massive titans like Argentinosaurus and fierce predators like Mapusaurus.

name: Citipati
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A tall, bony crest formed by the premaxilla and nasal bones on a short, toothless skull with a parrot-like beak
Approximately 2.5 to 2.9 meters long and weighing between 75 and 110 kilograms, likely an omnivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Famous fossil specimens found in a brooding position, sitting atop their nests with arms covering the eggs

name: Citipes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Possesses a distinctive strongly upturned beak tip and a square-shaped chin in lateral view
A small-bodied dinosaur measuring approximately 1 to 2.1 meters in length, likely weighing around 82 centimeters in height at the hip, and feeding on plants
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Often recognized as a small, bird-like dinosaur with a unique, sharply curved beak, historically confused with other genera like Chirostenotes and Ornithomimus due to fragmentary remains

name: Claosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pierre Shale / Niobrara Formation, approximately 72.2–89.8 million years ago.
It is classified as a hadrosauroid, a group of ornithopod dinosaurs that includes the ancestors of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs.
It was a relatively small herbivore, estimated to be about 3.7 meters (12 feet) long and weighing as much as a lion.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'broken lizard,' referring to the unusual position in which its fossilized remains were originally discovered.

name: Coahuilaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses hyperrobust, massive brow horns that are notably thick and lack constriction near the tips, a feature more similar to Pentaceratops than other close relatives.
This chasmosaurine ceratopsian measured approximately 6.7 meters in length and weighed between 4 and 5 tons, feeding on low-growing vegetation during the Late Cretaceous.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur from northern Mexico, distinguished by its exceptionally large and robust horns that rival the size of those found on Triceratops.

name: Coahuilasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive duck-billed snout and jaw structure characteristic of kritosaurin hadrosaurids.
It was a large herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 9 meters in length and weighing over 4 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the iconic group of dinosaurs commonly known as duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Coelophysis
