hints: Fossils recovered from the Chinle / Petrified Forest Formation, approximately 199.5–237.0 million years ago.
It possesses hollow bones, a feature reflected in its name which translates to 'false form'.
It was a small, slender, bipedal carnivore that grew up to 3 meters long and lived during the late Triassic period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
It is famous for the Ghost Ranch quarry discovery, where hundreds of skeletons were found preserved together in a single mass burial.

name: Coelurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Arundel Clay Formation, approximately 113.2–157.9 million years ago.
Its name translates to 'hollow tail', referring to the distinctive hollow, thin-walled vertebrae that characterize its skeleton.
It was a small, bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 2.4 meters in length and weighing between 13 and 20 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically significant as the first named small theropod dinosaur from the Morrison Formation, discovered during the famous 'Bone Wars'.

name: Colepiocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Foremost Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A steeply down-turned parietal bone and two incipient nodes tucked under the posterior margin of the parietosquamosal border, lacking the lateral and posteriosquamosal shelves found in related species.
A Late Cretaceous herbivore from Alberta, Canada, belonging to the group of thick-skulled dinosaurs known for their domed skulls.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'knucklehead,' referring to the curled, knuckle-like appearance of the bony dome on its skull.

name: Coloradisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Colorados Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A distinctive nearly 90-degree angle between the pterygoid and quadratojugal rami in the skull structure.
An estimated length of 3 meters (10 feet) and a mass of 70 kg (150 lb), classified as a plant-eating basal sauropodomorph.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
A Late Triassic herbivore from Argentina known for its relatively small size compared to later giant sauropods.

name: Comahuesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lohan Cura Formation, approximately 110.1–119.6 million years ago.
A thin keel on the bottom of the dorsal vertebrae, long prezygapophyses, and a very robust humerus
A large sauropod with a humerus measuring 67.5 cm and a femur measuring 113 cm, indicating a massive herbivorous body
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A long-necked, four-legged plant-eater from the Early Cretaceous period of Argentina

name: Compsognathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Alcobaça / Painten Formation, approximately 145.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses a slender, bird-like skeleton with a long tail and relatively small head, featuring a jaw named for its 'elegant' or 'refined' structure.

This theropod was approximately the size of a modern chicken, measuring up to 1.4 meters in length, and is one of the few dinosaurs with a confirmed diet of small, agile lizards preserved in its stomach cavity.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically famous for being the first theropod dinosaur discovered from a reasonably complete skeleton and was long considered the smallest known non-avian dinosaur.

name: Comptonatus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive bony crest on its snout, a feature that helps distinguish it from other closely related iguanodontians.
This early Cretaceous herbivore was a relatively large ornithopod, measuring approximately 5 to 6 meters in length and weighing around 1 ton.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is visually recognizable as a member of the 'Iguanodon family,' sharing the classic bulky body, short tail, and thumb spikes associated with these early duck-billed relatives.

name: Concavenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Huérguina Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed two extremely tall neural spines on its dorsal vertebrae that formed a tall, narrow, pointed crest or hump on its back.
It was a medium-sized carnivore measuring approximately 5 to 6 meters in length and weighing between 320 and 400 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is known for having quill-like knobs on its ulna bone, suggesting it may have had primitive feather-like structures on its arms.

name: Conchoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a toothless dinosaur with a powerful, bird-like beak and notably lacks the prominent head crest found in many of its close relatives.
It was a small theropod measuring only 1 to 2 meters long, likely possessing a strong bite force capable of crushing hard-shelled prey or tough vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is an oviraptorid from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, originally named for the hypothesis that it specialized in eating mollusks.

name: Condorraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 168.6–180.4 million years ago.
A shallow depression on the lateral surface of the tibia at the base of the cnemial crest, along with a distinct dorsal step on metatarsal IV
Approximately 4.5 meters long and weighing around 200 kg, this carnivorous theropod was among the earliest large South American predators
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a basal member of the megalosauroid group, closely related to Piatnitzkysaurus, and represents one of the few large predatory dinosaurs known from the Early Jurassic of South America

name: Convolosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Twin Mountains Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
The premaxilla bears four teeth, each featuring a distinctive vertical trough on the outside, and the rear end of the ischium is expanded into a 'foot'.
It was a small herbivore, with the subadult holotype measuring between 2.5 and 3 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is known as the 'flocking lizard' due to the discovery of a massive concentration of juvenile fossils at a single site in Texas.

name: Coronosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, crown-like arrangement of irregular, spiky bone masses fused to the rear margin of its large skull frill.
It was a mid-sized herbivore measuring approximately 5 meters in length and weighing around 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'crowned lizard,' referencing the distinctive crown-shaped ornamentation on the back of its head.

name: Corythoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hekou Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A tall, hollow crest on its head that resembles the casque of a modern cassowary, which was likely used for display and sound resonance.
A toothless, beaked dinosaur approximately 1.6 meters (5 feet) long that likely fed on nuts, seeds, and drought-resistant plants.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
The distinctive helmet-like crest on its head, which gives the animal its name meaning 'helmeted thief'.

name: Corythosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A tall, hollow bony crest on top of its head that resembles a Corinthian helmet or the casque of a cassowary bird, which likely functioned as a resonating chamber for vocalizations.
An herbivore measuring approximately 7.7 to 9 meters (25–30 ft) in length and weighing around 2.5 to 3.8 tonnes, with a diet consisting of conifer needles, seeds, twigs, and fruits.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive helmet-shaped cranial crest, which is the most recognizable feature and the reason for its name meaning 'helmeted lizard'.

name: Craspedodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Glauconie de Lonzée Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
Its name literally translates to 'edge tooth', referring to the distinctive strong ridges that cross its fossilized teeth.
It is a small herbivorous dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Europe, with its classification debated between early horned dinosaurs and iguanodont-like ornithopods.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as one of the earliest potential discoveries of horned dinosaurs (neoceratopsians) in Europe, though it remains a poorly understood and debated fossil.

name: Cratoavis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Crato Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
It is an enantiornithine, a diverse group of extinct birds that possessed a unique shoulder joint structure different from modern birds.
This Early Cretaceous bird was small, roughly the size of a modern crow or starling, and was a piscivore that ate fish.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaur-era birds preserved with clear evidence of its last meal, showing fish bones inside its stomach cavity.

name: Crichtonpelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sunjiawan Formation, approximately 89.8–106.3 million years ago.
The apex of its cheek horn points distinctly upwards, and its upper snout armor forms an amorphous mass rather than distinct tiles.
This herbivore measured approximately 3 to 4 meters in length, and it is uncertain whether it possessed a tail club.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name combines a reference to the author of Jurassic Park with the Greek word for 'small shield', reflecting its heavy armor.

name: Crittendenceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fort Crittenden Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive frill featuring forward-curving, hook-like flanges along the center, extensive epiparietals on the sides, and a short ridge on the squamosal portion.
A medium-sized herbivorous dinosaur from the late Cretaceous period of North America.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a horned, frilled plant-eater with a distinctive bony shield extending from the back of its skull.

name: Cruxicheiros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chipping Norton Limestone Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
Lower and broader spines along its back, along with distinctive leg and hip bones that differentiate it from other large theropods
A large theropod estimated to be approximately 9 meters (29.5 feet) long, which was a carnivore
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a large, predatory dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic period of England, known for its powerful build and sharp teeth

name: Cryolophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hanson Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
A distinctive, thin, and highly furrowed nasal crest that runs horizontally across the top of the head from side to side, resembling a pompadour hairstyle.
A large theropod measuring approximately 6 to 7.7 meters in length and weighing between 350 and 780 kilograms, which was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is famously known as the 'Elvisaurus' due to its unique crest, and it holds the distinction of being the first carnivorous dinosaur discovered in Antarctica.

name: Cumnoria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kimmeridge Clay Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
A prominent ridge on the sternal process of the coracoid and an oval muscle scar on the front of the deltopectoral crest of the humerus.
A small, slender, bipedal animal approximately 3.5 meters long that was an herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It was originally misidentified as a species of Iguanodon before being recognized as a distinct genus.

name: Daanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It is known only from the partial skeleton of a juvenile specimen, making its adult anatomy and definitive classification a subject of ongoing scientific debate.
This Late Jurassic sauropod lived in what is now Sichuan Province, China, approximately 163 to 145 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
As a long-necked plant-eater, it belongs to the group of massive dinosaurs famous for their extremely long necks and small heads.

name: Dacentrurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villar del Arzobispo / Lourinhã Formation, approximately 121.4–154.8 million years ago.
A row of large, diamond-shaped bony plates running along its back and a pair of long, sharp spikes at the end of its tail
An herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 9 meters long and weighing between 5 and 7.4 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Its distinctive silhouette featuring a row of plates on its back and a spiked tail, characteristic of stegosaurs

name: Daemonosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chinle Formation, approximately 201.4–205.7 million years ago.
A short, deep skull with a uniquely large prefrontal bone forming half the upper edge of the eye socket, and prominent, fang-like front teeth that are longer than the rest.
An estimated length of 1.5 to 2.2 meters (5 to 7 feet) and a weight comparable to a beaver, classified as a small, carnivorous predator.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
Its name translates to 'Demon Lizard,' referencing its demonic-sounding genus name and its large, sharp, fang-like teeth.

name: Dahalokely
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ambolafotsy Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A distinctive convex lamina connecting the prezygapophysis and epipophysis on the cervical vertebra, with the convex portion being longer than the vertebral body itself.
A small carnivorous theropod, estimated to be between 3.5 and 5.6 meters long, weighing significantly less than its larger abelisaurid relatives.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the only known dinosaur from Madagascar during the specific time period when the island was separated from Gondwana but still connected to India.

name: Dakotadon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lakota Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
Possesses a distinctive beaked snout and dental batteries characteristic of early iguanodonts, with a skull structure that suggests a more basal position within the iguanodont lineage.
An herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing around 1 metric ton.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few known dinosaur genera from the Early Cretaceous period of North America, specifically identified by its type specimen found in the Lakota Formation of South Dakota.

name: Dakotaraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a massive, sickle-shaped second toe claw that is approximately 29% the length of its thigh bone, along with long feathers on its arms resembling bird wings.
It was an exceptionally large dromaeosaurid, measuring between 4.35 and 6 meters in length and weighing up to 350 kilograms, making it one of the biggest members of its family.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It was discovered in the Hell Creek Formation of South Dakota and is potentially one of the last surviving species of its kind, living alongside Tyrannosaurus rex at the very end of the Cretaceous period.

name: Daliansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, enlarged and robust claw on the fourth digit of its foot, in addition to the typical sickle-shaped claw on the second digit.
It is a small, lightly-built, and likely feathered carnivore measuring approximately 1 metre (3 ft 3 in) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a small, bird-like dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of China, closely related to other troodontids like Sinovenator and Mei.

name: Dashanpusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A massive, column-like limb structure supporting a long neck and tail, characteristic of sauropods, with a partial pelvis and vertebrae found in the holotype specimen
A large, quadrupedal herbivore that lived during the Middle Jurassic period, with a body size typical of giant plant-eating dinosaurs
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its name literally translates to 'Dashanpu lizard', referencing the specific Chinese township where its fossilized remains were unearthed

name: Dasosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Itapecuru Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
A medium-to-large sauropod with a partial skeleton including phalanges, ribs, ulna, radius, tibia, fibur, femur, pubic bone, ischium, and at least ten disarticulated caudal vertebrae.
Approximately 20 metres (66 ft) long, this herbivorous dinosaur lived during the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Amazon rainforest region of Brazil.

name: Daspletosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A massive skull featuring a distinctive, thick, and bony brow ridge that gives the appearance of a muscular or brawny face.
A large bipedal predator measuring approximately 8.5 to 9 meters long and weighing between 2 and 3 metric tons, with small two-fingered forelimbs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the most fearsome and powerful apex predators of the Late Cretaceous, often considered a close relative or predecessor to Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Datai
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhoutian Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
It possesses a heavily armored body covered in bony plates called osteoderms and likely had a tail club formed by ossified tendons at the end of its tail.
This herbivorous dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in China and is known from articulated skulls and partial skeletons of immature individuals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of ankylosaur, a quadrupedal dinosaur famous for its tank-like appearance and extensive bony armor protecting it from predators.

name: Datanglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xinlong Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.

A distinctive pneumatic cavity in the last dorsal vertebra bounded by an enlarged posterior ridge, along with a shallow groove on the ilium for muscle attachment and a pin-and-socket connection between the hip bones
A large predatory theropod measuring approximately 8 to 9 meters in length, classified as a basal coelurosaur or megaraptoran
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known large-sized basal coelurosaurians from the Early Cretaceous period of China

name: Datonglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huiquanpu Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a unique dental structure where two functional teeth occupy each tooth position, with the main ridge of the inner tooth shifted backward and the secondary ridge well developed.
It was a herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now China.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Hadrosauroidea group, closely related to the duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Datousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–174.7 million years ago.
A deep, large skull that is unusually robust for a sauropod, along with elongated vertebrae that provided a higher reach and spoon-shaped teeth.
It was a herbivorous sauropod measuring approximately 15 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'big-head lizard' or 'chieftain lizard,' referencing its distinctive oversized skull compared to other long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Daurlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Longjiang Formation, approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
A nearly complete, perfectly articulated skull and evidence of plumage along the back of the skull, neck, and tail edges, alongside a preserved intestinal tract in the ribcage.
A mid-sized dromaeosaurid approximately 1.5 meters (4.9 feet) long that likely hunted small prey such as frogs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the rare dinosaur fossils where the internal digestive system, specifically the intestinal region, has been preserved in stone.

name: Daxiatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Upper Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses an exceptionally long neck, estimated at around 12 meters, which is a defining characteristic of its group.
It was a massive herbivore weighing approximately 23 tonnes and measuring between 23 and 30 meters in total length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered in China and is classified as a titanosaur or closely related basal euhelopodid.

name: Deinocheirus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Baynshire Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a prominent sail formed by tall neural spines along its back and arms that are disproportionately massive, measuring up to 2.4 meters long with large blunt claws.
It was a gigantic ornithomimosaur reaching approximately 11 meters in length and weighing around 6.5 tonnes, and it was omnivorous, consuming plants, fish, and gastroliths.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.

It is famously known as the 'horrible hand' dinosaur, a name derived from the initial discovery of its enormous forelimbs which remained a mystery for nearly 50 years before the rest of the skeleton was found.

name: Deinonychus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly / Antlers Formation, approximately 93.9–143.1 million years ago.
A massive, sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each hind foot
Approximately 3.4 meters long, weighing around 70 kilograms, and a carnivore that hunted ornithopods
This dinosaur lived in North America, South America during the Early Cretaceous.
The dinosaur that inspired the 'Velociraptor' in the Jurassic Park movies

name: Delapparentia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Camarillas / Blesa Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A large, sharp thumb spike on each hand, originally misinterpreted as a nose horn in early reconstructions
A bulky, large herbivore measuring up to 11 meters long and weighing around 4.5 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the first three dinosaur genera ever formally named, alongside Megalosaurus and Hylaeosaurus

name: Deltadromeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baharîje / Gara Sbaa Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed gracile, slender, and relatively long hindlimbs that indicate it was built for running, along with forelimbs that were proportionately longer than those of Allosaurus.
It measured approximately 8 to 12 meters in length and weighed around 1,050 kilograms, and it was a carnivore that lived during the mid-Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the gigantic theropods discovered in the Kem Kem Beds of North Africa, often studied alongside other massive predators like Spinosaurus and Carcharodontosaurus.

name: Demandasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Castrillo de la Reina Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
Teeth with a special ornamentation of ridges in its enamel and cervical vertebrae with unique bone structures not found in other dinosaurs.
A rebbachisaurid sauropod from the early Cretaceous of Spain, likely a herbivore with an estimated length typical of its family.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few known European rebbachisaurids and is closely related to the African dinosaur Nigersaurus.

name: Diabloceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wahweap Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive pair of very long spikes on its neck frill, similar to those found in Einiosaurus and Styracosaurus.
A medium-sized quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters (15 feet) in length and weighing around 1.3 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name translates to 'devil-horned face,' referencing the dramatic, spike-adorned appearance of its skull.

name: Diamantinasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Winton Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses opisthocoelous vertebrae with internal chambers (camellae) and a short axis vertebra, characteristic of its titanosaurian lineage.
This medium-sized sauropod measured approximately 16 meters in length and weighed up to 25 tons, feeding on vegetation as a quadrupedal herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is nicknamed 'Matilda' after the famous Australian song 'Waltzing Matilda', and its fossils were discovered in the Winton Formation of Queensland, Australia.

name: Dicraeosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru / Kadzi Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive Y-shaped neural spines on its neck vertebrae, which gave the genus its name meaning 'double-headed lizard'.
It was a mid-sized herbivore measuring approximately 14 to 15 meters in length and weighing around 5 to 6 metric tons, with a short, wide neck adapted for low-level browsing.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is a sauropod dinosaur that lacks the typical long, whip-like tail tip found in many of its close relatives.

name: Dilophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kayenta / Dharmaram Formation, approximately 184.2–201.4 million years ago.
A pair of longitudinal, arched crests on the top of its skull, which were likely enlarged by keratin and used for visual display rather than combat.
An Early Jurassic theropod measuring approximately 7 meters (23 feet) in length and weighing around 400 kg (880 lb), equipped with long, curved, serrated teeth for hunting.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its fictional portrayal in the movie Jurassic Park, where it was depicted with the ability to expand a neck frill and spit venom, despite these traits being scientifically inaccurate.

name: Diluvicursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Eumeralla Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A distinctive curled tail preserved in a deep, high-energy river deposit, along with robust lower leg bones and ankle structures.
A small ornithopod estimated to reach at least 2.3 meters in length for adults, roughly the size of a rhea, with a diet likely consisting of flowers, seeds, and mosses.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known from Australia, discovered in the Eumeralla Formation near Cape Otway.

name: Dineobellator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ojo Alamo Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed unique skeletal adaptations including quill knobs on the ulna indicating feathers, along with greater flexibility in the hands, feet, and tail base compared to other dromaeosaurs.
This medium-sized carnivore was roughly the size of Deinonychus, weighing significantly less than the apex predators of its time, and likely hunted smaller prey like mammals and turtles.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a feathered, bird-like predator from the very end of the Cretaceous period in North America, often compared to Velociraptor but distinguished by its specific anatomical features and recent discovery.

name: Dinheirosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
Its vertebrae feature massive neural spines that project upwards towards the posterior end, and its ribs lack pneumatization.
It was an average-sized diplodocid measuring approximately 20–25 meters in length and weighing around 8.8 metric tons, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, long-tailed sauropod from the Late Jurassic of Portugal, closely related to Supersaurus and known for having swallowed over one hundred gastroliths.

name: Diplodocus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive 'double-beamed' chevron bones located on the underside of its tail.
It was a massive quadrupedal herbivore, with some individuals reaching lengths of up to 35 meters and weighing around 23 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for its extremely long, whip-like tail that made up more than half of its total body length.

name: Diuqin
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A single preserved left humerus and fragmentary sacral and caudal vertebrae
A small theropod carnivore from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is named after a bird of prey and a witch from a 1975 Argentine film

name: Dongbeititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A camelar structure in its presacral vertebrae, pneumatocoels on the proximal ends of its dorsal ribs, and a medially bent proximal femur.
A large quadrupedal herbivore belonging to the Titanosauriformes group, which would have been massive in size.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of China.

name: Dongyangopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chaochuan Formation, approximately 93.9–113.2 million years ago.
A pelvic shield composed of numerous fused osteoderms arranged in a rosette pattern, accompanied by thin triangular plates, domed osteoderms, and cone-shaped osteoderms.
A plant-eating armored dinosaur with a robust femur measuring 46 cm in length, indicating a medium-sized body built for low browsing.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A heavily armored, hornless dinosaur from Asia that resembles a tank with a bony tail shield and spiked back plates.

name: Dongyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jinhua Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed eye-shaped pleurocoels on its dorsal vertebrae and a sacrum made of six fused vertebrae, a combination of features unique to somphospondylans.
This midsized sauropod measured approximately 15 meters in length and 5 meters in height, and it was a quadrupedal herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod known from fragmentary remains found in the Late Cretaceous deposits of Zhejiang province, eastern China.

name: Dornraptor
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Blue Lias Formation or Charmouth Mudstone Formation in Dorset, England, dating to the Late Sinemurian stage of the Early Jurassic, approximately 196 million years ago.

The dinosaur is identified by distinctive features in its leg bones, specifically a large scar on the medial side of the femur, a prominent anterior trochanter separated by a cleft, and an elongated cnemial crest on the tibia.
It was a small to medium-sized bipedal carnivorous theropod, likely comparable in size to Coelophysis, and is considered to have been the terrestrial apex predator of its local ecosystem.
This dinosaur lived during the Early Jurassic period on what is now the island of Great Britain, specifically in the Wessex Basin region of southern England.
It is notable for having been initially misidentified by Sir Richard Owen in 1859 as part of the armored dinosaur Scelidosaurus before being formally recognized as a distinct theropod genus in 2024.

name: Draconyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 145.1–149.2 million years ago.
It possesses maxillary teeth characterized by a strong vertical primary ridge on the distal side of the labial crown.
It was a small bipedal herbivore, estimated to reach approximately 3.5 meters in length and weigh around 150 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'dragon claw,' referencing the distinctive shape of its manual unguals found in the partial skeleton.

name: Dracopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Freixial Formation, approximately 143.1–145.1 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive small, flat osteoderms and paired circular plates with raised centers and rims that form a protective armor across its back.
This dinosaur was relatively small, measuring approximately 3 meters in length and weighing around 300 kilograms, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period known for its bony skin plates and shield-like appearance.

name: Dracoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Blue Lias Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses a furcula (wishbone), a skeletal feature rarely preserved in early theropod fossils, along with dagger-shaped teeth that have serrated edges running all the way to the root.
This was a small, bipedal carnivore that reached an adult length of approximately 3 meters and lived during the earliest stage of the Jurassic Period, about 201 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is widely recognized as the oldest known dinosaur skeleton ever discovered in Wales and one of the oldest dinosaurs from the Jurassic period globally.

name: Dracovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A kink in the upper jaws between the maxilla and premaxilla, along with a deep, oblique notch on the lateral surface of the articular bone.
A medium-sized bipedal carnivore measuring 5.5 to 6.5 meters in length and weighing around 250 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'dragon hunter,' referencing the Drakensberg mountains where its fossils were found.

name: Dreadnoughtus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Fortaleza Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a highly robust and massive skeletal structure, particularly characterized by extremely thick limb bones (humerus and femur) that were used to calculate its immense body mass.

It was a quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 26 meters (85 feet) in length, with weight estimates ranging from roughly 22 to 59 metric tons, making it one of the heaviest known land animals.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as one of the largest terrestrial vertebrates ever discovered, often cited in popular media for its staggering size and weight comparable to a large airplane.

name: Dromaeosauroides
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jydegaard Formation, approximately 137.1–139.1 million years ago.
Curved, finely serrated teeth resembling those of Dromaeosaurus
Approximately 2 to 3 meters long and weighing around 40 kilograms, this feathered carnivore possessed a large sickle claw on its feet
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as the first scientifically named dinosaur discovered in Denmark

name: Dromaeosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–110.1 million years ago.
A robust, deep skull with only nine teeth in each maxilla and heavily worn, cone-shaped teeth adapted for crushing rather than slicing.
A medium-sized carnivore approximately 2 meters (6.7 feet) long and weighing around 16 kilograms (35 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in North America, Asia/Europe, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Possessing a sharply curved 'sickle claw' on each foot, a hallmark feature of the dromaeosaurid family.

name: Dromiceiomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a short, deep skull with a toothless beak and a relatively short neck, distinguishing it from some of its longer-necked ornithomimid relatives.
This small theropod measured approximately 3 to 4 meters in length and weighed around 135 kilograms, feeding on a diet likely consisting of plants, fruits, and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'emu mimic,' reflecting its bird-like appearance and long legs, which made it one of the fastest-running dinosaurs of its time.

name: Drusilasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A large, elongated scapula that is approximately 143 centimeters long, along with a partial skeleton consisting of dorsal, sacral, and caudal vertebrae.
A massive titanosaurian sauropod, significantly larger than related species like Mendozasaurus, which was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the late Cretaceous period of Patagonia, Argentina.

name: Dryosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Marnes de Bléville Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possessed short arms with five fingers on each hand, contrasting with its long neck and long, slender legs.
It was a small, agile herbivore estimated to reach up to 3 meters in length and weigh around 100 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is often depicted as a fast-running, deer-sized dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period, frequently shown fleeing from larger predators.

name: Dryptosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lance / Marshalltown Formation, approximately 66.0–113.2 million years ago.
It possessed relatively long arms and large hands compared to later, more derived members of its family.
It was a large carnivore measuring approximately 7.5 meters in length and weighing between 756 and 1,500 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as one of the first theropod dinosaurs ever discovered and described by science in North America.

name: Dubreuillosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Calcaires de Caen Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
An unusually low and elongated skull where the length is three times the height, featuring a distinct kink at the upper corner of the maxilla's front edge.
A carnivorous theropod estimated to reach up to 9 meters in length and weighing around 250 kilograms as a subadult, likely hunting fish and marine prey.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Being a well-preserved Middle Jurassic predator from France that was originally misclassified as a species of Poekilopleuron before being recognized as its own genus.

name: Duonychus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a functionally two-fingered hand (didactyl manus), a rare trait among therizinosaurs that resembles the forelimbs of tyrannosaurids.
It was an herbivore weighing approximately 260 kilograms and measuring about 3 meters in length, using its large, strongly curved claws to grasp vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'two claws', directly referencing its unique two-digit hand structure.

name: Duriatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kimmeridge Clay / Mont-Lambert Formation, approximately 137.1–154.8 million years ago.
A distinctive deltopectoral crest on the upper arm bone (humerus)
A massive long-necked herbivore estimated to reach 25 meters in length
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Being a giant, four-legged plant-eater from the Late Jurassic period

name: Duriavenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Upper Inferior Oolite Formation, approximately 168.2–168.6 million years ago.
A deep groove on the upper surface of the jugal process of the maxilla, filled with numerous pneumatic foramina (air-filled openings).
A medium-sized theropod measuring 5–7 meters in length and weighing approximately 1 ton, classified as a carnivore within the family Megalosauridae.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically significant as one of the earliest fossil specimens ever assigned to the genus Megalosaurus, the first non-bird dinosaur to be scientifically named.

name: Dynamoterror
hints: Fossils recovered from the Menefee Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A very tall sagittal crest on the frontals that provided an expanded attachment area for large jaw-closing muscles, distinguishing it from tyrannosaurids with lower crests.
A subadult or adult individual measuring approximately 9 metres (30 feet) in length, belonging to the group of large carnivorous theropods known for their powerful bites.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the tyrannosaurid family, sharing the iconic reputation of being a massive, apex-predatory dinosaur with a massive skull and tiny arms.

name: Dyoplosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a tail club with a knob that is distinctly longer than it is wide, and features triangular unguals (claws) when viewed from above.
This armored herbivore measured approximately 4 to 4.5 meters in length and weighed around 1.2 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur covered in a mosaic of small, sub-angular bony plates known as osteoderms.

name: Dysalotosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
It lacked the large thumb spikes found in later iguanodontians and possessed short front limbs with powerful, long hind limbs adapted for bipedalism.
It was a small herbivore, estimated to be 2.5 meters long and weighing around 80 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs to provide evidence of a viral infection, specifically a condition similar to Paget's disease of bone.

name: Dyslocosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A sauropod dinosaur with a potential unique foot structure featuring four or five claws instead of the typical three found in other diplodocids.
A large, long-necked herbivore from the Late Jurassic period of North America, likely belonging to the Dicraeosauridae family.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Its scientific name translates to 'hard-to-place lizard,' reflecting the significant uncertainty and controversy regarding its fossil provenance and classification.

name: Dystrophaeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 154.8–157.9 million years ago.
A subtriangular projection on the base of the scapular blade, which serves as a distinctive anatomical marker for this taxon.
A moderately sized sauropod estimated to be approximately 13 meters long and weighing between 7 and 12 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the oldest known sauropods from the Morrison Formation, dating back to the Oxfordian stage of the Late Jurassic.

name: Dzharacursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
It is an ornithomimid theropod, a group of dinosaurs known for their bird-like appearance and long legs adapted for running.
This Late Cretaceous dinosaur was a relatively small bipedal carnivore or omnivore, with fossils including a femur and various limb bones discovered in Uzbekistan.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It belongs to the 'ostrich mimic' family of dinosaurs, characterized by their toothless beaks, long necks, and swift, ostrich-like movement.

name: Dzharaonyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A highly specialized, massively enlarged first finger claw on a reduced hand, adapted for digging or breaking into hard surfaces.
A tiny dinosaur, approximately 50 cm (1.6 ft) long and weighing around 1.75 kg, likely feeding on colonial insects such as ants or termites.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its miniature size and unique, mole-like forelimb structure that resembles a modern aardvark or anteater.

name: Dzharatitanis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A single anterior caudal vertebra featuring a distinctive high spinodiapophyseal lamina on the lateral side of the neural spine
A massive titanosaurian sauropod, likely reaching lengths of over 20 meters and weighing many tons, that was a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known from Central Asia (Uzbekistan) and is often compared to the giant South American titanosaur Argentinosaurus due to similar vertebrae

name: Echinodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lulworth / Morrison Formation, approximately 137.1–152.2 million years ago.
Its teeth feature prominent serrations along the sides, which inspired its name meaning 'prickly tooth'.
It was a very small, herbivorous dinosaur, with an estimated skull length of only 62 mm, making it one of the smallest non-avian dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is classified as a heterodontosaurid, a family of early ornithischian dinosaurs known for having different types of teeth in the same mouth.

name: Edmontonia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River / Aguja Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It features three large, forward-pointing, bifurcated spikes on its shoulders and rows of smaller oval bony plates covering its back and head.
It was a bulky, tank-like herbivore measuring approximately 6 to 6.6 meters long and weighing around three tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive shoulder spikes and heavy bony armor make it look like a prehistoric walking tank or a heavily armored bulldozer.

name: Edmontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses a broad, flattened skull that resembles a duck's bill, lacking the large hollow crests seen in some other duck-billed relatives.
It was a massive herbivore, growing up to 15 meters long and weighing nearly 16 metric tons, capable of walking on both two and four legs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for its exceptionally preserved 'mummified' fossils that retain soft skin impressions and even traces of ancient organic collagen.

name: Efraasia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Löwenstein Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A raised crescent-like ridge on the upper part of the inner side of the pubis shaft and a vaulted bony web between two lower extensions of a braincase bone.
A lightly built, medium-sized herbivore approximately 6 to 7 meters long that lived during the Late Triassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is considered a basal sauropodomorph, representing an early evolutionary stage in the lineage that would eventually lead to the giant long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Einiosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A large nasal horn that curves forward over the snout, accompanied by a row of spikes along the back edge of the frill
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters (15 feet) in length
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Bison Lizard', referencing the forward-curving shape of its distinctive nasal horn

name: Ekrixinatosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–113.2 million years ago.
A boxy, short, and deep skull with prominent supraorbital ridges above the eyes and facial bones sculptured with numerous small holes and spikes.
A large carnivore measuring between 6.5 and 8 meters in length and weighing approximately 800 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Abelisauridae family, a group of theropods known for having reduced forelimbs and robust, short hindlimbs.

name: Elaltitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses a unique autapomorphy in its anterior caudal vertebrae, characterized by dorsoventrally tall neural arches restricted to the anterior half of the centrum.
It is a massive lithostrotian titanosaur sauropod estimated to weigh between 23 and 47 tonnes, with a femur length comparable to the largest known sauropods.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, four-legged herbivore from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, known for being one of the largest land animals to have ever existed.

name: Elaphrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru / Morrison Formation, approximately 93.9–154.8 million years ago.
It has very short hindlimbs relative to its body length, with a tibia that is considerably longer than its femur, and a tail that ends in a rare downward bend.
It was a medium-sized dinosaur measuring approximately 6 to 6.2 meters long, weighing around 200 kilograms, and was likely omnivorous or herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Africa, North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is known as the 'light-footed lizard' due to its exceptionally slender, lightweight build and shallow chest compared to other theropods of similar size.

name: Elemgasem
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
A member of the Abelisauridae family, characterized by a short, deep skull and reduced forelimbs.
A small theropod measuring approximately 3.58 meters in length, likely a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
The first documented case of spondyloarthropathy (vertebral joint disease) found in a non-tetanuran theropod.

name: Elmisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is known primarily from fossilized foot bones, specifically a fused metatarsus and tarsalia, which inspired its name meaning 'foot sole lizard'.
It was a small theropod dinosaur, likely measuring around 1.5 to 2 meters in length, belonging to the caenagnathid group.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a rare example of a dinosaur whose initial identification and naming were based almost entirely on its feet rather than a complete skeleton.

name: Elopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sînpetru / Râul Mare Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a secondarily flightless body plan with robust, shortened limbs, a trait it shares with the closely related Balaur bondoc.
It was a small, bipedal paravian theropod that lived approximately 70 to 66 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its fossils were discovered in the Hațeg Island archipelago of modern-day Romania, contributing to the unique dwarfed fauna of that isolated ecosystem.

name: Elrhazosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A single left thigh bone and one upper arm bone, initially misidentified as a species of Valdosaurus before being reclassified into its own genus.
A lightly built, bipedal herbivore that was a fast runner, dating back approximately 115 million years.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
A basal iguanodontian dinosaur discovered in the Ténéré Desert of Niger, known for its reclassification from a European dryosaurid species.

name: Emausaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 182.9–184.2 million years ago.
It was covered in an armor of osteoderms, including three conical scutes and one tall, spiny element.
It was a small, low-dwelling herbivore that grew to approximately 3–4 meters in length and weighed up to 240 kg.
This dinosaur lived in Europe.
It is one of the few dinosaurs from the Toarcian age of the Early Jurassic with a formal scientific name.

name: Emiliasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mulichinco Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
A medium-sized ornithopod with a partial skeleton including a left scapula, coracoid, humerus, and a complete right hindlimb, known from subadult specimens showing ongoing bone modeling.
An herbivore from the Early Cretaceous of Argentina, estimated to be medium-sized in length and weight, feeding on plants like tree ferns and podocarp conifers.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A basal member of the styracosternan lineage found in South America, representing one of the oldest known ornithopods from the region and closely related to later rhabdodontomorphs.

name: Enigmacursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses specialized cursorial hindlimbs adapted for running, reflecting its name which translates to 'puzzle runner'.
This was a small, herbivorous neornithischian dinosaur that lived alongside giants in the Late Jurassic Morrison Formation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is a relatively recent discovery described in 2025, known from a well-preserved partial skeleton found in Colorado.

name: Enigmosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A highly distinctive, V-shaped fusion of the pubic feet and a tetraradiate process formed by the medial fusion of the obturator process and pubic body.
A medium-sized bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 5 meters in length and weighing between 454 and 907 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name literally translates to 'enigma lizard' or 'enigmatic lizard' due to the unusual and aberrant shape of its pelvic girdle.

name: Eoabelisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 174.7–184.2 million years ago.

It possesses a unique combination of primitive and derived traits in its forelimbs, specifically featuring a non-globular humeral head alongside a foreshortened manus with short, robust metacarpals and reduced claws, marking an early stage in the evolution of tiny arms.
This bipedal carnivore measured approximately 5.8 to 6.5 meters in length and weighed between 730 and 800 kilograms, hunting during the Middle Jurassic period in what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.

It is recognized as the oldest known member of the Abelisauridae family, pushing back the fossil record of this group by over 40 million years and serving as a crucial 'dawn' link in the evolutionary history of short-armed theropods.

name: Eocarcharia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A robust postorbital bone featuring a distinct, thick brow and a large, ovate orbital boss positioned above the back edge of the eye socket.
An estimated length of 6 to 8 meters, classifying it as a medium-sized carnivore among its relatives.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
The name meaning 'dawn shark,' referencing its early-diverging status within a family of large, shark-toothed predators.

name: Eocursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
Unusually large, grasping hands that resemble those of the Heterodontosauridae
A lightly built bipedal dinosaur approximately 1 meter long that was a swift runner with a tibia significantly longer than its femur
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
One of the most completely known early 'bird-hipped' dinosaurs, providing key insights into the origins of the group

name: Eodromaeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
A low, rectangular skull featuring an expansive antorbital fenestra edged by a sharp ridge, a promaxillary fenestra, and a rare row of tiny teeth on the palate bone
A lightly built, bipedal carnivore approximately 1.2 meters long and weighing around 7 kilograms, possessing long hindlimbs adapted for running at speeds up to 32 km/h
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
Often cited by paleontologists as resembling the predicted common ancestor to all dinosaurs, effectively representing the 'Eve' of the dinosaur lineage

name: Eolambia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
A straight quadrate bone and scapula that distinguish it from true lambeosaurines despite superficial similarities.
A large herbivore measuring up to 6 meters (20 ft) long that fed on gymnosperms, ferns, and flowering plants.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the most completely known iguanodonts from the Cenomanian to Santonian stages, with hundreds of bones discovered representing nearly every element of the skeleton.

name: Eomamenchisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhanghe Formation, approximately 161.5–174.7 million years ago.
A very long neck and posterior dorsal vertebrae that are fused together
Approximately 16 meters long and a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Jurassic period

name: Eoneophron
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A partial right hindlimb including the femur, tibia with a fused astragalo-calcaneum, and metatarsals III and IV
Approximately 78 kilograms (172 lb) in body mass and classified as a caenagnathid oviraptorosaur
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Known as the 'chicken from hell' due to its discovery in the Hell Creek Formation and its relation to Anzu

name: Eoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–233.6 million years ago.
It had a kink in its upper jaws between the maxilla and premaxilla, along with leaf-shaped teeth and a mid-mandibular joint in the lower jaw.
It was a small, lightly built bipedal runner measuring about 1.2 to 1.3 meters long and weighing less than 10 kilograms, with an omnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in South America.
It is widely recognized as one of the very first dinosaurs to ever exist, dating back to the Late Triassic period.

name: Eosinopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 152.2–168.2 million years ago.
A very short snout that is shorter than the diameter of its eye socket, along with slender feet and toes that lack the highly curved claws typical of predatory dinosaurs
A tiny body measuring approximately 30 centimeters in length, classifying it as a small non-avian theropod
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its name literally translates to 'dawn feather wing', referencing its status as a feathered dinosaur from the dawn of avian evolution

name: Eotrachodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mooreville Chalk Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive skull structure that places it as a sister taxon to both lambeosaurine and saurolophine subfamilies, representing a primitive hadrosaurid form
A Late Cretaceous herbivore from eastern North America, with a body size typical of medium-sized hadrosaurids
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaur genera discovered in Alabama, specifically from the Mooreville Chalk Formation

name: Eotriceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses an unusually pronounced and sharp jugal horn at the back of the cheek, along with extremely elongated, spindly skin ossifications lining the edge of its neck frill.
This massive herbivore measured approximately 8.5 to 9 meters in length and weighed around 11 metric tons, feeding on tough Cretaceous vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is visually defined by its iconic trio of facial horns—two long brow horns and a smaller nasal horn—framed by a large bony neck shield, making it instantly recognizable as a classic three-horned dinosaur.

name: Eotyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
It possesses serrated, D-shaped teeth and a distinctive concave notch on the front of its lower jaw that houses the first tooth socket.
This juvenile or subadult specimen measured up to 4.5 meters (15 feet) in length and was a carnivore that likely preyed on small mammals and other dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is an early, gracile relative of the famous 'tyrant lizards,' featuring long arms with three-fingered hands, distinguishing it from the short-armed giants that evolved later.

name: Eousdryosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
A unique foot structure where the first digit (big toe) consists of only a single phalanx bone, unlike most other ornithopods which typically have two.
A small bipedal herbivore estimated to be approximately 1.6 meters (5.2 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a basal relative of Dryosaurus, representing an early stage of iguanodontian evolution from the Late Jurassic period.

name: Epachthosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.

Distinctive autapomorphies include middle and caudal dorsal vertebrae with unique articular processes extending ventrolaterally from the hyposphene, a strongly developed intraprezygapophyseal lamina, and a specific pedal phalangeal formula of 2-2-3-2-0.

This titanosaurian sauropod was a massive herbivore, with referred specimens representing one of the most complete skeletons of its kind, though the exact length and weight remain estimated due to the fragmentary nature of the holotype.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most basal titanosaurian known to possess procoelous caudal vertebrae, a key evolutionary trait that distinguishes it from other members of the Lithostrotia clade.

name: Epichirostenotes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, slender, and highly flexible hand with three long, curved fingers that are adapted for grasping, a trait characteristic of its oviraptorosaurian lineage.
This small theropod measured approximately 1.5 to 2 meters in length and was a bipedal, likely omnivorous or herbivorous dinosaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a member of the group of beaked, bird-like dinosaurs that lacked teeth and were closely related to modern birds, often recognized by their distinctive crests or hand structures in popular media.

name: Epidexipteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
Four long, ribbon-like feathers extending from the tail that lack branched filaments, serving as ornamental plumes rather than for flight.
A tiny dinosaur measuring approximately 25 to 30 centimeters in length and weighing around 164 to 220 grams, with a skull featuring unusually long, forward-angled teeth only at the front of the jaws.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is recognized as the earliest known dinosaur in the fossil record to possess ornamental feathers.

name: Equijubus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A complete skull with articulated lower jaws and a notable absence of a palpebral bone, meaning the eyes lacked an upper bony rim.
A large herbivore estimated at seven meters in length and weighing 2.5 tonnes, likely a grazer based on evidence of grass consumption.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
The name translates to 'horse mane', referencing the Mazong Mountain region where the fossils were discovered.

name: Erectopus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baharîje / La Penthiève Beds Formation, approximately 93.9–125.8 million years ago.
A slender femur neck with a posterior curvature in its proximal half and a calcaneum that is twice as long as it is deep vertically.
A medium-sized theropod approximately 5 meters long and weighing between 200 and 315 kilograms, classified as a basal allosauroid.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'upright foot,' referring to the distinctive posture suggested by its leg bone structure.

name: Erketu
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses bifurcate anterior cervical neural spines, a rare anatomical trait among titanosauriforms, and its neck is composed of greatly elongated individual vertebrae.
This sauropod reached an estimated length of 15 meters and weighed approximately 5 tons, feeding as a high browser on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for having the longest neck relative to its body size of any known sauropod, with its neck estimated to be twice as long as its body.

name: Erliansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed enormous, strongly recurved, and pointed claws on its forelimbs, with the thumb claw being the largest.
This small to medium-sized bipedal herbivore measured approximately 2.6 to 4 meters in length and weighed between 91 and 400 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a therizinosaurian theropod known for its distinctive large hand claws and short neck vertebrae, discovered in the Iren Dabasu Formation of Inner Mongolia.

name: Erlikosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive combination of a sharp, bird-like beak at the snout tip and massive, elongated claws on its strong arms.
It was a relatively small herbivore, estimated to reach lengths of up to 3.4 meters and weigh between 150 and 250 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known to have had a backwards-directed pelvis, a unique anatomical trait among theropods.

name: Erythrovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candelária Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
The only known fossil is a partial left femur featuring a prominent pyramidal anterior trochanter offset from the shaft by a cleft, and a distinct lack of a dorsolateral trochanter.
It was a small, bipedal carnivore from the Late Triassic period, with the known leg bone measuring approximately 190 millimeters in length.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
Its name translates to 'red hunter,' referencing the red mudstone in which its fossil was discovered.

name: Eshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
A partial lower jawbone and teeth featuring six distinct characteristics that link it to therizinosaurs, despite some debate over whether the teeth resemble those of prosauropods.
An early Jurassic dinosaur known only from fragmentary jaw remains, making its exact body size and diet difficult to determine, though it is considered a potential early coelurosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being potentially the earliest known member of the therizinosaur lineage, predating other famous relatives by tens of millions of years and challenging the known timeline of coelurosaur evolution.

name: Eucnemesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 201.4–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses notably robust and thick tibiae, which is the specific anatomical feature referenced in its name meaning "good tibia lizard".

It is a basal sauropodomorph that was once mistakenly identified as a giant carnivorous theropod comparable in size to Allosaurus, but is now understood to be an early relative of the giant long-necked dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.

It is historically significant for being a Triassic dinosaur whose classification was confused for decades due to mixed fossil material, leading to its initial misidentification as a massive meat-eater similar to Herrerasaurus.

name: Euhelopus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mengyin / Shahai Formation, approximately 100.5–143.1 million years ago.
It possesses a relatively complete skull for a sauropod, featuring teeth that are distinctly inclined forward with a front buttress located closer to the top of the tooth.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be between 11 and 15 meters long, weighing approximately 3.5 to 20 tonnes depending on the study.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as the first dinosaur to be scientifically investigated from China, with its initial discovery dating back to 1913.

name: Euoplocephalus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Judith River Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A heavy bony club at the end of its tail and rows of large, high-ridged oval armor plates covering its body
Approximately 5.3 meters long and weighing 2 metric tons, it was a low-slung herbivore that used a horny beak to bite off plants
This dinosaur lived in North America, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive tail club, which it used as a weapon to defend against predators like Albertosaurus

name: Europasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Süntel Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive ankle bone (astragalus) that is twice as wide as it is tall, along with a prominent process on the back of its shoulder blade.
This dinosaur is exceptionally small for a long-necked herbivore, measuring only about 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing roughly as much as a large elephant.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for being the smallest known sauropod, a giant relative that shrank to a miniature size due to living in isolation on a prehistoric island.

name: Europatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Castrillo de la Reina Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive crest on the front surface of its dorsal ribs and triradiate laminae on the parapophysis of its cervical vertebrae.
It was a large herbivorous sauropod estimated to be approximately 20 to 21 meters long and weighing around 22 to 25 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Europe.

name: Europelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Escucha Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
It possesses laterally compressed, flanged osteoderms with a flat plate-like base, particularly towards the front of its pelvic shield, and a skull that lacks a distinct post-temporal notch.
It was approximately 5 metres (16 feet) long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes, feeding on low-lying vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most complete armored dinosaur skeleton ever discovered in Europe.

name: Eustreptospondylus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
Its dorsal vertebrae are opisthocoelous, meaning they are concave on the front side and convex on the back, a feature reflected in its name which translates to 'true twisted vertebra'.
It was an average-sized theropod, with adults reaching approximately 6 meters in length and weighing around 0.5 tonnes, and it was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is known for being the most complete skeleton of a large theropod ever discovered from the Jurassic period in Europe.

name: Falcarius
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–143.1 million years ago.
It possesses large, sickle-shaped hand claws that are ten to thirteen centimeters long and slightly recurved.
It was a bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length and weighing around 100 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is often described as a bizarre transitional dinosaur that resembles a cross between an ostrich, a gorilla, and Edward Scissorhands.

name: Ferenceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Densuş-Ciula / Sînpetru Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A narrow, strap-like scapula with an expansion at the end and a prominent, upwardly concave acromial process, alongside a coracoid featuring a very long, slender sternal process.
A small ceratopsian dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Europe, likely measuring a few meters in length and feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It was originally misidentified for over two decades as a small, bird-hipped ornithopod named Zalmoxes before being reclassified as a horned dinosaur.

name: Ferganasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Balabansai Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
It is known only from fragmentary postcranial remains, including dorsal and caudal vertebrae, a pelvis, and limb bones, with no cranial material ever discovered.
This massive herbivore reached an estimated length of 18 meters and weighed approximately 15 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, four-legged giant from the Middle Jurassic period of Central Asia, often compared to Rhoetosaurus.

name: Ferganocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Balabansai Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
Its teeth are notably smooth and lack the serrated edges typically found on many other dinosaur species.
It is known only from incomplete fossil remains, specifically teeth, making its exact size and diet difficult to determine with certainty.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is considered a 'dubious' or uncertain genus because the fossil evidence is too fragmentary to definitively classify it within a specific dinosaur family.

name: Ferrisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tango Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A bent ulna and a fused astragalus and tibia, characteristic of its classification as a leptoceratopsid ceratopsian.
A small, quadrupedal herbivore from the late Cretaceous period of North America.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the first non-avian dinosaur formally described from the province of British Columbia, Canada.

name: Fona
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It was likely a semi-fossorial animal that spent significant time in underground burrows, similar to its relative Oryctodromeus.
It was a small, herbivorous ornithischian dinosaur from the mid-Cretaceous period of Utah, United States.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the oldest known definitive member of the Thescelosaurinae subfamily.

name: Foraminacephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A thickened skull dome punctuated by many small pits (foramina) and featuring a tall, smooth squamosal bone bar underneath with six bony nodes along the bottom edge
A small, bipedal herbivore approximately 1.5 meters long and weighing around 10 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, pitted, dome-shaped head used for head-butting

name: Foskeia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Castrillo de la Reina Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A gracile, small-bodied ornithopod with limb bones indicating a developmental shift from quadrupedalism in juveniles to bipedalism as it matured.
An extremely small herbivore, reaching only about 0.5 meters (1.6 feet) in length, making it one of the smallest known ornithopods.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its generic name is derived from Ancient Greek words meaning 'light' and 'to forage', referencing its diminutive size and plant-eating habits.

name: Fosterovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A nearly-complete right tibia (shin bone) with a co-ossified (joined) astragalus, alongside a separate fibula from a larger individual.
A small Late Jurassic theropod, with the adult fibula measuring approximately 27.5 cm in length, indicating a relatively diminutive carnivorous predator.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
One of the many small theropod fossils discovered during the famous 'Bone Wars' expeditions at Como Bluff, Wyoming, in 1879.

name: Fruitadens
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive enlarged canine-like tooth in the lower jaw with a corresponding gap in the upper jaw, plus a unique small peg-like tooth located in front of the canine.
It was one of the smallest known ornithischian dinosaurs, measuring only 65 to 75 cm in length and weighing between 0.5 and 0.75 kg, and it is interpreted as an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
