It is recognized as one of the latest-surviving members of the heterodontosaurid family, persisting into the Late Jurassic period.

name: Fujianvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nanyuan Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
A small, feathered theropod with proportionately very long legs, where the tibia is twice the length of the femur, and flexible grasping fingers on its hands.
Weighing only about 641 grams, roughly the size of a modern koklass pheasant, and likely hunting small prey in a swampy environment.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A tiny, bird-like dinosaur from the Late Jurassic that was discovered in China and named for its 'bizarre' nature and origin in Fujian Province.

name: Fukuiraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kitadani / Sebayashi Formation, approximately 113.2–132.6 million years ago.
It possessed very large, flat hand claws that were initially mistaken for foot claws, along with distinctive blade-like teeth featuring wrinkled enamel and oblique blood grooves.
This medium-sized theropod measured approximately 4 to 5 meters in length and weighed between 175 and 590 kilograms, functioning as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the few dinosaur species discovered in Japan, with its name translating to 'thief of Fukui' after the prefecture where its fossils were found.

name: Fukuisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kitadani Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A skull structure where the tooth-bearing maxilla is strongly fused to the vomer, rendering the skull non-kinetic and preventing sideways chewing motion
A relatively small herbivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters in length and weighing around 400 kg, capable of moving on both two and four legs
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A basal ornithopod from the Early Cretaceous period of Japan, often compared in general build to Iguanodon

name: Fukuititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kitadani Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive stalk-like epipophysis on its cervical vertebrae, along with a relatively wide humerus and metacarpals that are about half the length of its radius.
This medium-sized sauropod measured approximately 16.1 meters in length and weighed around 5.75 tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Japan, known for its massive size and four sturdy legs.

name: Fukuivenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kitadani Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed a unique combination of heterodont teeth, featuring flattened, spatulate teeth at the front of the upper jaw and pointed, recurved, unserrated teeth further back.
This dinosaur measured approximately 2.45 meters (8 feet) in length and weighed around 25 kilograms (55 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is notable for being the most complete non-avian dinosaur skeleton ever discovered in Japan, found within a remarkably small 50 by 50 centimeter area.

name: Furcatoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive forked nasal horn that splits into two prongs, characteristic of the Nasutoceratopsini clade
A moderately-sized subadult specimen measuring approximately 5 to 6 meters in length, functioning as a plant-eating herbivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique 'forked' facial horn structure, which distinguishes it from other horned dinosaurs with single or paired horns

name: Fushanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from a single complete right femur bone.
It was a massive plant-eating sauropod estimated to be around 30 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the longest dinosaurs ever discovered.

name: Fusuisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xinlong Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It is a basal titanosauriform sauropod known only from fragmentary postcranial remains, including an ilium, pubis, anterior caudal vertebrae, dorsal ribs, and the distal end of the left femur.
It was a very large herbivore, estimated to reach 22 meters (72 feet) in length and weigh approximately 35 tonnes, with a relatively light body mass for its size.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few sauropods discovered in the Guangxi region of southern China, with its name translating to 'Fusui lizard' after the county where its fossils were found.

name: Futalognkosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
Its neck vertebrae featured extremely tall neural spines that formed a distinctive 'shark-fin' shape.
It was a massive herbivorous titanosaur measuring approximately 24 to 30 meters long and weighing around 30 to 50 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as one of the largest known dinosaurs, often cited in popular media for its colossal size and the nickname 'giant chief lizard.'

name: Fylax
hints: Fossils recovered from the Figuerola Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A nearly complete left dentary bone, characteristic of a hadrosauroid ornithopod
A plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Spain, known primarily from jaw fragments
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the very last surviving non-hadrosaurid hadrosauromorphs, living right up to the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event

name: Galeamopus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive braincase with a curved paroccipital process on the back of the skull and teeth featuring paired wear facets.
This herbivorous diplodocid sauropod lived during the Late Jurassic period in what is now the western United States, specifically within the Morrison Formation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, four-legged dinosaur closely related to Diplodocus, known for having some of the most complete and well-preserved skeletons among its relatives.

name: Galleonosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the "Wonthaggi" / Eumeralla Formation, approximately 110.1–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive kink in the posterior third of the upper jawbone (maxilla) that deflects posterolaterally, along with an S-bend shape at the base of the tooth roots.
A small, bipedal herbivore that lived approximately 125 million years ago in what is now Victoria, Australia.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'galleon lizard' because the shape of its upper jaw resembles the upturned hull of a large sailing ship.

name: Gallimimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 66.0–93.9 million years ago.
A long, slender neck with vertebrae resembling those of modern chickens, a toothless keratinous beak, and three-fingered hands with curved claws.
Approximately 6 meters long, weighing between 400 and 490 kilograms, and likely an omnivore or herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its appearance as a fast-running, ostrich-like dinosaur in a famous stampede scene from the movie Jurassic Park.

name: Galvesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villar del Arzobispo Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
It is a massive sauropod with a long neck and tail, belonging to the brachiosaurid family characterized by forelimbs longer than hindlimbs.
This giant herbivore likely reached lengths of around 15 meters and weighed several tons, feeding on high vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is visually recognizable as a giant, four-legged plant-eater with an extremely long neck, similar to a Brachiosaurus or Diplodocus.

name: Gandititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhoutian Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
Distinctive hollowed-out or cavity-patterned tail vertebrae
A massive, plant-eating giant estimated at 14 meters long
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A colossal, long-necked herbivore from the age of dinosaurs

name: Gannansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A K-shaped arrangement of vertebral laminae on its dorsal vertebrae, a unique skeletal trait shared with Euhelopus.
An estimated length of 25 meters, classifying it as a massive plant-eating sauropod.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a long-necked, four-legged herbivore from the late Cretaceous period of China.

name: Ganzhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive toothless, parrot-like beak, a key anatomical trait of its family.
This medium-sized dinosaur measured approximately 2.5 meters in length and was primarily herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is instantly recognizable by its bird-like skull and lack of teeth, resembling a modern parrot or chicken in head shape.

name: Gargoyleosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
A unique midline osteoderm on the first set of cervical plates, whereas most other armored dinosaurs have paired osteoderms on each side of the spine.
A small armored herbivore, approximately 3 to 3.5 meters long and weighing between 300 and 750 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known armored dinosaurs with reasonably complete fossil remains, often displayed alongside baby Stegosaurus skeletons.

name: Garrigatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by a massive, pillar-like body plan and a long neck, with fossil evidence including a sacrum, vertebrae, and limb bones.
It was a herbivore that could reach lengths of up to 16 meters and weigh around 2.5 metric tons, representing a large quadrupedal plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few named titanosaur dinosaurs discovered in Europe, specifically from the late Cretaceous period in southern France.

name: Garudimimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–93.9 million years ago.
It possesses a toothless skull with a rounded snout tip and short, stocky hindlimbs that indicate it was not built for high-speed running.
This medium-sized dinosaur measured approximately 3.5 meters in length and weighed between 77 and 98 kilograms, feeding on a diet of plants and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a primitive relative of the ostrich-like theropods, often recognized for its bird-like appearance and lack of teeth despite being a meat-eating lineage ancestor.

name: Garumbatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Arcillas de Morella Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.

A well-developed lateral bulge on the femur that was possibly more pronounced than in any other sauropod, along with a foot structure featuring the absence of a calcaneum and a reduced claw on the third toe.
A massive sauropod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of Spain, reaching giant proportions typical of titanosauriforms, and feeding exclusively on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur whose name literally translates to 'giant'.

name: Gasosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A carnivorous theropod with strong legs and notably short arms, featuring laterally compressed teeth and a pubic peduncle of the ilium that is substantially larger than the ischial peduncle.
An estimated length of 3.5 to 4 meters and a weight ranging from 150 to 400 kilograms, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its unusual name is derived from the English word for gasoline, honoring the fuel company that discovered the fossil site during the construction of a gas facility.

name: Gasparinisaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed unique low, plate-like triangular chevrons on its tail that were expanded towards the rear, a trait distinct among its ornithopod relatives.
This small bipedal herbivore measured approximately 1.7 meters in length and weighed around 13 kilograms, relying on clusters of polished gastroliths in its stomach to grind plant matter.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the few ornithopod dinosaurs discovered in South America, specifically from the Late Cretaceous deposits of Argentina.

name: Gastonia (dinosaur)
hints: Discovered in the limestone strata of the lower Cedar Mountain Formation in Yellow Cat Quarry, Grand County, Utah, dating to the Valanginian stage approximately 139 to 134.6 million years ago.
Possesses a distinctive sacral shield and large shoulder spikes, with a tail that is moderately long but lacks a tail club.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 5 to 6 meters in length and weighing around 1.9 tons, characterized by a flat, very broad rump and a strongly protruding belly.
Lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous period.
Known for its heavy armor plating, including bone rings around the neck and large triangular spikes along its sides and rump, giving it a formidable, spiked appearance.

name: Geminiraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
A distinctive triangular cross-section of the maxilla caused by a large pneumatic chamber, along with square-shaped alveoli separated by small bone walls.
A small, bipedal carnivore weighing between 5 and 20 pounds and measuring approximately 4.9 feet in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known troodontid dinosaurs, a group of small, bird-like theropods often depicted with large eyes and sickle-shaped claws.

name: Genusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marnes bleues Formation, approximately 106.3–110.1 million years ago.
A strongly extended and upwardly curved cnemial crest on the shinbone, along with a distinctive boss on the fibula.
A small theropod measuring approximately 3 to 3.6 meters long and weighing between 35 and 130 kilograms, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the smallest known members of the abelisaurid family, a group of theropods often characterized by reduced arms and specialized skulls.

name: Genyodectes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Barcino Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
Possesses four premaxillary teeth arranged in an overlapping en-echelon pattern, differing from the three found in Ceratosaurus.
An estimated 6.25 meters long and weighing 790 kilograms, this carnivore lived during the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It was the second non-avian dinosaur ever described from South America and remained the most completely known South American theropod for decades.

name: Gideonmantellia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Camarillas Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It is a basal ornithopod dinosaur known from a partial articulated skeleton of a juvenile individual, including thirty-three vertebrae, hip bones, and hindlimbs, but notably lacking a skull.
This small herbivore lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Spain and is considered one of the most primitive members of the ornithopod lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known relative of the famous Hypsilophodon, sharing a close evolutionary position as a small, bipedal, plant-eating dinosaur from the early Cretaceous.

name: Giganotosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros / Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A low skull featuring a ridge-like crest on the lacrimal bone in front of the eye and a flattened lower jaw with a downward-projecting 'chin' at the tip.
A massive carnivore estimated to reach lengths of 12 to 13 meters and weigh up to 13.8 tons, making it one of the largest known terrestrial predators.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being widely recognized as a primary rival to Tyrannosaurus rex in the scientific debate over which species was the largest theropod dinosaur.

name: Gigantoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A toothless, broad, shovel-like lower jaw ending in a keratinous beak, with sharply developed shelves on the top edges of the dentary bones.
A massive body size of approximately 8 to 8.6 meters in length and weighing between 1.4 and 2 metric tons, classified as a ground-dwelling omnivore or herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the largest known member of the oviraptorosaur group, a family typically characterized by small, feathered theropods, making its giant stature highly unusual for its lineage.

name: Gigantspinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
It possesses greatly enlarged shoulder spines that are twice the length of its shoulder blades, contrasting with relatively small triangular plates on its neck.
This moderately sized herbivore measured approximately 4.2 meters in length and weighed around 700 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a stegosaurian dinosaur known for its distinctive row of bony plates and spikes along its back, a silhouette instantly recognizable to the public.

name: Gilmoreosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a basal hadrosauromorph with a skull structure that is partly iguanodont and partly hadrosaurine, representing an ancestral form of the duck-billed dinosaurs.
It lived approximately 96 million years ago in what is now Inner Mongolia and Uzbekistan, and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the earliest known duck-billed dinosaurs from Asia, often cited in discussions about the continental origin of hadrosaurs.

name: Giraffatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses extremely elongated forelimbs that are significantly longer than its hindlimbs, giving it a steeply sloping back and a permanently elevated head posture.
It was a massive plant-eater estimated to reach lengths of 23 to 26 meters and weigh up to 48 metric tons, making it one of the tallest dinosaurs ever discovered.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Its common name translates to 'titanic giraffe,' reflecting its distinctive long-necked appearance that resembles the modern African mammal.

name: Glacialisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hanson Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
A robust medial epicondylar ridge on the lower femur and a kidney-shaped adductor ridge extending from the upper end of the femoral medial condyle.
An estimated length of 6.2 meters (20 feet) and a herbivorous diet, featuring a long neck, small head, and leaf-shaped teeth.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being one of the few dinosaurs discovered in Antarctica, with its name translating to 'icy lizard'.

name: Glyptodontopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ojo Alamo / Kirtland Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a heavy suit of bony armor called osteoderms, specifically featuring large, fused, flat plates on its back.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now New Mexico.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of armored dinosaur known as a nodosaur, characterized by its lack of a tail club and extensive body armor.

name: Gnathovorax
hints: Fossils recovered from the Santa Maria Formation, approximately 227.3–233.6 million years ago.
A unique additional small fenestra (hole) positioned above the subnarial fenestra on the snout, along with serrations on both the front and rear edges of its blade-like teeth.
A small to medium-sized carnivore from the Late Triassic period, approximately 3 to 4 meters long, that hunted small vertebrates like cynodonts and rhynchosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in South America.
Being one of the best-preserved and most complete skeletons of its specific group of early carnivorous dinosaurs ever discovered, providing rare insights into their anatomy.

name: Gobihadros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a double-layered tomial edge on its premaxilla and up to three teeth per tooth position in the lower jaw, traits typically associated with later duck-billed dinosaurs.
It was a medium-sized herbivore that reached a maximum length of approximately 7.5 meters (25 feet) and weighed several tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the most completely known basal members of the duck-billed dinosaur lineage discovered in Asia.

name: Gobiraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A thickened lower jaw symphysis with a rudimentary grinding plate pierced by four small oval holes, adapted for crushing hard food
A small, juvenile-sized dinosaur that likely weighed very little and consumed a diet of bivalves and seeds rather than meat
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A tiny, beaked dinosaur from the Gobi Desert with a specialized jaw for cracking shells, distinct from its larger, more famous relatives

name: Gobisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Miaogou / Haoling Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A broad, kite-shaped snout and a skull featuring short horns on the squamosal bones and cheek horns, but lacking the extensive bony sculpting found in some close relatives.
A large herbivore with a skull measuring approximately 46 centimeters in length, belonging to the armored dinosaur family Ankylosauridae.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a heavily armored, low-slung plant-eater from the Gobi Desert region that possesses a stiffened tail, though it lacks the massive bony club seen in more derived members of its group.

name: Gobititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
It possesses a relatively high number of caudal vertebrae and retains the fifth digit on its foot, traits that distinguish it from more advanced titanosaurs.
This herbivorous sauropod reached an estimated length of 20 meters and weighed approximately 20 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its genus name references the Titans of Greek mythology to highlight its massive body size, and it was discovered in the Gobi Desert region of China.

name: Gobivenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A pointed fusion of the parietal bones on the skull roof and a distinct hollow (fossa) located on the lower jaw bone just in front of the rear opening.
A small, bird-like predator measuring approximately 1.7 meters (5.6 feet) in length and weighing around 9 kilograms (20 pounds), with a diet consisting of small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the most complete and well-preserved skeleton of a Late Cretaceous troodontid ever discovered, offering rare three-dimensional preservation of its skull and body.

name: Gojirasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bull Canyon Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A robust tibia that is thicker than that of its relative Coelophysis, along with taller neural spines on its mid-to-posterior dorsal vertebrae.
An estimated length of 5.5 to 6 meters and a weight of around 190 kilograms, identifying it as one of the largest carnivorous dinosaurs of the Late Triassic Period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Triassic.
Its name is derived from the Japanese movie monster Godzilla, referencing its relatively large size compared to other Triassic theropods.

name: Gondwanatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Adamantina / Cambambe Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Distinctively heart-shaped middle caudal vertebrae with elongated centra and anteriorly angled neural spines
A fairly small sauropod measuring approximately 7 meters (23 ft) in length and weighing about 1 tonne, feeding on tough shoots and leaves from treetops
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A titanosaurian sauropod discovered in Brazil that was briefly classified as a species of Aeolosaurus before being recognized as a separate genus

name: Gongbusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou / Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–168.2 million years ago.
It is known almost exclusively from isolated teeth, including a premaxillary tooth from the beak and a maxillary tooth from the cheek, making its physical appearance highly uncertain.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 1.3 to 1.5 meters in length, likely a strong runner.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a poorly understood and dubious genus from the Late Jurassic of China, with its classification debated between ornithopods and ankylosaurs due to the lack of skeletal remains.

name: Gongpoquansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A basal hadrosauroid with a partial skull and postcranial skeleton, representing an early stage in the evolution of duck-billed dinosaurs before the emergence of true hadrosaurids.
An herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period of China, likely comparable in size to other early hadrosauroids such as Probactrosaurus.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as the dinosaur originally described in 1997 as Probactrosaurus mazongshanensis before being recognized as a distinct genus in 2014.

name: Gongxianosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ziliujing Formation, approximately 174.7–184.2 million years ago.
It possessed massive vertebrae lacking pleurocoels (deep lateral excavations) and was the only known sauropod with ossified distal tarsals.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore that reached approximately 14 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a large, four-legged dinosaur with a long neck and tail from the early Jurassic period.

name: Gonkoken
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dorotea Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is a hadrosauroid ornithopod, a group of herbivorous dinosaurs closely related to the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, characterized by a blend of ancestral and derived skeletal features.
This dinosaur was relatively small for its group, measuring approximately 4 meters (13 feet) in length, and it fed on plants.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'similar to a wild duck or swan', reflecting its resemblance to waterfowl among non-avian dinosaurs.

name: Gorgosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A tall, distinct crest rising from the lacrimal bone directly in front of each eye, combined with a circular eye socket rather than the keyhole shape seen in many other tyrannosaurids.
A large bipedal predator measuring 8 to 9 meters long and weighing 2 to 3 metric tons, equipped with dozens of large, sharp teeth for hunting ceratopsids and hadrosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

Being one of the best-represented tyrannosaurids in the fossil record, with dozens of specimens including a virtually complete growth series, making it one of the most well-understood members of its family.

name: Goyocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
A skull roof featuring many small bony bumps along the posterior edge that served as the base for small horns, combined with heterodont dentition including large caniniform teeth in the premaxilla.

A primitive pachycephalosaurian from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, likely a bipedal herbivore with a sturdy build characterized by a strongly bowed humerus and a wide, sub-rectangular postacetabular process on the ilium.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known flat-headed or low-domed relatives of the famous 'bone-headed' dinosaurs, distinguished by its decorated skull and lack of a thick, rounded dome.

name: Graciliceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A fragile skull frill featuring large openings (fenestrae) bounded by very slender bony struts, along with a tibial-femoral ratio of 1.2:1.
An estimated adult length of around 2 meters (6.6 ft) and a weight of approximately 2.27 to 9.1 kg, classified as a plant-eating neoceratopsian.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as a small, basal member of the horned dinosaur lineage, often compared in size and frill structure to the more famous Protoceratops.

name: Graciliraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses extremely elongated middle tail vertebrae connected by thin bone sheaths that stiffen the tail, alongside very long lower leg bones.
It was a small, lightweight carnivore measuring approximately one meter in length and weighing around 1.5 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is an early member of the dromaeosaurid group, closely related to the four-winged Microraptor, representing one of the oldest known dromaeosaurids with good fossil remains.

name: Gryphoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Milk River Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive beaked head that inspired its name meaning 'griffin horned face'.
It was a small, herbivorous dinosaur, representing the smallest known adult ceratopsian from North America.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the oldest known member of the leptoceratopsid family of horned dinosaurs.

name: Gryposaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive narrow, arching nasal hump on its snout, often described as resembling a 'Roman nose'.
This large herbivore reached approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and likely inhabited river settings.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur easily recognized by the prominent arch on its face, which may have been used for species identification or combat.

name: Guaibasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Caturrita Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses a resting posture with hindlimbs tucked underneath the body and forelimbs flexed to the side, a position similar to that of modern birds.

It was a small dinosaur measuring between 2 and 3 meters in length and weighing approximately 10 to 35 kilograms, with a classification that remains debated between early plant-eaters and meat-eaters.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs, discovered in the Late Triassic rocks of southern Brazil.

name: Gualicho
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses significantly reduced forelimbs and hands with only two or possibly three digits, a trait convergent with tyrannosaurids.
This carnivorous theropod measured approximately 6 to 7 meters in length and lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is frequently compared to Tyrannosaurus rex due to its similar body plan featuring a large body and tiny, vestigial arms.

name: Guanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
A large, thin, and delicate cranial crest that was likely used for display purposes, which is more elaborate and proportionately larger than those of similar dinosaurs like Dilophosaurus.
A small, bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 3 to 3.5 meters in length and weighing around 125 kilograms, possessing three long fingers on its hands.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is considered one of the earliest known relatives of Tyrannosaurus rex, representing a primitive stage in tyrannosauroid evolution from the Late Jurassic period.

name: Guemesia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Blanquitos Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive, nearly complete braincase that reveals it belongs to the Abelisauridae family, characterized by a short, tall skull and reduced forelimbs.
A small theropod, measuring approximately 2 to 3 meters in length, weighing around 100 kilograms, and acting as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the smallest known members of the abelisaurid group, which includes the famous Carnotaurus, making its diminutive size its most notable trait among its relatives.

name: Gyposaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng / Zhenzhuchong Formation, approximately 174.7–201.4 million years ago.
A basal sauropodomorph with a partial skeleton including dorsal and caudal vertebrae, ribs, gastralia, and limb bones, originally described from the Upper Elliot Formation.

An early Jurassic prosauropod from South Africa and China, typically interpreted as a juvenile specimen of other genera like Massospondylus or Lufengosaurus, with a name meaning 'vulture lizard' due to outdated carnivore hypotheses.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A small, early-diverging dinosaur relative of the giant long-necked sauropods, often confused with or considered a young version of more famous prosauropods from the same era.

name: Hadrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marshalltown / Woodbury Formation, approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a shortened pectoral crest on the humerus that is slightly over 40% of the total bone length, along with a notably robust fibula compared to other members of its family.
It was a large herbivore measuring approximately 7 to 8 meters in length and weighing between 2 to 4 tons, equipped with a keratinous beak and specialized dental batteries for processing foliage.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the historic distinction of being the first dinosaur skeleton ever mounted and displayed in a standing pose, as well as serving as the official state dinosaur of New Jersey.

name: Haestasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hastings Beds Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
A distinctive protruding inner front corner on the humerus forming a processus entepicondylaris anterior, alongside a unique combination of a robust ulna and a slender radius.
A large quadrupedal herbivore with a long neck, estimated to be substantial in size based on a 599-millimeter humerus, belonging to the Macronaria group.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs known from preserved skin impressions that show non-overlapping hexagonal scales with small covering protuberances.

name: Hagryphus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a member of the Oviraptorosauria group, characterized by a shortened snout, massive toothless jaws, and often elaborate head crests.
It was a relatively large oviraptorosaur, estimated to be about 2.4 to 3 meters (8 to 10 feet) long and weighing around 50 kilograms (110 lbs), likely feeding on plants or small vertebrates.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Ha's griffin', referencing the mythological bird-like creature, and it is known from a single incomplete hand and wrist fossil found in southern Utah.

name: Halszkaraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A spoon-shaped, flattened snout with eleven closely packed, elongated premaxillary teeth and an extremely elongated neck that makes up half of its body length.
Approximately the size of a mallard duck, it was a carnivore that likely hunted small aquatic prey in a semiaquatic lifestyle.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique combination of a long, snake-like neck and a flattened, duck-like bill among feathered dinosaurs.

name: Hamititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shengjinkou Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It possesses a series of seven caudal vertebrae accompanied by three chevrons, characteristic of its classification as a titanosaur.
This massive sauropod reached giant proportions, typical of its group, and was a herbivore that fed on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as a colossal, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China.

name: Hanssuesia
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation (76.5–75 Ma), the Oldman Formation (77.5–76.5 Ma), and the upper Judith River Formation in Alberta and Montana.
It possessed a thick, domed skull roof with a depressed parietal region, a wide frontoparietal dome, and a broad nasal boss on the frontals.
This herbivorous dinosaur had a skull dome adapted for head-butting, though its exact body size is estimated to be comparable to other medium-sized pachycephalosaurs.
It lived during the Late Cretaceous period in North America, specifically in what are now Canada and the United States.
It is easily recognized by its distinctive, thick, rounded skull dome, a feature shared with its close relatives like Stegoceras.

name: Haolong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A nearly complete juvenile skeleton preserving unique hollow, keratinous spines interspersed with scales along its body, which resemble porcupine quills.
An early-diverging hadrosauroid ornithopod measuring approximately 2.45 meters (8.0 feet) in length, likely herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'spiny dragon,' referring to the distinctive quill-like integumentary structures that cover its skin.

name: Haplocanthosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
Dorsal vertebrae that lack cranial centrodiapophyseal laminae and possess elongate intrapostzygapophyseal laminae
A relatively small sauropod measuring approximately 14.8 meters (49 feet) in length and weighing around 12.8 metric tons, which was a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
One of the few sauropods from the Morrison Formation known to have a complete enough skeleton to be mounted for public display

name: Haplocheirus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses heterodont dentition, featuring circular, unserrated premaxillary teeth alongside flattened, serrated maxillary teeth, and retains three functional claws on each hand.
It was a relatively small theropod, estimated to be about 2 meters long and weighing between 21 and 41 kilograms, likely feeding on small prey or insects.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the most exceptionally preserved Jurassic coelurosaurs known, with its holotype skeleton preserved in near-perfect three-dimensional detail.

name: Harenadraco
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A small, bird-like theropod with a distinctive sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each foot, characteristic of the troodontid family.
A lightweight carnivore approximately 1 to 1.5 meters long, likely feeding on small prey such as insects, lizards, and mammals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'sand dragon,' reflecting its discovery in the arid, sandy deposits of the Baruungoyot Formation in Mongolia.

name: Harpymimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khuren Dukh Formation, approximately 100.5–110.1 million years ago.
It retained small, cylindrical teeth restricted to the lower jaw, a primitive trait lost in most of its close relatives.
It was a medium-sized theropod estimated to weigh over 146 kilograms, possessing a beak and long legs for an omnivorous or carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the very few ornithomimosaurs known to have possessed teeth, distinguishing it from the toothless 'ostrich mimics'.

name: Helioceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quantou Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A deep and short lower jaw ramus with a steeply inclined facet for the predentary, and lower jaw teeth featuring asymmetrically grouped denticles and secondary ridges
A small herbivore estimated at approximately 1.3 meters in length and weighing around 20 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a basal neoceratopsian from the Middle Cretaceous of China, closely related to Auroraceratops, representing an early stage in the evolution of horned dinosaurs

name: Herrerasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
It possessed a partially open bony acetabulum and a backward-pointing pubis bone, representing a mix of primitive archosaur and derived dinosaur traits.
This lightly built carnivore measured up to 6 meters in length and weighed approximately 350 kilograms, using its long tail for balance while running on two legs.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is widely recognized as one of the earliest and most primitive dinosaurs, often cited in discussions about the very first members of the dinosaur family tree.

name: Hesperonychus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive 'sickle claw' on its second toe, a hallmark feature of its family of feathered theropods.

This dinosaur was exceptionally tiny, measuring only about 1 meter (3.3 feet) in length and weighing between 1.5 and 2 kilograms, making it one of the smallest non-avian dinosaurs found in North America.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a small, bird-like predator from the Late Cretaceous period that helps fill the ecological gap for small carnivores in its ancient habitat, often associated with the iconic sickle-shaped claws of dromaeosaurids.

name: Hesperonyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
Distinctive hand and toe claws, along with forelimb and hindlimb bones such as the ulnare, tibia, and fibula
An estimated length of 3 to 4 meters, classified as an ornithopod herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
A Late Jurassic dinosaur discovered in Portugal whose name literally translates to 'western claw'

name: Hesperornithoides
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A short rear branch of the lacrimal bone and a pneumatised jugal bone
Approximately 89 centimeters long and a small carnivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is a tiny, bird-like dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period

name: Hesperosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive back plates that are longer from front to rear than they are tall, along with four spikes on its tail end.
This quadrupedal herbivore was a large dinosaur, measuring approximately 6.5 meters in length and weighing up to 5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is easily recognized by the two rows of large, vertical bony plates running along its back and the spiked tail, characteristic of stegosaurian dinosaurs.

name: Heterodontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot / Clarens Formation, approximately 184.2–201.4 million years ago.
It possessed a unique set of heterodont teeth, featuring long, canine-like tusks in the upper jaw that were separated from chisel-like cheek teeth by a distinct gap.
It was a small, bipedal ornithischian dinosaur measuring approximately 1.2 to 1.75 meters in length and weighing between 2 and 10 kilograms, likely feeding on plants or omnivorously.
This dinosaur lived in Africa, North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is widely recognized for its unusual, mismatched dentition where sharp, tusk-like fangs are mixed with other tooth shapes, a trait that defined its genus name meaning 'different toothed lizard'.

name: Hexing
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive downward-curving snout tip that reaches below the lower jaw, a large antorbital fossa covering most of the maxilla, and a crest formed by the parietal bones joining at the midline.
A small theropod approximately 1.6 meters in length, classified as an ornithomimosaurian, which possessed a toothless beak and likely fed on a varied diet including plants and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a feathered, bird-like dinosaur with long legs and slender arms, often recognized as part of the 'ostrich dinosaur' group that resembles modern flightless birds.

name: Hexinlusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A marked concavity that extends over the lateral surface of the postorbital bone
A small, fleet-footed herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
An anatomically conservative basal ornithischian from the Middle Jurassic of China

name: Heyuannia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dongyuan / Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A toothless skull with a short, steep snout and very short arms where the first digit is reduced
A medium-sized dinosaur approximately 1.5 meters long and weighing around 20 kilograms, likely an omnivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
The discovery of thousands of eggs with blue-green shells due to preserved pigments, indicating specific parental care

name: Hippodraco
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
Shield-shaped dentary tooth crowns
Approximately 4.5 meters long and weighing between 227 to 454 kg, this herbivore was a relatively small iguanodontian
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its elongated skull shape resembles that of a horse

name: Histriasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
A long-necked, four-legged herbivore with a tail that likely ended in a whip-like structure, characteristic of diplodocoid sauropods.
An estimated length of around 10 to 12 meters and a weight of several tons, feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the oldest known members of the Rebbachisauridae family, discovered in the coastal limestone of Croatia.

name: Homalocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A flat, wedge-shaped skull roof that is notably rough with multiple nodules, contrasting with the domed heads of many relatives.
Approximately 1.8 meters long, possibly omnivorous, with long legs for a fast gait and an unusually broad pelvis.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few pachycephalosaurs known for having a distinctly flat head rather than a rounded dome.

name: Hoplitosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lakota / Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
A quadrupedal armored dinosaur featuring tall, asymmetric, hollow-based caudal plates and ridged ossicles, but notably lacking the sacral shield of armor found in its relative Polacanthus.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore standing approximately 1.2 meters (4 feet) tall at the hips, relying on its heavy body armor as its primary defense mechanism.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known North American relative of the more famous Stegosaurus, sharing the general body plan of an armored, plant-eating dinosaur with distinctive back spines and tail plates.

name: Horshamosaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in 1985 at the Rudgwick Brickworks in West Sussex, England, within grey-green marl beds of the lower Weald Clay dating to the Barremian stage, over 125 million years ago.

This dinosaur possessed distinctive dermal armour osteoderms with a circular base, featuring a front underside that was excavated and a rear underside that was solid, differing from the oval-based plates of its relative Polacanthus.
It was a herbivore measuring approximately five metres in length, making it about thirty percent longer than the type species of Polacanthus.
It lived during the Early Cretaceous period on the continent of Europe, specifically in what is now England.
It is an armored, plant-eating dinosaur known as a nodosaurid, characterized by its heavy bony plates and spikes covering its back.

name: Huabeisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huiquanpu Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses subcylindrical teeth with wrinkled enamel that are intermediate in slenderness between broad and narrow-crowned sauropods, lacking the strong twist seen in brachiosaurids.
This midsized sauropod measured approximately 20 meters (66 feet) in length and stood 5 meters (16 feet) high at the hips, feeding as a quadrupedal herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China, recognized for its relatively complete skeleton which provides key insights into Asian titanosauriform evolution.

name: Huadanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A prominent surangular shelf on the lower jaw and a deep skull adapted for high bite force, along with preserved filamentous integument around the tail.
An approximate length of 1 metre (3.3 ft) with a fairly large skull relative to its body, and a diet consisting of small mammals swallowed whole.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Evidence of reddish-brown or chestnut-colored feather-like filaments derived from fossilized melanosomes.

name: Hualianceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
A distinctive process on the rear branch of the jugal bone that points obliquely upward and backward, along with a deep and short dentary bone in the lower jaw.
A small herbivore measuring approximately 1.2 meters (3.9 feet) in length, roughly the size of a spaniel, with a skull length of about 25 centimeters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the earliest known horned dinosaurs from the Late Jurassic period in China, representing a primitive stage in the evolution of ceratopsians.

name: Huallasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Alamitos Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive duck-like bill and a crest structure that is less elaborate than the hollow crests of other hadrosaurs
Approximately 9 meters long, weighing several tons, and a herbivore that likely fed on low-lying vegetation
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its common nickname, 'duck lizard,' derived from the shape of its snout

name: Huanansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hekou Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive skull feature where the transverse nuchal crest on the rear skull roof is not very prominent, and a lower jaw with an obliquely protruding front point.
A medium-sized, toothless, bird-like dinosaur from the late Cretaceous period of China, likely omnivorous or herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as an oviraptorid, a group of dinosaurs famously known for their bird-like posture and often preserved in brooding positions over their nests.

name: Huanghetitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Upper Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
Fragmentary skeletal remains including caudal vertebrae, an almost complete sacrum, rib fragments, and a left shoulder girdle

One species measured approximately 12 meters long and weighed 3 metric tons, while the other possessed among the deepest body cavities of any known dinosaur with estimated masses up to 55 metric tons; both were herbivores
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A massive titanosauriform sauropod from the Early Cretaceous of China, recognized for having one of the deepest body cavities ever discovered in the dinosaur fossil record

name: Huangshanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongqin Formation, approximately 161.5–174.7 million years ago.

A distinctive skeletal combination where the proximal end of the humerus has a transverse length equal to 36% of its total length, and the craniomedial process on the ulna is longer than the craniolateral one.
A large, long-necked herbivore belonging to the Mamenchisauridae family, native to the Anhui province of China.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A massive sauropod dinosaur with an exceptionally elongated neck, known from a partial forelimb discovered during highway construction.

name: Huashanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wangmen Formation, approximately 161.5–201.4 million years ago.

A fragmentary partial skeleton including a dorsal vertebral centrum, partial dorsal rib, ends of the humerus and ulna, manual phalanx, part of the ilium, ends of the femur and fibula, and a pedal ungual.
An early-diverging eusauropod sauropod from the Early to Middle Jurassic of China, likely a large, long-necked herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being one of the very few sauropods discovered in the Guangxi region of southern China and named in 2025.

name: Huaxiagnathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A very long posterior process of the premaxilla that overlaps the antorbital fossa, along with large manual claws where the first two are subequal in length and significantly longer than the third.
Approximately 1.8 meters (5.9 feet) in length, this small carnivore fed on vertebrates, as evidenced by partially digested bones found within its skeleton.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a small, early relative of tyrannosaurs known from immature specimens found in the fossil-rich Yixian Formation of China.

name: Huaxiazhoulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tangbian Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A heavy, bony armor covering its back and sides, along with a co-ossified tail featuring a distinct bony club at the end.
A large, quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its appearance as a heavily armored, tank-like dinosaur with a spiked tail club, resembling a living prehistoric bulldozer.

name: Huayangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A quadrupedal herbivore featuring a double row of vertical, spike-like plates along its arched back and a tail equipped with horizontal spikes known as a thagomizer.
It was a relatively small stegosaurian, measuring approximately 4 meters (13 feet) in length and weighing around 500 kilograms, and it consumed plants.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is an early, smaller cousin of the famous Stegosaurus, distinguished by having premaxillary teeth in the front of its mouth which later stegosaurs lost.

name: Huayracursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Santo Domingo Formation, approximately 205.7–237.0 million years ago.
It is an early sauropodomorph with a nearly complete, articulated skeleton that includes cranial remains.
This Late Triassic herbivore was a relatively small, bipedal runner from the Carnian age.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
Its name translates to 'wind runner,' evoking the image of a swift, lightweight dinosaur from ancient Argentina.

name: Hudiesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kalazha Formation, approximately 143.1–152.2 million years ago.
Its vertebra features a distinctive bifurcated neural spine, meaning the upper part of the bone is split into two branches, which provided strong lateral control over its neck.
It was a massive quadrupedal herbivore, potentially reaching lengths of up to 32 meters (105 feet) and weighing around 55 tonnes, feeding on vegetation with a horizontal neck posture.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the largest known dinosaurs, belonging to the mamenchisaurid family which is characterized by having exceptionally long necks and shorter tails compared to other sauropods.

name: Huehuecanauhtlus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cutzamala Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses tall neural spines on its posterior vertebrae that are between 3.5 and 4 times taller than their corresponding centra.
It was a medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing between 1 and 1.4 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a hadrosauroid, a group of dinosaurs commonly known as duck-billed dinosaurs due to their distinctive flattened snouts.

name: Huinculsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It is distinguished by elongated articular facets on the prezygapophyses, pneumatic foramina on the underside of the dorsal vertebrae, and an accessory lamina on the dorsal neural arches.
It was an immature individual estimated to be around 3.42 metres (11.2 ft) long, belonging to a group of theropods that likely had a carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known only from five partial vertebrae discovered in the Huincul Formation of Argentina, making it one of the most fragmentarily known dinosaurs from that region.

name: Hulsanpes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive, highly specialized neck structure that is long, flexible, and S-shaped, resembling the neck of a swan or goose.
A small theropod, likely under 1 meter in length, that was an omnivore or piscivore adapted for swimming in aquatic environments.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique posture and neck shape, which make it look like a prehistoric waterfowl or duck rather than a typical predatory dinosaur.

name: Hungarosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Csehbánya Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possessed a body completely encased in hundreds of bony plates called osteoderms, with forelimbs that were unusually long for its group, nearly matching the length of its hind legs.
This small, four-legged herbivore measured approximately 4 to 4.8 meters in length and weighed between 650 and 1,000 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most complete armored dinosaur skeleton ever discovered from the Cretaceous period in Europe.

name: Hylaeosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tunbridge Wells Sand / Bückeberg Formation, approximately 110.1–139.1 million years ago.
It possesses a row of long, sharp spines projecting from its shoulder area and is covered in bony armor plates.
It was a herbivore measuring approximately five meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It was one of the original three dinosaurs used by Richard Owen to define the group Dinosauria in 1842.

name: Hypacrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine / Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A tall, hollow, rounded crest on the skull that is more pointed along the top and features a small bony point at the rear, combined with exceptionally tall neural spines on its back vertebrae.
An herbivore measuring approximately 9.1 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing up to 4.0 tonnes, equipped with dental batteries containing hundreds of continuously replacing teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being famous for the discovery of extensive nesting grounds containing eggs and hatchlings, which provided the largest collection of baby skeletal material for any single hadrosaur species.

name: Hypnovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ohyamashimo Formation, approximately 106.3–113.2 million years ago.
It is a small, bird-like theropod with a large braincase, long arms, and distinctive sickle-shaped claws on its second toes.
It was a meat-eating predator measuring approximately 1 to 1.5 meters in length, living during the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'sleep hunter,' referencing the unique fossil evidence that it was preserved in a sleeping position with its head tucked under its arm.

name: Hypselospinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wadhurst Clay / Speeton Clay Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
It possesses long, narrow, and steeply inclined neural spines on its vertebrae, which inspired its name meaning 'high thorn'.
It was a lightly built herbivore estimated to be approximately 6 meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is an early relative of the famous Iguanodon, often found in the same English formations.

name: Hypsilophodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex / Vectis Formation, approximately 100.5–154.8 million years ago.
It possessed a sharp, toothless beak at the front of its snout, similar to that of a modern parrot, used for cropping vegetation.
It was a small, agile bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 1.5 to 2 meters in length and weighing around 20 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as one of the first dinosaurs to be named and is often depicted as a classic example of a small, early ornithopod.

name: Iani
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A nearly complete, disarticulated skull and partial skeleton including cervical, dorsal, and caudal vertebrae, ribs, and limb bones
An iguanodontian dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period, serving as a transitional form between Tenontosaurus and Rhabdodontidae
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Named after Ianus, the Roman god of transitions, reflecting its evolutionary position between earlier and later ornithopod groups

name: Iberospinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Papo Seco Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive straight ventral edge on the lower jaw bone and a single opening within the jaw's internal groove, along with elongated neural spines on its back vertebrae.
A large carnivore reaching approximately 8 to 9 meters in length, likely weighing several tons, that specialized in catching fish with its elongated snout.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known dinosaur species discovered and named in Portugal, representing a unique branch of the fish-eating spinosaurid family.

name: Ibirania
hints: Fossils recovered from the São José do Rio Preto Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses highly pneumatized vertebrae with preserved pneumosteum, indicating a bird-like air sac system within its bones despite its small size.
It is a dwarf titanosaur estimated to be only 5.7 meters (19 ft) long, making it one of the smallest known sauropods, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the tiniest long-necked dinosaurs ever discovered, comparable in size to a large horse rather than the massive giants of its family.

name: Ichthyovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Grès supérieurs Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A unique sail on its back formed by tall neural spines that curved downwards over the hips and split into two separate sections, unlike the single continuous sail of other spinosaurids.
An estimated length of 8.5 to 10.5 meters and a weight of around 2.4 tonnes, with straight, conical, non-serrated teeth adapted for catching aquatic prey.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early Cretaceous.
Its distinctive double-humped or wave-like sail structure, which is visually distinct from the single tall sail of its famous relative, Spinosaurus.

name: Igai
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quseir Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A slender titanosaur skeleton featuring vertebrae with large, oval-shaped pleurocoels and a fifth metacarpal with two distinct tubercles on its medial side.
An estimated length of 10 to 15 meters, classifying it as a medium-sized herbivorous sauropod.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'the forgotten lord of the oasis,' referencing its long period of obscurity in scientific literature before being formally described in 2023.

name: Ignavusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A long, slender neck and tail, with small, leaf-shaped teeth adapted for a plant-based diet
A small body size, measuring approximately 1.5 meters in length and weighing around 22.5 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a basal sauropodomorph from the Early Jurassic of Lesotho, often compared to Massospondylus

name: Iguanacolossus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive shield-shaped and lozenge-shaped teeth that resemble those of modern iguanas, along with a prominent fibula and a pubis that tapers to a blunt point.
This robust ornithopod measured approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighed around 5 tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Mighty Iguana Colossus,' highlighting its massive size and the iconic, leaf-shaped dental structure characteristic of its iguanodontian lineage.

name: Iguanodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Arcillas de Morella / Weald Clay Formation, approximately 66.0–154.8 million years ago.
A large, sharp thumb spike on each hand, which was historically misidentified as a nose horn.
A bulky, herbivorous body measuring up to 11 meters long and weighing around 4.5 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the first three dinosaur genera ever formally named, alongside Megalosaurus and Hylaeosaurus.

name: Ilokelesia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive skull structure featuring an expansion of the postorbital bone above the eye socket and a flange inside the orbit, along with a T-shaped postorbital bone.
A medium-sized carnivore estimated to be between 4 and 5.8 meters long and weighing up to 840 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the Abelisauridae family, a group of theropods known for having short, boxy skulls and tiny, vestigial forelimbs.

name: Imperobator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Snow Hill Island Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
The calcanea (heel bones) are fused with the fibulae, and the second metatarsal is uniquely parallelogram-shaped in cross-section rather than circular.
It was a carnivorous theropod approximately 2 to 3 meters long that lacked the characteristic sickle claw found in many related dromaeosaurids.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of only two non-avian theropod dinosaurs known from Antarctica, discovered on James Ross Island.

name: Inawentu
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A wide, squared-off snout deflected strongly downward and a neck consisting of only twelve vertebrae, which is fewer than in any other known titanosaur
A large titanosaurian sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, likely a plant-eater with a proportionately shorter neck than most of its relatives
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique jaw shape demonstrates convergent evolution with rebbachisaurids like Nigersaurus, featuring a broad, square-shaped mouth adapted for browsing

name: Incisivosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fuxin / Yixian Formation, approximately 100.5–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses large, flattened, rodent-like front teeth that are highly unusual for a theropod dinosaur.
It was a small, likely herbivorous or omnivorous animal measuring approximately 1 meter in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its distinctive incisor-like teeth give it a striking resemblance to a giant prehistoric rodent.

name: Indosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A thickened, conservative skull roof that lacks the prominent horns or domes found in some relatives, featuring a broad transverse crest above and behind the eye sockets.
A carnivorous theropod weighing approximately 700 kg (1,500 lb), likely preying on large sauropods in a semi-arid or tropical humid environment.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the first member of the Majungasaurinae subfamily to be discovered, representing a distinct lineage of abelisaurid dinosaurs found in India.

name: Indosuchus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A crested skull that is flattened on the top, with a frontonasal suture placed anteriorly compared to the lacrimal.
A bipedal carnivore approximately 7 metres long and weighing about 1.2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A theropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of India whose name translates to 'Indian crocodile'.

name: Ingentia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quebrada del Barro Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses highly pneumatic, short but tall robust cervical vertebrae and a very expanded distal scapula blade.
It was one of the first very large sauropodomorphs to evolve, reaching massive sizes during the Late Triassic period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is named after its status as an early giant, representing the 'first huge one' among the ancestors of long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Invictarx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Menefee Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

Its body was covered in bony armor plates called osteoderms, which featured a distinctive smooth surface texture with abundant randomly distributed pits and lacked significant projecting bumps or rugosity.
It was a heavily armored herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period, known from incomplete postcranial skeletons including dorsal vertebrae, ribs, and limb fragments.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It belongs to the group of dinosaurs famous for their extensive bony armor and lack of horns, often referred to as 'armored dinosaurs' or 'nodosaurids'.

name: Irisosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 184.2–192.9 million years ago.
A large and deep neurovascular foramen on the perinarial fossa, a higher-than-long premaxillary ramus of the maxilla, and a strongly asymmetrical proximal half of metacarpal V
An early sauropodomorph dinosaur, likely a small to medium-sized herbivore or omnivore that walked on two legs
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A recently discovered 'iridescent lizard' from China that is closely related to Mussaurus

name: Irritator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Romualdo Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
A long, shallow, and slender snout lined with straight, unserrated conical teeth, topped by a thin sagittal crest running lengthwise along the head.
An estimated length of 6 to 8 meters and a weight of around 1 tonne, with a generalist diet consisting mainly of fish and small prey animals.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its skull was famously altered by fossil traffickers who used car body filler and plaster to artificially elongate the snout, leading to the dinosaur's name meaning 'irritation'.

name: Isaberrysaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Molles Formation, approximately 168.6–170.9 million years ago.
Heterodont dentition featuring recurved premaxillary teeth and lanceolate maxillary and dentary teeth, alongside a skull with a deep infratemporal fenestra and a triradiate jugal.
An estimated length of 5 to 6 meters with a potentially omnivorous diet, evidenced by fossilized cycad seeds preserved in the rib cage.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the few dinosaurs known from a holotype that includes preserved stomach contents, specifically a mass of fossilized seeds.

name: Isanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nam Phong Formation, approximately 174.7–192.9 million years ago.
Distinctly opisthocoelous neck vertebrae with convex fronts and hollow backs, forming ball-and-socket joints, alongside amphicoelous tail vertebrae that are concave at both ends.
An estimated length of 6.5 meters with a robust, straight thigh bone indicating column-like legs, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the oldest known members of the long-necked, four-legged dinosaur lineage that eventually included giants like Brachiosaurus and Diplodocus.

name: Isasicursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chorrillo Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Its sacrum is curved downwards and features a unique pin-and-hole connection between the first and second sacral vertebrae.
It was a small, herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the youngest known iguanodontian dinosaurs from the Southern Hemisphere.

name: Ischioceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A uniquely shaped ischium bone with a robust shaft resembling a recurved bow and an elliptical hole in its obturator process, which is a feature found in no other known dinosaur.
A small, quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 2 meters (6.6 feet) in length that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few horned dinosaurs known primarily from skeletal remains rather than a skull, with a distinctive elevation forming a crest on the proximal part of its tail.

name: Isisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta / Vitakri Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a notably short, vertically directed neck and unusually long forelimbs, distinguishing it from other sauropods.
This medium-sized titanosaur reached approximately 18 meters in length and weighed around 15 metric tons, feeding on tree leaves as indicated by fungal spores found in coprolites.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the most complete sauropod skeletons known from the Late Cretaceous of India, often found alongside theropods like Rajasaurus in the Lameta Formation.

name: Issi
hints: Fossils recovered from the Malmros Klint Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A skull with a weakly developed depression around the bony nostril, a small foramen below the nostril, and a quadrate bone that is tall relative to the snout height.
A basal sauropodomorph from the Late Triassic, represented by juvenile specimens with skulls approximately 24 centimeters long, which were herbivorous or omnivorous plant-eaters.
This dinosaur lived in during the Triassic.
It is the first non-avian dinosaur ever described from Greenland, with a name meaning 'cold bone' in the local Greenlandic language.

name: Istiorachis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
It possesses notably elongated neural spines on its vertebrae that form a distinctive sail-like structure along its back.
It was an iguanodontian ornithopod from the Early Cretaceous period of England, likely a herbivore with a body size typical of medium-sized ornithopods.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its most recognizable feature is the tall, sail-shaped ridge running down its spine, similar to that of Spinosaurus or Ouranosaurus.

name: Itapeuasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Alcântara Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A long neck supported by tall, box-like vertebrae and a humerus that is significantly longer than the femur, characteristic of rebbachisaurid sauropods.
A large herbivore estimated to be around 10 meters long, feeding on low-lying vegetation in a coastal environment.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the most complete diplodocoid dinosaur skeleton ever discovered from South America.

name: Itemirus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
