A damaged fossil braincase (neurocranium) that revealed details about the inner ear and brain structure, specifically the medulla oblongata.
A small, agile predator approximately 2 to 3 meters long, belonging to the dromaeosaurid family of feathered theropods.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the 'raptor' family, closely related to Velociraptor, though less famous than its larger cousins.

name: Iyuku
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirkwood Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It is a basal iguanodontian dinosaur known from a unique bonebed containing at least 27 juvenile and hatchling individuals, suggesting a nesting or nursery site behavior.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore from South Africa was very small, with subadult tibiae measuring only 35 to 42 cm in length, indicating a diminutive body size compared to adult iguanodontians.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
Its generic name translates to 'hatchling' in the Xhosa language, directly referencing the fact that the fossil material consists almost entirely of immature, young individuals rather than adults.

name: Jaculinykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A unique hand structure featuring a massively enlarged first digit and a greatly reduced second digit, representing an intermediate stage between three-toed and one-toed hands.
A small, lightweight theropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Mongolia, likely insectivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'speedy claw' or 'jaculus claw', referencing its rapid movement and distinctive single-clawed hand adaptation.

name: Jainosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod with a massive, pillar-like limb structure, specifically characterized by a humerus measuring 134 centimeters in length.
This giant herbivore reached an approximate length of 18 meters and weighed around 15 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of India, known for its enormous size and heavy build.

name: Jakapil
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
Possesses a predentary bone and uniquely deep lower jaw, along with large, low osteoderms forming a shield-like armor
A small, bipedal herbivore measuring less than 1.5 meters in length and weighing between 4.5 and 7 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'shield bearer', referencing the distinctive dermal armor plates that protected its back

name: Jaklapallisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maleri / Dharmaram Formation, approximately 201.4–227.3 million years ago.
A highly asymmetrical astragalus bone, which is the defining feature referenced in its specific name.
A Late Triassic sauropodomorph from central India, likely a small, bipedal herbivore or omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Triassic.
One of the few dinosaur genera discovered and named from the state of Telangana in India.

name: Janenschia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
It possesses a heavy build characterized by robust hindlimb bones, specifically a thick and sturdy right femur that served as the basis for its original classification.
It was a large herbivorous sauropod dinosaur that lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 155 to 145 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically significant as one of the earliest named titanosaurs, originally discovered in the Tendaguru Formation of Tanzania and initially misidentified under the name Gigantosaurus.

name: Jaxartosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dabrazhin / Ulungar Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a large cranial crest, likely used for visual identification or vocalization, which classifies it as a member of the lambeosaurine subfamily.
This herbivorous dinosaur reached a substantial size, comparable to its relative Corythosaurus, and fed on low-lying vegetation during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few hadrosaurid genera discovered in Central Asia, with fossils found in Kazakhstan and potentially China, named after the ancient name of the Syr Darya river.

name: Jeholosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A row of small foramina on the outer surface of the nasal bone immediately above the premaxillary articulation
A small bipedal herbivore with a total length of approximately 71 centimeters and a rapid maxillary tooth replacement rate of 46 days
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs discovered in the fossil-rich Yixian Formation of China, often preserved alongside early birds and feathered dinosaurs

name: Jeyawati
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moreno Hill Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive skull morphology characterized by a rugose, or rough and bumpy, surface texture on the facial bones.
This hadrosauroid was a medium-sized herbivore, likely measuring around 6 meters in length, which used its specialized jaw structure to grind tough plant material.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is visually recognizable as an early relative of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, featuring a broad, flat snout and a beak-like mouth adapted for cropping vegetation.

name: Jianchangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed a toothless, beaked premaxilla and small, lanceolate teeth with three equal-sized denticles on each side, adapted for slicing plant fibers.
This small, bipedal herbivore measured approximately 2 meters (6.6 feet) in length and weighed around 26 kilograms (57 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the earliest known members of the therizinosaur group, a lineage of dinosaurs famous for their bizarre combination of bird-like features and massive claws.

name: Jiangjunosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses distinctive diamond-shaped or circular plates on its neck, which is unusual as most stegosaurs only had plates on their backs.
It was a medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters in length and weighing around 2.5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a type of armored dinosaur famous for the rows of bony plates and spikes along its back and tail.

name: Jiangshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jinhua Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A unique hook-shaped process at the end of the lower shaft of the ischium bone and inwardly positioned ridges on the tail vertebrae
An estimated length of eleven meters and a weight of 2500 kilograms, feeding on plants as a titanosauriform sauropod
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a massive, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China

name: Jiangxisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A toothless jaw with a weakly down-turned tip and a very elongated mandible, alongside a three-fingered hand where the middle finger is notably elongated
A medium-sized omnivore from the Late Cretaceous period of southern China
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as an oviraptorid, a group of beaked, bird-like dinosaurs often associated with brooding over nests

name: Jiangxititan
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Known only from a partial skeleton consisting of articulated cervical and dorsal vertebrae with their associated ribs.
A massive, long-necked herbivorous sauropod dinosaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A giant, four-legged plant-eater with an extremely long neck, typical of the titanosauriform group.

name: Jianianhualong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
Asymmetric feathers on the tail, marking the first record of such plumage among troodontids.
Approximately 1.12 meters long and weighing 2.4 kilograms, this small carnivore had short-crowned, strongly recurved teeth with fine serrations.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs to possess asymmetrical tail feathers, providing key evidence for the early evolution of flight-related plumage in paravians.

name: Jinbeisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huiquanpu Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
Distinctive features of the maxilla, teeth, and pubis that distinguish it from other tyrannosaurs.
A medium-sized carnivorous theropod from the Upper Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the tyrannosauroid lineage, positioned between early forms and the famous giant tyrannosaurids.

name: Jinchuanloong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xinhe Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.

A distinctive skull with a notably broad snout in dorsal view, accompanied by a nearly complete skull and mandible articulated with the first five cervical vertebrae and the posteriormost caudal vertebrae.
An estimated body length of 10 metres (33 ft) as a herbivorous eusauropod sauropod, with fossil evidence suggesting the specimen may have been a juvenile due to unfused vertebrae.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic of China, characterized by its enormous size and quadrupedal stance typical of sauropods.

name: Jinfengopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huajiying Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive enlarged, sickle-shaped claw on its second toe, a feature characteristic of troodontids.
It was a small dinosaur, measuring approximately 55 cm in length, and likely had an omnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few non-avian dinosaurs known to have preserved extensive impressions of pennaceous feathers on its body and tail.

name: Jingiella
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dongxing Formation, approximately 149.2–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses tall and narrow neural arches on its dorsal vertebrae, with the neural spine height being approximately 4.4 times its minimum width, a trait shared with Mamenchisaurus.
As a mamenchisaurid sauropod, it was a massive, long-necked herbivore, though the specific fossil remains recovered only include fragmentary vertebrae, limb bones, and ulnae.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a giant, four-legged plant-eater from the Late Jurassic period of China, characterized by its extremely long neck and tail.

name: Jingshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
It is a prosauropod with a relatively small, narrow skull and long neck, representing one of the last surviving members of this group before the rise of giant sauropods.
This dinosaur was approximately 5 meters (16 feet) long and weighed around 4.3 tonnes, feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a small, early Jurassic herbivore from China that bridges the evolutionary gap between earlier bipedal plant-eaters and the massive, long-necked giants of the later Mesozoic.

name: Jintasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive postorbital skull structure that lacks both the jugal and quadratojugal bones, a specific anatomical trait noted in its holotype specimen.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore was a hadrosauriform dinosaur that lived in what is now northwestern China, contributing to the theory that hadrosaurs originated in Asia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as a key piece of evidence supporting the Asian origin hypothesis for the evolution of duck-billed dinosaurs and their close relatives.

name: Jinyunpelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Liangtoutang Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
A hexagonal tail club that is the widest point near its rear edge, marking it as the earliest known ankylosaur with a proper tail club.
A heavily armored, low-slung herbivore from the Cretaceous period of China, estimated to be several meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Jinyun shield', referring to the extensive bony armor plating covering its back.

name: Jinzhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A small crest on top of an uncommonly wide back of the skull, an elongated snout with large nares, and a lower jaw with at least seventeen tooth positions
Approximately 7 meters (23 feet) long, this Early Cretaceous herbivore possessed a skull about half a meter in length
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as a basal member of the Hadrosauroidea, representing an early evolutionary stage of the duck-billed dinosaurs

name: Joaquinraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A large, curved claw on the second finger of its hand, which is a defining characteristic of its family.
A massive carnivore measuring over 7 meters (23 feet) in length and weighing more than 1,000 kilograms (2,284 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the most complete skeletons known for its specific group of theropods, discovered in the Late Cretaceous of Argentina.

name: Jobaria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiourarén Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
A primitive sauropod with a simple backbone and tail, lacking the complex vertebrae and whiplash tail found in later relatives like Diplodocus.
A massive herbivore measuring approximately 18 meters (60 feet) in length and weighing around 22 tonnes, capable of rearing up on its hind legs.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
One of the most complete sauropod skeletons ever discovered, with over 95% of its bones preserved from a mass-death site in the Sahara desert.

name: Judiceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses postorbital horns with an unusual teardrop-shaped cross section and a frill featuring a very broad, platelike bar in the center.
It was a large, quadrupedal herbivore that lived approximately 78 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur with a prominent bony frill and horns, representing the oldest known member of the chasmosaurine group.

name: Juratyrant
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kimmeridge Clay Formation, approximately 143.1–145.1 million years ago.
An ischial apron with a 'folded' appearance and a fibular flange that continues as a distinct low ridge to the proximal end of the tibia
Approximately 5 meters (16 feet) in length and weighing between 300 and 650 kilograms, this was a carnivorous basal tyrannosauroid
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known relatives of the giant Tyrannosaurus rex, representing a small, agile predator from the Late Jurassic period of England

name: Juravenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Painten Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
A small, bipedal theropod with a notably short humerus and high-based hand claws that suddenly narrow transversely in the middle.
A juvenile specimen measuring approximately 75 centimeters in length, classified as a small predator.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
The discovery of extensive scales on its tail alongside faint impressions of primitive, filament-like feathers, challenging early assumptions about uniform feather coverage in its relatives.

name: Kaatedocus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 149.2–154.8 million years ago.
A skull with a distinctive 'hooked' prefrontal bone and a relatively small, beam-like cranial structure compared to its close relatives.
An estimated length of approximately 14 meters (46 feet) and a herbivorous diet consisting of high-growing vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Possessing an exceptionally long neck composed of at least 14 vertebrae, which studies suggest was held in a rather upright position.

name: Kaijiangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
Straight-edged jugal bone, megalosaurid-type teeth with few serrations near the tip, and strongly curved hand claws
Approximately five meters long and a carnivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A fragmentary Middle Jurassic theropod from China whose classification is debated and may be a synonym of Gasosaurus

name: Kaijutitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sierra Barrosa Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
Possesses bifid neural spines on its anterior cervical vertebrae and elongated deep pneumatic cavities within the neck bones.
A basal titanosaur from Argentina that reached a massive size, typical of giant long-necked sauropods, and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked dinosaur whose name translates to 'Kaiju titan', referencing the famous Japanese movie monsters.

name: Kamuysaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hakobuchi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a unique low-positioned notch on the quadrate bone where it meets the quadratojugal, a trait not found in any other known hadrosaurid.
This herbivore measured approximately eight meters in length and weighed between four and five tonnes, feeding on vegetation during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the most complete dinosaur skeletons ever discovered in Japan, preserving over 60% of its skeletal elements.

name: Kangnasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive lower jaw tooth and possible partial thigh bones
A small, bipedal herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the few dinosaur species discovered and named from South Africa

name: Kansaignathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yalovach Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A thin and deep dentary bone with parallel edges along its length, a down-turned anterior process, and two rows of vascular foramina on the outside edge.
A small-bodied carnivore approximately 2 meters long, estimated to be similar in size to Velociraptor.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the first and only dinosaur species to be described and named from the country of Tajikistan.

name: Kaririavis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Crato Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from the fossilized remains of a single foot.
This Early Cretaceous ornithuromorph dinosaur is estimated to be small in size and was likely an omnivore or insectivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the oldest known bird-like dinosaur discovered on the ancient supercontinent of Gondwana.

name: Karongasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Beds Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A U-shaped lower jaw with slender, conical, and cylindrical teeth
A titanosaur sauropod, likely reaching lengths of around 10 meters, that was a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in during the Early Cretaceous.
It is known primarily from fragmentary jaw and tooth fossils discovered in Malawi

name: Katepensaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive opening in the transverse processes of the dorsal vertebrae, which gives the animal its name meaning 'hole lizard'
A large, long-necked plant-eater from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of sauropod dinosaur known for having a complex system of air-filled cavities within its backbone bones

name: Kayentavenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kayenta Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
It possesses a pubic fenestra, a distinct opening in the pubis bone that separates it from contemporaneous relatives like Dilophosaurus.
It was a small carnivore, with the known juvenile specimen standing only about 0.5 meters tall at the hip.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as the oldest known tetanuran dinosaur discovered in North America.

name: Kazaklambia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dabrazhin Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive cranial structure featuring a thickened dome lateral to the main frontal dome and a nasal bone that domes above and in front of the eye socket.
This herbivorous lambeosaurine was a juvenile specimen, part of a group of duck-billed dinosaurs that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in Central Asia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a crested dinosaur, belonging to the subfamily known for having hollow, elaborate structures on top of their skulls, similar to the more famous Corythosaurus or Lambeosaurus.

name: Kelmayisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lianmuqin Formation, approximately 100.5–137.1 million years ago.
A distinctive deeply inset accessory groove on the lateral side of the dentary, which is the main toothbearing bone of the lower jaw.
An apex predator measuring approximately 10 to 12 meters in length that belonged to the carcharodontosaurian family.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a large, carnivorous theropod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of China, often associated with other giant meat-eating dinosaurs like Giganotosaurus and Carcharodontosaurus.

name: Kelumapusaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A duck-billed herbivore with a solid, blunt crest on its snout, characteristic of the Saurolophinae subfamily
An herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 9 meters in length
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the few duck-billed dinosaurs discovered in South America

name: Kentrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It features a double row of small plates along its neck and back that transition into prominent spikes on its hips and tail, including a long spike on each shoulder.
It was a relatively small quadrupedal herbivore, measuring approximately 4 to 4.5 meters in length and weighing between 700 and 1,600 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is an African stegosaurid known for its extensive array of defensive spikes, particularly the long thagomizer spikes on its tail used for protection.

name: Kerberosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Udurchukan Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Narrow frontals, a unique braincase structure, and a distinct separation between the bone surrounding the nostrils and the surrounding skull bone
Approximately 8 meters (26 feet) long, this large herbivore consumed plant matter using complex dental batteries
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur from the late Cretaceous period of Russia, closely related to Saurolophus

name: Khaan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A distinctive 'heel'-like expansion on the anterior chevrons in some individuals, which was identified as a sexually dimorphic trait likely related to egg-laying space in females or tail fan support in males.
An oviraptorid measuring approximately four feet in length that possessed a very strong bite force, suggesting a diet of tough vegetation, molluscs, and small vertebrates.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being part of a famous pair of fossilized skeletons discovered in Mongolia that were informally nicknamed 'Romeo and Juliet' due to their preserved position together.

name: Khankhuuluu
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A shallow skull with a strictly laterally directed bony horn on the lacrimal bone and smooth postorbital bones, lacking the angular suborbital flange seen in later relatives.
A medium-sized predator approximately 4 meters long and weighing around 750 kg, with long, slender legs adapted for running.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is an early ancestor in the lineage of the giant 'tyrant lizards,' representing a transitional form between primitive tyrannosauroids and the massive tyrannosaurids.

name: Kholumolumo
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.

A distinctive shinbone that is unusually short and robust, with a circumference measuring 53% of its length, which is significantly thicker relative to its length than in other non-sauropod sauropodomorphs.
A large body size estimated at up to 9 meters long, with a weight ranging from approximately 1,750 to 3,960 kilograms depending on whether it walked on two or four legs, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in during the Triassic.
It is a basal sauropodomorph dinosaur discovered in Lesotho, closely related to Sarahsaurus, and its name refers to a type of dragon or giant reptile from local Basuto mythology.

name: Khulsanurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

It possesses distinctive cervical vertebrae that lack pleurocoels, carotid processes, and epipophyses, along with caudal vertebrae featuring prominent infrapostzygapophyseal fossae and anteriorly pointing prezygapophyses.
This small alvarezsaurid theropod lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now the Gobi Desert of Mongolia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the alvarezsaurid family, a group of theropods known for their highly specialized, bird-like skeletons and reduced forelimbs.

name: Kileskus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Itat Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
A short ventral margin of the premaxilla and elongated external nares inclined posterodorsally at a 40-degree angle to the skull.
A medium-sized carnivorous dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic period of Russia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is an early relative of the famous T-Rex family, known for having a skull structure similar to Proceratosaurus.

name: Kinnareemimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sao Khua Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.

A semi-arctometatarsalian foot structure where the third metatarsal remains visible in proximal view and narrows distally before flaring into a triangular cross-section, along with ventrally flattened toe claws.
An estimated length of approximately 1.8 meters and a weight of around 13 kilograms, classifying it as a small, bipedal, likely omnivorous or herbivorous dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name is derived from the Kinnaree, a mythical creature from Thai folklore with the head of a woman and the legs of a bird, referencing its bird-like feet.

name: Kiyacursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ilek Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive foot structure where the third metatarsal is large and the second metatarsal is significantly reduced, a morphology similar to that of modern ostriches.
An estimated body length of 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) as a subadult, with an adult size likely larger, and a carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being identified as the first Early Cretaceous ceratosaur discovered in Asia.

name: Klamelisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
Distinctive deep foramina (openings) on the sides of the neck vertebrae and irregular plate-like extensions on the ridges extending forward from the neural spines.
A herbivore measuring approximately 13 meters (43 feet) long and weighing around 6 tonnes, with a neck that accounted for half of its total body length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its classification as a sauropod with an exceptionally long neck relative to its body size, characteristic of the Mamenchisauridae family.

name: Koleken
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Colonia Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

A member of the Abelisauridae family, this dinosaur possessed a skull and skeletal structure that distinguishes it from its larger relative Carnotaurus, particularly in the anatomy of its vertebrae and leg bones.
This sub-adult specimen measured approximately 4.97 meters (16.3 feet) in length and belonged to a group of meat-eating theropods.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a close relative of the famous horned dinosaur Carnotaurus, sharing the same distinctive short, horned skull shape characteristic of South American abelisaurids.

name: Koreaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tando beds Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.

It possessed caudal vertebrae with exceptionally tall neural spines, which were over five times the height of the vertebral centra, a feature some researchers have hypothesized may have been an adaptation for swimming.
This basal ceratopsian was a small herbivore, with the type specimen estimated to be approximately 1.3 meters (4.3 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of South Korea, known for having very limited fossil material consisting mainly of tail bones and hind limbs rather than a skull.

name: Koreanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Seonso Conglomerate Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a small, quadrupedal ornithischian dinosaur with a partial skeleton that notably lacks a skull.
It measured approximately 2 to 2.4 meters in length and is believed to have been a burrowing herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few known dinosaur species discovered in South Korea.

name: Koshisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kitadani Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
Possesses an antorbital fossa and three subsidiary ridges on its maxillary teeth
A basal hadrosauroid from the Early Cretaceous of Japan, representing a smaller, primitive relative of the large duck-billed dinosaurs
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
An early Asian ancestor of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, distinguished by its primitive skull features like the antorbital fossa

name: Kosmoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

It possessed fifteen well-developed horns and horn-like structures, including a blade-like nasal horn, upward-pointing brow horns, and ten hook-like epiossifications on the neck frill, making it the most ornate skull of any known dinosaur.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore that grew rapidly, reaching an estimated length of 4.5 meters (15 feet) and weighing approximately 1.2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for having the most elaborate and numerous facial horns and frill spikes of any dinosaur species, earning it the nickname 'ornate horned face'.

name: Kotasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kota Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive tail club, a feature shared with only a few other basal sauropods like Omeisaurus and Shunosaurus, likely used for defense or combat.
This Early Jurassic herbivore reached an estimated length of nine meters and weighed approximately 2.5 tonnes, feeding on vegetation with spoon-shaped teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the most primitive (basal) sauropods known, retaining some ancestral traits similar to prosauropods while being a fully quadrupedal giant from India.

name: Koutalisaurus
hints: Fossil remains were recovered from the Maastrichtian-aged Tremp Formation near Abella de la Conca, Lleida, Spain.
The holotype specimen is a dentary bone featuring a long, toothless front portion bent steeply downward and inward to create a distinctive spoon-like shape.
It was a medium-sized herbivorous hadrosaurid dinosaur, likely comparable in size to other lambeosaurines but with unusual jaw proportions.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period on the continent of Europe.
Its name literally translates to 'spoon lizard' due to the unique shape of its jawbone.

name: Kritosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland / Fruitland Formation, approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive arched nasal crest that gives the snout a Roman-nose profile, formed by the upward curve of the nasal bones.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 10 to 11 meters in length, weighing several tons, and feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classic duck-billed appearance with a broad, flat snout, which was historically confused with other similar dinosaurs due to fragmented fossil remains.

name: Kryptops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive upper jaw bone (maxilla) featuring deep striations, grooves, and rugosities that suggest it was covered in a tightly adhering keratinous integument rather than scales
An estimated length of 6 to 7 meters and a carnivorous diet, characterized by highly serrated teeth and a robust, short, deep skull
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the oldest known members of the Abelisauridae family, representing an early stage in the evolution of this group of theropods

name: Kulindadromeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ukureyskaya Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
It possesses three distinct types of filamentous integument, including hair-like structures on the trunk and unique ribbon-like bundles on the lower legs, alongside various scales.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore measuring 1 to 1.5 meters in length and weighing approximately 2 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for providing key evidence that feather-like structures were present in ornithischian dinosaurs, suggesting they may have been ancestral to all dinosaurs rather than just theropods.

name: Kunbarrasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Toolebuc Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.

A flat skull roof with a large, centrally placed nostril opening and a lack of horns or bosses on the cheeks, combined with body armor consisting of small keeled scutes in parallel rows and spike-like scutes on the hips.
A small quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 2.5 to 3.5 meters in length, with direct evidence of its diet found in preserved gut contents.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.

Being the most complete dinosaur skeleton ever discovered in Eastern Gondwana (Australia, Antarctica, Madagascar, and India) and the most complete ankylosaurian skeleton from the entire Gondwanan landmass.

name: Labocania
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Bocana Roja / Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
Robust cranial elements with strongly thickened frontals and flat, gradually recurving maxillary teeth
A medium-sized carnivore estimated to reach up to 8.2 meters in length and weighing approximately 2.6 long tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
A fragmentary theropod from the Late Cretaceous of Mexico whose classification has shifted between tyrannosaurids, abelisaurids, and carcharodontosaurids

name: Laevisuchus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

Distinctive cervical and dorsal vertebrae featuring blunt pre- and postzygapophyses, a central neural spine position in dorsal view, and a top-heavy appearance due to a neural arch significantly broader and longer than the centrum
A small-bodied bipedal carnivore estimated to be around 2 metres in length
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A poorly known Late Cretaceous theropod from India known only from a few lost and rediscovered vertebrae, often confused with or compared to Masiakasaurus

name: Laiyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jingangkou Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive, solid, rounded crest on the top of its skull, formed by the expansion of the nasal bones, which is a hallmark of the saurolophine subfamily.
This plant-eating dinosaur measured approximately 9 to 10 meters in length and weighed around 4 tons, utilizing a beak-like structure to crop vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.

It is visually recognizable as a large, bipedal or quadrupedal herbivore with a long, duck-like bill and a prominent, smooth, dome-shaped crest on its head, similar in general silhouette to the Edmontosaurus but with a more solid cranial ornamentation.

name: Lajasvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mulichinco Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It belongs to the Carcharodontosauridae family, a group of large theropods characterized by their serrated, blade-like teeth designed for slicing flesh.
It was a relatively small member of its family, measuring approximately 3.5 meters (11 feet) in length, and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the oldest known carcharodontosaurids from the Cretaceous period of South America, representing an early stage in the evolution of this lineage.

name: Lambeosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A highly distinct, hollow cranial crest that projects well above the eye and slightly over the snout, which adults of some species possess a backwards spur.
An adult length of around 7 to 7.7 meters (23-25 feet) weighing between 2.6 and 3.4 tons, feeding on low-lying vegetation in closed habitats.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
The prominent, axe-shaped or hatchet-like hollow crest on its head, which served as a resonating chamber for vocalizations.

name: Lametasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Possibly possessing dermal scutes or osteoderms, which are bony plates embedded in the skin, though the exact nature of these armor pieces is debated.
Estimated to weigh around 3,600 kg (7,900 lb), with a robust tibia suggesting a heavy, substantial build.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification is highly controversial and often considered a 'chimera' or nomen dubium, meaning the fossils may belong to multiple different animals rather than a single species.

name: Lamplughsaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dharmaram Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
Teeth with coarse denticles only on the mesial edges or completely absent, and tail vertebrae with neural spines that disappear towards the tip
A large, heavily built quadrupedal herbivore reaching up to 10 meters (33 feet) in length
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
One of the few known sauropodomorph dinosaurs discovered in India

name: Lanzhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
Possesses astonishingly huge teeth, among the largest for any herbivorous creature, with enamel that grew very rapidly.
An ornithopod dinosaur estimated to be about 10 meters (33 ft) in length and weighing 6 metric tons, feeding on plants.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A large, plant-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of China known for its massive jaw and enormous teeth.

name: Lapampasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a robust skeletal structure with distinct vertebrae and limb bones characteristic of a large, quadrupedal herbivore.
This dinosaur reached substantial lengths and weighed several tons, feeding on low-lying vegetation during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the duck-billed dinosaur family, recognizable by its broad, flattened snout adapted for cropping plants.

name: Laplatasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A large, elegantly built sauropod with limb elements and vertebrae, potentially associated with osteoderms forming an armored plating on its back that featured smaller ridges than those of Saltasaurus.
Approximately 18 meters (59 feet) long, this titanosaurian sauropod was a herbivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in South America.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the giant, long-necked titanosaur dinosaurs from Argentina, often compared to Saltasaurus due to its similar size and armored appearance.

name: Lapparentosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Isalo III Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
Pyramid-shaped neural spines on anterior dorsal vertebrae that taper rather than flare distally, along with the loss of the spinodiapophyseal lamina
A large, fast-growing herbivore that took 31 to 45 years to reach sexual maturity, with fossils indicating it was a basal eusauropod from the Middle Jurassic
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
One of the few dinosaurs known exclusively from Madagascar, with all known specimens consisting of vertebrae and limb bones but no skull material

name: Laquintasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Quinta Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
Distinctive triangular-shaped teeth with sharp denticles and striations, differing from the leaf-shaped teeth of related ornithischians.
A small, lightly built animal approximately 1 meter long that was a capable bipedal runner with a bird-like hip bone.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is the first dinosaur ever identified from Venezuela and is known for being one of the most primitive ornithischians in the fossil record.

name: Latenivenatrix
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A retroverted pubis forming a 17-degree angle with an anteriorly curved shaft and a large muscle scar on its lateral surface
A large troodontid measuring 3 to 3.5 meters in length that was likely a semi-omnivorous biped
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being recognized as the largest known member of the troodontid family

name: Latirhinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive, broad nasal bone structure that gives the creature its 'broad nose' identity
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 8.7 meters in length and weighing around 3.75 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the first dinosaur skeleton from Mexico to be mounted for public display

name: Lavocatisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Rayoso Formation, approximately 110.1–121.4 million years ago.
An elongated skull shape that closely resembles that of Diplodocus, along with evidence suggesting a beak-like keratinous sheath covered the front of its snout
A medium-sized sauropod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Argentina, likely feeding on low-lying vegetation
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A lesser-known relative of the more famous long-necked dinosaurs, belonging to the Rebbachisauridae family

name: Leaellynasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Eumeralla Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
Possibly enlarged eyes and proportionally large optic lobes, suggesting an adaptation to the extended periods of darkness experienced in its polar habitat.
A small herbivore measuring approximately 1.8 meters in length that fed on low-growing vegetation such as ferns, cycads, and horsetails.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It lived in what is now Australia during the Early Cretaceous, when the region was located within the Antarctic Circle and subject to months of continuous darkness.

name: Ledumahadi
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possessed massive, robust forelimbs that were naturally flexed rather than straight and columnar like those of later giant sauropods.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore that reached an estimated weight of 12 tonnes, making it one of the largest land animals of the Early Jurassic.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is known by a name meaning 'giant thunderclap' in the Sesotho language.

name: Leinkupal
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajada Colorada Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.

Its front tail vertebrae feature extremely long transverse processes that are as wide as the vertebral body itself, along with robust reinforcing ridges and paired pneumatic openings on the rear joint processes.
It was a relatively small diplodocid sauropod, measuring approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is the youngest known diplodocid dinosaur globally and the only unequivocal member of its family found in South America.

name: Leonerasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Las Leoneras Formation, approximately 184.2–192.9 million years ago.

It possesses procumbent (forward-angled) teeth that are spoon-shaped in the front of the jaw but feature serrated edges toward the back, a transitional trait between early ancestors and later giant herbivores.
This animal was a small, lightweight dinosaur measuring approximately 2.4 meters in length and weighing around 70 kilograms, classifying it as a basal sauropodomorph from the Early Jurassic.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a primitive relative of the massive long-necked giants, representing an early stage in the evolutionary lineage that would eventually produce the largest land animals to ever walk the Earth.

name: Leptoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Scollard / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a very reduced frill and completely lacks horns, distinguishing it from other horned dinosaurs.
It was a small herbivore, measuring approximately 2 meters in length, with a unique mammal-like jaw rotation for chewing.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a primitive, hornless relative of the famous Triceratops, often recognized for its small size and lack of facial horns.

name: Leshansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
Possession of a sharp central ridge on the supraoccipital bone, elongated frontal bones that are nearly three times as long as they are wide, and a horseshoe-shaped atlas intercentrum.
A medium-sized theropod measuring six to seven meters in length with a hip height of approximately one and a half meters, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A Late Jurassic predator from China whose classification has been debated between the families Sinraptoridae and Megalosauridae, often studied alongside the closely related Piveteausaurus.

name: Lesothosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possessed a small, narrow, and pointed skull with large eye sockets and a horny beaklike structure at the tip of its jaws.
It was a lightly built, bipedal animal measuring between 1 and 2 meters in length, likely an opportunistic omnivore that sliced food with its beak rather than chewing.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.

It is recognized as one of the most completely known early ornithischian dinosaurs, with numerous well-preserved skull and postcranial fossils discovered in the Upper Elliot Formation of Southern Africa.

name: Lessemsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Colorados Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A massive, pillar-like limb structure with robust, thick-walled bones adapted for supporting extreme weight, characteristic of early giant herbivores.
An estimated length of 10 to 12 meters and a weight exceeding 7 metric tons, feeding on high vegetation as a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
Being one of the earliest known dinosaurs to evolve a gigantic body size, representing a key evolutionary step toward the colossal sauropods of the Jurassic and Cretaceous periods.

name: Levnesovia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
It possesses a broad, flattened snout and a dental battery with hundreds of tightly packed teeth, characteristic of a specialized plant-eating beaked dinosaur.
This hadrosauroid was a medium-sized herbivore, estimated to reach lengths of around 4 to 5 meters and weighing approximately 1 to 2 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few known dinosaurs from the Uzbek desert, representing a rare glimpse into the diverse fauna of the Late Cretaceous Central Asian ecosystems.

name: Leyesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 174.7–201.4 million years ago.
A nearly complete skull measuring 18 centimeters in length, accompanied by an articulated mandible and postcranial remains including the vertebral column, scapular and pelvic girdles, and hindlimbs.
An estimated body length of 2.5 meters, belonging to a massospondylid sauropodomorph from the Lower Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
A small, early dinosaur relative of Massospondylus, known primarily from a remarkably preserved skull and jaw structure found in Argentina.

name: Liaoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.

A small skull featuring only jugal horns located under and behind the eyes, along with a small skull frill, but completely lacking the large orbital horns and true neck shield seen in later relatives.
A diminutive body size reaching approximately 50 cm (1.6 ft) in length and weighing around 2 kg (4.4 lb), classified as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.

It is recognized as a very small, early ancestor in the lineage of famous horned dinosaurs, offering a glimpse into the primitive origins of the group that would eventually produce giants like Triceratops.

name: Liaoningosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a tail club, a feature that makes it the oldest known ankylosaurid to have this defensive structure.
It was a very small dinosaur, with the largest known specimens measuring less than 45 centimeters in total length, and it is one of the few armored dinosaurs speculated to have eaten fish.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a juvenile armored dinosaur from China that is famous for its paradoxical combination of heavy armor and a carnivorous or omnivorous diet.

name: Liaoningotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses imbricated upper teeth with narrow, D-shaped spatulate crowns that lack labial grooves or denticles, alongside a maxilla with a convex ventral margin.
It was a medium-sized sauropod reaching approximately 10 metres in length and 2 metres in shoulder height, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of China, known for its massive size and distinctive tooth structure.

name: Liaoningvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A slender postorbital bone that radiates into three distinct processes, a feature unique among troodontids.
A small theropod measuring approximately 69 cm in length, equipped with numerous small, closely packed, and serrated teeth for a carnivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its classification as a troodontid, a group of small, bird-like dinosaurs known for their large brains and keen senses.

name: Ligabueino
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Amarga Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It is an abelisauroid theropod, a group of carnivorous dinosaurs characterized by reduced or absent teeth, short snouts, and often cranial ornamentation such as horns or crests.
This specific specimen is extremely fragmentary and small, measuring only 79 cm (2.6 ft) in length, and was identified as a juvenile due to unfused vertebrae.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the many theropods discovered in the La Amarga Formation of Patagonia, Argentina, during the Early Cretaceous period.

name: Ligabuesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lohan Cura Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
It possesses anteroposteriorly narrow neural spines and unique laminae on its posterior cervical vertebrae.
It was a massive plant-eater estimated to reach lengths of around 18 meters and weigh between 14.5 and 29 tons.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked quadrupedal herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period of South America.

name: Liliensternus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Trossingen / Klettgau Formation, approximately 201.4–227.3 million years ago.
It possessed an unusually short ilium (hip bone) and a skull crest that resembles the double-crested appearance of Dilophosaurus.
This bipedal carnivore measured between 5 and 9 meters in length and weighed approximately 127 to 200 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is recognized as the best-represented and one of the largest theropod dinosaurs discovered from the Triassic period in Europe.

name: Limaysaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros / Alcântara Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses unusually tall, straight, I-shaped neural spines on its back vertebrae rather than the typical V-shaped ones found in many other sauropods.
This medium-sized sauropod measured approximately fifteen meters in length and weighed around seven tonnes, feeding on vegetation with the aid of stomach stones called gastroliths.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a Cretaceous-era plant-eating dinosaur from South America that shares a close evolutionary link with African species, supporting the theory of a past land bridge between the continents.

name: Limusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a beak in adulthood and exhibits drastic digit reduction in its forelimbs, specifically with reduced first and fourth fingers.
It was a small, slender theropod measuring approximately 1.7 meters in length and weighing around 15 kilograms, shifting from an omnivorous diet as a juvenile to herbivory as an adult.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for undergoing a complete loss of teeth as it matured, transitioning from a toothed juvenile to a toothless adult with a beak.

name: Lingwulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 161.5–174.7 million years ago.

It possesses a distinctive U-shaped snout, contrasting with the square-shaped snouts typical of most other diplodocoids, and features highly elaborated ornamentation along the upper margin of its orbital area.

This Middle Jurassic neosauropod was a large, herbivorous quadruped, with fossil evidence suggesting individuals ranged from small juveniles to adults, representing one of the earliest known members of its lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is scientifically significant as the first definitive diplodocoid discovered in East Asia, challenging previous hypotheses that this group was absent from the region during the Jurassic period.

name: Lingyuanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive ilium with a strongly convex upper edge and a concave lower edge, along with a lamina on the dorsal vertebrae separating the infradiapophyseal fossae.
This theropod was relatively small, measuring approximately 2.2 meters in length and weighing around 12 kilograms, and is believed to have been herbivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.

It is a member of the therizinosaur family, a group of dinosaurs famous for evolving massive, sickle-shaped claws on their hands, though this specific species had smaller, more functional claws with a proximodorsal lip.

name: Linhenykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wulansuhai Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is the only known non-avian dinosaur to possess exactly one functional digit on each forelimb, a unique trait among dinosaurs.
This tiny creature measured approximately 50 centimeters in length and weighed around 500 grams, and it likely fed on insects like ants and termites.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'single claw', highlighting its most distinctive anatomical feature of having just one finger on its hand.

name: Linheraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wulansuhai Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed the characteristic enlarged, sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each foot, a defining trait of its family.
This bird-like predator measured approximately 1.8 meters (5.9 feet) in length and weighed around 25 kilograms (55 pounds), feeding on small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the dromaeosaurid family, closely related to the famous Velociraptor, sharing similar agile, predatory features.

name: Linhevenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wulansuhai Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive lateral flange on the jugal bone and a surangular crest that angles forward and downward.
A small, bird-like theropod weighing about 23 kg and measuring up to 2.7 meters long, likely an omnivore or insectivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the Troodontidae family, known for having large brains relative to body size and keen senses among non-avian dinosaurs.

name: Lirainosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sierra Perenchiza / Vitoria Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Presence of a lamina in the interzygapophyseal fossa of the most proximal caudal vertebrae and a spinopostzygapophyseal structure that is not posteriorly projected in the posterior caudal vertebrae
A relatively small titanosaur sauropod measuring approximately 4 to 6 meters in length and weighing between 2 and 4 metric tons, classified as a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
A Late Cretaceous armored titanosaur from Spain that is closely related to Saltasaurus and shares characteristics with other small, heavily built sauropods

name: Lishulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 174.7–199.5 million years ago.
A skull measuring 40 centimeters long, which is larger than any other sauropodomorph from the Lufeng Formation, accompanied by very large and elongated cervical vertebrae.
A small Early Jurassic sauropodiform dinosaur from China, likely herbivorous, with a body length estimated to be significantly smaller than its relative Yunnanosaurus.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known members of the lineage that would eventually evolve into the giant long-necked sauropods, representing a primitive stage of sauropodomorph evolution.

name: Liubangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xinlong Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
Its dorsal vertebrae exhibit a distinctive 'Y-shape' when viewed along their transverse plane, featuring specific modifications to the parapophyses, diapophyses, and neural spines.
It was a massive quadrupedal herbivore estimated to reach lengths of 26 meters and weigh approximately 26 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of China, known primarily from a small set of articulated tail vertebrae.

name: Llukalkan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, air-filled sinus in the middle ear zone called the recessus tympanicus caudalis, which is an autapomorphy not found in other abelisaurids.
It was a small theropod measuring approximately 3.95 meters (13.0 feet) in length and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Abelisauridae family, a group of theropods known for having tiny, vestigial forelimbs and robust skulls, often compared to the more famous Carnotaurus.

name: Lohuecotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villalba de la Sierra Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
Its braincase features a unique occipital condyle that is significantly wider than it is tall, and the neural spines of the tail vertebrae appear V-shaped from the side.
It was a massive titanosaurian sauropod that lived in what is now Spain during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur with a long neck and tail, belonging to the group of sauropods known for their enormous size.

name: Lokiceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It lacks a nasal horn and features uniquely large, bladelike epiparietals on its frill that curve laterally, resembling the curved blades associated with the Norse god Loki.
It was a massive plant-eater measuring approximately 6.7 meters (22 feet) in length and weighing around 5 tonnes, making it one of the largest known members of its subfamily.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous whose frill ornamentation is bilaterally asymmetrical, similar to the antlers of a caribou or reindeer.

name: Lophorhothon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mooreville Chalk Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive bony crest located on the nasal bones of the skull, which gives the animal its name meaning 'crested nose'
An estimated adult length comparable to other large hadrosaurs, likely reaching lengths similar to Hadrosaurus or Gryposaurus, and classified as a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being recognized as the very first dinosaur genus ever discovered in the state of Alabama

name: Lophostropheus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moon-Airel Formation, approximately 199.5–205.7 million years ago.
It possesses prominent dorsal and ventral laminae (crests) on its cranial cervical vertebrae, which is the feature its name references.
It was a small to medium-sized, bipedal carnivore that grew up to approximately 3 to 5.2 meters long and weighed around 100 to 136 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is one of the very few dinosaur genera known to have survived the mass extinction event that marked the boundary between the Triassic and Jurassic periods.

name: Loricatosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marnes à Belemnopsis latesulcatus / Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
It possesses a row of bony plates and spikes along its back and tail, though it notably lacks the large shoulder spines found in some close relatives.
This armored herbivore measured approximately 4 to 5 meters in length and weighed around 1 to 2 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a classic example of a 'roofed lizard' with distinctive flat, diamond-shaped bony plates running down its spine, similar to the more famous Stegosaurus.

name: Losillasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villar del Arzobispo / Praia da Amoreira-Porto Novo Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
Distinctive neural spines on the proximal tail vertebrae and heart-shaped teeth demonstrating heterodonty
A subadult specimen with a 143 cm humerus suggests an adult length of 15–18 meters and a weight of 12–15 tons, feeding on plants
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
A massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period of Spain

name: Lourinhanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
It is one of the few theropod dinosaurs for which gastroliths (stomach stones) have been definitively assigned to the animal itself.
This sub-adult specimen measured approximately 4.5 meters (15 feet) in length and weighed around 160 kilograms (350 pounds), and it was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Fossils of this dinosaur were discovered in Portugal alongside well-preserved dinosaur eggs and embryos, some containing embryonic bones and skin.

name: Lourinhasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã / Alcobaça Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
It has very tall sacral neural spines and a ventral concavity in its anterior-to-middle dorsal vertebrae.
It was a large herbivorous sauropod measuring approximately 18 meters in length and weighing around 5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a close relative of Camarasaurus, sharing similar skeletal features like opisthocoelic dorsal vertebrae and a broadly expanded distal scapular blade.

name: Luanchuanraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Qiupa Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A very stiff tail tip indicated by short neural spines on the caudal vertebrae, connected anterior chevrons, and a large opening on the coracoid
A moderate-sized carnivore measuring approximately 1.1 to 1.8 meters in length and weighing between 2.2 to 2.5 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the first Asian dromaeosaurid dinosaurs described from outside the Gobi Desert or northeastern China

name: Lucianovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quebrada del Barro Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.

A unique combination of deep pits (fossae) on its neck vertebrae, including a distinctive long and deep pit along the edge of the bone connecting the rib to the spine, and anterior cervical ribs that are more than five times as long as the vertebrae they attach to.
A small, lightweight carnivore from the Late Triassic period, likely measuring around one to two meters in length, belonging to the Coelophysidae family of early theropods.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.

It is one of the very few known species of advanced meat-eating dinosaurs (neotheropods) discovered in South America, representing a rare glimpse into the continent's prehistoric fauna during the time of Pangea.

name: Lufengosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng / Ziliujing Formation, approximately 168.2–201.4 million years ago.
Distinctive bony bumps located just behind its large nostrils and on its cheeks, along with closely spaced, serrated teeth suited for shearing leaves

An early sauropodomorph measuring approximately 6 to 9 meters in length and weighing up to 2.3 tonnes, which was primarily herbivorous but possessed sharp claws suggesting possible omnivorous tendencies
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It was the first dinosaur skeleton to be fully mounted and displayed in China, an event celebrated by the issuance of a commemorative postage stamp in 1958

name: Luoyanggia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Haoling Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It belongs to the oviraptorid family, a group of theropods characterized by their bird-like skulls and often toothless beaks.
It was a small dinosaur, measuring approximately 1.2 to 1.5 meters in length and weighing between 8 and 9 kilograms, and it was an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a relatively obscure dinosaur known primarily from fragmentary remains found in the Early Cretaceous deposits of central China.

name: Lurdusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
A massive, spike-like thumb on its forelimb combined with a rotund torso and short, stout legs that gave it a quadrupedal stance with a belly barely off the ground.
An estimated length of 7 to 9 meters and a weight of up to 5.5 tons, making it unusually heavy for its size, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
Its body shape is often compared to that of a modern ground sloth or hippopotamus due to its bulky, low-slung build.

name: Lusotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lourinhã Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
A large sauropod with notably long forearms, where the humerus and femur both measure approximately 2 meters in length, and a large sternal plate that may indicate even greater size.
A massive herbivore reaching 21 meters (69 feet) in length and weighing between 25 and 30 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
One of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered in Portugal, living alongside predators like Allosaurus and Torvosaurus in the Late Jurassic coastal region.

name: Lusovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Freixial / Praia da Amoreira-Porto Novo Formation, approximately 143.1–152.2 million years ago.
A large carcharodontosaurian theropod with a partial postcranial skeleton including cervical and dorsal vertebrae, ribs, and pelvic elements, classified within the Carcharodontosauria clade.
A juvenile individual estimated to be a large carnivore, representing a significant predator from the Late Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
A massive, blade-toothed apex predator from Portugal, closely related to other giant meat-eaters like Giganotosaurus and Carcharodontosaurus.

name: Lycorhinus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot / Clarens Formation, approximately 184.2–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses long, canine-like teeth in its jaws, a distinctive feature for a herbivore.
It was a small dinosaur, measuring approximately 1.2 meters in length, and primarily ate plants.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known ornithischian dinosaurs, originally misidentified as a mammal-like reptile due to its unique dental structure.

name: Lythronax
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wahweap Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A very broad, robust skull with forward-facing eye sockets that provided binocular vision, and a maxilla featuring only 11 tooth sockets with an S-shaped outer margin.
A heavily built predator measuring between 5 and 8 meters in length and weighing up to 2.5 tons, equipped with small two-fingered forelimbs and powerful hindlimbs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the oldest known member of the tyrannosaurid family, serving as an evolutionary 'great-uncle' to the more famous Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Machairasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bayan Mandahu Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Its hand claws are extremely elongated and blade-like, measuring four times the height of the joint they are attached to.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore that grew to approximately 1.5 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is an oviraptorid dinosaur known for having a beak and being found in a brooding position near a nest of eggs.

name: Machairoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wahweap Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive pair of long, curved, sword-like spikes projecting forward from the top of the neck frill, with a complete absence of smaller bony bumps along the frill's edges.
A quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length, weighing around 1 to 2 tons, and feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'bent sword horned face,' referring to the unique sickle-shaped horns on its head that resemble the weapon used by the Greek god Cronus.

name: Macrocollum
hints: Fossils recovered from the Caturrita Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses an elongated neck and was one of the earliest dinosaurs to exhibit an air sac system within its skeleton.
It was a relatively small, bipedal herbivore that lived during the Late Triassic period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is recognized as one of the oldest known dinosaurs, dating back to approximately 225 million years ago.

name: Macrogryphosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sierra Barrosa Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses a series of mineralized thoracic plates along the sides of its torso, which contributed to a stiff, birdlike torso structure.
It was a large herbivore, estimated to reach lengths of up to 6 meters and weigh around 300 kilograms, making it the largest elasmarian dinosaur known.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the largest ornithopods from South America, distinguished by its graceful, lightly built frame and elongate neck compared to other iguanodontians.

name: Magnamanus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Golmayo Formation, approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
A broad hand featuring a prominent, protruding thumb spine and a fifth finger, with a wrist composed of fused radial and ulnar carpal elements.
A large herbivore measuring 9 to 10 meters in length and weighing approximately 3 metric tons, belonging to the iguanodontian group.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its genus name directly references its notably large hands, derived from the Latin words for 'large' and 'hand'.

name: Magnapaulia
hints: Fossils recovered from the El Gallo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

It possessed exceptionally elongated chevrons at the base of its tail, measuring four times the length of the vertebral centra, along with a tall, continuous crest of neural spines running along its back and tail.
This herbivorous lambeosaurine was one of the largest of its subfamily, reaching lengths of up to 16.5 meters and weighing nearly 10 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is visually defined by its extremely tall, narrow tail profile, which was originally misinterpreted by early researchers as an adaptation for swimming.

name: Magnosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Middle Inferior Oolite Formation, approximately 168.6–170.9 million years ago.
It possesses a dentary with an expanded front tip and an enlarged third tooth, distinguishing it from similar contemporaries like Eustreptospondylus.
This Middle Jurassic theropod was a bipedal carnivore, estimated to reach lengths of roughly 4 meters and weigh up to half a tonne.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically significant as one of the earliest recognized megalosaurids, often confused with or included within the genus Megalosaurus due to fragmentary remains.

name: Magyarosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sebeş / Densuş-Ciula Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Possesses dermal armor (osteoderms) on its skin, a rare trait among sauropods.
It is one of the smallest known adult sauropods, measuring less than 3 meters (9.8 ft) in length and weighing under 1,000 kg.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for being a 'dwarf dinosaur' that evolved on an island to survive limited food resources and a lack of predators.

name: Mahuidacursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It is a basal ornithopod, representing an early branch in the evolutionary tree of bird-hipped dinosaurs that walked on two legs.
This dinosaur was relatively small, measuring approximately 1.5 meters in length, and was a herbivore that fed on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'mountain runner,' evoking the image of a swift, agile creature navigating the rugged terrain of ancient Patagonia.

name: Maiasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A prominent, short, solid crest-like structure situated between the eyes, which may have been used in headbutting contests.
A large herbivore reaching approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing up to 4 metric tons, with a distinctive duck-billed mouth and hundreds of teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
