Being the first dinosaur discovered with clear evidence of parental care, including nesting colonies where adults fed their altricial young.

name: Maip
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chorrillo Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a notably large thoracic cavity, a feature highlighted in its specific name which translates to 'large chest'.
This large theropod measured approximately 9 to 10 meters in length and was a carnivorous apex predator.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive South American predator from the very end of the Cretaceous period, belonging to the megaraptorid family.

name: Majungasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maevarano Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A single rounded horn on the roof of its skull and a very rough, thickened texture on the top of its snout
A medium-sized bipedal predator measuring around 7 meters long and weighing over 1 ton that primarily ate sauropods
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs with direct fossil evidence of cannibalism

name: Malarguesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Bastos Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
A robust titanosauriform sauropod with distinctive tail vertebrae, chevrons, ribs, and limb bones
A large quadrupedal herbivore, representing one of the biggest land animals to ever live
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A massive, long-necked dinosaur with a small head and pillar-like legs

name: Malawisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Beds Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed dermal scutes, or bony armor plates, embedded in its skin, a feature shared with some other titanosaurs.
This titanosaur was relatively small for a sauropod, measuring approximately 15 meters in length and weighing around 10 tonnes, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known exclusively from the African continent, specifically discovered in the Dinosaur Beds of Malawi.

name: Malefica
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aguja Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A partial left maxilla (upper jaw bone) containing unique diagnostic traits that distinguish it from other hadrosaurids.
A hadrosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Texas, likely a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name means 'witch' or 'sorceress', referencing the Spanish word for witch found in the canyon where its fossil was discovered.

name: Maleriraptor
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Upper Maleri Formation in the Pranhita–Godavari Basin of India, dating to the Late Triassic Norian stage approximately 210 million years ago.
It is classified as a herrerasaurian, an early saurischian dinosaur characterized by an open acetabulum and a straight pubis shaft, distinguishing it from later theropods and sauropodomorphs.
This bipedal carnivore was a relatively small predator, with the known partial skeleton indicating a body length of roughly one to two meters.
It lived during the Late Triassic period on the landmass that is now the Indian subcontinent, sharing its ecosystem with early relatives like Alwalkeria.
As one of the earliest known saurischians, it represents a primitive stage in dinosaur evolution before the split into the lineages that would become birds and sauropods.

name: Mamenchisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao / Ulan Malgait Formation, approximately 113.2–168.2 million years ago.
Its neck vertebrae are exceptionally elongated, accounting for nearly half of the animal's total body length.
It was a massive herbivorous sauropod that typically measured between 15 and 26 meters long, with some estimates reaching up to 35 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is widely recognized for having one of the longest necks relative to body size of any known dinosaur.

name: Mandschurosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Grès supérieurs / Yuliangzi Formation, approximately 66.0–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed a broad, flat beak and a long tail, characteristic features of its family.
This large herbivore could reach lengths of up to 11.24 meters and weigh around 1.5 to 2 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first dinosaur genus ever named from China.

name: Manidens
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 170.9–184.2 million years ago.
Its posterior lower teeth are uniquely shaped like a human hand, which inspired its name.
This small dinosaur measured approximately 60 centimeters (2 feet) in length and weighed around 500 grams, and it was a herbivore with high-crowned teeth adapted for plant-eating.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a member of the Heterodontosauridae family, a group of early ornithischian dinosaurs known for possessing different types of teeth in their jaws, including canine-like fangs.

name: Manipulonyx
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Khermeen Tsav locality of the Nemegt Formation in Mongolia, dating to the Maastrichtian age of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 72 to 66 million years ago.
It possessed a functionally monodactyl hand with a single greatly enlarged, curved claw on the second digit, accompanied by unique ossified spikes on the wrist.
This small theropod likely measured around one meter in length and is interpreted as an ovivore that used its robust forelimbs to grasp and puncture dinosaur eggs.
It lived in Asia during the final period of the Mesozoic Era, sharing its habitat with giants like Tarbosaurus and Nemegtosaurus.
It is best known for its bizarre, one-fingered hand with a massive claw, which was adapted for breaking open eggs rather than hunting live prey.

name: Mansourasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quseir Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Each lower jaw dentary bears exactly ten teeth and features a prominent chin-like structure where the two halves meet.
This juvenile specimen was approximately 8 to 10 meters long and weighed around 5,000 kilograms, similar to a bull African elephant, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period, notable for being one of the few sauropod fossils discovered in Africa.

name: Mantellisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex / Arcillas de Morella Formation, approximately 119.6–143.1 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive, thickened spike on its thumb, which was historically mistaken for a nose horn in early reconstructions.
It was a lightly built, herbivorous ornithopod that could walk on two or four legs, with a body length estimated at around 6 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is closely associated with Gideon Mantell, the scientist who first discovered and described Iguanodon, and was originally classified as a species of Iguanodon.

name: Mapusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed very small, vestigial arms that were proportionally similar in size to those found in tyrannosaurids.
It was a giant carnivore measuring between 10 and 12.6 meters long and weighing up to 6 tons.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for being one of the primary candidates, alongside Giganotosaurus, for the title of the largest known land carnivore.

name: Maraapunisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
It possesses the tallest and largest neural spine of any known animal, measuring up to 2.7 meters high.
It is a massive sauropod estimated to reach lengths of 35 to 40 meters and weigh between 70 and 120 tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for being potentially the largest dinosaur ever discovered, despite its only known fossil bone having mysteriously disappeared.

name: Marshosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A unique upward-curving (convex) suture between the pubic peduncle and the pubic bone
A medium-sized carnivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters (15 ft) in length and weighing around 200 kilograms (440 lb)
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
A Late Jurassic predator from the Morrison Formation that lived alongside giants like Allosaurus and Stegosaurus

name: Martharaptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
Its hand claws are uniquely deep and taller than they are long, with the second and third fingers bearing claws of equal size, distinguishing it from other therizinosaurs.

It was a medium-sized theropod from the Early Cretaceous of Utah, likely herbivorous or omnivorous, with a skeletal structure that represents an evolutionary intermediate between early and advanced therizinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the therizinosaur family, a group of theropods famous for their bizarre combination of bird-like features and massive, sickle-shaped claws.

name: Masiakasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maevarano Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Its front teeth project forward instead of pointing straight down, and they are long, spoon-shaped, and hooked.
It was a small bipedal predator measuring about 1.8 to 2.1 meters long and weighing around 20 kilograms, likely eating fish and small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a theropod from Madagascar with a unique jaw structure where the front teeth stick out horizontally like a shovel.

name: Massospondylus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot / Mpandi Formation, approximately 174.7–237.0 million years ago.
It possessed a very sharp, sickle-shaped thumb claw on each hand, likely used for uprooting vegetation or pulling down branches.
It was a relatively small dinosaur, measuring 4 to 6 meters in length, and was primarily a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest dinosaurs to be scientifically named and is famous for having some of the oldest known dinosaur eggs and embryos in the fossil record.

name: Matheronodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Its upper jaw teeth are fused into pairs within shared sockets, emerging alternately to function like a pair of scissors.
It was a bipedal herbivore approximately 5 meters long that specialized in slicing tough monocot plant leaves.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal ornithopod relative of Rhabdodon and Zalmoxes, known for its unique scissor-like dental mechanics rather than complex chewing batteries.

name: Maxakalisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Adamantina Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed ridged teeth, a feature that is unusual among sauropod dinosaurs.
It was a medium-sized titanosaur measuring approximately 12.1 meters (40 feet) in length and weighing around 5 tonnes, which was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Brazil that had bony armor plates (osteoderms) embedded in its skin.

name: Mbiresaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pebbly Arkose Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.

It is a basal sauropodomorph, meaning it possesses the early anatomical precursors to the long necks and massive bodies of later giant plant-eating dinosaurs, including a skull with leaf-shaped teeth adapted for browsing.

This early dinosaur lived during the Late Triassic period in what is now Zimbabwe and was likely a bipedal herbivore of moderate size, representing one of the earliest definitive dinosaurs known from the African continent.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.
It is recognized as one of the oldest and most complete dinosaur skeletons ever discovered in Africa, providing crucial evidence for the early evolution and spread of dinosaurs on that continent.

name: Medusaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive frill featuring large, thick, snake-like spikes that curve down the sides, a trait that inspired its name referencing the mythological figure Medusa.
It was a plant-eating herbivore that reached an estimated length of roughly 6 meters (20 feet).
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a horned dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period, known for the unique, Medusa-like ornamentation on its neck frill.

name: Megalosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Forest Marble / Cotswold Slate Formation, approximately 66.0–205.7 million years ago.
It possessed a large head equipped with long, curved, serrated teeth designed for slicing flesh.
It was a robust, bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing around 700 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It holds the historical distinction of being the very first dinosaur genus to be formally named and described in scientific literature.

name: Megapnosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Forest Sandstone / Nyamandhlovu Sandstones Formation, approximately 192.9–237.0 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive fused tarsal bone in its foot, which was the basis for its original genus name Syntarsus.
It was a small, lightweight carnivore measuring up to 2.2 meters long and weighing around 13 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa, Asia, North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is historically significant as one of the first dinosaurs to be portrayed with feathers in popular media, despite a lack of direct fossil evidence.

name: Megaraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed exceptionally large, sickle-shaped claws on its hands, with the claw on the first finger measuring up to 35 centimeters in length.
It was a large carnivore measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and weighing around 1 ton.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for having massive hand claws that were initially mistaken for foot claws, leading to early depictions as a giant raptor.

name: Melanorosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–227.3 million years ago.
It possessed massive, heavy limb bones that were structurally similar to those of true sauropods, indicating a sturdy build and quadrupedal movement.
This dinosaur reached an approximate length of 8 meters and weighed around 1.3 metric tons, and it was an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.

It is recognized as a large, early ancestor in the evolutionary lineage that led to the giant long-necked dinosaurs, often described as a transitional form between smaller bipedal herbivores and massive quadrupedal giants.

name: Mendozasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sierra Barrosa Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
It possessed a long neck characterized by exceptionally wide cervical neural spines, a distinctive anatomical trait shared with other members of the Lognkosauria clade.
This massive titanosaurian sauropod measured approximately 20 to 22 meters in length and weighed around 16 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaurs from the Cretaceous period, often recognized by its enormous size and quadrupedal stance typical of titanosaurs.

name: Menefeeceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Menefee Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a member of the horned dinosaur family Ceratopsidae, specifically belonging to the centrosaurine subfamily, which is characterized by short frills and prominent nasal horns.
This dinosaur was relatively small for its group, measuring approximately 4 to 4.5 meters in length, and was a herbivore that fed on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the oldest known ceratopsid and the only centrosaurine described from the state of New Mexico, dating back to the early Campanian stage.

name: Menucocelsior
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by a massive, quadrupedal body plan with a long neck and tail, though specific distinctive bony armor or crests are not highlighted in its primary description.
This medium-sized dinosaur reached an approximate length of 8 meters (26 feet) and was a herbivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a lesser-known sauropod from Argentina, distinguished by its classification as a 'taller one' relative to other titanosaurs found in the same fossil bed, lacking the iconic spikes or plates of more famous armored dinosaurs.

name: Meraxes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.

A massive skull measuring approximately 1.27 meters long, paired with significantly reduced forelimbs that feature a second toe with an enlarged, sickle-shaped claw nearly twice the length of the fourth toe's claw.
A giant carnivore weighing around 4.26 metric tons and reaching a body length of up to 11.6 meters, which lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the largest known theropods from the Southern Hemisphere and was named after a dragon from the 'A Song of Ice and Fire' series, with its specific name meaning 'giant' in Greek.

name: Meroktenos
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 201.4–227.3 million years ago.
A compact femur with a straight shaft and a fourth trochanter oriented obliquely from the upper inner side to the lower outer side
A small basal sauropodomorph approximately four meters in length that lived during the Late Triassic period
This dinosaur lived in during the Triassic.
It is a relatively obscure early dinosaur known primarily from fragmentary remains found in Lesotho

name: Metriacanthosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.

It possesses distinctive vertebral neural spines that are about 1.5 times taller than the body of the vertebrae, a feature that is noticeably elevated but not as extreme as those found in its famous relative Acrocanthosaurus.
This medium-sized theropod reached approximately 8 meters in length and weighed around 1 tonne, functioning as a carnivore hunting in the Late Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.

It is historically significant as the only named member of its specific dinosaur family found in Europe, having been originally misidentified as a species of Megalosaurus before being given its own genus name meaning 'moderately-spined lizard'.

name: Mexidracon
hints: Found in the late Cretaceous Cerro del Pueblo Formation of Coahuila, Mexico, with fossils dating back approximately 70 million years.
It is distinguished by extremely lengthened metacarpals in its forelimbs, a unique trait among ornithomimids that likely aided in gathering vegetation.
This herbivorous theropod was roughly the size of a large ostrich, possessing long arms and slender feet that suggest it was less adapted for high-speed running than its relatives.
It lived in North America during the final period of the Mesozoic Era, specifically in what is now northern Mexico.
Its scientific name literally translates to 'Mexican dragon,' referencing both its geographic origin and its classification as a bird-like dinosaur.

name: Micropachycephalosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiangjunding Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
Possessed a thickened skull roof, a defining trait of the dome-headed herbivores it was originally classified with, though the specific bone is missing from the holotype specimen.
A very small bipedal herbivore, reaching only about 1 meter in length, that lived in China during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the record for having the longest genus name of any dinosaur, consisting of 23 letters.

name: Microraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed long pennaceous feathers on its hind legs in addition to its arms, creating a unique four-winged silhouette.
This small dromaeosaurid measured approximately one meter in length and likely consumed a diet of small vertebrates and insects.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as the 'four-winged dinosaur' famous for its ability to glide or potentially fly using all four limbs.

name: Microvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a primitive skull structure and long legs that suggest it was a swift runner, distinguishing it as an early member of the oviraptorosaur group.
This dinosaur was quite small, with the known juvenile specimen measuring approximately 1.3 meters in length, and it was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the earliest known oviraptorosaurian dinosaur discovered in North America.

name: Mierasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
Heart-shaped teeth at the rear of the jaw and a sharp ridge on the bottom edge of the lower jaw
A large, long-necked plant-eater from the Early Cretaceous period
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the most completely known sauropod skeletons discovered in North America

name: Migmanychion
hints: Fossils recovered from the Longjiang Formation, approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive claw mixture with varying morphology in the manual unguals
A small maniraptoran theropod from the Early Cretaceous of China
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A recently discovered dinosaur known primarily from an incomplete left forelimb and hand

name: Minimocursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Phu Kradung Formation, approximately 137.1–149.2 million years ago.
A basal neornithischian dinosaur with a postcranial skeleton that is more than 50% complete, including pes material and an isolated dentary.
Estimated to reach a fully grown length of 2 meters (6.6 ft), this small herbivore lived during the Late Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its genus name translates to 'smallest runner', highlighting its diminutive stature compared to other dinosaurs of its time.

name: Minotaurasaurus
hints: Late Campanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 75 to 71 million years ago, found in the Djadochta Formation of Mongolia.
A broad, bull-like skull featuring two distinct supraorbital horns and a tail club covered in protective bony armor plates.
A medium-sized armored herbivore estimated to be at least 4.2 meters (13.8 feet) long with a barrel-shaped body.
Lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period.
Its name is derived from the Minotaur, referencing the distinctive bull-like appearance of its skull.

name: Minqaria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Couche III Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive beak-shaped skull structure with fused braincase bones, characteristic of lambeosaurine hadrosaurs.
An estimated length of 3.5 meters and a weight of 250 kilograms, indicating a small, herbivorous dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very few hadrosaurid dinosaurs discovered in Africa, highlighting its rare presence on the continent.

name: Mirischia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Romualdo Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
Its left ischium bone features an oval perforation, while the right ischium has an open notch in the corresponding position, creating a distinct asymmetry in the hip structure.
It was a small, bipedal predator measuring approximately 2.1 meters in length and weighing around 7 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known to have preserved soft tissue remains, including a petrified section of intestine and evidence of post-cranial air sacs.

name: Mnyamawamtuka
hints: Fossils recovered from the Galula Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
The rear face of its middle tail vertebrae is widened to form a distinctive heart shape.
It was a relatively small titanosaur, measuring approximately 7.6 meters in length and weighing around 1.5 tonnes, and it was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked sauropod dinosaur from the Cretaceous period of Tanzania.

name: Moabosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
Extremely low neural spines that appear as thin, transverse ridges on the neck and back vertebrae, along with strongly procoelous tail bones
A juvenile specimen measured approximately 10 meters in length, and as a sauropod it was a large, plant-eating herbivore
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Utah, known for having a massive bonebed containing over 5,500 bones from at least 18 individuals

name: Mochlodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Csehbánya Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A distinctive bar-like median ridge on its teeth, which is the origin of its generic name derived from the Greek words for 'bar' and 'tooth'.
A small, herbivorous ornithopod measuring approximately 2 to 3 meters in length, which lived during the Late Cretaceous period in Europe.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the few dinosaur genera ever named from fossil discoveries made in Austria, alongside Struthiosaurus.

name: Mongolosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the On Gong Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive single, blunt tooth (haplodon) and a basioccipital bone from the back of the skull, characteristic of its classification as a titanosauriform sauropod.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore was a massive quadrupedal browser, typical of long-necked sauropods that dominated the landscape of ancient China and Mongolia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.

It is one of the few dinosaurs named after Mongolia, with its genus name literally translating to 'Mongolian lizard,' reflecting its discovery in Inner Mongolia by an American Museum of Natural History expedition.

name: Mongolostegus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dzunbain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive caudosacral vertebra and tail structure characterized by the lack of dorsal processes on the transverse processes and unexpanded apices of the neural spines.
A medium-sized herbivorous dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Mongolia, representing one of the youngest known members of its group.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the very last surviving stegosaurs, a group typically associated with the earlier Jurassic period, making it a rare Cretaceous representative of the 'roof-lizard' lineage.

name: Monoclonius
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman / Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A large bony frill extending from the back of the skull and a single prominent horn located on the nose
A large quadrupedal herbivore that likely reached lengths of up to 6 meters
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very first horned dinosaurs ever discovered and named during the late 19th century

name: Monolophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
A large, single, triangular-crested ridge running along the midline of the snout, formed primarily by the nasal bones.
A mid-sized carnivore approximately 5 to 5.5 meters long and weighing around 475 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
The prominent single crest on top of its head that gives the dinosaur its name.

name: Mononykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Djadokhta Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed highly reduced, stubby forelimbs ending in a single, massive claw on each hand.
It was a tiny, lightweight theropod measuring about 1 to 1.2 meters long and weighing roughly 3.5 kilograms, likely feeding on insects.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its bizarre, shovel-like arms and specialized inner ear structure, resembling that of a barn owl, suggest it was a feathered insectivore that may have had a facial disc.

name: Montanoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the St. Mary River / Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a proportionally large, deep, and heavy nasal bone with a well-developed horncore, which was originally mistaken for a nose horn but is now identified as a misplaced cheek horn.
It was a small, quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) in length and weighing around 170 kilograms (370 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is one of the few ceratopsians known to have retained claws on its feet rather than the hooves found in more advanced relatives, and it had teeth in its upper jaw instead of a completely toothless beak.

name: Morelladon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Arcillas de Morella Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.

Its dorsal vertebrae feature exceptionally tall neural spines, reaching at least 4.3 times the height of their vertebral bodies, along with a unique deep depression between the rear articular processes and the base of the neural spine.
It was a medium-sized herbivore, estimated to be six meters long and weighing two tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a relatively obscure Early Cretaceous ornithopod discovered in Spain, known primarily from a partial skeleton that notably lacks a skull.

name: Morrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Snow Hill Island Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive S-shaped greater trochanter on the femur and a fourth metatarsal bone with a prominent rearward projection that wraps around the third metatarsal.
An adult herbivore estimated to be 4.5 to 5 meters long, adapted for running with a narrow foot and stout skeletal build.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs discovered in Antarctica, living in a polar environment during the late Cretaceous period.

name: Mosaiceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiaguan Formation, approximately 72.2–93.9 million years ago.
A distinctive groove between the premaxilla and maxilla, an inverted T-shaped jugal bone, and a premaxilla that lacks teeth.
A small dinosaur measuring approximately 1 meter in length and weighing around 10 kilograms, classified as a plant-eating ceratopsian.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a horned dinosaur from the Cretaceous period, representing a basal form in the evolutionary lineage leading to later horned dinosaurs.

name: Murusraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sierra Barrosa Formation, approximately 85.7–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses unique hollow, tube-like sacral ribs and short, transversely flattened ischia that are distinct within the entire Theropoda group.
This immature specimen measured approximately 6.4 meters in length and was a carnivore belonging to the disputed megaraptoran lineage.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a large, predatory dinosaur from Late Cretaceous Patagonia known for its elongated snout and a skull that shares anatomical similarities with tyrannosaurids.

name: Musankwa
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pebbly Arkose Formation, approximately 205.7–237.0 million years ago.
An incomplete hindlimb consisting of an articulated partial right leg, including the femur, tibia, and astragalus, was the sole fossil material used to describe this genus.
It was a basal sauropodomorph massopodan that likely stood as an obligate biped and weighed approximately 386 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.
It is one of only four dinosaur genera ever named from Zimbabwe, discovered in the Pebbly Arkose Formation near Lake Kariba.

name: Mussaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Laguna Colorada Formation, approximately 192.9–199.5 million years ago.
It is one of the earliest dinosaurs known to have lived in complex social groups, with over 100 individuals of various ages found together in a single locality.
It was a medium-sized herbivore that grew up to 8 meters long and weighed up to 1.6 metric tons, transitioning from a quadrupedal stance as a juvenile to a bipedal stance as an adult.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'mouse lizard' because the first fossils discovered were tiny infant skeletons that were initially mistaken for belonging to a completely different, much smaller animal.

name: Muttaburrasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allaru Mudstone / Mackunda Formation, approximately 93.9–106.3 million years ago.
A distinctive, hollow, upward-bulging nasal muzzle on the snout, known as a bulla nasalis, which may have been used for display or producing calls.
An herbivore measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and weighing around 2.8 metric tons, equipped with powerful jaws and shearing teeth adapted for tough vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is Australia's most completely known dinosaur from skeletal remains and was selected as the official fossil emblem of the State of Queensland.

name: Muyelensaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Plottier Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
A slender body structure with a long neck and tail, characteristic of titanosaurian sauropods
A massive herbivore measuring approximately 12 meters in length, weighing several tons, and feeding on high vegetation
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A giant, four-legged plant-eater with a very long neck, resembling a massive land-based sea monster

name: Mymoorapelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.

It possesses four distinct horns on its skull: two large ones pointing backward from the brow and two smaller ones pointing backward and down from the cheekbones, along with a variety of armor types including elongated spines and a giant sacral shield.
It was a small, low-browsing herbivore, reaching approximately 3 meters in length and weighing between 300 and 562 kilograms, feeding on cycads and conifers.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known nodosaurids and the first Jurassic ankylosaur named from North America, recognizable by its heavy bony armor and spiked tail rather than a club.

name: Naashoibitosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A low nasal crest that peaks in front of the eyes without strongly arching backward
A large herbivore with a sophisticated skull featuring dental batteries of hundreds of continuously replacing teeth for grinding plants
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
A 'flat-headed or solid-crested' duckbill dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of New Mexico

name: Nambalia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maleri Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It is a basal sauropodomorph, an early relative of the giant long-necked dinosaurs, characterized by a primitive body plan.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Triassic period in what is now central India and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Triassic.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known from the fossil record of India, discovered near the village of Nambal.

name: Nanantius
hints: Fossils recovered from the Toolebuc Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
Possessed clawed wings, a feature linking it to its dinosaur ancestors.
A small bird approximately the size of a modern blackbird that likely fed on marine invertebrates and small fish.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its remains were discovered inside the stomach of an ichthyosaur, providing direct evidence of its diet.

name: Nankangia
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A U-shaped mandibular symphysis and a non-downturned lower jaw tip, distinguishing it from other oviraptorosaurs that have downturned beaks
A small, herbivorous dinosaur that fed on leaves and seeds, with a femur and tibia of approximately equal length
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A beaked, bird-like dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of China, closely related to the famous Oviraptor

name: Nanningosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A high and sharp ascending branch of the maxilla combined with a transversely wide lower quadrate bone.
An estimated body length of 7.5 meters and a weight of 2.5 tonnes, feeding on plants as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a type of duck-billed dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Nanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
A small, bipedal body plan with a long tail and hindlimbs adapted for running, historically classified among generalized ornithopods.
Approximately the size of a fox, weighing only a few kilograms, and feeding on low-lying vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Its name literally translates to 'dwarf lizard,' reflecting its status as one of the smallest dinosaurs known from the Morrison Formation.

name: Nanotyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A slender, low skull with small crests or horns immediately in front of the eyes, and upper front teeth that are not serrated while the rest are compressed side-to-side.
A mid-sized predator weighing between 700 and 1,200 kg, significantly smaller than its famous relative, with longer legs suggesting it was a pursuit hunter.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
The intense scientific debate over whether it is a distinct genus of small tyrannosaur or simply a juvenile specimen of the giant Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Nanshiungosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou / Yuanpu Formation, approximately 66.0–113.2 million years ago.
It possessed large, side-to-side flattened claws on its arms and a keratinous beak.
It was a large herbivore, approximately 5 meters long and weighing up to 900 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a therizinosaur, a dinosaur known for its bizarre combination of bird-like features and massive claws.

name: Nanuqsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Prince Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A thin, rostrally forked median spur on the fused parietals of the skull roof that overlaps and separates the frontals within the sagittal crest.
A tyrannosaurid theropod estimated to be 5 to 9 meters long and weighing up to 2 metric tons, which was likely covered in feathers for insulation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known as the 'polar bear lizard' and was one of the few dinosaurs that lived year-round in the high-latitude Arctic environment of Alaska.

name: Nanyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Majiacun Formation, approximately 72.2–93.9 million years ago.
It lacks a thumb spike on its hand, with the first digit and metacarpal completely absent.
It was a small herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is an early relative of the duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Napaisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xinlong Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from pelvic bones, specifically a right ilium and ischium, which are key components of the hip structure.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore was a basal iguanodontian ornithopod, a group of plant-eating dinosaurs that typically walked on two or four legs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first basal iguanodontian dinosaur formally named from the southern China region.

name: Narambuenatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed the characteristic pillar-like limbs and massive, barrel-shaped body typical of titanosaur sauropods, with vertebrae showing internal pneumatic cavities.
This giant herbivore likely reached lengths of around 15 meters and weighed several tons, feeding on high vegetation using its long neck.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is visually recognizable as a massive, four-legged plant-eater with a small head on a long neck, resembling a real-life version of a Brontosaurus.

name: Narindasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Isalo III Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
Heart-shaped teeth and caudal vertebrae with an asymmetric lateral fossa placed in a posterior position
A large herbivorous sauropod dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic period
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
A massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur discovered in Madagascar

name: Nasutoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

It possesses the longest known brow horns of any centrosaurine, which point forward and are approximately 40% of the total skull length, resembling those of a Texas Longhorn bull, while its nasal horn is low and blade-like.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore with an estimated body length of 4.5 meters and a weight of 1.5 tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized for its unusually large, deep snout and prominent forward-pointing horns, a distinctive appearance that led to its inclusion in the Jurassic World films.

name: Natovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A wide groove on the premaxilla and an hourglass-shaped second metacarpal bone
A tiny theropod measuring about 0.7 meters long and weighing 0.3 kilograms that ate fish
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known swimming dinosaurs with a streamlined body and flipper-like forelimbs

name: Navajoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive, elongated brow horn that is significantly longer than the nasal horn, a feature characteristic of the Chasmosaurinae subfamily
A large herbivore measuring approximately 5.5 meters in length, known from a partial skull found in the late Cretaceous of New Mexico
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Navajo horned face', referencing the indigenous people of the region where its fossils were discovered

name: Nebulasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhanghe Formation, approximately 168.2–174.7 million years ago.

The fossil record consists solely of a well-preserved braincase, distinguished by specific skull features such as the exoccipitals nearly excluding the supraoccipital from the foramen magnum and a frontoparietal fenestra located at the frontal-parietal suture.
As a basal eusauropod, this creature was a massive, long-necked herbivore that likely reached lengths of several meters and weighed several tons, feeding on high-growing vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a rare Middle Jurassic sauropod from China, notable for being one of the few basal eusauropods discovered in Asia and representing a clade previously unknown from that region.

name: Nedcolbertia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
A distinctive thumb claw that was much larger than the second claw of the hand, along with a pubic bone featuring a large posterior process.
A small theropod measuring approximately 1.5 to 3 meters in length, classified as a coelurosaur or ornithomimosaur.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its genus name is a direct tribute to the famous paleontologist Edwin Harris Colbert, who was known by the nickname 'Ned'.

name: Neimongosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu / Bostobe Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It possessed a remarkably elongated neck, with one of the longest cervical column counts among non-avian theropods, and a U-shaped jaw symphysis.
It was a small herbivore measuring approximately 2 to 3 meters in length and weighing around 350 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the therizinosaur family, a group of dinosaurs famously characterized by their enormous, clawed hands.

name: Nemegtomaia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot / Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A deep, narrow, and short skull featuring a distinctive arched cranial crest and a parrot-like beak.
A small, feathered dinosaur approximately 2 meters long and 40 kilograms that likely had a herbivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being found in a brooding position on top of a ring of eggs, confirming it was a caring parent rather than an egg thief.

name: Nemegtonykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a highly specialized, enlarged single claw on its forelimb, a trait characteristic of the Alvarezsauridae family.
This small theropod measured approximately one meter in length and likely fed on insects such as ants and termites.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Nemegt claw', directly referencing the distinctive oversized digit found on its arm.

name: Nemegtosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tamayama / Subashi Formation, approximately 66.0–89.8 million years ago.
A long, low skull with slender, pencil-shaped teeth that are ground down at a sharp angle to form a chisel-like tip
A medium-sized sauropod measuring approximately 12 to 13 meters in length and weighing around 20 tons
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very last surviving sauropods on Earth, living alongside giant predators like Tarbosaurus in the Late Cretaceous

name: Neovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
The snout is covered in distinct rugosities (rough textures), suggesting an extensive blood supply or neurovascular system for thermoregulation or tactile sensing.
It was a mid-sized, lightly built predator measuring approximately 7 to 10 meters in length and weighing around 1 metric ton, feeding on prey like Mantellisaurus.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a large, carnivorous theropod from the Early Cretaceous period of England, often recognized as a close relative or British equivalent of the more famous Allosaurus.

name: Neuquenraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
A distinctive sickle-shaped claw on the second toe, characteristic of the dromaeosaurid family.
An estimated length of 1.8 to 3.5 meters and a weight of around 75 kilograms, indicating it was a small, agile carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the first members of its family discovered in the Southern Hemisphere, challenging previous beliefs that these dinosaurs were restricted to the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Neuquensaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto / Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed armor-like osteoderms and had a sacrum composed of seven vertebrae, which is unique among all sauropods.
It was a relatively small sauropod, measuring 7–9 meters long and weighing between 1.8 and 3.5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina known for its bony skin armor.

name: Nevadadromeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Willow Tank Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A small, bipedal ornithischian dinosaur with anatomical features linking it to the Thescelosaurinae subfamily, making it the oldest known member of that group in North America
A small herbivore, likely measuring only a few feet in length, that lived during the Late Cretaceous period
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the first non-avian dinosaur to be formally named from the state of Nevada

name: Newtonsaurus
hints: Fossils were found in the Lilstock Formation of South Wales, dating to approximately 202 million years ago during the Late Triassic (Rhaetian stage).
It is known only from a partial lower jaw (dentary) that features uniquely elongated foramina (holes for nerves and blood vessels) along the outer side of the bone.
It was a large bipedal predator, estimated to be between 5 and 7 meters long, making it one of the biggest theropods of the Triassic period.
It lived in what is now Great Britain, a region that was part of the supercontinent Pangaea and transitioning from arid desert to shallow marine environments.
Its jaw shape is very similar to that of Dilophosaurus, but it is slightly less deep and lacks the distinctive head crests associated with that more famous relative.

name: Ngwevu
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A distinctive ridge on the lateral surface of the backside of the jugal bone
An adult individual approximately 10 years old, proportionately more robust than related species, with a wide snout and broad palate suggesting a generalist diet
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being a grey-skulled, early Jurassic plant-eater from South Africa that was once mistaken for a younger version of Massospondylus

name: Nhandumirim
hints: Fossils recovered from the Caturrita Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A unique semicircular facet for the ascending process of the astragalus on the fibula, combined with a diagonal mound-like tuberosity on the tibia that is found in no other known dinosaur.
A small, gracile, long-legged bipedal dinosaur from the Late Triassic, likely measuring only about one meter in length, with a diet consisting of plants or small insects.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.

Being one of the earliest known dinosaurs, it is famous for having skeletal features so similar to later theropods that some scientists initially classified it as the earliest known meat-eating dinosaur.

name: Nigersaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.

Its skull was extremely delicate and hollow, with bone struts less than 2 mm thick, and featured a unique transverse rotation of its jaws so that all 500+ teeth faced forward like a vacuum cleaner nozzle.
It was a small sauropod, measuring 9 to 14 meters long and weighing about as much as a modern elephant, and it was a herbivore that browsed on low-lying soft plants like ferns.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is often nicknamed the 'Mesozoic cow' due to its habit of keeping its head close to the ground to graze, resembling a vacuum cleaner or Darth Vader in appearance.

name: Ningyuansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A long, triangular skull with a straight lower jaw and closely packed, non-serrated teeth, which is unusual for its group.
A small dinosaur, likely under one meter in length, that may have been partially a seed-eater based on fossilized stomach contents.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its distinctive combination of a bird-like body with feathers and a reptilian-looking head with many teeth.

name: Ninjatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajada Colorada Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
It possessed anterior caudal vertebrae with large pleurocoels, a feature unusual for titanosaurs but similar to the non-titanosaurian Triunfosaurus.
This Early Cretaceous sauropod from Argentina was estimated to be a giant, though its exact classification within Titanosauria remains debated due to its age and skeletal features.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is known as the 'Ninja giant,' named after the nickname of the paleontologist who discovered it, and was once considered the oldest known titanosaur.

name: Niobrarasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Niobrara Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
Heavily armored with bony scutes covering its back and sides, lacking a tail club and featuring prominent spikes along its shoulders and flanks
A medium-sized herbivore estimated to be between 5 and 6.5 meters long and weighing up to 4 tonnes
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, spiky armored appearance characteristic of nodosaurid dinosaurs

name: Nipponosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It is a lambeosaurine hadrosaur, a type of duck-billed dinosaur known for possessing a hollow, elaborate crest on its skull.
The known specimen is a juvenile measuring approximately 4 meters (13 feet) in length, and it would have been a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as the first dinosaur species to be named based on fossils discovered on Japanese territory.

name: Noasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lecho Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses large, strongly curved, sickle-shaped claws on its forelimbs that are functionally similar to those of spinosaurids, rather than the foot claws seen in dromaeosaurids.
This small theropod measured approximately 1.6 to 2 meters in length and weighed around 15 kilograms, acting as an opportunistic mesopredator that likely fed on small vertebrates and fish.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.

It is a relatively obscure South American dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous, often confused with or compared to Velociraptor due to its similar size and claw structure, despite belonging to the distinct Ceratosauria group.

name: Nodocephalosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A skull heavily armored with fused, bulbous polygonal osteoderms and a prominent horn projecting from the quadratojugal bone.
An estimated length of 4.5 meters and a weight of 1.5 tonnes, classified as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive 'knob-headed' appearance caused by the unique, rounded bony plates covering the top of its skull.

name: Nodosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Frontier Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.

Approximately 4 to 6 meters long and weighing around 3.5 tonnes, this herbivore had a short neck, four short legs, and a long, stiff, clubless tail

name: Nopcsaspondylus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A single, now-lost dorsal vertebra characterized by a small vertebral body and large hollows, which is the only known specimen of this genus.

name: Normanniasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Poudingue ferrugineux Formation, approximately 106.3–110.1 million years ago.
Presacral vertebrae featuring a hyposphene-hypantrum attachment and a cancellous internal texture, alongside deeply procoelous anterior caudal vertebrae with axially elongated neural spines.

Being one of the few known sauropods to have lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous period.

name: Notatesseraeraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Klettgau Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A distinctive mix of anatomical traits, combining features typically found in dilophosaurids with those of coelophysoids, creating a 'mosaic' of characteristics.

name: Nothronychus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moreno Hill / Tropic Shale Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.

name: Notoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

name: Notocolossus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Plottier Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
Its humerus is exceptionally wide, with an upper edge nearly three times wider than the shaft diameter, and its foot features truncated, rough toe claws that are unique among sauropods.

This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the heaviest land animals ever discovered, often cited alongside Argentinosaurus and Dreadnoughtus as a contender for the title of the largest dinosaur.

name: Notohypsilophodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A partial juvenile skeleton consisting mainly of vertebrae, ribs, and limb bones, but notably lacking a skull.
A small, bipedal herbivore estimated to be approximately 1.3 meters long and weighing around 6 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the few ornithopod dinosaurs discovered in South America, specifically from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia.

name: Nqwebasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirkwood Formation, approximately 132.6–143.1 million years ago.
A long, three-fingered hand featuring a partially opposable thumb with a recurved claw, alongside maxillary teeth that lack serrations and a reduced dentition.
A small to medium-sized animal estimated to be around 1 meter (3.3 feet) long and weighing approximately 1 kilogram, with evidence of gastroliths suggesting an omnivorous diet.

name: Nullotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chorrillo Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

name: Nuthetes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lulworth Formation, approximately 137.1–143.1 million years ago.

name: Obelignathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Grès à Reptiles Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A robust right dentary (jawbone) with distinctive dental anatomy that initially led to its classification within the genus Rhabdodon before being recognized as a separate genus.
A Late Cretaceous herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur from southern France, known from fossil remains found in the Grès à Reptiles Formation.

Its genus name is a playful reference to the fictional character Obelix from the Asterix comics, alluding to the strong and robust nature of its jawbone.

name: Oblitosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villar del Arzobispo Formation, approximately 143.1–152.2 million years ago.

name: Oceanotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Praia da Amoreira-Porto Novo Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
A sagittal groove on the top of the first tail vertebrae neural spines, along with lateral depressions on the edges of anterior and middle tail vertebrae neural spines.

name: Ohmdenosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Posidonia Shale Formation, approximately 180.4–182.9 million years ago.
A distinctive cnemial crest projecting from the upper front of the shinbone, along with a sandal-shaped ankle bone (astragalus) that is rotated 90 degrees relative to the tibia.
A small, four-legged plant-eater estimated to be only 3 to 4 meters long, which is notably tiny for its group of long-necked giants.

name: Olorotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Udurchukan Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A large, flaring hollow crest on its skull that is distinct from its North American relatives, supported by an unusually elongated neck with eighteen vertebrae.

name: Omeisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 143.1–174.7 million years ago.
It possesses a tail club at the end of its caudal vertebrae, a feature unusual for a sauropod.
This herbivorous sauropod ranged from 14 to over 20 meters in length and weighed between 4 and nearly 10 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Jurassic period of China, often displayed in museums with its long neck held high.

name: Ondogurvel
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

name: Oohkotokia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A heavy, bony tail club formed by enlarged bone lumps at the end of the tail, used for defense.

name: Opisthocoelicaudia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

name: Oplosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 121.4–137.1 million years ago.

name: Orkoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Fortaleza Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

name: Ornatops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Menefee Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

name: Ornitholestes

name: Ornithomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lance / Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.

name: Ornithopsis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex / Portland Stone Formation, approximately 119.6–145.1 million years ago.

name: Orodromeus

hints: Fossils recovered from the Lakota / Morrison Formation, approximately 121.4–154.8 million years ago.

name: Ostromia

name: Ouranosaurus

name: Overoraptor

name: Oviraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta / Dabrazhin Formation, approximately 72.2–89.8 million years ago.

name: Oxalaia

name: Pachyrhinosaurus

name: Padillasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Paja Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.

name: Paludititan

name: Pampadromaeus

name: Pamparaptor

name: Panamericansaurus

name: Pandoravenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Calcáreo Formation, approximately 149.2–157.9 million years ago.

name: Panguraptor

name: Panoplosaurus

name: Panphagia

name: Pantydraco
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.

name: Papiliovenator

name: Paralitherizinosaurus

name: Paralititan

name: Pararhabdodon

This was a large herbivorous hadrosaurid that lived during the Late Cretaceous period in Europe.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is notable for being one of the last non-avian dinosaurs to exist before the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event.

name: Parasaurolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland / Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A long, tubular cranial crest that curves backward from the skull, which is hollow and likely used for acoustic resonance.
A large herbivore reaching over 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing more than 5 metric tons, capable of walking on both two and four legs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive backward-curving head crest, which resembles a trombone slide or a trumpet, making it one of the most recognizable duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Paraxenisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a stiff, rod-like anterior tail with vertically-oriented vertebral articulations and retains a first toe digit, lacking the arctometatarsus condition seen in most other ornithomimosaurs.
It was approximately 5.7 meters long and weighed around 600 kilograms, living as a bipedal theropod in Late Cretaceous North America.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Deinocheiridae family, a group of large, often bizarre-looking theropods known for their massive, hooked hand claws.

name: Pareisactus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Conques Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is known exclusively from a single scapula bone, making it one of the most fragmentary dinosaur genera ever described.
This herbivorous ornithopod lived during the Late Cretaceous period and belonged to the rhabdodontid family, a group of beaked, plant-eating dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its genus name translates to 'intruder' because it was found as a single, isolated bone amidst hundreds of other hadrosaurid fossils in the same rock formation.

name: Parksosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed thin, partly ossified cartilaginous plates along its ribs and a robust shoulder girdle.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 2.5 meters in length and weighing around 45 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few described non-hadrosaurid ornithopods from the very end of the Cretaceous period in North America.

name: Paronychodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–154.8 million years ago.
A small, elongated, and recurved tooth with a D-shaped cross-section, lacking serrations and possessing low vertical ridges.
The fossil evidence consists solely of isolated teeth approximately one centimeter long, indicating a small carnivorous theropod, likely a juvenile.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a notorious 'tooth taxon' where the fossils are so fragmentary and varied that scientists cannot agree on which specific dinosaur family they belong to.

name: Parvicursor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Extremely short and stubby forelimbs with hands reduced to a single, large, powerful claw on each hand, while possessing long, slender legs built for speed.
One of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs, measuring only about 39 centimeters in length and weighing approximately 162 grams, with a diet likely consisting of insects like termites.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'small runner,' highlighting the contrast between its tiny body size and its specialized anatomy for fast movement.

name: Patagonykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Portezuelo Formation, approximately 85.7–93.9 million years ago.
A highly specialized, massively enlarged single claw on the first digit of its forelimb, which was disproportionately large compared to its tiny, reduced other fingers.
A very small dinosaur, approximately 1 meter (3.3 feet) long and weighing only about 3.5 kilograms (7.7 pounds), likely an insectivore that fed on termites or ants.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Patagonian Claw,' highlighting the bizarre and oversized single claw on its hand that defined its unique appearance.

name: Patagopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A heavily armored body covered in bony plates (osteoderms) and distinctive large crests on its neck armor and thigh bones.
A small, low-slung herbivore measuring approximately 2 meters (6.6 feet) in length, weighing significantly less than larger armored dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as a 'shield' dinosaur from Patagonia, South America, featuring a unique frond-like tail weapon similar to a macuahuitl.

name: Patagosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 168.6–182.9 million years ago.
It possesses a unique neural canal enlargement in its sacral vertebrae, forming a well-defined cavity that extends from the first to the third vertebra.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be approximately 16.5 meters long and weighing around 7.9 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a classic example of an early, unspecialized sauropod with a long neck, small head, and very long tail, resembling its relative Cetiosaurus.

name: Patagotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Barcino Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.

It possesses unique accessory vertebral articulations known as hyposphene-hypantrum between only one pair of vertebrae at the level of the scapular blade, a weight-bearing adaptation not seen in any other sauropod.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore estimated to reach lengths of approximately 31 to 37 meters and weigh between 50 and 77 tonnes, making it one of the largest land animals ever discovered.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as one of the largest dinosaurs known to science, often featured in museum exhibits as a massive titanosaur that rivals or exceeds the size of Argentinosaurus.

name: Pawpawsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Paw Paw Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
It possessed bony eyelids, a rare trait among nodosaurid ankylosaurs.
It was a medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, plant-eating dinosaur known for its bony plates and spikes covering its body.

name: Pectinodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lance / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
Distinctive comb-like serrations on the rear edge of its small, labio-lingually compressed teeth
A small, bird-like, feathered bipedal omnivore with proportionally large eyes and a sickle-claw on its second toe
This dinosaur lived in North America, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being known exclusively from fossilized teeth, which led to its name meaning 'comb tooth'

name: Pedopenna
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
It possesses long, pennaceous feathers attached directly to its metatarsus (foot), a feature that inspired its name meaning 'foot feather'.
It was a very small dinosaur, measuring approximately one meter in length, and is classified as a maniraptoran with a bird-like skeletal structure.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for its 'hind wings' or foot feathers, which provide key evidence for the evolutionary link between non-avian dinosaurs and modern birds.

name: Pegomastax
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses an unusually tall, wedge-shaped predentary bone at the tip of its snout that is much taller than it is long, supporting a keratinous beak.
It was a small herbivore, with a skull length of approximately 73 millimeters, featuring a large, straight canine tooth at the front of its jaw followed by a gap before the cheek teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a heterodontosaurid, a group of early ornithischian dinosaurs known for having teeth of different shapes, specifically combining a parrot-like beak with prominent canine-like teeth.

name: Pelecanimimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Huérguina Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses approximately 220 tiny, unserrated teeth, which is the highest tooth count of any known ornithomimosaur and any other theropod dinosaur.
It was a small dinosaur measuring about 1.9 to 2.5 meters in length and weighing between 17 and 30 kilograms, likely feeding on fish and small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It had a distinctive throat pouch similar to that of a modern pelican, which it used to store caught prey.

name: Pellegrinisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
The mid-posterior and posterior caudal vertebrae feature elongated, dorsoventrally depressed neural spines that likely reduced tail movement.
It was a massive titanosaurian sauropod measuring 20–25 meters long and weighing approximately 50 metric tons, which fed on plants.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a large, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of South America.

name: Peloroplites
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses small, blunt squamosal horns and low, conical postorbital horncores that are much less prominent than those of related nodosaurids like Sauropelta.
It was a large herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing around 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period, known for its bony plates and spikes covering its back.

name: Pelorosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tunbridge Wells Sand Formation, approximately 110.1–145.1 million years ago.
It possesses a massive, pillar-like humerus that was historically used to prove it was a land-dwelling animal rather than a sea creature.
It was a giant titanosauriform sauropod reaching approximately 24 meters in length that fed on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It was one of the very first dinosaurs ever identified as such, with its name meaning 'monstrous lizard'.

name: Pendraig
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
An articulated vertebral series and pelvic girdle, including sacral vertebrae and a complete left ilium, pubis, and ischium
A small theropod measuring approximately 1 meter in length, belonging to the coelophysoid group
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
A Late Triassic dinosaur discovered in South Wales that was originally misclassified as a species of Syntarsus

name: Penelopognathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bayangobi Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive jaw structure that resembles that of a wild duck
A hadrosauroid dinosaur measuring approximately 5 meters in length and 2 meters in height, which was a plant-eater
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the early relatives of the duck-billed dinosaurs

name: Pentaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland / Fruitland Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.

It possesses a distinctive five-horned facial structure, featuring a short nose horn, two long forward-curving brow horns, and two additional long horns projecting from the jugal bones beneath the eyes.
This herbivorous ceratopsid measured approximately 5.5 to 6 meters in length and weighed around 2.5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is visually characterized by a massive, rectangular skull frill that is considerably longer than that of Triceratops and is decorated with large triangular bony spikes along its edge.

name: Perijasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Quinta Formation, approximately 170.9–180.4 million years ago.
A single broken dorsal vertebra measuring approximately 55 cm tall and 45 cm long, which served as the holotype for the species.
A basal eusauropod sauropod that lived approximately 175 million years ago during the early to middle Jurassic period, inhabiting a tropical lowland forest environment.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It holds the distinction of being the northernmost sauropod ever recorded from South America and the first dinosaur discovered in the Colombian portion of the La Quinta Formation.

name: Petrobrasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Plottier Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses a titanosaurian sauropod skeleton characterized by robust, pillar-like limbs and a relatively short neck compared to other long-necked dinosaurs.
This massive herbivore likely reached lengths of approximately 10 meters and weighed several tons, feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is visually defined by its enormous, elephantine body and four thick, columnar legs supporting a heavy torso.

name: Petrustitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sînpetru / Sebeş Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A massive, pillar-like limb bone structure characteristic of titanosaurian sauropods, specifically including a left tibia and fibula.
A giant herbivorous quadruped that reached significant lengths and weights, typical of Late Cretaceous eutitanosaurians.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a giant, long-necked dinosaur discovered in Romania that was originally classified under the name Magyarosaurus.

name: Philovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wulansuhai Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A very elongated and narrow tarsometatarsus that is 25% longer than the thighbone, featuring a distinctive plate-like cnemial crest on the shinbone and a flange along the fourth metatarsal
A small, bird-like dinosaur with a total body length of approximately two feet, possessing raptorial hands and sickle-claws indicative of a carnivorous diet
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A member of the Troodontidae family, known for having some of the highest non-avian encephalization quotients, suggesting advanced behavior and keen senses

name: Phuwiangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sao Khua / Khok Kruat Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
