Its teeth are remarkably slender and peg-like, with a height-to-width ratio over four to one, resembling those of diplodocoids rather than typical titanosaurians.
This mid-sized sauropod measured approximately 15 to 20 meters in length and weighed around 17 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few sauropods known from numerous baby fossils, providing rare insights into the growth and development of these giant reptiles.

name: Phuwiangvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sao Khua Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
Short furrows on the ventral side of the sacral vertebrae and a uniquely sloping fourth metatarsal with the lowest corner relative to the third metatarsal of any known theropod
Approximately 6 meters long, this large carnivorous hunter likely preyed on other dinosaurs during the Early Cretaceous period
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Megaraptora group, a lineage of theropods famous for possessing massive, sickle-shaped claws on their hands

name: Piatnitzkysaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Asfalto Formation, approximately 180.4–182.9 million years ago.
A prominent hook-like basisphenoidal wing in the braincase and a parasphenoid recess, along with a shorter, wider scapular blade compared to more derived tetanurans.
A moderately large, lightly built bipedal carnivore measuring approximately 4.3 meters (14 feet) in length and weighing around 450 kilograms (990 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its strong anatomical resemblance to Allosaurus, often leading to confusion between the two despite key differences in limb proportions and pelvic structure.

name: Pilmatueia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mulichinco Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It possessed tall, deeply forked neural spines on its neck and back vertebrae that inclined forward, distinguishing it from relatives with backswept spines or those lacking such structures.
This relatively small sauropod measured less than 12 meters in length and lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Dicraeosauridae family, a group of sauropods known for their distinctive, often bifurcated, neck and back spines.

name: Pinacosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta / Bayan Mandahu Formation, approximately 66.0–143.1 million years ago.
A flat, low-slung body covered in an armor of keeled osteoderms and bone tiles, featuring a distinctive tail club used for active defense.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 5 meters in length and weighing up to 1.9 metric tons, which used a smooth beak to bite off low-growing plants.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as an armored, tank-like dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous, notable for having one of the largest collections of juvenile skeletons found in a single bonebed.

name: Pisanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–233.6 million years ago.
It possesses an open acetabulum (hip socket) and short ilium peduncles, with an upper ischium that is wider than the pubic bone.
It was a small, lightly built herbivore measuring approximately 1 to 1.3 meters in length and weighing around 2 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in South America.
It is widely recognized as one of the oldest known ornithischian dinosaurs, representing a very early branch of the bird-hipped lineage.

name: Pitekunsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Anacleto Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by a distinctive braincase structure that closely resembles that of the aeolosaurid Muyelensaurus.
This Late Cretaceous herbivore was a massive quadrupedal dinosaur native to what is now Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known member of the titanosaur lineage, often overshadowed by more famous giants like Argentinosaurus or Patagotitan from the same region and era.

name: Piveteausaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marnes de Dives Formation, approximately 154.8–165.3 million years ago.
A partial braincase that is comparable in size to that of a large Allosaurus and resembles that of the megalosauroid Piatnitzkysaurus.
An estimated length of 11 meters (36 feet), classified as a theropod carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
A fragmentary skull discovered in the Middle Jurassic rocks of Normandy, France, which has been historically confused with other genera like Eustreptospondylus and Proceratosaurus.

name: Planicoxa
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a uniquely flat ilium (hip bone) created by the horizontal folding of its rear portion, which serves as a functional antitrochanter.
This ornithopod measured approximately 4.5 meters (15 feet) in length and weighed around 450 kilograms (990 pounds), feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a medium-sized, early relative of the more famous iguanodonts, known primarily from fossilized hip bones and leg bones found in Utah and South Dakota.

name: Plateosauravus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 201.4–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses a long neck and tail with robust limbs, characteristic of early sauropodomorphs, though its exact skeletal proportions remain less defined than its more famous relatives.
This herbivore reached an estimated length of around 5 to 6 meters and weighed approximately 1 ton, representing a medium-sized plant-eater of the Late Triassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.
It is often considered a synonym or closely related to the more widely recognized genus Euskelosaurus, which is famous for being one of the first dinosaurs discovered in Africa.

name: Plateosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Löwenstein / Trossingen Formation, approximately 199.5–237.0 million years ago.
It possessed a small skull on a long, flexible neck, sharp but plump plant-crushing teeth, and grasping hands with large claws on three fingers.
It was a bipedal herbivore that could grow between 4.8 and 10 meters long and weigh up to 4,000 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs and is famous for the massive number of skeletons found in Germany, leading to its nickname 'Swabian lindworm'.

name: Platypelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive skull feature where the squamosal horns at the rear corners are low, rough, and have a broad base with a pitted point, along with a constricted snout just in front of the eye sockets.
A large herbivore estimated to be six meters in length, covered in heavy armor including large osteoderms on its rump that exceed twenty-five centimeters in length with a sharp keel.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur with a bony tail club and extensive skin impressions preserved, resembling a living tank from the Late Cretaceous.

name: Platytholus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A partial skull featuring a thickened, flattened, and broad dome on the top of the head, characteristic of the pachycephalosaurid family.
A small herbivorous dinosaur, likely measuring around 1.5 to 2 meters in length, that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive 'flat dome' skull shape, which sets it apart from the more commonly known rounded-domed boneheads like Pachycephalosaurus.

name: Plesiohadros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Alagteeg Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive skull structure characteristic of early hadrosauroids, featuring a robust snout and specialized jaw mechanics for shearing vegetation.
This plant-eating dinosaur reached an estimated length of approximately 4 to 5 meters and weighed around 1 to 2 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the duck-billed dinosaur family, recognizable by its broad, flattened beak and complex dental batteries designed for grinding tough plant material.

name: Pneumatoraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Csehbánya Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A narrow, nearly circular shoulder blade featuring a large opening for a hollow air sac cavity, giving the genus its name meaning 'air thief'.
A small paravian theropod approximately one-third the size of Velociraptor, likely a carnivore from the late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'air thief', referencing the distinctive hollow, pneumatic structure of its preserved shoulder bone.

name: Poekilopleuron
hints: Fossils recovered from the Calcaires de Caen Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
It possessed unusually long and powerful forelimbs with elongated humeri and short, robust forearms, lacking the olecranon process on the ulna.
As a medium-sized carnivorous theropod from the Middle Jurassic of France, it likely measured around 6 meters in length and weighed approximately 1 ton.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'varied ribs,' referring to the discovery of three distinct types of rib bones in its fossil remains.

name: Polacanthus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex / Vectis Formation, approximately 100.5–139.1 million years ago.
A large, fused pelvic shield covering the hips, surrounded by numerous smaller ossicles and rows of keeled osteoderms.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length and weighing around 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being heavily armored with many spikes and plates, living up to its name which translates to 'many thorns'.

name: Portellsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Mirambel Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.

Possesses a distinctive deep oval cavity on the middle of the lower jaw's adductor fossa below the eleventh-twelfth tooth position, which internally connects with the last nutrient foramina in the dentary.
It is a hadrosauroid dinosaur from the Lower Cretaceous period of Spain, known only from a partial right dentary, placing it within the broader group of duck-billed relatives.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a relatively obscure dinosaur known from very limited fossil material, specifically just a single jawbone fragment, making it less recognizable than major hadrosaurids.

name: Powellvenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Colorados Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It is a small, agile carnivore belonging to the Coelophysoidea group, characterized by a lightweight skeleton and sharp, serrated teeth adapted for hunting.
This Triassic predator measured approximately 1.5 to 2 meters in length and weighed only a few kilograms, feeding on small prey.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is one of the earliest known theropod dinosaurs, representing a primitive stage of bipedal carnivorous evolution before the rise of the giant Jurassic and Cretaceous predators.

name: Pradhania
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dharmaram Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It is a massospondylid sauropodomorph, a group characterized by their distinctive body plan that includes a small head, long neck, and a body supported by four legs.
This dinosaur was of modest size, reaching only about four meters (13 feet) in length, and lived during the Early Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known from the Early Jurassic of India, represented by fragmentary remains found in the Upper Dharmaram Formation.

name: Prenocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Kirtland Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A rounded, sloping skull dome adorned with a distinct row of small bony spikes and bumps, lacking the paired grooves above the eyes found in similar species.
A small, stout-bodied dinosaur measuring approximately 2.2 meters (7.2 feet) in length and weighing around 40 kilograms (88 pounds), likely herbivorous or omnivorous.
This dinosaur lived in Asia, North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive 'sloping head' dome shape, which gives the genus its name and sets it apart from the flat-headed relatives found in the same region.

name: Prenoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine / Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive sloping head profile with a lower, more inclined shape compared to its close relative Leptoceratops.
A small herbivore measuring approximately 1.3 meters in length and weighing around 20 kilograms, feeding on ferns, cycads, and conifers.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a basal member of the horned-face dinosaur group (Ceratopsia) from the Late Cretaceous period of North America.

name: Probactrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Miaogou Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed specialized tooth batteries consisting of superimposed double rows of flattened cheek teeth, along with a narrow snout and an elongated lower jaw.
This lightly built herbivore reached approximately 5.5 meters in length and weighed around one tonne, featuring relatively long, slender arms with only a small thumb spike.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as a basal ancestor-like relative of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, sharing early evolutionary traits with later hadrosaurs while lacking their distinctive crests or large beaks.

name: Probrachylophosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A massive, horizontally oriented skull crest formed entirely by the nasal bones, which overhang the supratemporal fenestrae and feature a pointed, triangular cross-section at the midline
A large herbivore measuring approximately 10 meters in length and weighing around 5 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its nickname 'Super-Duck', reflecting its status as one of the largest known specimens in its specific evolutionary lineage

name: Proceratosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the White Limestone Formation, approximately 165.3–168.2 million years ago.
A distinctive cranial crest that begins at the junction between the premaxilla and nasal bones, resembling the base of a nasal horn.
A small to medium-sized carnivore, approximately 3 meters (10 feet) long and weighing around 30 to 50 kilograms, with heterodont teeth including D-shaped front teeth and serrated side teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is widely recognized as one of the earliest known members of the Tyrannosauroidea lineage, making it a distant, feathered ancestor to the famous Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Procompsognathus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Löwenstein Formation, approximately 211.2–215.4 million years ago.
A long, slender snout filled with many small teeth and a stiff tail, combined with hind legs that are approximately 20% longer than the femurs to support fast running.
A small, lightly built bipedal carnivore measuring about 1 meter (3.3 feet) in length and weighing roughly 1 kilogram, likely feeding on insects and lizards.
This dinosaur lived in Europe.
Its appearance in Michael Crichton's novel Jurassic Park and its sequel The Lost World, where it is depicted as a small, agile predator.

name: Propanoplosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Patuxent Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
A distinctive cross-pattern of bone plates on the snout, formed by triangular osteoderms on the maxillae, with armor restricted primarily to the skull in this developmental stage.
A tiny neonate with a total estimated length of only 24 to 28 centimeters, classified as a herbivorous nodosaurid.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being the first nodosaurid skeleton discovered from the eastern seaboard of the United States and the first neonate dinosaur found in that region.

name: Prosaurolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Two Medicine Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A small, solid, triangular crest formed by the nasal bones that sits directly in front of the eyes, with concave sides forming depressions.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 8.5 meters (28 feet) in length and weighing around 3 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'before Saurolophus,' indicating it is the earlier evolutionary relative of the dinosaur with the longer, spike-like head crest.

name: Protarchaeopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed vaned feathers on its body and tail, along with hollow, bird-like bones and a wishbone, indicating a close evolutionary relationship to birds.
This turkey-sized dinosaur measured approximately 0.7 to 1 meter in length and weighed around 1.6 kilograms, likely feeding on plants or small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a small, feathered, flightless dinosaur from the Cretaceous period that is often compared to Archaeopteryx but lived millions of years later.

name: Protathlitis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Arcillas de Morella Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
A partial skeleton including a maxillary fragment, caudal vertebrae, and a tooth, originally classified as a basal member of the Baryonychinae subfamily.
An Early Cretaceous theropod from the Arcillas de Morella Formation in Spain, dating to approximately 129.4 to 125 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
A spinosaurid dinosaur whose genus name means 'champion' in Greek, named after the 2020–21 UEFA Europa League victory of Villareal C.F.

name: Protoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta / Bayan Mandahu Formation, approximately 66.0–121.4 million years ago.
A large, bony neck frill and a small, parrot-like beak at the tip of the jaws.
A small, quadrupedal herbivore measuring about 2 to 2.5 meters long and weighing up to 104 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
The famous 'Fighting Dinosaurs' fossil specimen showing it locked in combat with a Velociraptor.

name: Protognathosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A fragmentary lower jaw (dentary) featuring eighteen tooth positions with sharp replacement teeth
A herbivorous sauropod from the Middle Jurassic of China, though its exact size is unknown due to fragmentary remains
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is primarily known to paleontologists as a 'nomen dubium' (dubious name) because the fossil material is too incomplete for definitive classification

name: Protohadros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lewisville Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A partial skull and hand ungual from a basal hadrosauroid, representing an early stage in the evolution of duck-billed dinosaurs.
A relatively small herbivore, likely measuring around 3 meters in length, that fed on low-lying vegetation during the Late Cretaceous.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being recognized as one of the earliest known relatives of the iconic duck-billed dinosaurs, serving as a 'missing link' in their evolutionary history.

name: Psittacosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian / Ilek Formation, approximately 66.0–161.5 million years ago.
A distinctive parrot-like beak and the absence of teeth in the front part of the upper jaw
An obligate biped measuring approximately 2 meters (6.6 feet) in length that ate plants
This dinosaur lived in Asia, Asia/Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the earliest known relatives of the horned dinosaurs like Triceratops

name: Puertasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Fortaleza Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
Its most famous fossil is a dorsal vertebra that is the broadest known of any sauropod, featuring massive, wing-like transverse processes.
It was a colossal herbivore estimated to be around 30 meters long and weighing between 50 and 80 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered, often cited alongside Argentinosaurus as a contender for the title of the biggest land animal.

name: Pulanesaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A transitional sauropodiform with a relatively short neck that has not yet evolved the extreme elongation seen in later, more advanced sauropods.
A medium-sized herbivore that lived during the Early Jurassic period in what is now South Africa.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is considered a basal or early member of the lineage that would eventually produce the giant, long-necked sauropods.

name: Pulaosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 154.8–165.3 million years ago.
It possesses a rare ossified larynx (voicebox), a feature that suggests it may have produced bird-like vocalizations.
It was a small, immature herbivore measuring approximately 72 centimeters in length, with gut contents indicating a diet of plant seeds.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the very few non-avian dinosaurs known to preserve a larynx, making its potential ability to make sounds a unique and famous trait.

name: Punatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ciénaga del Río Huaco Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.

It possessed a partial skeleton including a specific anterior portion of a posterior cervical vertebra, middle dorsal vertebrae, a partial sacrum, and 13 articulated caudal vertebrae with haemal arches.
This titanosaurian sauropod measured approximately 14 meters in length and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina.

name: Pycnonemosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A hatchet-shaped cnemial crest on the tibia and a small pubic foot, along with awl-like projections on the tail vertebrae that diminish toward the rear.
An estimated length of approximately 9 meters (29.5 feet) and a weight of around 3.6 tonnes, classified as a carnivore that preyed on titanosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the largest formally described member of the Abelisauridae family found in Brazil.

name: Pyroraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Posa / Vitoria Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive enlarged, sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each foot, measuring approximately 6.6 cm in length.
It was a small, bird-like predatory theropod covered in feathers, with flattened, backward-curving teeth that featured finer serrations on their rear margins.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'fire thief' because its fossil remains were discovered in southern France after a forest fire.

name: Qantassaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the "Wonthaggi" Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
Distinctive vertical ridges on the outer side of its leaf-shaped cheek teeth, along with a short, stocky face and a foreshortened jaw.
Approximately 1.8 meters long and 1 meter high, this small, fast-running herbivore fed on ferns and horsetails.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known to have lived in Antarctica's former polar region during the Cretaceous period, surviving cold, dark winters.

name: Qianjiangsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhengyang Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A fan-shaped prepubic process of the pubis with a length-to-height ratio of approximately 0.79, along with a coronoid process at a right angle to the dentary and seven fused sacral vertebrae.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length, possessing up to 30 tooth positions in the dentary with no more than five teeth per socket.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a transitional hadrosauroid from the Late Cretaceous of China that bridges the gap between early hadrosauroids and later duck-billed dinosaurs, indicating faunal connections with Mongolia.

name: Qianzhousaurus
hints: The holotype specimen was unearthed in the Nanxiong Formation in Ganzhou, southern China, during the summer of 2010.
It possessed a highly distinctive, elongated snout with narrow teeth, setting it apart from the deep-jawed, robust tyrannosaurids like T. rex.
This medium-sized predator measured approximately 6.3 meters (20.7 feet) in length and weighed between 750 and 757 kilograms, feeding on small, fast prey.
It lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous period, specifically the Maastrichtian age, just before the mass extinction event.
Often nicknamed the 'Pinocchio rex' due to its unusually long, narrow nose compared to other famous tyrannosaurs.

name: Qiaowanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
Deeply bifurcated neural spines and three fossae on the lateral sides of the cervical vertebrae
An estimated length of 12 to 20 meters and a weight of 6 to 13 tons, feeding on vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A large, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur discovered in the Gobi Desert region of China

name: Qijianglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Suining Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.

The rear neck vertebrae feature pneumatic openings (pneumatopores) located in the depression between the upper rib joint process and the neural spine, and the rear joint processes of the neck vertebrae have additional finger-shaped extensions that stiffen the neck horizontally.
This herbivorous sauropod reached an approximate length of fifteen meters and fed on vegetation, likely representing a large but immature individual.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Late Jurassic or Early Cretaceous period of China, known for its extremely elongated neck and distinctive pneumatic features in the vertebrae.

name: Qingxiusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a titanosaurian skeletal structure characterized by robust limb bones, specifically including humeri and sternal plates, along with a distinctive neural spine from a vertebra.
As a massive quadrupedal herbivore, it was a giant titanosaur sauropod that likely reached lengths of around 15 meters and weighed several tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known sauropod from the Late Cretaceous of China, often overshadowed by more famous titanosaur relatives due to its fragmentary fossil record.

name: Qiupalong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Qiupa Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

It possesses an arctometatarsalian condition where the third metatarsal is compressed between the second and fourth metatarsals near the ankle, and its toe claws curve downwards like those of more primitive ancestors.
It was a bipedal carnivore approximately 2.85 meters long and weighing around 63 kilograms, belonging to the ostrich-mimic dinosaur group.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the very few dinosaur genera known to have existed in both Asia and North America (Laramidia) during the Late Cretaceous.

name: Qiupanykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Qiupa Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A large quadrangular crest on the tibia and a strong keel on the underside of the sacral vertebrae
A tiny dinosaur weighing approximately 0.5 to 0.6 kg with a leg length of about 25 cm, likely an insectivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs, representing a late-surviving member of the alvarezsaurid family

name: Quaesitosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
An elongated, low skull with frontally located peg-like teeth and a high nasal opening positioned at the level of the eye socket
A large titanosaur sauropod estimated to be around 12 meters long that fed on vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A massive, four-legged herbivore with a long neck and tail, representing the classic 'lizard' body plan of giant dinosaurs

name: Quetecsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Lisandro Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A uniquely shaped humerus with a strongly S-shaped proximal border and an angular corner, along with an atlas bone featuring prominent anteroventral borders and enlarged posteroventral processes.
A massive titanosaurian sauropod that likely reached lengths of around 15 meters and was a herbivore, with fossil evidence recovered from Late Cretaceous red mudstones in Argentina.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the age of dinosaurs, belonging to the titanosaur group known for their enormous size and pillar-like legs.

name: Quilmesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A robust, boxy femur featuring a prominent mesiodistal crest and a tibia with a large, hatchet-shaped cnemial crest that ends in a downward-pointing spur.
An estimated length of 5.3 meters (17 feet), classifying it as one of the smallest derived members of its family, and it was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a South American abelisaurid theropod closely related to Carnotaurus, known primarily from leg bones found in the Upper Cretaceous of Argentina.

name: Qunkasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Villalba de la Sierra Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed the characteristic osteoderms or bony armor plates embedded in its skin, a defensive trait shared with its saltasaurid relatives.
This Late Cretaceous titanosaur was a massive herbivore, likely reaching lengths of around 10 meters and weighing several tons.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the age of dinosaurs, known for its bony skin armor.

name: Rahiolisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A short, heavily constructed skull with short teeth and a sagittal crest that widens into a triangular surface towards the front of the head.
An estimated length of 6.2 to 8 meters and a weight of around 2 metric tons, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the last non-avian dinosaurs to exist, discovered in the Lameta Formation of India during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Rahonavis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maevarano Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a raised sickle claw on the second toe, a hallmark feature of dromaeosaurids, yet its forearm bones display quill knobs typically associated with feathered birds.
This small predator measured approximately 70 centimeters in length and weighed between 0.45 and 2.27 kilograms, feeding on small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is frequently depicted in art and media as a bird-like dinosaur capable of flight, despite ongoing scientific debate over whether it was a true bird or a feathered dinosaur.

name: Rajasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lameta Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A low, stump-like horn located on the forehead, formed primarily by the nasal bone rather than the frontal bone.
An ambush predator measuring approximately 6.6 meters (22 ft) in length and weighing around 4 metric tons, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the first theropod dinosaur from India to be formally described with a partial skeleton, earning it the title 'King Lizard'.

name: Rapetosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Maevarano Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A long, narrow snout with nostrils positioned on the top of its skull, resembling that of a diplodocid.
An adult length of approximately 15 to 16.5 meters and a weight comparable to an elephant, feeding on leaves with small, pencil-like teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the only titanosaur dinosaur discovered with an almost perfectly intact skeleton, including a skull attached to the body.

name: Raptorex
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive tyrannosaurid body plan featuring a disproportionately large skull, powerful hind limbs, and notably tiny, two-fingered forearms
A carnivorous theropod that grew to be a massive apex predator, though the specific specimen described was a juvenile approximately 3 meters long
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its iconic silhouette as a giant, bipedal meat-eater with a massive head and vestigial arms, resembling the most famous dinosaur of all time

name: Ratchasimasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khok Kruat Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive elongated and flat lower jawbone (dentary) with a unique buccal shelf between the tooth row and the coronoid process.
A medium-sized herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A plant-eating dinosaur with a beak-like mouth and no teeth in its lower jaw, belonging to the iguanodontian group.

name: Rativates
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a unique pelvic structure where the left and right shafts of the ischia are entirely fused together on their back surfaces, lacking the vertical cleft seen in other ornithomimids.
It was a small, swift-running carnivore, measuring approximately half the size of the largest individuals of Struthiomimus, and lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Canada.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of 'ostrich mimic' dinosaur, known for its bird-like appearance, long legs built for speed, and a toothless beak, resembling the ancestors of modern flightless birds.

name: Rayososaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Candeleros Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive racquet-shaped scapula, a unique bone structure that helped classify it within its specific family.
This medium-sized sauropod weighed approximately 9,353 kilograms and was a herbivore that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known relative of more famous long-necked dinosaurs, often recognized by paleontologists for its specific shoulder blade morphology rather than popular culture imagery.

name: Rebbachisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Gara Sbaa Formation, approximately 93.9–106.3 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive, unusually tall, ridged back with a spine or sail lying atop its dorsal vertebrae.
It was a medium-sized sauropod measuring around 14 meters long and weighing up to 12 metric tons, acting as a generalist ground-height browser.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a sauropod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Africa, notable for the sail-like structure on its back.

name: Regaliceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the St. Mary River Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.

A single, midline epiparietal ossification that is offset from the plane of the frill towards the snout, combined with a prominent midline ridge on the parietal that merges with this median epiparietal.
An adult length of approximately 5 metres (16 ft) and a body mass of 2 metric tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its skull was nicknamed "Hellboy" due to the difficult excavation process and the presence of small postorbital horncores with resorption pits.

name: Rhabdodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marnes Rouges Inférieures / Grès à Reptiles Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses teeth with a distinctive fluted or grooved surface structure, which is the literal meaning of its genus name.
It was a robust, heavy-bodied herbivore measuring between 4 and 6 meters in length and weighing approximately 250 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is often recognized as a large, stocky, and primitive-looking relative of the more famous iguanodonts and hadrosaurs.

name: Rhinorex
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nelsen Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A prominent, bony, arching crest that extends from the top of the snout, resembling a duck's bill with a raised ridge.
A large, plant-eating herbivore that walked on both two and four legs, reaching approximately 9 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive duck-like face with a high, arched bony crest on the nose, often compared to a king's crown or a duck's bill.

name: Rhoetosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Walloon Coal Measures Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a primitive skeletal structure with robust hind limbs and a series of 16 consecutive tail vertebrae that were among the first fossils discovered.
This Late Jurassic sauropod reached an approximate length of 15 meters and weighed around 9 tonnes, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Late Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the most well-known and significant sauropod specimens ever found in Australia, representing a key piece of Gondwanan prehistoric history.

name: Rhomaleopakhus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kalazha Formation, approximately 143.1–152.2 million years ago.
A highly robust forelimb featuring a thickened humerus, ulna, and radius, which convergently evolved to support a flexed forearm posture for efficient feeding.
A large, quadrupedal herbivore that likely functioned as a low to medium browser, utilizing its sturdy arms to help shift its center of mass while moving between sparse food sources.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being a long-necked sauropod dinosaur with a name that literally translates to 'strong forearm' in Greek.

name: Riabininohadros
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A distinctive femur that lacks morphological equivalents within Iguanodontia, combined with a primitive astragalus structure.
A large, herbivorous dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Crimea.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the Styracosterna clade, originally misclassified as a hadrosaurid.

name: Richardoestesia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Milk River Formation, approximately 66.0–154.8 million years ago.
Its teeth are very small and feature an exceptionally high density of fine serrations, with five to six denticles per millimeter.
It was a small theropod dinosaur, likely measuring only a few feet in length, and may have had a piscivorous diet.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known primarily as a 'morphogenus,' meaning it is classified almost exclusively based on its distinctive fossilized teeth rather than complete body skeletons.

name: Rinchenia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A well-preserved, highly developed cassowary-like casque or dome on the skull that incorporates many bones free of the crest in related genera
A small-sized dinosaur estimated at 1.7 meters (5.6 feet) in length and weighing about 25 kilograms (55 pounds)
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the Oviraptoridae family, closely related to the famous feathered dinosaur Oviraptor

name: Rinconsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
Distinctive tail vertebrae featuring a rare and complex sequence of alternating shapes, including procoelous, amphicoelous, opisthocoelous, and biconvex types.
An unusually slender titanosaur estimated to be 11 meters long and weighing between 3 and 5 tonnes, which used pencil-chisel-like teeth to strip foliage.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its specific name literally translates to 'amazing tail,' highlighting the unique and bizarre structure of its spinal column that distinguishes it from other giant long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Riojasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Colorados Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possessed leaf-shaped, serrated teeth and a unique skeletal structure featuring four sacral vertebrae instead of the typical three found in other early sauropodomorphs.
This herbivore measured approximately 6.6 to 10 meters in length and weighed between 800 kilograms and 3 metric tons, with dense, massive leg bones suited for a heavy body.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is a Late Triassic dinosaur from Argentina that is notable for being the only known member of its specific family (Riojasauridae) to have lived in South America.

name: Riojavenatrix
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
A member of the Spinosauridae family, characterized by a long, crocodile-like snout and conical teeth adapted for catching fish.
An Early Cretaceous theropod that lived in what is now Spain, likely reaching a length of several meters and feeding primarily on aquatic prey.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a spinosaurid, it shares the iconic long, narrow snout and semi-aquatic appearance similar to its famous relative, the Baryonyx.

name: Riparovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
It belongs to the Spinosauridae family, characterized by a crocodile-like snout and specialized adaptations for catching aquatic prey.
This Early Cretaceous predator from Britain was a semi-aquatic hunter that primarily fed on fish and other small animals found near riverbanks.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a relative of the famous Baryonyx and Suchomimus, sharing the distinctive elongated, crocodilian head shape associated with fish-eating theropods.

name: Rocasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Allen Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Highly pneumatic skeleton with air-filled bones, characteristic of its saltasaurid relatives
A small sauropod measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length that was a plant-eater
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
A massive, long-necked quadrupedal herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period

name: Ruehleia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Trossingen Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
It possesses a long neck and tail, characteristic of early sauropodomorphs, with a skeleton that includes cervical, dorsal, and caudal vertebrae along with limb bones.
This Late Triassic herbivore lived approximately 216 to 208 million years ago in what is now central Germany.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is one of the earliest known relatives of the giant long-necked dinosaurs that would dominate the Jurassic period.

name: Rugops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Echkar Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A textured skull roof featuring a row of seven small depressions on the nasal bones and an armour-like papillate dermis on the snout.
A medium-sized carnivore measuring approximately 4.4 to 6 meters in length and weighing between 410 and 750 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the earliest known abelisaurid theropods from the Late Cretaceous period in Africa.

name: Ruixinia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
Distinctive pentagonal-shaped chevrons on the 20th caudal vertebra and a rodlike structure formed by fused tail vertebrae
A mid-sized sauropod approximately 12 meters long that likely fed on low-lying vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A long-necked, four-legged herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period of China

name: Rukwatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Galula Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It is a titanosaur, a type of sauropod characterized by a massive, barrel-shaped body and pillar-like legs.
It measured approximately 30 feet (9.1 meters) in length and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs discovered in Tanzania, specifically found near Lake Rukwa.

name: Ruyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Haoling Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A massive, pillar-like limb bone measuring 207 cm in length, characteristic of a giant quadrupedal herbivore.
An estimated length of up to 35 meters and a weight exceeding 50 tonnes, classifying it as a 'mega-sauropod' that fed on high-growing vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the largest dinosaurs ever discovered in Asia, representing the extreme size potential of Cretaceous titanosauriforms.

name: Saichania
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baruungoyot / Huiquanpu Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a heavy tail club used for defense and bulbous armor tiles covering its skull.
It was a robust, low-slung herbivore measuring 5 to 7 meters in length and weighing up to 2 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, tank-like dinosaur with a broad, flat body and short legs.

name: Saltasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lecho Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It was the first sauropod discovered to possess crocodile-like bony armor (osteoderms) embedded in its skin.
It was a relatively small, herbivorous titanosaur, measuring approximately 6 to 8.5 meters in length and weighing up to 2.5 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive body armor, which challenged the assumption that giant sauropods relied solely on their massive size for defense.

name: Saltriovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Saltrio Formation, approximately 192.9–199.5 million years ago.
A fragmentary skeleton including a wishbone, humeri, and a lower jaw, with no complete skull or crest preserved.
A large, bipedal carnivore estimated to be seven to eight meters long and weighing over one ton.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is considered the largest known theropod dinosaur to have lived before the Aalenian stage of the Middle Jurassic.

name: Sanjuansaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ischigualasto Formation, approximately 227.3–237.0 million years ago.
The pubis bone points toward the head (cranium), which is unusual for its group, and it possesses shelf-like transverse processes on its neck vertebrae.
It was a medium-sized carnivore, roughly comparable to a large dog or small horse in length, that hunted in the Late Triassic.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is an early, primitive meat-eating dinosaur from the time when dinosaurs were just beginning to appear on Earth.

name: Sanpasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ziliujing Formation, approximately 174.7–184.2 million years ago.

It possesses the elongated neck and tail characteristic of sauropods, with a skeletal structure including vertebrae, scapulae, and limb bones that distinguish it from the ornithopod classification it was initially assigned to.
This Early Jurassic herbivore was a large quadrupedal dinosaur native to what is now Sichuan, China, belonging to the basal gravisaurian lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a classic example of a long-necked, plant-eating dinosaur from the Jurassic period, often recognized by its massive body size and four-legged stance.

name: Santanaraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Romualdo Formation, approximately 113.2–119.6 million years ago.
A juvenile partial skeleton preserved with rare soft tissue, including skin scales, muscle fibers, and possibly blood vessels.
An estimated length of 1.5 meters and a weight of 15 kilograms, classified as a coelurosaurian theropod.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known tyrannosauroid relatives discovered in South America (Gondwana) during the Early Cretaceous.

name: Sanxiasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xintiangou Formation, approximately 170.9–184.2 million years ago.
A partial postcranial skeleton consisting of 55 bones, including cervical, dorsal, sacral, and caudal vertebrae, both forelimbs, hindlimbs, and parts of the feet.
An early ornithischian from the Middle Jurassic of China, estimated to be around 5 meters long, that was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as the oldest known neornithischian dinosaur discovered in Asia.

name: Sarahsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kayenta Formation, approximately 184.2–199.5 million years ago.
Possesses very large, powerful grasping hands with a prominent S-shaped deltopectoral crest on the upper arm bone, alongside a rod-like clavicle connecting the shoulder girdles.
A mid-sized basal sauropodomorph estimated at approximately 4 meters (13 feet) in length and 200 kilograms (440 pounds) in weight, likely an omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the few basal sauropodomorphs discovered in North America, notable for its primitive bipedal stance and robust build that hints at its evolutionary link to giant long-necked giants.

name: Sarcolestes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oxford Clay Formation, approximately 161.5–165.3 million years ago.
A partial left mandible containing a single entire tooth and two crown tips, lacking a predentary bone, which originally led to its misclassification as a meat-eating theropod.
A small, herbivorous armored dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic period of England, known only from fragmentary jaw remains.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.

It is one of the few armored dinosaurs known from the Middle Jurassic, an era when most ankylosaurs are known from later periods, and its classification has been debated for over a century due to its limited fossil record.

name: Sarcosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Broadford Beds / Blue Lias Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses a pelvis where the ilia are fused to the proximal portion of the pubis, a skeletal feature shared with other early neotheropods like Dilophosaurus.
This bipedal predator measured between 3.5 and 5 meters in length and weighed up to 140 kilograms, hunting small prey during the Early Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the earliest known Jurassic theropods and one of only a few described theropod genera from the lowermost Jurassic of the United Kingdom.

name: Sarmientosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, cable-shaped ossified tendon running along the side of its neck vertebrae, a feature never before documented in any fossil dinosaur.
This giant herbivore was a massive titanosaurian sauropod that lived in South America approximately 95 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the rarest titanosaurs known to science because its discovery included an almost complete skull, which is exceptionally uncommon for this group of long-necked dinosaurs.

name: Sasayamagnomus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ohyamashimo Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A member of the neoceratopsian group, characterized by a beaked mouth and herbivorous dentition typical of early horned dinosaurs.
An extremely small dinosaur, with the holotype individual measuring only about 80 centimeters (2.6 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its scientific name translates to 'gnome from Sasayama,' reflecting its diminutive size and the location where its fossils were discovered in Japan.

name: Saurolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Horseshoe Canyon Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A solid, spike-like crest made primarily of nasal bones that projects upward and backward from the skull at a 45-degree angle.
A large herbivore measuring between 8 to 13 meters in length and weighing up to 11 tonnes, capable of moving on both two and four legs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia, North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very few dinosaur genera known to have lived on multiple continents, specifically North America and Asia, at the same time.

name: Sauropelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly / Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
A heavily built quadrupedal herbivore with a wide body, a flat and thick skull roof, and an extensive covering of bony armor including large, pointed spines projecting from its neck.
It measured approximately 5.2 to 6 meters in length and weighed between 1.5 and 2 metric tons, feeding on plant material using leaf-shaped teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'lizard shield,' referring to the distinctive bony plates and spines that covered its body for protection.

name: Sauroplites
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
Its sacral shield features unique rosettes with a large central osteoderm surrounded by a single ring of smaller scutes.
It was a herbivorous ankylosaurian dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of China, equipped with extensive body armor including flat central osteoderms and asymmetric low-keeled oval plates.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, quadrupedal herbivore known for its distinctive 'shield bearer' body plating.

name: Sauroposeidon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly / Antlers Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
Its neck vertebrae are the longest known in any dinosaur, reaching up to 1.4 meters in length and featuring a honeycombed internal structure with tiny air cells.
It was a massive plant-eater estimated to be 27–34 meters long and weighing 40–60 tons, with an extended neck height of up to 18 meters.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It was initially misidentified as petrified wood because its bones were so large, and its name references the Greek god of earthquakes due to the ground-shaking weight of its steps.

name: Saurornithoides
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
A large, curved, retractable sickle-shaped claw on the second toe of each foot
A small, agile predator approximately 2 meters long that hunted small mammals and reptiles
This dinosaur lived in Asia, North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its exceptionally large eye sockets indicating excellent night vision and stereoscopic sight

name: Saurornitholestes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A long, curving, blade-like sickle claw on the second toe
A small, bipedal carnivore measuring about 1.3 to 1.8 meters long and weighing between 5 and 22.5 kg
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Asia/Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its close resemblance to Velociraptor, including large fanglike teeth and similar body plan

name: Savannasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Winton Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed unusually wide hips, measuring over 1.1 meters at their widest point, which helped distribute its weight on muddy floodplains.
It was a medium-sized sauropod measuring approximately 15 meters in length and weighing around 20 tonnes, feeding on vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the most complete sauropod specimen ever discovered in Australia.

name: Scansoriopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possessed an unusually elongated third finger on its hand, which is the longest digit on the limb, potentially supporting a membranous wing structure similar to a bat.
This tiny, sparrow-sized creature was likely an insectivore or omnivore and lived during the Jurassic period, with adults possibly growing slightly larger than the juvenile specimen found.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.

It is famous for its tree-climbing adaptations, including a reversed big toe for grasping branches and a long, stiffened tail that may have acted as a prop while climbing, resembling a modern woodpecker.

name: Scelidosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Charmouth Mudstone / Waterloo Mudstone Formation, approximately 174.7–205.7 million years ago.
It possesses long horizontal rows of keeled oval scutes that form a protective armor along its neck, back, and tail.
This Early Jurassic herbivore was a medium-sized quadruped, measuring approximately 4 meters in length and weighing around 270 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the oldest and most primitive members of the armoured dinosaur group Thyreophora.

name: Schleitheimia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Klettgau Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A broad, rounded ridge on the iliac blade that ends in a large, rounded expansion, along with a very robust fourth trochanter on the hip bone.
A large, quadrupedal plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Triassic period, belonging to the lineage that would eventually lead to giant long-necked sauropods.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Triassic.
It is a lesser-known early relative of the giant long-necked dinosaurs, often confused with Plateosaurus due to its similar basic body plan and historical classification.

name: Scipionyx
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
A distinctive feature is the exceptional preservation of petrified soft tissues and internal organs, including muscles and intestines, which is extremely rare in the fossil record.
It was a small, bipedal predator approximately 46 centimeters long that likely ate lizards and fish, though it is known only from a juvenile specimen.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is famous for being the first dinosaur discovered in Italy and for the unique visibility of its internal anatomy in museum displays.

name: Sciurumimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Torleite Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
A distinctive bushy tail that resembles the tail of a modern tree squirrel, along with preserved filamentous plumage on the tail base and body.
A very small, juvenile carnivore with a proportionally large skull and short forelimbs, comparable in size to a modern bird or small mammal.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the earliest known dinosaurs to possess feather-like filaments, challenging the traditional image of dinosaurs as purely scaly reptiles.

name: Scolosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A heavy, bony armor plating covering the back and neck, featuring a distinctive cervical collar made of fused, pointed osteoderms.
A quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 5.6 meters (18 feet) in length and weighing around 2.2 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its appearance as a low-slung, heavily armored tank-like dinosaur with a clubbed tail, often serving as the default reconstruction for similar ankylosaurs.

name: Scutellosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kayenta Formation, approximately 184.2–201.4 million years ago.
It possessed over 300 small, bony plates called osteoderms arranged in parallel rows along its neck, back, and tail.
It was a very small, lightly-built herbivore measuring approximately 1.3 meters (4.3 feet) in length and weighing around 3 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the earliest and most primitive examples of an armored dinosaur, representing the basal form of the Thyreophora group.

name: Secernosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is a hadrosaurid, characterized by a duck-billed snout and a complex dental battery for grinding tough vegetation.
It was a relatively small herbivore, measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length, and lived in the floodplains of Late Cretaceous Patagonia.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is notable for being one of the very few duck-billed dinosaurs known to have lived in South America, having migrated from North America.

name: Sefapanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elliot Formation, approximately 199.5–227.3 million years ago.
A distinctive cross-shaped astragalus (talus) bone in its ankle.
An early herbivorous sauropodomorph from the Late Triassic or Early Jurassic, representing a transitional form between bipedal ancestors and giant quadrupedal sauropods.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Triassic.
Its generic name is derived from the Sesotho word for 'cross', referring to the unique shape of its ankle bone.

name: Segisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Navajo Sandstone Formation, approximately 174.7–192.9 million years ago.
It possesses a bird-like furcula (wishbone) and solid limb bones, distinguishing it from the hollow-boned Coelophysis.
It was a tiny, bipedal predator measuring only 1 meter long and weighing 4-7 kilograms, likely feeding on insects.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is the only dinosaur species ever discovered in Tsegi Canyon, Arizona, and its fossil was found in a posture resembling a sitting hen.

name: Segnosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire / Jobu Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
It possesses three-fingered forelimbs equipped with massive claws and a backward-pointing pubic bone, a pelvic configuration typically associated with birds.
This herbivorous theropod measured approximately 6 to 7 meters in length and weighed around 1.3 tons, utilizing a beak and long neck for browsing vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is widely recognized as one of the few bipedal dinosaurs that were primarily plant-eaters, contrasting sharply with the carnivorous nature of most other theropods.

name: Seitaad
hints: Fossils recovered from the Navajo Sandstone Formation, approximately 174.7–192.9 million years ago.
It is known from an articulated partial skeleton that notably lacks the head, neck, and tail.
This Early Jurassic sauropodomorph was a relatively small herbivore, likely measuring around two to three meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its name is derived from a Navajo mythological sand monster that buried its victims in dunes, reflecting the fossil's discovery in sandstone deposits.

name: Sektensaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lago Colhué Huapi Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.

It is an ornithopod dinosaur, meaning it was a bird-hipped, likely bipedal or facultatively bipedal herbivore with a beak, known from postcranial elements like vertebrae, limb bones, and parts of the pelvis.
This small dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Patagonia, Argentina, specifically in the Lago Colué Huapí Formation near Antarctica.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It was discovered on an ephemeral island that emerged during periods of low lake levels, a fact reflected in its name which translates to 'island lizard'.

name: Serendipaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the "Wonthaggi" Formation, approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
A single lower arm bone (ulna) that is approximately sixteen centimeters long and has a flattened shaft, with classification debates ranging from horned dinosaur to armored dinosaur.
A small herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period of Australia, known only from fragmentary remains so its exact size and weight remain uncertain.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name honors the science fiction author Arthur C. Clarke, who was a close friend of the paleontologists who named it.

name: Serikornis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a tuberculous coracoid that is elongated to form a distinct crest, along with very large anterior maxillary teeth that are about twice as long as the others.
It is a small, terrestrial dinosaur that was unlikely to be a flier, possessing symmetrical, barbule-free remiges on its forelimbs and elongated feathers on its hind limbs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its name translates to 'Ge Sun's silk bird', referencing the striking resemblance of its delicate hindlimb filaments to the plumage of the modern Silky breed of domestic chicken.

name: Shamosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dzunbain Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses two cervical halfrings with six segments each to protect its neck, along with keeled osteoderms and flat oval scutes covering its body.
It was a medium-sized herbivore, estimated to be between 5 and 7 meters long and weighing around 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a heavily armored, low-slung dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period known for its bony skin plates and short, rounded horns on the back of its skull.

name: Shanag
hints: Fossils recovered from the Öösh Formation, approximately 100.5–143.1 million years ago.
It possessed sharp, curved claws on its middle toes, a feature characteristic of dromaeosaurids that were likely used to grip and pin down prey.
This small predator measured approximately 1.5 meters in length and weighed around 5 kilograms, feeding on small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a feathered dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Mongolia, closely related to the famous four-winged Microraptor.

name: Shantungosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya / Huiquanpu Formation, approximately 66.0–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a toothless beak and a jaw structure packed with approximately 1,500 tiny chewing teeth, along with a large nasal opening that may have been covered by an inflatable skin flap.
It was the largest known hadrosaur, reaching lengths of 15 to 17 meters and weighing between 13 and 16 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the largest member of the duck-billed dinosaur family, significantly outsizeing its famous North American relatives like Edmontosaurus.

name: Shanyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shanyang Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is a theropod dinosaur known only from fragmentary remains, including a partial sacrum, scapula, humeri, femur, tibia, and foot bones, with hollow bones suggesting it belongs to the Coelurosauria.
This bipedal carnivore lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now Shaanxi, China, and is estimated to have been a medium-sized predator.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a poorly understood dinosaur where the specific family remains unknown due to the limited fossil material available for study.

name: Shaochilong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Miaogou Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A deep sagittal crest on the frontal bone and a reduced, nearly absent maxillary antorbital fossa.
An estimated length of 5 to 6 meters and a weight of approximately 500 kilograms, identifying it as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its classification as a carcharodontosaurid, a family of giant carnivorous dinosaurs, despite being unusually small for the group.

name: Shenzhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A straight ischial shaft and an ilium with an acuminate posterior end, along with a first metacarpal that is only half the length of the second metacarpal.
A small theropod dinosaur with a skull measuring approximately 185 mm, possessing reduced dentition restricted to the front of the jaw, and likely consuming a varied diet supplemented by gastroliths.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the earliest known members of the ostrich-mimic dinosaur group, characterized by its bird-like skeleton and lack of feathers compared to later relatives.

name: Shidaisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Chuanjie Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
Its second neck vertebra features a tall, sharply pointing epipophysis with a thin lamina between it and the neural spine that is broader than in other theropods.
It was a medium-sized predator measuring between 6 and 7.1 meters long and weighing up to 950 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a basal member of the allosauroid group, closely resembling its relative Sinraptor in skull structure and braincase features.

name: Shingopana
hints: Fossils recovered from the Galula Formation, approximately 72.2–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed cervical vertebrae with a distinctive bulbous expansion, likely serving to strengthen its neck.
It was a quadrupedal sauropod measuring approximately 8 meters in length, making it smaller than the average member of its group.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a titanosaurian dinosaur discovered in Tanzania, closely related to the South American Aeolosaurini family.

name: Shishugounykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
A highly specialized, reduced forelimb with a single, massive, spade-like claw on the hand, while the other digits are vestigial or absent.
A small, bipedal dinosaur, likely measuring around one meter in length, that was probably an insectivore or termite-eater.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its bizarre, mole-like front limbs and giant digging claw, which look completely unlike the typical arms of most other dinosaurs.

name: Shixinggia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pingling Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It belongs to a group of feathered theropods known for having beaks and often possessing distinctive head crests or bony knobs, despite this specific specimen lacking a skull.
This Late Cretaceous dinosaur was a relatively small theropod, likely measuring around 2 meters in length, and was part of the Caenagnathoidea superfamily.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a rare Chinese oviraptorosaurian named after Shixing County, distinguished by its post-cranial remains which indicate it was a more derived member of its lineage than Nomingia.

name: Shri (genus)
hints: Fossils have been recovered from the Barun Goyot and Djadokhta formations in Mongolia, dating to the Late Cretaceous period approximately 75 to 84 million years ago.
This dinosaur possessed a notably enlarged, sickle-shaped claw on the first digit of its hand, a feature that distinguishes it from many of its close relatives.
It was a small carnivore, likely measuring around one meter in length, with a stouter snout and stronger bite force compared to the famous Velociraptor.
It lived in the arid desert environments of what is now Mongolia during the Late Cretaceous epoch.
It is a close relative of Velociraptor, often recognized by fans of dinosaur paleontology for its similar body plan and predatory nature.

name: Shuangbaisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
A pair of thin, midline crests on the skull that uniquely extend backwards over the level of the eyes, formed primarily by the frontal bone.
A large theropod with a robust skull approximately 54 centimeters long, likely a carnivore from the Early Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being one of the very few dinosaurs known to possess paired cranial crests, similar in concept to the famous frills or crests of Dilophosaurus.

name: Shuangmiaosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sunjiawan Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
It is an ornithopod dinosaur with a beaked mouth and specialized jaw bones, including a maxilla and dentary, characteristic of iguanodontians.
It was a large herbivore, estimated to reach 7.5 meters in length and weigh around 2.5 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China, often grouped with relatives like Iguanodon and hadrosaurs.

name: Shunosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–174.7 million years ago.
A tail club equipped with two successive spikes formed by cone-shaped osteoderms, which was likely used as a defensive weapon against predators.
A relatively small sauropod measuring approximately 9.5 meters in length and weighing around 3 metric tons, which was a low-browsing herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being one of the few sauropods known to possess a tail club, a feature typically associated with stegosaurs.

name: Shuvuuia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed uniquely short, powerfully built forelimbs with a massively enlarged thumb, which were specialized for digging.
It was a tiny dinosaur measuring about 1 meter in length and weighing only 3.5 kilograms, likely feeding on insects and small mammals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is often nicknamed the 'desert bird' due to its bird-like appearance and the presence of beta-keratin feathers.

name: Siamodon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khok Kruat Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive maxilla shaped like an isosceles triangle with a strong longitudinal bulge on the medial surface and teeth featuring a prominent median primary ridge.
An iguanodontian ornithopod from the Early Cretaceous of Thailand, likely a herbivore, with a maxilla measuring 230 millimeters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'Siam tooth,' highlighting the specific dental and jawbone features that define this early Asian relative of iguanodons.

name: Siamotyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sao Khua Formation, approximately 121.4–125.8 million years ago.
Possession of two vertical ridges on the ilium, a unique skeletal trait among theropods
A large carnivore estimated to range from 6 to 10 meters in length and weighing between 500 kg and 1.75 tonnes
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Siamese tyrant', reflecting an initial classification as a tyrannosaurid relative from Thailand

name: Siamraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khok Kruat Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive straight lower edge on the jugal bone and a deep oval excavation on the rear of the surangular bone with four rear foramina.
A large carnivorous theropod from the Early Cretaceous period of Southeast Asia, classified within the carcharodontosaurian lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the first definitive named carcharodontosaurian species discovered in Southeast Asia.

name: Siats
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
Broad neural spines on the dorsal vertebrae and a subtriangular cross section of the distal caudal vertebrae
A massive carnivore estimated at roughly 3.9 metric tons and comparable in size to Allosaurus despite being skeletally immature
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name is derived from a man-eating monster in Ute mythology

name: Sibirotitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ilek Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a unique, particularly high hyposphene ridge on its dorsal vertebrae and has only five sacral vertebrae, which is an evolutionary reversal for its group.
It was a massive herbivorous sauropod estimated to weigh around 20 tonnes, comparable in size to Apatosaurus.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Siberia.

name: Sidersaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
Its caudal vertebrae possess haemal arches with projections that form a distinctive star-like shape, a feature that inspired its generic name.
This massive herbivore measured approximately 18 to 20 meters in length and weighed around 15 tons, making it one of the largest known members of the rebbachisaurid family.
