This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of Argentina, characterized by its immense size relative to other sauropods of its specific subgroup.

name: Sierraceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hall Lake Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A long, robust cheek horn on the jugal bone and short, massive brow horns, along with a long frill featuring large holes separated by an oval-shaped midline bar.
A medium-sized herbivore that likely reached lengths of around 5 meters and weighed approximately 1 ton.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as a horned dinosaur with a distinctive bony frill and prominent facial horns.

name: Sigilmassasaurus
hints: Fossils were recovered from the Kem Kem Formation in the Tafilalt Oasis region of Morocco, dating to the Cenomanian stage of the Late Cretaceous, approximately 100 to 94 million years ago.
The genus is defined by distinctive posterior neck vertebrae that are unusually wide from side to side, being about 50% wider than they are long from front to back.
It was a moderately built, bipedal carnivore that likely had semiaquatic habits and a partially fish-based diet, with estimates suggesting it could grow larger than its close relative Spinosaurus.
This dinosaur lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now northern Africa, coexisting with other large theropods in the same ecosystem.
It is a controversial spinosaurid whose validity is debated, with many scientists considering its fossils to be synonymous with the closely related Spinosaurus aegyptiacus.

name: Silutitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shengjinkou Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
Six cervical vertebrae with all neural spines intact were the primary fossils used to define the species.
This Early Cretaceous sauropod from China was a large, long-necked herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known member of the euhelopodid group, often overshadowed by more famous Chinese dinosaurs like Mamenchisaurus or Diplodocus-like forms in popular media.

name: Silvisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dakota Formation, approximately 66.0–106.3 million years ago.
A primitive nodosaurid ankylosaur featuring a toothed beak rather than a completely toothless one, along with bony spines on its shoulders and tail.
An herbivore approximately 4 meters (13 feet) in length with a skull measuring 33 centimeters long.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
The official state land fossil of Kansas, discovered in the 1950s in the Dakota Formation.

name: Similicaudipteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed a dagger-shaped pygostyle, which is the fused bone at the end of the tail where long feathers anchor, a feature rare among non-avian dinosaurs.
It was a small, lightweight creature estimated to be about one meter long and weighing seven kilograms, likely feeding on plants or small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a feathered dinosaur from the Cretaceous period that looks like a bird with a short tail and a beak, often depicted with a fan of tail feathers.

name: Sinankylosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A partial right ilium bone, originally described as belonging to an armored dinosaur but later deemed too fragmentary to confirm its classification.
It is a dubious genus from the Late Cretaceous period of China, with no confirmed body size or diet due to the lack of complete skeletal remains.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a 'nomen dubium' (dubious name) in paleontology, meaning it is a fossil so incomplete that scientists cannot reliably identify what the animal actually looked like.

name: Sinocalliopteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive anatomical feature is its exceptionally elongated hands, which are as long as its upper arms combined, along with simple filamentous protofeathers covering the hips and thighs.

It was a carnivorous bipedal predator measuring approximately 2.37 meters (7.8 feet) in length and weighing around 20 kilograms, with evidence of it consuming other small dinosaurs like dromaeosaurids.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is famous for being a 'giant' member of a group typically known for being very small, and for preserving a rare fossilized meal of a smaller dinosaur inside its stomach cavity.

name: Sinocephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ulansuhai Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive heart-shaped profile when viewed from above, created by a deep embayment in the middle of the bony shelf on the back of the skull.
A small, bipedal herbivore with a thickened skull dome estimated to be around 7.5 centimeters thick.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few known pachycephalosaurs discovered in Asia, specifically from the Cretaceous period in Inner Mongolia, China.

name: Sinoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It features a short, hooked nasal horn, lacks brow horns, and possesses a short neck frill adorned with a crown-like series of forward-curving hornlets and unique low knobs on top.
It was a quadrupedal herbivore that grew up to 5 metres (16 ft) in length and weighed approximately 2 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the only known ceratopsid dinosaur discovered in Asia, making it a unique outlier among its famous North American relatives.

name: Sinornithoides
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhonggou / Ejinhoro Formation, approximately 100.5–137.1 million years ago.
It possesses a large, sickle-shaped claw on its second toe and raptorial hands, along with closely spaced, non-serrated teeth in its elongated skull.
It was a small theropod measuring approximately 1 to 1.1 meters in length and weighing around 2.5 kilograms, likely feeding on invertebrates and small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Troodontidae family, a group of small, bird-like dinosaurs known for having some of the highest brain-to-body size ratios among non-avian dinosaurs.

name: Sinornithomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ulansuhai Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.

Possession of a quadrate bone with a unique depression containing a smaller opening divided by a vertical sheet of bone, and a depression on the lateral side of the posterior process of the parietal bone
A small ornithomimid measuring approximately 2.5 meters in length and weighing around 91 kilograms, which was herbivorous as evidenced by the presence of gastroliths in its stomach area
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the most completely known members of its group due to the discovery of numerous nearly intact skeletons found in close association, suggesting gregarious behavior

name: Sinornithosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive sickle-shaped toe claw, a hallmark feature of the dromaeosaurid family.
It was a very small predator, measuring approximately 1.2 meters in length and weighing only 3 to 5 kilograms, with a diet likely consisting of small animals.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is famous for being one of the earliest feathered dinosaurs discovered, with fossilized impressions showing it was covered in downy and vaned feathers similar to modern birds.

name: Sinosauropteryx
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses an unusually long tail relative to its body and short arms, with fossil evidence showing a distinct banded pattern along the tail.
This small theropod measured approximately 1.07 meters in length and weighed around 0.55 kilograms, indicating it was a lightweight, carnivorous hunter.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is historically significant as the first non-avian dinosaur discovered with clear evidence of filament-like feathers and preserved coloration patterns.

name: Sinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A pair of large, axe-shaped cranial crests formed from the nasal and lacrimal bones that sit above the antorbital fenestra.
A large theropod estimated to be around 5.85 meters (19.2 feet) long and weighing up to 895 kilograms, with laterally compressed, backward-curving teeth featuring fine serrations on both edges.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Its striking double-crested head, which superficially resembles the iconic crests of Dilophosaurus.

name: Sinotyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive concave dorsal margin on the maxilla bone, contrasting with the convex shape found in later tyrannosaurids, and potentially possessing a tall nasal crest.
A massive predator measuring approximately 9 to 10 meters (30–33 ft) in length and weighing around 2.5 metric tons, classified as a large basal tyrannosauroid.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being one of the largest known members of the early tyrannosaur family tree, representing a surprisingly giant exception among Early Cretaceous theropods.

name: Sinovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A straight, vertically oriented front edge of the antorbital fenestra and a T-shaped cross-section in the surangular bone of the lower jaw
Approximately one meter in length and weighing around 2.5 kilograms, this small, bird-like carnivore possessed closely spaced teeth and raptorial hands
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a small, chicken-sized theropod from the Early Cretaceous of China, belonging to the troodontid family known for their large brains and keen senses

name: Sinraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou / Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–168.2 million years ago.
It possesses blade-like teeth similar to those of Allosaurus, which were used to inflict massive, fatal wounds on prey.
This large theropod measured approximately 7.6 to 8.8 meters (25 to 29 feet) in length and weighed around 1.3 metric tons, preying on medium-sized dinosaurs like stegosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a Jurassic-era carnivore from China whose name translates to 'Chinese Robber,' yet it is not closely related to the feathered dromaeosaurids often called raptors in popular culture.

name: Sinusonasus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A distinctive undulating or wave-like profile of the nasal bones on the snout, combined with large, non-serrated front teeth.
A small theropod measuring approximately one meter in length and weighing around 2.5 kilograms, likely an insectivore or small carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
The presence of a retractable sickle claw on the second toe, a feature famously associated with dromaeosaurids but also found in this troodontid.

name: Sirindhorna
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khok Kruat Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a well-preserved skull, which is rare for ornithopods from Southeast Asia, including distinct jaw bones like the premaxilla and dentary.
It was a herbivorous hadrosauroid that lived during the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 125 to 112 million years ago.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is the first ornithopod from Southeast Asia to be described with such a complete skull, named in honor of Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn.

name: Skorpiovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive 'keyhole' shaped eye socket formed by a large postorbital bone that almost touched the lacrimal on the opposite side.
It was a large predator estimated to reach 6–6.2 meters in length and weigh nearly 900 kilograms, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It had a very short, blunt skull covered in rugose bone texture and large facial scales, giving it a heavily armored and wrinkled appearance.

name: Smitanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 145.1–152.2 million years ago.
It is a dicraeosaurid sauropod, a subgroup of long-necked dinosaurs characterized by having short, forked neural spines on their neck vertebrae.
This Late Jurassic herbivore from the Morrison Formation was a relatively small sauropod compared to giants like Brachiosaurus, likely measuring around 8 to 9 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.

It is one of the few known sauropods to have lived in North America during the Late Jurassic, a time and place where these long-necked giants were otherwise rare compared to their presence in Gondwana.

name: Sonidosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Iren Dabasu Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A small titanosaur sauropod with possible saltasaurid affinities, characterized by a posterior centrodiapophyseal lamina on its dorsal vertebrae.
Approximately 9 meters (30 feet) long, this herbivorous dinosaur inhabited the Late Cretaceous of what is now Inner Mongolia.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the only known sauropod from the Iren Dabasu Fauna, sharing its habitat with giants like Gigantoraptor and Alectrosaurus.

name: Sonorasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Turney Ranch Formation, approximately 93.9–106.3 million years ago.
It is a massive, long-necked herbivore with a body structure similar to a giraffe, featuring extremely long front legs that make its shoulders significantly higher than its hips.
This dinosaur reached approximately 15 meters in length and 8 meters in height, weighing around 10 metric tons, and sustained itself by browsing on high vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It was officially designated as the state dinosaur of Arizona in 2018.

name: Soriatitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Golmayo Formation, approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
It possesses anteriorly deflected anterior-middle caudal neural spines, a distinctive feature shared with Cedarosaurus and Tastavinsaurus.
This Early Cretaceous brachiosaurid sauropod measured approximately 13 to 14 meters in length and weighed around 8 tons, feeding on conifer trees.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the only known brachiosaurid described from the Early Cretaceous period of Europe.

name: Spectrovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quiricó Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.

A small, basal abelisaurid theropod with a distinctive skull featuring vertically oriented grooves on the maxilla, a nuchal crest with a thin smooth dorsal margin, and a transversely concave nasal bone.
An estimated body length of 1.42 to 2.2 meters (4.7 to 7.2 feet), classifying it as a small predator that likely hunted small prey with a relatively weak bite force compared to later abelisaurids.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a 'ghost hunter' from the early Cretaceous of Brazil, known for its unexpectedly complete skeleton found beneath the holotype of another dinosaur, Tapuiasaurus.

name: Sphaerotholus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Kirtland Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A thick, rounded, bone-dome skull roof bordered by a single row of small bony nodes on the back edge.
Approximately 2 meters long and weighing around 40 kilograms, it was a small, bipedal herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, thick, domed skull, which resembles a hard helmet or a ball-shaped roof.

name: Spiclypeus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A distinctive frill edge featuring six fused, spike-like horn ossifications (epiparietals) that form a continuous bone sheath, with the first two pairs curling downward and the third pair pointing backward.
An estimated length of 4.5 to 6 meters and a weight of three to four tonnes, classified as a plant-eating chasmosaurine ceratopsian.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name translates to 'spike shield,' directly referencing the unique, heavily ornamented neck frill covered in large, fused spikes.

name: Spicomellus
hints: Fossils recovered from the El Mers III Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
Its osteoderms are uniquely fused directly to the ribs, a trait not found in any other vertebrate, and it possesses extremely elongated spikes around the neck and pelvis.
It is an early ankylosaurian from the Middle Jurassic of Morocco, likely possessing a tail weapon, though its exact size remains uncertain due to fragmentary remains.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is famous for being the oldest known definitive ankylosaur and for having spikes that grow directly out of the bones rather than being embedded in the skin.

name: Spinophorosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Irhazer II Formation, approximately 165.3–170.9 million years ago.
Spatulate (spoon-shaped) teeth with large, spaced denticles at the top of the crown, and a tail rear section that was rather rigid due to long and overlapping chevron bones.
Approximately 13 to 14 meters long, standing about 4 meters tall at the shoulder, weighing around 7 metric tons, and classified as a plant-eating sauropod.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It was the first sauropod dinosaur to have its skeleton digitally replicated and 3D printed.

name: Spinops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive spine-like projection located on the face, derived from the Latin word for spine and Greek for face.
A medium-sized herbivore measuring approximately 4.5 meters (15 ft) in length and weighing around 1.3 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
A horned dinosaur with a large bony frill, known for its unique facial ornamentation that distinguishes it from other centrosaurines.

name: Spinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Gara Sbaa / Ifezouane Formation, approximately 72.2–143.1 million years ago.
A distinctive sail-like structure formed by extremely long neural spines extending from its vertebrae along its back
A massive carnivore reaching approximately 14 meters in length and weighing around 7.4 tons, with a crocodile-like skull and conical teeth
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique paddle-like tail and semi-aquatic lifestyle, making it the only known dinosaur adapted for swimming and hunting in water

name: Spinostropheus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Echkar / Tiourarén Formation, approximately 93.9–161.5 million years ago.
Prominent, dorso-ventrally flattened epipophyseal processes on its cervical vertebrae
A relatively small theropod measuring between 4 to 6.2 meters in length and weighing around 200 kilograms, which was a carnivore
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
A Middle Jurassic predator from the Niger desert whose name literally translates to 'spine vertebra'

name: Staurikosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Santa Maria Formation, approximately 205.7–237.0 million years ago.
It possesses only two sacral vertebrae connecting the pelvis to the spine, a distinctly primitive trait shared with few other early dinosaurs.
It was a small, agile bipedal predator measuring approximately 2.1 to 2.25 meters in length and weighing around 12 kilograms, with a diet consisting of meat sliced by serrated, curved teeth.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs, discovered in Brazil and named after the Southern Cross star constellation visible in the Southern Hemisphere.

name: Stegoceras
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River / Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A thick, broad, and relatively smooth dome on the top of the skull, along with a thick ridge over the eyes and small horns on the back shelf of the skull.
A small, bipedal dinosaur approximately 2 to 2.5 meters long and weighing between 10 to 40 kilograms, likely herbivorous with small serrated teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive, thick, rounded skull dome, which is the most recognizable feature of this group of dome-headed dinosaurs.

name: Stegopelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Frontier Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A fused section of armor located over the pelvis (sacrum) and shoulder spines that may have been split
A slow-moving, quadrupedal herbivore that fed low to the ground
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being an armored dinosaur with a 'roofed shield' appearance, often compared to Glyptodon due to its fused armor plates

name: Stegosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Alcobaça Formation, approximately 93.9–161.5 million years ago.
A row of large, kite-shaped bony plates running along its back and four sharp spikes at the end of its tail.
A large, heavy, four-legged herbivore that could grow up to 25 feet long and weigh over 5 tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Its iconic silhouette featuring upright back plates and a spiked tail, making it one of the most recognizable dinosaurs.

name: Stegouros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dorotea Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A unique tail weapon consisting of seven pairs of large, fused osteoderms that form a flat, macuahuitl-like structure rather than a bony club
A very small body size of approximately 1.8 to 2 meters in length, classified as a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being the only known dinosaur with a tail weapon made of fused bony plates instead of a club or spikes

name: Stellasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Two Medicine Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A very long, erect, and recurved nasal horn that is compressed laterally, combined with long, straight spines on the neck frill.
A medium-sized herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period of Montana.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name means 'star lizard' because of the distinctive star-like shape of the ornamentation on its head.

name: Stenonychosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park / Oldman Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive enlarged claw on the second toe, a feature characteristic of early paravians
A small, agile bipedal carnivore with a relatively large brain, typical of troodontid dinosaurs
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
The subject of Dale Russell's famous 1982 speculation about an intelligent, humanoid descendant known as the 'dinosauroid'

name: Stenopelix
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bückeberg Formation, approximately 137.1–139.1 million years ago.
A distinctive kink in the middle of the ischium shaft and an ilium that tapers to a rounded point
A small herbivore measuring approximately 1.4 meters in length and weighing around 10 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few dinosaurs known exclusively from a partial skeleton that completely lacks a skull

name: Stokesosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A swollen rim around the articular surface of the pubic peduncle on its hip bone
A small, bipedal predator measuring approximately 2.5 to 4 meters long and weighing around 60 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
An early, primitive ancestor of the giant T-Rex lineage from the Late Jurassic period

name: Streptospondylus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 154.8–165.3 million years ago.
Its vertebrae feature a unique bifurcation, or split, in the hypapophyses (processes extending from the lower surface of the spine).
It was a medium-sized predator measuring approximately 6 to 7 meters long and weighing around 500 kilograms, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is historically significant as the first dinosaur remains ever scientifically described by Georges Cuvier in 1808, although he initially misidentified them as belonging to a crocodile.

name: Struthiomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Oldman / Kirtland Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A toothless, concave beak and long, slender arms with three roughly equal-length fingers that resemble a sloth's limbs.
Approximately 4.3 to 4.8 meters long, weighing between 150 and 350 kilograms, and likely an opportunistic omnivore that ate plants, insects, and small animals.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'ostrich mimic' due to its long legs, bipedal stance, and overall resemblance to modern ostriches.

name: Struthiosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Grünbach / Sînpetru Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possessed a body covered in bony armor plates known as osteoderms, characteristic of the nodosaurid family.
It was a small herbivore, measuring approximately 2 to 3 meters in length and weighing between 300 and 400 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its skull features a unique, bird-like braincase that was so distinct it initially led scientists to mistakenly classify it as a giant lizard or even a bird.

name: Styracosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Dinosaur Park Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It features four to six long parietal spikes extending from its neck frill, a smaller jugal horn on each cheek, and a single prominent horn protruding from its nose.
It was a bulky herbivore measuring 5 to 5.5 meters in length and weighing between 1.8 and 2.7 metric tons, with a short tail and four short legs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its most iconic visual trait is the dramatic array of long, sharp spikes radiating from the back of its head, giving it a crown-like appearance.

name: Suchomimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Elrhaz Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
An elongated, low, and narrow skull resembling that of a crocodile, featuring a flared rosette-shaped snout tip and numerous finely serrated conical teeth.
A large theropod measuring approximately 9.5 to 11 meters in length and weighing between 2.5 and 3.8 metric tons, primarily hunting fish in shallow waters.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
A low dorsal sail running along its back, formed by long neural spines of its vertebrae.

name: Supersaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
It possesses an exceptionally elongated neck and tail, characteristic of diplodocid sauropods, with a shoulder girdle that stands approximately 2.4 meters tall.
It is one of the longest dinosaurs ever discovered, reaching lengths of 33 to over 40 meters, with an estimated body mass of 35 to 44 metric tons, and it was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is famously known as the 'super lizard' and holds the record for being among the longest dinosaurs ever found, often cited in lists of giant prehistoric creatures.

name: Suskityrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moreno Hill Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
Possesses the phylogenetically earliest known arctometatarsalian foot structure among tyrannosauroids, where the third and fourth metatarsals are bound together for most of their length.
A small primitive tyrannosauroid that reached an adult size significantly smaller than later giant tyrannosaurids, with a skull length of approximately 25–32 cm.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a small, early relative of the famous T. rex, representing a juvenile or small-bodied stage in the evolution of the tyrant lizard family.

name: Suuwassea
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A distinctive short, forked neural spine structure on its neck vertebrae, typical of the dicraeosaurid family
An herbivore measuring approximately 15 meters (49 feet) in length and weighing around 5 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
Being a sauropod dinosaur from the Morrison Formation, sharing its ancient habitat with other famous 'thunder lizards' like Apatosaurus

name: Suzhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou / Zhonggou Formation, approximately 110.1–121.4 million years ago.
A prominent keratinous beak, elongated neck, and unusually long claws on its hands.
A large herbivore measuring approximately 6 meters (20 feet) in length and weighing around 3.1 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its specific name, megatherioides, references a resemblance to giant ground sloths, and it is often depicted with a bulky, sloth-like body and a small head.

name: Tachiraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Quinta Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
A uniquely sharp rear-pointing projection on the upper surface of the shinbone (tibia) that extends further back than the inner projection.
A small bipedal carnivore measuring just over 1.5 meters (4.9 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known members of the Neotheropoda group, representing a rare 'stem-averostran' from the Early Jurassic period.

name: Talarurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A tail club with a distinctive woven or basket-like appearance formed by interlaced bony struts, along with heavy armor plates adorned with pleated spines.
A medium-sized, heavily built quadrupedal herbivore measuring 5 to 6 meters in length and weighing between 454 and 907 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as an armored ankylosaurid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of Mongolia, known for its specialized rectangular muzzle used for grazing.

name: Talenkauen
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro Fortaleza Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A set of smooth, ovoid, and extremely thin plates attached along the sides of its rib cage
A small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 4.7 meters in length and weighing around 300 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the very few ornithopods discovered in the southern hemisphere that included a preserved hatchling specimen found alongside the adult

name: Taleta
hints: Fossils were discovered in the upper Couche III layer of the Sidi Chennane locality within the Oulad Abdoun Basin of Morocco, dating to the Maastrichtian age of the Late Cretaceous.
The known remains consist of partial upper jaw bones (maxillae) that feature unusually large teeth and a reduced number of tooth sockets compared to other members of its group.
It was a small-bodied herbivore, with close relatives estimated at around 3.5 meters in length, belonging to the duck-billed dinosaur family Hadrosauridae.
This dinosaur lived in what is now North Africa during the very end of the Cretaceous period, shortly before the mass extinction event.
It is a lambeosaurine, a type of crested duck-billed dinosaur, and is notable for being one of the few hadrosaurids known from the African continent.

name: Tamarro
hints: Fossils recovered from the Talarn Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It is classified as a troodontid, a group of theropods known for having large brains and keen senses, with the only known fossil being a single partial metatarsal bone.
This dinosaur was a small, agile predator that likely fed on insects and small vertebrates, growing rapidly during its early life stages.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It represents a rare discovery of a small theropod from the Late Cretaceous period in Europe, specifically from the Ibero-Armorican Island region of modern-day Spain.

name: Tambatitanis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ohyamashimo Formation, approximately 110.1–113.2 million years ago.
It is a titanosauriform sauropod known from a partial skeleton that includes 22 caudal vertebrae and 17 chevrons, excavated from reddish mudstone.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore was a massive quadruped, with estimates placing its length at around 10 to 12 meters and its weight at several tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the few sauropods discovered in Japan, with its name translating to 'Tamba giant' after the region where the fossils were found.

name: Tameryraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bahariya Formation, approximately 93.9–100.5 million years ago.
A distinctive horn-like protuberance measuring 3 cm in height located on the snout between the nasal bones, accentuated by a depression behind it.
A large, lightly-built carcharodontosaurid theropod estimated to be 8–9 meters long, possessing sturdy vertebrae with air sac depressions and a skull bearing 12 or 13 teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few large theropods discovered in Egypt, with its original holotype specimen famously destroyed during the bombing of Munich in World War II.

name: Tangvayosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Grès supérieurs Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a partial pelvis, several back and tail vertebrae, ribs, and a humerus, with one specimen including 38 tail vertebrae and most of a hind limb.
This basal somphospondylan sauropod measured approximately 15 meters (49 feet) in length and was a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a giant, long-necked, four-legged plant-eating dinosaur from the Cretaceous period, known from fossil remains found in Laos.

name: Tanius
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jingangkou / Jiangjunding Formation, approximately 72.2–89.8 million years ago.
A flat and elongated back of the skull
Approximately 7 meters long and weighing 2 metric tons, this herbivore fed on plants
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A hadrosauroid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of China

name: Tanycolagreus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison Formation, approximately 143.1–154.8 million years ago.
A T-shaped quadratojugal bone, a straight humeral shaft, and a bowed radius distinguish its skeletal anatomy.
This small, lightly built carnivore measured between 3.3 and 4 meters in length and weighed approximately 120 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Jurassic.
It is a slender, long-limbed hunter from the Morrison Formation that closely resembles Coelurus but possesses distinctively longer limbs.

name: Tanystrosuchus
hints: The fossil was discovered in the Middle Stubensandstein formation in present-day Germany and dates to the Norian stage of the late Triassic, approximately 208 million years ago.
The only known specimen is a single neck vertebra, which is insufficient for precise classification, leading scientists to label this creature as a nomen dubium.
It was a theropod dinosaur, meaning it was a bipedal, meat-eating predator that lived alongside early dinosaurs.
This prehistoric animal roamed the supercontinent of Pangaea during the late Triassic period.
Its name literally translates to 'long crocodile,' reflecting the elongated shape of the fossilized neck bone that led to its initial identification.

name: Taohelong
hints: Fossils recovered from the 5 Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
A mirrored 'S'-shaped profile of the outer rim of the ilium and irregular osteoderms forming a sacral shield over the hips
An armored herbivore from the Early Cretaceous of China, known from partial skeletal remains including tail vertebrae, ribs, and hip bones
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a nodosaurid ankylosaurian, it is characterized by its heavy bony armor plating and lack of a tail club

name: Tapuiasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Quiricó Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A nearly complete skull preserved within a partial skeleton, which is a rare find for titanosaurian sauropods
A massive plant-eating titanosaur that lived during the Early Cretaceous period in what is now Brazil
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a giant, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur with a barrel-shaped body, representing the classic sauropod silhouette

name: Tarascosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It belongs to the Abelisauridae family, a group of theropods characterized by short, boxy skulls and reduced forelimbs.
It was a relatively small carnivore, measuring approximately 2.5 to 3 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Tarasque lizard,' referencing a famous devouring monster from Occitan and Spanish folklore.

name: Tarbosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Udurchukan Formation, approximately 66.0–89.8 million years ago.
It possesses the smallest arms relative to body size of all tyrannosaurids, featuring disproportionately tiny, two-fingered hands.
It was a large bipedal apex predator measuring approximately 10 meters long and weighing up to 5 metric tons, feeding on large dinosaurs like ankylosaurids and hadrosaurids.
This dinosaur lived in Asia, Asia/Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is the Asian counterpart to the famous North American giant, sharing nearly identical skull mechanics and being considered by many experts as either a close relative or the same species.

name: Tarchia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt / Baruungoyot Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a heavy, bony tail club used for active defense, along with extensive skin ossifications (osteoderms) covering its broad, low-slung body.
This medium-sized herbivore measured approximately 5.5 to 6.7 meters in length and weighed between 2.5 and 3 metric tons, feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'brainy one,' referencing a skull structure that scientists historically believed indicated a larger brain size compared to its close relatives.

name: Tastavinsaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xert / Forcall Formation, approximately 119.6–121.4 million years ago.
It had a proportionally shorter shin bone, with the tibia measuring only 55% of the femur's length.
This plant-eating giant reached approximately 16 meters in length and weighed around 8 tonnes.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked herbivore from the Early Cretaceous period of Spain.

name: Tatankacephalus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
A domed skull with a large bone ridge running transversely across the back, round eye-sockets, and hollow, conical osteoderms.
An herbivore approximately 7 meters (23 ft) long.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'bison head' due to its distinctive rounded, dome-shaped skull.

name: Tataouinea
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aïn el Guettar Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
Extensive pneumaticity in its bones, indicating a birdlike respiratory system, and classification within the Rebbachisaurinae subfamily.
Approximately 14 meters long and 2.5 meters tall at the hips, this herbivorous sauropod fed on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early Cretaceous.
It is the first dinosaur discovered in Tunisia and its name honors the historical figure Hannibal.

name: Taurovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A strongly rugose postorbital bone that projects outward like a distinct horn, setting it apart from the orbital bosses of other carcharodontosaurids.
A massive carnivore weighing approximately 5.7 metric tons, making it one of the largest terrestrial predators known, though slightly smaller than Giganotosaurus.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a giant, flesh-eating relative of Giganotosaurus and Mapusaurus from the Late Cretaceous of Argentina, often compared to Tyrannosaurus in terms of its apex predator status.

name: Tazoudasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 174.7–192.9 million years ago.
It possesses a primitive, prosauropod-like lower jaw with spatulate teeth bearing denticles, and its front feet feature a prominent, large thumb claw.
This was a small-bodied herbivore measuring approximately 11 to 14 meters in length, making it one of the smallest known sauropods.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is notable for being the only sauropod species named from Africa and one of the few from the Early Jurassic period on the Gondwana supercontinent.

name: Tehuelchesaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cañadón Calcáreo Formation, approximately 143.1–157.9 million years ago.
It possesses dorsal vertebrae with pseudopleurocoels, which are deep depressions in the centra that lack internal chambers, and opisthocoelous centra.
This moderately large sauropod was approximately 15 meters long and was a plant-eater.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, four-legged herbivore from the Late Jurassic period found in Patagonia, Argentina.

name: Telmatosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sînpetru / Densuş-Ciula Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A skull with a relatively short, blunt snout and a lack of the elaborate head crests seen in later duck-billed dinosaurs
A small body size of approximately 5 meters in length and 600 kilograms, feeding on C3 plants, shrubs, and seeds
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Being one of the few dinosaurs known to have lived on an island (Hațeg Island) and exhibiting insular dwarfism

name: Tendaguria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–149.2 million years ago.
Distinctive cavities on the diapophyses of the anterior dorsal vertebrae and a defined ridge on the anteriormost dorsal spines
A large herbivorous sauropod estimated to be twenty meters long
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
A massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period of Tanzania

name: Tengrisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Murtoi Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It possesses strongly procoelous caudal vertebrae with highly pneumatic neural spines and solid bone structure in the centra, characteristic of lithostrotian titanosaurs.
This Early Cretaceous titanosaur was a massive herbivorous sauropod, though only isolated vertebrae from Russia have been identified so far.
This dinosaur lived in Asia/Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the oldest named definitive titanosaurs, representing an early and derived member of the clade Colossosauria.

name: Tenontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly / Antlers Formation, approximately 93.9–121.4 million years ago.
It possessed an unusually long, broad tail that was stiffened by a dense network of bony tendons running along its back and tail vertebrae.
This medium-sized herbivore weighed approximately 1,000 kilograms and measured between 6 to 8 meters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'sinew lizard,' a reference to the extensive system of stiffening tendons found in its skeleton.

name: Teratophoneus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kaiparowits Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

A skull that is proportionally shorter between the lacrimal bone and snout tip but significantly deeper than that of Albertosaurus, potentially allowing for stronger jaw muscles and increased bite force.
An estimated adult length of approximately 8 to 8.7 meters and a weight of around 2.5 tons, classifying it as a large carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a member of the tyrannosaurid family, sharing the iconic large-headed, bipedal predator appearance with its famous relatives like Tyrannosaurus rex.

name: Terminocavus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive heart-shaped upper frill with a very narrow median notch and thin, broad parietal bars
A large herbivore roughly similar in size to Pentaceratops, known from limited skull fragments including a parietal and sacrum
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unique heart-shaped frill which distinguishes it from other horned dinosaurs with wide U-shaped notches

name: Tethyshadros
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aurisina Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses a unique combination of primitive and derived anatomical traits, including a beaked mouth, fleshy hand pads, and three-toed feet, distinguishing it from more advanced hadrosaurids.
This herbivorous dinosaur measured approximately 4.5 meters in length and weighed between 338 and 584 kilograms, representing a subadult and adult individual respectively.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
It is famous for being the most complete large dinosaur skeleton found in Europe since the 19th-century discovery of Iguanodon remains at Bernissart, Belgium.

name: Texasetes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Pawpaw Formation, approximately 100.5–106.3 million years ago.
It possessed a horizontally oriented ilium and a scapula with a prominent acromion, while its teeth featured a unique large ridge leading up to the apex of the crown.
As a nodosaurid ankylosaur, it was covered in large armored osteoderms and lacked a tail club, with an estimated body length typical of medium-sized armored dinosaurs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a lesser-known armored dinosaur from the Cretaceous period of Texas, often overshadowed by more famous relatives like Ankylosaurus or Stegosaurus in popular culture.

name: Thanatotheristes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Foremost Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A broad and rounded sagittal crest on the frontal bone that extends anterior to the supratemporal ridge, along with a prefrontal featuring two posteriorly projecting prongs.
An estimated length of 8 meters (26 feet) as a subadult, belonging to the tyrannosaurid family which were carnivorous predators.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a member of the tyrannosaurid family, closely related to the more famous T-rex and Daspletosaurus, representing a 'reaper of death' from the Late Cretaceous.

name: Thecodontosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Forest Sandstone / Magnesian Conglomerate Formation, approximately 192.9–246.7 million years ago.
Its teeth were set in deep individual sockets rather than being fused directly to the jawbone, a feature reflected in its name meaning 'socket-tooth lizard'.
It was a small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 2 meters (6.6 feet) in length and weighing around 11 kilograms (24 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in Europe, Africa during the Triassic.
It is one of the very first dinosaurs ever discovered and named, originally described in 1836 from fossils found in southern England.

name: Theiophytalia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Purgatoire Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A longer, heavier, and more rugose snout compared to Camptosaurus, along with a wider dorsal process on the maxilla and a proportionally smaller antorbital fenestra.
A herbivorous iguanodontian from the Early Cretaceous of Colorado, approximately 112 million years old, with a skull that is stouter and has a bulbous articulation for the lower jaw.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Garden of the Gods,' referencing the Colorado park where its only known fossil, a partial skull, was discovered in 1878.

name: Therizinosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses the longest known manual unguals (claw bones) of any land animal, reaching over 50 cm in length.
It was a colossal herbivore measuring up to 10 meters long and weighing over 5 tons, equipped with a wide torso and a horny beak.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its massive, scythe-like hand claws are its most iconic feature, which initially led scientists to mistakenly classify the fossils as belonging to a giant turtle.

name: Thescelosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–93.9 million years ago.
It possessed a long, low snout ending in a toothless beak, and its tail was stiffened by rod-like ossified tendons.
It was a small, two-legged herbivore measuring roughly 3 to 4 meters in length and weighing between 200 and 300 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It gained significant media attention in 2000 when a specimen was initially interpreted as containing a fossilized heart.

name: Thyreosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the El Mers III Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
Distinctive oblong dermal armor plates with an asymmetrical texture that were likely arranged recumbent (lying flat) on its back rather than held erect
An adult body length of approximately 6 meters (20 feet) and a herbivorous diet
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
Being a stegosaurian dinosaur known for its unique flat, shield-like back plates instead of the typical upright spikes

name: Tianchisaurus
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Toutunhe Formation of China, dating to the Late Jurassic period (Oxfordian-Kimmeridgian stages).
This dinosaur possessed a bony armor of scutes covering its body but notably lacked the tail club found in many later ankylosaurids.
It was a medium-sized, herbivorous quadruped that moved on all fours.
It lived in what is now Asia during the Late Jurassic era.
It is an armored, plant-eating dinosaur known for its bony skin plates and club-less tail.

name: Tianyulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 152.2–168.2 million years ago.
A row of long, singular, and unbranched filamentous integumentary structures covering the back, neck, and tail.
A small herbivore approximately 70 cm in length with a large head, short neck and forelimbs, and long legs and tail.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.

name: Tianyuraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses unusually short forelimbs, with arms being only 53% of the length of its hindlimbs, and a small, extremely slender wishbone.
It was a medium-sized carnivore that lived approximately 122 million years ago in what is now western Liaoning, China.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a short-armed, bird-like dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period that is considered a transitional species bridging northern and southern dromaeosaurid groups.

name: Tienshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
It is a sauropod dinosaur, characterized by a long neck and tail, though its skull remains unknown due to fragmentary fossil evidence.
This Late Jurassic herbivore reached an estimated body length of twelve meters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a member of the Mamenchisauridae family, a group of sauropods known for having exceptionally long necks relative to their body size.

name: Tietasaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Marfim Formation, approximately 125.8–137.1 million years ago.
A distinctive bone fragment: the distal end of a left femur, which was initially misidentified as belonging to a large crocodile-like reptile.
An ornithopod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of Brazil, known only from a single incomplete leg bone, making its exact size and weight estimates difficult to determine precisely.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
It holds the distinction of being the very first ornithischian dinosaur ever formally named from Brazil.

name: Timimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Eumeralla / Griman Creek Formation, approximately 93.9–113.2 million years ago.
A slender thighbone lacking the extensor groove between the condyles, with a flattened femoral head and a high-positioned anterior trochanter.
Approximately 2.5 to 4.3 meters long and weighing around 200 kilograms, this lithe predator likely hunted small prey in polar forests.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the very few dinosaur fossils ever discovered in Australia, found alongside other species in the southern tip of the continent.

name: Timurlengia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bissekty Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A large, well-developed inner ear with prominent semicircular canals and a long cochlear duct adapted for hearing low-frequency sounds.
A subadult specimen measured approximately 3 to 4 meters in length and weighed between 170 and 270 kilograms, functioning as a pursuit hunter with blade-like teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a key transitional tyrannosauroid that evolved sophisticated sensory abilities and large heads long before its lineage developed the massive body sizes seen in later giants like T. rex.

name: Titanoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fruitland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A massive skull featuring an exceptionally long frill that can extend up to 2.65 meters, making it a candidate for the longest skull of any land animal.
An estimated weight of 6.55 tonnes and a body length of approximately 6.8 meters, classifying it as one of the largest horned dinosaurs to have ever lived.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'titanic horned face,' referencing its status as a giant relative of Triceratops and Pentaceratops from the Late Cretaceous.

name: Titanomachya
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Colonia Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
An astragalus bone modified for improved weight-bearing, showing morphology intermediate between colossosaurs and saltasauroids
A relatively small titanosaurian sauropod measuring approximately 6 meters in length and weighing around 7.8 tonnes, which was a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
One of the last surviving giant herbivores from the end of the Cretaceous period in Patagonia, Argentina

name: Tlatolophus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A comma-shaped supracranial crest on the skull that resembles an Aztec glyph for 'word'
A large herbivore measuring approximately 8 meters (26 feet) in length and weighing nearly 3 metric tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive crest shape, which is unique among hadrosaurids and inspired its name meaning 'word crest'

name: Tochisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Nemegt Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Its foot is functionally didactyl, meaning it has only two weight-bearing toes, similar to an ostrich.
It was a relatively large, bipedal troodontid theropod that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is known as the 'Ostrich lizard' due to the unique two-toed structure of its feet.

name: Tonganosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yimen Formation, approximately 174.7–201.4 million years ago.
It is a mamenchisaurid sauropod with a front-to-rear limb ratio of 0.80, featuring a straight and thick tibia that is 75% the length of its femur.
It was an herbivore that reached an estimated length of about 12 meters (39 feet) in life.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as the oldest known member of the mamenchisaurid family, predating other members of its group by approximately 15 million years.

name: Tongnanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Suining Formation, approximately 143.1–161.5 million years ago.
It possesses a long neck and tail, characteristic of mamenchisaurid sauropods, with specific vertebral structures found in the dorsal and caudal regions.
This herbivore reached an estimated length of 25 to 26 meters, making it one of the largest members of its family.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur discovered in the Late Jurassic rocks of Chongqing, China, and named after the district where it was found.

name: Tongtianlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hekou Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A dome-shaped skull crest with its highest point located just behind the eye socket and a very rounded front edge on the toothless beak.
A sheep-sized omnivore that lived during the late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'Tongtianyan dragon' and it is known for being preserved in a mud-trapped pose with splayed arms.

name: Tornieria
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tendaguru Formation, approximately 143.1–152.2 million years ago.
It possesses plesiomorphic hindlimb proportions characterized by a notably short lower leg, distinguishing it from its close relatives like Diplodocus.
This large sauropod reached approximately 25 meters in length and weighed around 10 metric tons, feeding on vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Africa during the Late Jurassic.
It is a long-necked, quadrupedal dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period, recognized for its elongated neck vertebrae and long forelimbs.

name: Torosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A massive skull featuring an elongated frill with large, window-like openings (fenestrae) and five or more pairs of hornlets along the back edge.
A giant herbivore measuring 7.5 to 9 meters long and weighing up to 11 metric tons, possessing the largest skull of any known land animal.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive frill with large holes, which visually separates it from its close relative that has a solid, solid-frilled head shield.

name: Torvosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Morrison / Lourinhã Formation, approximately 143.1–165.3 million years ago.
It possessed extremely powerful, short forelimbs and notably gigantic thumb claws.
It was a massive carnivore measuring up to 10 meters long and weighing as much as 5 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in North America, Europe, Africa, South America during the Late Jurassic.
It was one of the largest land predators in North America during the Late Jurassic period.

name: Tototlmimus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Packard Shale Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.

It possesses an arctometatarsus, a distinctive foot structure where the third metatarsal bone becomes significantly narrower at the top, and its foot claws are narrow, asymmetric, and feature shallow grooves on both sides.
This medium-sized theropod measured several meters in length and was a bipedal runner that likely fed on a varied diet including plants, small animals, and eggs.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a featherless, ostrich-like dinosaur known for its long legs, long neck, and beak, belonging to the group of dinosaurs that mimicked birds in their appearance and movement.

name: Tralkasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huincul Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A maxilla with a deep body and an antorbital fenestra whose front margin slopes forwards and upwards, along with rod-like parapophyses on the dorsal vertebrae.
A medium-sized predator measuring approximately 6 meters in length, belonging to the abelisaurid family of theropods.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a South American meat-eater from the Late Cretaceous period, closely related to famous short-armed dinosaurs like Carnotaurus and Majungasaurus.

name: Transylvanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A notably wide and flattened skull shape, indicated by the specific name meaning 'wide head'.
A medium-sized herbivorous ornithopod dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its discovery in the Hațeg Basin of Romania, an island ecosystem known for its unique dwarfed dinosaurs.

name: Tratayenia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possessed large, curved claws on its hands, which were likely used as weapons for hunting prey.
It was a medium-sized carnivore measuring up to 8 meters (26 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It was an apex predator in Late Cretaceous South America, closely related to the famous Megaraptor.

name: Traukutitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive flat posterior surface on the centra of its middle tail vertebrae, rather than the convex shape found in many other titanosaurids.
This large titanosaurid sauropod lived during the late Cretaceous period in what is now Argentina and likely consumed vast quantities of vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked herbivorous dinosaur belonging to the titanosaur group, known for its enormous size and pillar-like legs.

name: Triceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lance / Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A large bony frill extending from the back of the skull and three distinct horns: two long ones above the eyes and a shorter one on the nose
A massive, four-legged herbivore measuring approximately 8 to 9 meters in length and weighing up to 10 tons
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its iconic three-horned face and large neck shield, making it one of the most recognizable dinosaurs in popular culture

name: Trierarchuncus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hell Creek Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed short arms ending in a single, large, hook-shaped claw on each hand.
This small, bird-like dinosaur likely weighed only a few kilograms and probably fed on insects or small prey.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the very last non-avian dinosaurs to exist, surviving until the mass extinction event that ended the Cretaceous period.

name: Trinisaura
hints: Fossils recovered from the Snow Hill Island Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A large deltopectoral crest on the humerus (upper arm bone) and a distinctive shape of the ischium (back pelvic bone).
A small, lightly built bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 1.5 meters (4.9 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the few dinosaurs known to have lived in Antarctica during the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Triunfosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Rio Piranhas Formation, approximately 125.8–143.1 million years ago.
A unique ischium where the part touching the pubis is half the height of the entire bone, with the shaft angled at less than 70 degrees to the hip socket
A large, long-necked herbivorous sauropod from the Early Cretaceous of Brazil, likely reaching lengths of several meters
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
A massive, four-legged plant-eater with a long neck and tail, representing one of the earliest known relatives of the heavily armored titanosaurs

name: Tsaagan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Djadokhta Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A robust skull with unique long paroccipital processes and basipterygoids at the back, along with a jugal bone that touches the squamosal
