hints: Fossils recovered from the Winton Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A massive, pillar-like limb structure characteristic of a quadrupedal sauropod, with robust forelimbs and a long neck supported by numerous vertebrae.
A giant herbivore estimated to be around 10 to 12 meters long, weighing several tons, that fed on low-lying vegetation such as ferns and gymnosperms.
This dinosaur lived in Australia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the few sauropods discovered in Australia, sharing its ancient riverine habitat with other titanosauriforms like Diamantinasaurus.

name: Wudingloong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yubacun Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.

A partial skeleton including a damaged skull and mandible, a complete series of ten cervical vertebrae, the first seven dorsal vertebrae, dorsal ribs, the right scapulocoracoid, and the right forelimb with most of the manus
An early-diverging sauropodomorph from the Early Jurassic period, representing the oldest known member of this group in East Asia
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is recognized as the earliest-diverging Asian member of the massopodan sauropodomorph clade

name: Wuerhosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lianmuqin / Ejinhoro Formation, approximately 100.5–137.1 million years ago.
A broad-bodied stegosaur with a pelvis featuring ilia that strongly flare outwards, indicating a very wide belly, and exceptionally tall neural spines at the base of its tail.
The larger species reached approximately 7 meters in length and weighed around 4 metric tons, while the smaller species was about 5 meters long and weighed 1.2 metric tons; both were herbivores.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is one of the youngest known stegosaurs in the fossil record, living in the Early Cretaceous of China, and is closely related to Stegosaurus.

name: Wulagasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yuliangzi Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
A slender dentary (tooth-bearing lower jaw bone) and unique upper arm bone articulations with distinctive muscle attachment placements
Approximately 9 meters (30 feet) long and weighing 3 tonnes, it was a herbivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a duck-billed dinosaur (hadrosaurid) with no hollow crest, belonging to the saurolophine group

name: Wulatelong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wulansuhai Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A toothless, parrot-like beak and the presence of an elaborate cranial crest
A medium-sized theropod, approximately 1.5 to 2 meters long, that was herbivorous or omnivorous
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its distinctive bird-like appearance with a beak and crest, characteristic of the oviraptorid family

name: Xenoceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Foremost Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive bony frill featuring two thick midline knobs, each accompanied by a single long flattened spike pointing laterally and to the rear, with a smooth midline bar lacking bumps.
An adult-sized herbivore from the Late Cretaceous period, likely possessing large brow horns and a long, low nasal structure.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'alien horned face', referencing its unique and unusual frill ornamentation that set it apart from other horned dinosaurs in its region.

name: Xenoposeidon
hints: Fossils recovered from the Ashdown Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.
A single partial posterior back vertebra with a neural arch that leans anteriorly at 35 degrees and a large, teardrop-shaped neural canal
A quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be between 15 and 20 meters long and weighing between 2.8 and 7.6 tonnes
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
A sauropod dinosaur known from only one unusual vertebra that was discovered in the 1890s but ignored for over a century before being formally described

name: Xenotarsosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo Barreal Formation, approximately 89.8–100.5 million years ago.
A uniquely fused ankle bone structure where the astragalus and calcaneum are completely joined without sutures, forming a single element attached to the tibia
A medium-sized carnivore approximately 5.4 to 6 meters long and weighing between 430 and 750 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name literally translates to 'strange ankle lizard', highlighting its most distinctive skeletal feature

name: Xenovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland / Cerro del Pueblo Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A thickened, rugose skull roof with tightly interlocking zig-zag sutures, resembling the dome of a pachycephalosaur.
A small troodontid theropod from the Late Cretaceous of North America, likely a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its unusual domed skull, which suggests it may have engaged in head-butting combat with other members of its species.

name: Xianshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Haoling Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses procoelous caudal vertebrae, meaning the tail bones have a ball-and-socket joint structure with concave front faces and convex back faces, a trait typically associated with titanosaurs.
This was a large herbivorous sauropod, though notably smaller than other giant contemporaries in its region, and it fed on vegetation using spoon-shaped teeth that lacked serrations.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eater from the Early Cretaceous period of China, recognized by its distinctive ball-and-socket tail vertebrae and simple, ridge-marked teeth.

name: Xiaosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 154.8–170.9 million years ago.
A mediolaterally straight humerus, which serves as a unique derived trait for this genus.
A small bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 1 metre in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A diminutive, early ornithischian dinosaur from the Middle Jurassic of China.

name: Xiaotingia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tiaojishan Formation, approximately 152.2–168.2 million years ago.
A small, feathered theropod with long pennaceous feathers covering its head, body, and all four limbs, including unusually long feathers on its hind legs.
Approximately 60 centimeters in length and weighing less than one kilogram, this creature was a carnivore that likely inhabited arboreal environments.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.

Its discovery and classification sparked significant scientific debate and popular headlines suggesting that Archaeopteryx might not be a true bird, due to its close phylogenetic ties to early avialans and dromaeosaurids.

name: Xingtianosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
The holotype specimen is missing its skull, a feature that inspired the genus name referencing a headless Chinese deity.
This small theropod measured approximately one meter in length and was an omnivore or herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a member of the Caudipteridae family, known for having long, pennaceous feathers on their tails.

name: Xingxiulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 174.7–201.4 million years ago.
A sacrum containing four vertebrae, a pubis with an exceptionally long top portion, and wide, robust femurs and scapulae.
A medium-to-large bipedal herbivore, with adults of the type species measuring 4–5 meters long and a larger second species reaching 8–10 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a basal sauropodomorph from the Early Jurassic of China that exhibits a mix of primitive traits and early sauropod-like adaptations for supporting high body weight.

name: Xinjiangovenator
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lianmuqin Formation, approximately 100.5–137.1 million years ago.

A distinctive anatomical feature is the lateral condyle of the tibia, which extends further backward than the outer edge of the tibia near the knee, along with a longitudinal groove on the front edge of the proximal fibula.
This small coelurosaurian dinosaur is estimated to have reached a length of 3 meters and weighed approximately 70 kilograms, indicating it was a lightweight, likely carnivorous hunter.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
As a member of the Maniraptora group, it shares close evolutionary ties with birds and dromaeosaurids, representing a small, agile predator from the Early Cretaceous period of China.

name: Xinjiangtitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Qiketai Formation, approximately 143.1–168.2 million years ago.
A distinctive ventral keel on the penultimate cervical vertebra that forms a small semicircular process, along with an extremely robust femur and relatively short hind limbs.
A massive herbivore measuring 30 to 32 meters in length and weighing up to 40 metric tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Possessing the longest complete neck ever discovered for any animal, stretching nearly 15 meters.

name: Xiongguanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
A very elongate pre-orbital region of the skull and a short, concave sagittal crest on the fused parietal bones.
An estimated length of about 5 metres (16 ft) and a weight of approximately 200 to 270 kilograms, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is considered a noteworthy transitional species between the basal tyrannosauroids of the Jurassic period and the derived tyrannosaurids of the Late Cretaceous.

name: Xixianykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Majiacun Formation, approximately 83.6–89.8 million years ago.

It possessed highly specialized, elongated hind legs with a modified foot structure (arctometatarsalian pes) designed for speed, along with tiny, robust arms ending in a single large claw on each hand.
This tiny dinosaur measured approximately 50 centimeters in length and weighed less than one kilogram, and it likely fed on colonial insects using a narrow, tubular snout.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a small, feathered theropod from the Late Cretaceous period of China, notable for its bird-like running adaptations and distinctive single-clawed hands characteristic of alvarezsaurids.

name: Xixiasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Majiacun Formation, approximately 72.2–89.8 million years ago.
Its teeth lacked the serrated edges found in most other troodontids, featuring smooth and sharp carinae instead, and its lower jaw had a distinctively down-turned front.
It was a small, lightly built dinosaur approximately 1.5 meters long and weighing around 8 kilograms, likely feeding on plants given its unserrated teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It possessed the classic bird-like body plan of a troodontid, including grasping hands and a large, sickle-shaped claw on its second toe.

name: Xixiposaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 192.9–201.4 million years ago.
A member of the prosauropod group, characterized by a long neck and likely possessing a robust, herbivorous skull structure typical of early sauropodomorphs.
An early Jurassic herbivore from China, estimated to be a medium-sized dinosaur with a body length of approximately 4 to 5 meters.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a lesser-known, obscure dinosaur from the Lufeng Formation that is rarely featured in popular media or museums compared to more famous contemporaries like Plateosaurus.

name: Xiyunykus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shengjinkou Formation, approximately 132.6–137.1 million years ago.
It possesses an intermediate forelimb structure that is neither fully typical of early theropods nor as highly reduced as later alvarezsaurids, featuring a unique claw morphology.
This Early Cretaceous theropod was a small carnivore, with a body length estimated at around one meter.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a key transitional fossil that bridges the 70-million-year evolutionary gap between primitive alvarezsauroids and their specialized, short-armed descendants.

name: Xuanhanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
It retains a vestigial fourth metacarpal in its hand, a primitive trait rarely seen in later theropods.
It was a medium-sized carnivore measuring approximately 4.8 meters in length and weighing around 265 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the few theropods historically suggested by its discoverer to have potentially walked on all four legs.

name: Xuanhuaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Tuchengzi Formation, approximately 132.6–152.2 million years ago.
It possesses a single tooth on each premaxilla, distinguishing it from close relatives that have two.
It was a small dinosaur, approximately one meter long and weighing around six kilograms, likely capable of facultative bipedalism.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is recognized as one of the earliest known members of the horned dinosaur lineage (Ceratopsia).

name: Xunmenglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Huajiying Formation, approximately 125.8–132.6 million years ago.
Possesses a pelvis, tail base, and hindlimbs that were originally found mixed with fossils of other animals in a chimera specimen
Measures approximately 50 centimeters in length and was a small carnivore
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Identified as one of the smallest known members of its dinosaur family

name: Xuwulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xiagou Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a complete cranium and an almost complete axial skeleton, characteristic of a hadrosauroid with a robust skull structure.
This Early Cretaceous herbivore lived in what is now Gansu Province, China, and represents a basal member of the duck-billed dinosaur lineage.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is an early ancestor-type of the famous duck-billed dinosaurs, recognizable by its beaked mouth and plant-eating adaptations.

name: Yamaceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Javkhlant Formation, approximately 72.2–85.7 million years ago.
It possessed a primitive ceratopsian skull with a small, unadorned frill and lacked the large horns or extensive bony ornamentation seen in later relatives.
It was exceptionally small for a ceratopsian, measuring only about 50 centimeters (1.6 feet) in length and weighing approximately 2 kilograms (4.4 pounds).
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the smallest known horned dinosaurs, often described as being roughly the size of a large chicken or turkey.

name: Yamanasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Río Playas Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possessed a protective armor made of osteoderms, a feature shared with other saltasaurine sauropods.
It was a relatively small, stocky quadrupedal herbivore, estimated to be around 6 meters (20 feet) long.
This dinosaur lived in during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the first non-avian dinosaur to be scientifically described from the country of Ecuador.

name: Yamatosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kita-ama Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
It possesses a distinctive set of teeth adapted for grinding plant material, characteristic of a basal member of the duck-billed dinosaur family.
This herbivorous dinosaur reached an estimated length of approximately 9 meters and weighed around 2 tons.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is recognized as one of the very few dinosaur species discovered and named from Japan, specifically from Awaji Island.

name: Yanbeilong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zuoyun Formation, approximately 100.5–113.2 million years ago.
A row of large, bony plates running along its back and a pair of spikes on its tail.
A large, quadrupedal herbivore estimated to be several meters long.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
The distinctive silhouette of a dinosaur with plates on its back and spikes on its tail.

name: Yandusaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 168.2–170.9 million years ago.
Its teeth feature a unique pattern of parallel vertical ridges that Chinese researchers compared to the fingers of Buddha statues.
It was a fast-moving biped herbivore that grew to approximately three to three point eight meters in length and weighed around one hundred forty kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a small, agile, two-legged plant-eater from the Jurassic period with large eyes and four toes on each foot.

name: Yangchuanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao / Suining Formation, approximately 113.2–170.9 million years ago.
It possessed a distinctive bony ridge on its nose along with multiple hornlets and ridges, a feature similar to Ceratosaurus.
This large theropod measured between 8 and 11 meters in length and weighed approximately 3 to 3.4 metric tons, functioning as a powerful bipedal carnivore with serrated teeth.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is often described as the Asian equivalent of Allosaurus, sharing a similar body plan and occupying the role of the apex predator in the Middle to Late Jurassic ecosystems of China.

name: Yantaloong
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Middle Jurassic Zhanghe Formation of China.
It is a large, long-necked eusauropod dinosaur known only from six presacral vertebrae.
This massive herbivore likely reached lengths of over 15 meters and weighed several tons.
It lived in Asia during the Middle Jurassic period, approximately 165 to 170 million years ago.
It is a giant, four-legged plant-eater with a very long neck and tail.

name: Yaverlandia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Wessex Formation, approximately 121.4–132.6 million years ago.
The skull features two small domes, one on each frontal bone, with a dorsal surface covered in small pits.
It is a small theropod dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous period of the Isle of Wight.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Early Cretaceous.
Its classification has been heavily debated, with early researchers believing it was an early relative of the dome-headed pachycephalosaurs.

name: Yehuecauhceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Aguja Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive ridge near the bottom of the squamosal bone featuring a single roughened protuberance that likely supported a small horn.
A small herbivorous dinosaur measuring approximately 3 meters (9.8 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its classification as a horned dinosaur with a bony frill, belonging to the centrosaurine group known for their elaborate cranial ornaments.

name: Yeneen
hints: Fossils recovered from the Bajo de la Carpa Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
It is a titanosaurian sauropod, characterized by a massive, quadrupedal body plan with a long neck and tail, typical of the Saltasauridae family.
This dinosaur reached an estimated length of 10 to 12 meters and weighed several tons, feeding exclusively on low-lying vegetation as a herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of several recently discovered titanosaur species found in the Bajo de la Carpa Formation of Argentina, contributing to the known diversity of Late Cretaceous sauropods in that region.

name: Yi (dinosaur)
hints: Fossils were discovered in the Tiaojishan Formation of Hebei, China, dating to approximately 159 million years ago during the Late Jurassic.
It possessed a unique, elongated bony strut extending from the wrist, known as a styliform element, which supported its wing membrane.
This dinosaur was very small, weighing only about 380 grams, and likely fed on insects or small prey.
It lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic period, roughly 159 million years ago.
It is famous for having bat-like wings made of skin membranes rather than feathers, a trait unique among all known dinosaurs.

name: Yimenosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 199.5–201.4 million years ago.
It is a plateosaurid sauropodomorph with a relatively complete skull and mandible preserved in the fossil record.
It reached an approximate length of 9 meters and weighed around 2 tonnes, feeding as a plant-eating dinosaur.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the most significant early sauropodomorphs discovered in China, representing a key piece of Early Jurassic paleontology in Yunnan Province.

name: Yingshanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
A row of large, paired semi-triangular plates along the neck and back, accompanied by a prominent shoulder spine and likely a tail equipped with defensive spikes.
A medium-sized, quadrupedal herbivore measuring approximately 4 to 5 meters in length, characterized by short forelimbs and long hindlimbs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
The distinctive silhouette created by the double row of bony plates running along its back and the spiked tail used for defense.

name: Yinlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.
A virtually frill-less and totally hornless skull that lacks the prominent horns and large neck frills seen in its later relatives.
A small herbivore measuring approximately 1.2 meters in length and weighing around 10 kilograms, likely walking on two legs.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
Being the earliest known member of the horned dinosaur family, representing the primitive ancestor of the famous Triceratops.

name: Yixianosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses an exceptionally elongated hand where the digits are 140% the length of the humerus, with the second finger being the longest and tipped with large, recurved claws.
This small theropod measured approximately 1 meter (3.3 feet) in total length and weighed around 1 kilogram (2.2 pounds), likely feeding on small prey.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a feathered dinosaur from the Early Cretaceous of China, notable for its long arms and fingers which suggest it may have been adapted for climbing or limited aerial locomotion.

name: Yizhousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng Formation, approximately 192.9–199.5 million years ago.
A nearly complete and exquisitely preserved skeleton including an intact skull, which is rare for basal sauropods.
A basal sauropodiform from the Lower Jurassic of China, measuring several meters in length and feeding on low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Being one of the most complete early long-necked dinosaur specimens ever found, with a well-preserved skull.

name: Yongjinglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the 5 Formation, approximately 113.2–125.8 million years ago.
Its premaxillary teeth are long and spoon-shaped, and its middle dorsal vertebrae feature ridges that form a distinctive 'XI' pattern.
It was a medium-sized sauropod from the Early Cretaceous period of China.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur known as a titanosaur.

name: Ypupiara
hints: Fossils recovered from the Serra da Galga Formation, approximately 66.0–72.2 million years ago.
Non-serrated, conical teeth similar to those of gavialoid crocodylians
A small theropod dinosaur that likely fed on fish
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Late Cretaceous.
The first dromaeosaurid discovered in South America

name: Yuanmouraptor
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhanghe Formation, approximately 168.2–174.7 million years ago.

A skull bone forming the back of the eye socket features a sheet-shaped process at the front and a ventral ramus with a laterally twisted trough, alongside cervical vertebrae with strongly elongated projections above the back articular process.
A medium-sized carnivore with a reconstructed skull length of approximately 60 centimeters, placing it in the same size range as other large theropods from the Middle Jurassic period.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is a member of the Metriacanthosauridae family, a group of large, bipedal predators known for their robust builds and presence in Asian fossil records.

name: Yuanmousaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhanghe Formation, approximately 161.5–174.7 million years ago.
Possesses elongated neck vertebrae characteristic of mamenchisaurids, with forelimbs proportionally longer than those of short-necked basal sauropods but shorter than those of Omeisaurus
A relatively large sauropod estimated to reach approximately 17 meters (56 feet) in length, classified as a basal member of the Eusauropoda
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
Known from incomplete remains discovered in 2000 in Yuanmou County, China, with its exact systematic position remaining unclear and potentially dubious among paleontologists

name: Yuanyanglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Miaogou Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A distinctive toothless, beaked skull with a short, deep snout typical of oviraptorosaurs.
A small theropod, likely under 2 meters in length, that was an omnivore or herbivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Its name translates to 'lovebird dragon,' referencing the discovery of two associated skeletons found together, symbolizing a pair.

name: Yueosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Liangtoutang Formation, approximately 93.9–113.2 million years ago.
Possesses a partial but well-preserved articulated postcranial skeleton including cervical, dorsal, and caudal vertebrae, scapula, ribs, hip bones, and partial limbs.
A basal neornithischian ornithopod from the Cretaceous period of China, representing the southernmost basal ornithopod known from Asia at the time of its discovery.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the first basal ornithopod dinosaur discovered in China and is considered the southernmost example of its kind in Asia.

name: Yunganglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhumapu Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A deep, U-shaped, intercondylar extensor groove on the femur that is partially enclosed by the expansion of the medial and lateral condyles.
A ground-dwelling herbivore capable of walking on both two and four legs, with a skull that is relatively wide and low.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the hadrosauroid group, representing an early evolutionary stage before the development of the elaborate crests seen in later duck-billed dinosaurs.

name: Yunmenglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Haoling Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
It possesses a massive, pillar-like right femur measuring approximately 1.92 meters in length, characteristic of a giant quadrupedal herbivore.
This giant sauropod reached an estimated length of 20 to 27 meters and weighed around 30 tons, feeding on high-growing vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a massive, long-necked dinosaur from the Cretaceous period of China, belonging to the same broad group as Titanosaurs but representing a more basal lineage.

name: Yunnanosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Lufeng / Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 184.2–201.4 million years ago.
It possesses over sixty spoon-shaped teeth that are unique among early sauropodomorphs because they are self-sharpening, wearing against each other as the animal fed.
It was a large, moderately-built quadrupedal herbivore that could also walk bipedally, with the largest species reaching approximately 13 meters (42 feet) in length.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is one of the earliest known giant plant-eating dinosaurs from the Early Jurassic period, discovered in the Lufeng Formation of Yunnan Province, China.

name: Yunyangosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Xintiangou Formation, approximately 170.9–184.2 million years ago.
It possesses a partial skeleton including eleven presacral vertebrae, cervical and dorsal ribs, and chevrons.
This theropod reached an approximate length of 4.7 meters (15.4 feet) and was a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a relatively obscure megalosauroid from the Late Jurassic of China, known primarily from fragmentary fossil remains rather than a complete skeleton.

name: Yurgovuchia
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cedar Mountain Formation, approximately 132.6–139.1 million years ago.

It is a dromaeosaurid theropod distinguished by specific vertebral features, including cervical vertebrae with epipophyses located above the postzygapophyseal facets and caudal vertebrae with round cranial centrum faces.
This adult dinosaur measured approximately 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) in length and was a carnivore that lived during the Early Cretaceous period.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
It is a smaller, closely related cousin of the massive Utahraptor, sharing the same bone bed in Utah's Cedar Mountain Formation but representing a distinct, smaller species of raptor-like predator.

name: Yutyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A high midline crest on the snout formed by the nasals and premaxillae, along with distinctive wavy crests on the sides of the snout.
A large bipedal predator measuring approximately 9 meters (30 feet) in length and weighing around 1.4 tonnes, classified as a carnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is famous for being the largest known dinosaur species to have direct fossil evidence of feathers, with filamentous plumage preserved on its body.

name: Yuxisaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Fengjiahe Formation, approximately 184.2–192.9 million years ago.
Possesses over 120 bony armor plates called osteoderms, including distinctive 'pup-tent' shaped plates on the tail and keeled, spine-like structures fused to the neck vertebrae.
An early armored dinosaur from the Early Jurassic period, likely herbivorous, with a body size typical of basal thyreophorans.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early/Middle Jurassic.
It is a primitive relative of later armored dinosaurs like ankylosaurs and stegosaurs, representing one of the earliest known examples of body armor in the dinosaur lineage.

name: Yuzhoulong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shaximiao Formation, approximately 161.5–168.2 million years ago.
A massive, long-necked quadruped with a partial skull and postcranial skeleton characteristic of basal macronarian sauropods
An immature individual estimated to be a large herbivore typical of Middle Jurassic sauropods
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
A giant, four-legged plant-eater with an extremely long neck and tail

name: Zalmoxes
hints: Fossils recovered from the Sînpetru / Sebeş Formation, approximately 66.0–83.6 million years ago.
A large, triangular-shaped head equipped with a sharp, beak-like mouthpart for cropping vegetation
A small, bipedal herbivore measuring approximately 2 to 2.5 meters in length and weighing around 45 kilograms
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Cretaceous.
Its name is derived from Zalmoxis, a deity from ancient Thracian mythology who retreated to a crypt for three years before being resurrected

name: Zapalasaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the La Amarga Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
Distinctive cervical neural arches and caudal vertebrae that double in length from front to back
A long-necked, ground-level browsing herbivore belonging to the Rebbachisauridae family
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Early Cretaceous.
An Early Cretaceous sauropod discovered in Patagonia, Argentina, named after the city of Zapala

name: Zapsalis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Milk River / Lance Formation, approximately 66.0–85.7 million years ago.
The fossils consist exclusively of teeth, which feature a straight rear edge, vertical grooves, and rounded denticles, with the type specimen lacking serrations on the front edge.
It was a small, carnivorous dromaeosaurid theropod, with the holotype tooth measuring only 12 millimeters in length.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a tooth taxon often considered dubious due to the fragmentary nature of its fossils, and its teeth were later identified as belonging to the second premaxillary tooth of Saurornitholestes.

name: Zaraapelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Baynshire Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
A robust, pyramid-shaped squamosal horn on the rear corner of the skull with a sharp cutting edge that abruptly changes from smooth bone to a rough, rugose texture.
A medium-sized herbivore with a skull approximately forty centimeters long, belonging to the heavily armored Ankylosauridae family.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name translates to 'hedgehog shield', referencing the prickly, osteoderm-covered appearance typical of its armored body.

name: Zavacephale
hints: Fossils recovered from the Khuren Dukh Formation, approximately 100.5–119.6 million years ago.

A well-developed dome structure on the skull formed primarily by the frontal bone with very little involvement of the parietal bone, along with a tail covered in ossified tendons that did not yet form a rigid 'caudal basket'.
An immature individual measuring approximately 1 metre (3.3 ft) in length and weighing around 5.85 kilograms (12.9 lb), likely a bipedal herbivore or omnivore.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
Being the oldest definitive member of its group, dating back to the Early Cretaceous period, which is significantly older than the Late Cretaceous species most people recognize.

name: Zby
hints: Fossils recovered from the Praia da Amoreira-Porto Novo / Lourinhã Formation, approximately 149.2–152.2 million years ago.
A prominent posteriorly projecting ridge on the humerus at the level of the deltopectoral crest, along with extreme anteroposterior compression of the proximal radius.
An herbivore measuring approximately 16 to 18 meters (52 to 59 feet) in length that likely fed on cycads, ferns, and low-lying vegetation.
This dinosaur lived in Europe during the Late Jurassic.
It is a large, long-necked sauropod dinosaur from the Late Jurassic period of Portugal.

name: Zephyrosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Cloverly Formation, approximately 100.5–121.4 million years ago.
A steep face featuring a raised knob on the upper jaw and a larger boss on the cheekbone, along with evidence of cranial kinesis allowing movement within the skull.
A small, bipedal herbivore approximately 1 to 2 meters long that likely fed on low-lying vegetation using beak-like teeth.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Early Cretaceous.
Being a small, swift, and agile ornithopod from the Early Cretaceous period, often associated with burrowing behavior similar to its relative Orodromeus.

name: Zhanghenglong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Majiacun Formation, approximately 83.6–85.7 million years ago.
A strongly deflected posterior third of the maxilla relative to the anterior two-thirds, along with dentary teeth featuring crowns with both median and distally offset primary ridges
Approximately 6 to 7 meters long, weighing around 1.5 tons, and a herbivore that likely fed on low-lying vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
A primitive 'duck-billed' dinosaur from China that represents a key evolutionary link between early iguanodonts and the more famous, advanced hadrosaurids

name: Zhenyuanlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
It possesses extremely short arms with a proportionally thin radius, yet these tiny limbs support massive, broad wings with a surface area of about one square meter each.
It was a mid-sized predator, estimated to reach up to 2 meters in total length when including its tail feathers, and weighed approximately 10 to 15 kilograms.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.

It is famously known as the 'chicken with wings' due to its paradoxical combination of a stout, chicken-like body and disproportionately huge, bird-like wings that could not have been used for flight.

name: Zhongjianosaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Jiufotang Formation, approximately 113.2–121.4 million years ago.
Proportionally long ossified uncinate processes fused to the dorsal ribs, a widely arched furcula with slender posteriorly curved clavicular rami, and an arctometatarsalian foot structure.
Weighing only 0.31 kg (0.68 lb) and measuring as one of the smallest known non-avian theropods, this creature was likely omnivorous and arboreal.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
It is recognized as the smallest known member of the Microraptoria, a group of feathered dinosaurs famous for having four wings.

name: Zhongornis
hints: Fossils recovered from the Yixian Formation, approximately 119.6–125.8 million years ago.
A primitive bird skeleton with a beaked mouth, no teeth, a short tail of thirteen vertebrae lacking a fused pygostyle, and a third finger with only two phalanges
A juvenile specimen approximately eight centimeters in length that likely ate small prey typical of early avialans
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Early Cretaceous.
A tiny, feathered fossil from China that represents a crucial evolutionary link between long-tailed dinosaurs like Archaeopteryx and modern birds

name: Zhuchengceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A particularly massive and deep 50-centimeter-long mandible that is thin transversely
Approximately 2.5 meters in length and weighing around 175 kilograms, this herbivore fed on vegetation
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a small, horned-face dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous period of China, known for its unexpected articulated skeleton discovery

name: Zhuchengtitan
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive humerus with an extremely wide top end and a prominent deltopectoral crest that features a hook-like process about one-third of the way down its length.
A massive titanosaurian sauropod, likely measuring over 10 meters in length based on its robust humerus, that was a herbivore living in Late Cretaceous floodplains.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is one of the giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaurs from China that lived alongside the massive duck-billed Shantungosaurus and the giant tyrant Zhuchengtyrannus.

name: Zhuchengtyrannus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Hongtuya Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A distinctive horizontal shelf on the lateral surface of the base of the ascending process of the maxilla and a rounded notch in the anterior margin of the maxillary fenestra.
A large carnivore estimated to be about 10 meters long and weighing 5 metric tons, with teeth featuring serrations that extend all the way to the base of the tooth crown.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
Being a massive, Asian relative of the Tyrannosaurus rex, often described as a 'tyrant' from the Late Cretaceous period.

name: Ziapelta
hints: Fossils recovered from the Kirtland Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
It possesses deep, anteriorly curved squamosal horns on the rear corners of its skull and a large, triangular central bone plate on its snout.
It was a heavily armored herbivore, likely reaching several meters in length, protected by bony osteoderms embedded in its skin.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a type of 'armored dinosaur' known for its low-slung, tank-like body covered in bony plates and spikes.

name: Zigongosaurus
hints: Shaximiao Formation, Sichuan, China, Middle to Late Jurassic (approx. 160-145 million years ago)
Sauropod with distinctive weak bifurcation (splitting) of the vertebral neural spines
Large quadrupedal herbivore, approximately 15 meters (50 feet) long
Found in Asia (China) during the Jurassic Period
A giant, long-necked plant-eating dinosaur

name: Zuniceratops
hints: Fossils recovered from the Moreno Hill Formation, approximately 89.8–93.9 million years ago.
A long, low skull featuring a well-developed pair of brow horns but completely lacking a nasal horn
A relatively small ceratopsian measuring 2.2 to 3.5 meters in length and weighing around 175 kilograms, likely a plant-eater
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Discovered by an eight-year-old child, this dinosaur represents a key evolutionary link between early horned dinosaurs and later giants with large frills

name: Zuolong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Shishugou Formation, approximately 154.8–161.5 million years ago.

A skull with a triangular shape that tapers significantly toward the snout, featuring large lateral-facing orbits and a pronounced anterior process of the lacrimal bone that creates the appearance of a small crest above the eyes.
A small, lightweight bipedal carnivore estimated to be between 3 and 3.35 meters long and weighing around 43 to 50 kilograms, with d-shaped premaxillary teeth similar to tyrannosauroids.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Jurassic.
It is one of the oldest known coelurosaurs, discovered in the Late Jurassic period of China, representing a rare glimpse into early coelurosaur evolution from that era.

name: Zuoyunlong
hints: Fossils recovered from the Zhumapu Formation, approximately 83.6–100.5 million years ago.
The back sheet of its ilium is uniquely short, measuring only half the length of the main body, and its ischium shaft ends in a large, downward-pointing 'foot'.
This herbivorous ornithopod had a preserved ilium length of 62 centimeters and lived during the Late Cretaceous period in what is now China.
This dinosaur lived in Asia during the Late Cretaceous.
It is a basal member of the Hadrosauroidea group, representing one of the earliest known relatives of the duck-billed dinosaurs outside of Asia.

name: Zupaysaurus
hints: Fossils recovered from the Los Colorados Formation, approximately 205.7–227.3 million years ago.
A distinctive kink in the snout between the premaxillary and maxillary bones of the upper jaw, and a highly pneumatized antorbital recess in the lacrimal bone.
A medium-sized bipedal predator approximately 4 to 6 meters long and weighing between 70 and 250 kilograms, feeding on other animals.
This dinosaur lived in South America during the Triassic.
The original description featured two thin parallel crests running along the top of the skull, similar to those of Dilophosaurus, though this feature is now debated.

name: Zuul
hints: Fossils recovered from the Judith River Formation, approximately 72.2–83.6 million years ago.
A flat, vertically flattened tail club with a height less than a fifth of its length, and squamosal horns on the rear corners of the skull featuring conspicuous longitudinal grooves.
An herbivore estimated to be 6 meters (20 feet) long, known for preserving soft tissues like keratin and skin, earning it the nickname 'dinosaur mummy'.
This dinosaur lived in North America during the Late Cretaceous.
Its generic name was inspired by the demon 'Zuul' from the 1984 movie Ghostbusters because its head resembles the creature's appearance in the film.
